about: Machine-readable blurbs for the DOMY symbolic cosmology. The narrative canon lives in SYMBOLISM.md; these one-to-three-sentence texts are the extended info the app may surface (hovers, info panels, docs).

philosophy.white_light: White light is the sum of all colors. Sunday sits in the center because the Sun is not one color among six - the six arms are sunlight decomposed, the star is the prism, Sunday is the source.
philosophy.paint_and_light: Before Newton the world knew color as pigment: yellow, red and blue, mixed into orange, green and purple - the painter's wheel of six. Newton's prism opened the second world: red, green and blue light with cyan, magenta and yellow between them. DOMY's 'paint' and 'light' palette styles are exactly these two worlds.
philosophy.gold_and_silver: Alchemy gives one metal to each luminary: gold to the Sun, silver to the Moon. On the dial the top is solar noon and the bottom is midnight, so gold crowns the top tip and silver the bottom - the day metal above, the night metal below.
philosophy.cross_cure: Every body carries a bright and a dark face - a virtue and a vice. Across each axis of the star, one body's virtue is the cure for the opposite body's vice.
philosophy.professions: Medieval astrology drew the 'children of the planets': each planet rules the callings born under it. The seven archetypal professions of the old world land one per body, and each snaps onto its body's virtue and vice.
philosophy.theological_trio: Faith, Hope and Love are a separate set from the seven virtue/vice pairs (Love alone belongs to both, as in tradition). They span three kinds of places: Faith at the center (Sunday, toward God - the vertical), Hope on the dawn arm (west, toward what comes - the horizon), Love on Venus' arm (toward people - the human).
philosophy.religion_alternates: The owner's art holds fourteen religions in two full sets. The basic seven sit one per body: Freemasonry on Sunday (the quest for Light fits the source better than the war-day it left), Islam on Monday (the purely lunar calendar), Buddhism on Tuesday (Mara's army met and beaten without a weapon), Taoism on Wednesday (unchanged - the water-star day), Hinduism on Thursday (unchanged - Guruvara, Jupiter's own day), Christianity on Friday (Good Friday, agape, forgiveness over punishment), Judaism on Saturday (unchanged - Shabbat, Shabtai is Saturn itself). The alternate seven each take the day they fit best: Mithraism on Sunday (the Unconquered Sun, dies Solis for its holy day), Druidism on Monday (lunar reckoning, groves and tides), Zoroastrianism on Tuesday (the fire Atar, asha as the courage of truth), Shamanism on Wednesday (the walker between worlds on the messenger's day), Sikhism on Thursday (Guruvar taken literally - ten Gurus, langar generosity), Babylon on Friday (Ishtar IS Venus; Babylon invented the planetary week), Voodoo on Saturday (Baron Samedi, 'Baron Saturday', keeper of the crossroads).

blurbs.day: Sunday is the source, not one day among six: as white light contains every color, the week's center contains every day.
blurbs.profession: The Sun is the royal planet, and its child is the Ruler - whose lesson is Humility and whose sin is Pride.
blurbs.greek: Helios drives the sun chariot across the sky each day; the Greeks named this day hemera Heliou, the day of the Sun.
blurbs.norse: Sol (Sunna) is the Norse sun goddess, chased across the sky by the wolf Skoll; sunnudagr is her day.
blurbs.religion: Christianity worships on the Lord's Day; in 321 Constantine made the 'day of the Sun' the day of rest, fusing Sun and Church in history. Sunday is also the day of Faith - with Hope at dawn and Love on Venus, the three theological virtues span the dial.
blurbs.color: White-gold: the sum of all colors and the Sun's own metal.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Pride is the self at the center of everything; Humility is its only cure. The center heals itself.
blurbs.religion_alt: Rome's soldier-mysteries crowned the Unconquered Sun: seven grades of initiation under the seven planets, dies Solis for their holy day — the same Sunday this dial keeps at its white-gold center.
blurbs.egypt: Ra is the sun itself, not its god: sailing the day barque from horizon to horizon, dying into the Duat each night and reborn at every dawn. Sunday's white-gold center is his oldest throne — no pantheon fused sun, king and temple more completely.
blurbs.slavic: Dažbog is the giving sun whose grandchildren the Slavs called themselves — he pours out grain, gold and warmth from the center and keeps nothing back.
blurbs.alchemy: Gold, the sun made solid, never rusts and never tarnishes — the incorruptible metal that is the goal of the whole Great Work.
blurbs.japan: Nichiyobi is the Sun's day — 日, the very glyph that opens the name of Japan — ruled by Amaterasu and heralded by Yatagarasu, the three-legged crow who rides the solar disc.

blurbs.day: Monday is the Moon's day in every tongue that kept the planetary week - Selene's day in Greek, Mani's in Norse, Somvar in Sanskrit.
blurbs.profession: Medieval medicine followed the Moon - bloodletting calendars ran on its phases - and the physician keeps the night watch at the sickbed: a calm hand against the patient's fear. (The Moon's traditional alternate child is the Sailor, ruler of tides.)
blurbs.greek: Selene is the Titan goddess of the Moon, crossing the night sky in a silver chariot drawn by white horses.
blurbs.norse: Mani steers the Moon through the night, hunted by the wolf Hati until the world's end.
blurbs.religion: The Buddhist calendar is lunar: Uposatha observance days follow the Moon's phases, and Vesak - the Buddha's birth, awakening and passing - falls on a full moon.
blurbs.color: Blue is the still hour before dawn: moonlight on a sleeping world.
blurbs.virtue_vice: The Moon's bright face is Serenity, its dark face Fear - the night brings both peace and dread. Courage, from the opposite arm, is Fear's cure.
blurbs.religion_alt: The druids reckoned time by the Moon - groves, tides and the lunar count of nights; the serenity of the sacred wood, and the fear of its dark.
blurbs.egypt: Khonsu, "the traveller", is the moon crossing the night sky — Thebes' young healer and reckoner of months, wrapped like the dead yet carrying the regalia of life; the serenity of the crossing, and the old fear of the dark it passes through.
blurbs.slavic: Hors is the pale light of the night, the moon-disc whose path the werewolf-prince Vseslav crossed as he ran through the dark before dawn.
blurbs.alchemy: Silver mirrors the light it is given and blackens in the air, keeping the Moon's own habit of waxing bright and waning dark.
blurbs.japan: Getsuyobi is the Moon's day, ruled by Tsukuyomi, where Japan sees not a man in the moon but tsuki no usagi, the rabbit forever pounding mochi at his mortar.

blurbs.day: Tuesday belongs to the red planet: Ares' day to the Greeks, Tyr's day (tysdagr) to the Norse, Mangalvar in the Vedic week.
blurbs.profession: Mars' child is the Soldier, whose two faces are the body's virtue and vice themselves: Courage in defense, Wrath unleashed.
blurbs.greek: Ares is the storm of battle itself - the Greeks honored his ferocity and feared his rage in the same breath.
blurbs.norse: Tyr, god of war AND justice, gave his right hand to bind the wolf Fenrir - courage in the service of order.
blurbs.religion: Freemasonry is sworn on oaths - and Tyr, this arm's god, gave his hand to seal one. The square and compasses build the inner temple with a soldier's discipline: Courage set to work, Wrath bound by ritual.
blurbs.color: Orange is the working afternoon: the ember of the day's fire.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Mars carries both Courage and Wrath - the same fire that defends can destroy. Serenity, from the Moon's arm, quells Wrath; Courage cures the Moon's Fear.
blurbs.religion_alt: Zoroastrianism keeps the sacred fire Atar burning, and its heart is asha - truth and justice - the courage of the flame against the lie.
blurbs.egypt: Montu is the raised arm of Egypt's armies — the falcon-headed war god of Thebes whose name was the highest praise a fighting pharaoh could earn: Courage under the king's command, Wrath the same blade turned loose.
blurbs.slavic: Svetovid is the four-faced war-god of Arkona whose sacred white horse, led over crossed spears, foretold whether the campaign would be blessed.
blurbs.alchemy: Iron is the soldier's metal and the iron in our blood — Mars really does run, red and breath by breath, in every vein.
blurbs.japan: Kayobi is Fire-day, whose planet 火星 Kasei is literally the 'fire star,' Mars — a day of the forge, the flame, and the samurai's crossed katana.

blurbs.day: Wednesday is Mercury's day - Hermes' day in Greek, Woden's (Odin's) day in the north, Budhvar in Sanskrit, the water-star day in East Asia.
blurbs.profession: Hermes is the god of roads and trade, and his child is the Merchant - who weighs with Wisdom and falls to Greed.
blurbs.greek: Hermes is the messenger between worlds: god of roads, merchants, thieves and cunning speech, guide of souls into the underworld.
blurbs.norse: Odin hung nine nights on the world-tree and gave an eye at Mimir's well - all of it for wisdom. Wednesday is literally Wodnesdaeg, his day.
blurbs.religion: In the East Asian week Wednesday is the day of the water star - and water is the Tao's central image: the soft thing that overcomes the hard, the way that flows without striving.
blurbs.color: Purple is midnight: the mourning color, the day's grave and its deepest mystery.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Mercury's bright face is Wisdom, its dark face Greed - the merchant god counts both coins and stars. Generosity, from Jupiter's arm, cures Greed; Wisdom curbs Jupiter's Excess.
blurbs.religion_alt: The shaman walks between worlds on the messenger's day - Odin hanging on the tree for wisdom is itself a shamanic initiation.
blurbs.egypt: Thoth is the ibis-headed scribe of the gods — inventor of writing, reckoner of years and moons, recorder of the verdict when the heart meets Ma'at's feather; Hermes' elder mirror on the messenger's midnight arm.
blurbs.slavic: Veles is the horned cattle-god and serpent at the world-tree's roots, sworn beside Perun in the treaties and grandsire of the bard Boyan.
blurbs.alchemy: Quicksilver is the only metal liquid at room temperature, a mirror you can pour — Mercurius, the messenger who carries every metal from one state to another.
blurbs.japan: Suiyobi is Water-day, whose planet 水星 Suisei is the 'water star,' Mercury — ruled by Ryujin, the sea-dragon who commands the tides with his glowing jewels.

blurbs.day: Thursday is the king planet's day: Zeus' day in Greek, Thor's day in the north, Guruvar - the guru's day - in the Vedic week.
blurbs.profession: Jupiter's children are priests, judges and teachers - Brihaspati IS the guru of the gods, and Thursday is the teacher's day: generous of hand, jovial of table.
blurbs.greek: Zeus rules sky and thunder from Olympus, king of gods and keeper of oaths and guests.
blurbs.norse: Thor, thunder itself, guards gods and men with the hammer Mjolnir; thorsdagr became Thursday.
blurbs.religion: In Hindu tradition Thursday IS Brihaspativar: Brihaspati, guru of the gods, is the planet Jupiter itself, and his day is the week's most devotional.
blurbs.color: Yellow is noon at the top of the dial - and 'jovial', joyful, literally means 'of Jupiter'.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Jupiter's bright face is Generosity - the open royal hand; its dark face is Excess, the jovial table overflowing. Wisdom, from Mercury's arm, curbs Excess; Generosity cures Greed.
blurbs.religion_alt: Sikhism takes Guruvar literally - a faith of ten Gurus whose daily practice is langar, generosity served at a common table.
blurbs.egypt: Amun is the Hidden One — king of the gods, joined with the sun as Amun-Ra, the god the Greeks themselves named Zeus Ammon; Thursday's crown arm wears his twin plumes.
blurbs.slavic: Perun is the oak-crowned thunder-god whose six-petalled storm-mark the clock's hexagram echoes, and whose rain, wrung from the serpent, is the sky's generosity.
blurbs.alchemy: Tin makes bells and bronze ring, and cries out with the faint 'tin cry' when you bend it — Jupiter's benevolent, sounding metal.
blurbs.japan: Mokuyobi is Wood-day, whose planet 木星 Mokusei is the 'wood star,' Jupiter — the living element of the sacred pines that Shinto girdles with shimenawa rope.

blurbs.day: Friday is the day of the evening star: Aphrodite's day in Greek, Freya's day in the north, Shukravar in Sanskrit.
blurbs.profession: Venus' children in the old woodcuts are musicians, painters and lovers - the Artist, who works from Love and burns with Jealousy.
blurbs.greek: Aphrodite, born of sea-foam, rules love, beauty and desire - the planet Venus is her lamp at dusk.
blurbs.norse: Freya, goddess of love and of the slain, rides a chariot drawn by cats and weeps tears of gold; frjadagr is her day.
blurbs.religion: Islam gathers on Friday: Jumu'ah, the congregational prayer, makes this the blessed day of the week.
blurbs.color: Red is the evening: the sunset's blood-glow, the hour when Venus rises.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Venus carries Love and its shadow Jealousy - the fear of losing what is loved. Patience, from Saturn's arm, heals Jealousy; Love dissolves Saturn's Envy.
blurbs.religion_alt: Babylon named the planets gods and gave this clock its seven-day week; Ishtar IS Venus - the evening star, love and war in one body.
blurbs.egypt: Hathor - lady of love, music, beauty and joy, the Lady of the West who receives the setting sun into her country; the Greeks called her Aphrodite, and Venus' red evening is her hour.
blurbs.slavic: Mokoš is the great spinning goddess and Moist Mother Earth, the lone goddess of Kyiv's pantheon, who lived on as St Paraskeva venerated every Friday.
blurbs.alchemy: Copper is aes cyprium, the metal of Venus' island of Cyprus, and her glyph ♀ is the very hand-mirror it was first polished into.
blurbs.japan: Kin'yobi is Metal-day, whose planet 金星 Kinsei is the 'gold star,' Venus — emblemized by Yata no Kagami, the sacred mirror that lured Amaterasu from her cave.

blurbs.day: Saturday is Saturn's day - Cronus' day to the Greeks, Shanivar in Sanskrit. The Norse alone gave it no god: laugardagr, washing day; the Sabbath stands there instead.
blurbs.profession: Cronus carries the harvest sickle, and Saturn's child is the Farmer - who sows and WAITS (Patience) and eyes the neighbor's field (Envy).
blurbs.greek: Cronus, Titan of time and the harvest, devoured his own children out of envy and fear of being replaced - until Zeus, hidden away, returned to overthrow him.
blurbs.norse: Loki stands on Saturday (owner canon): the bound trickster, chained beneath the serpent venom as Cronus was cast down - the north gave its rest day to the god who never rests. His envy of Baldr brought the brightest god down.
blurbs.religion: Shabbat is Judaism's seventh day of rest - and in Hebrew the planet Saturn is named Shabtai, after the Sabbath itself: the day named the planet.
blurbs.color: Green is the morning: dew, growth, the week renewing itself on the day of rest.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Saturn, keeper of time, teaches Patience; his shadow is Envy, the bitter green of wanting what another has. Love, from Venus' arm, dissolves Envy; Patience heals Jealousy.
blurbs.religion_alt: Voodoo's keeper of the crossroads is Baron Samedi - Baron Saturday by name - the patience of the grave and the envy of the living.
blurbs.egypt: Osiris is the harvest that dies and returns — lord of the underworld and of rebirth, green-skinned like sprouting grain on Saturn's green morning; the patience of the buried seed, with Set's envy the vice he answered from the grave.
blurbs.slavic: Morana is the goddess of winter and death whose straw effigy is drowned every spring equinox — death as the hinge that lets the year be born again.
blurbs.alchemy: Lead is the heaviest, slowest metal, the prima materia the Work begins from — the base matter alchemy promises to turn into gold.
blurbs.japan: Doyobi is Earth-day, whose planet 土星 Dosei is the 'earth star,' Saturn — the patient element of Mount Fuji, the rice terraces, and the weathered stone lantern.

octa_extras.blurb: The west arm sits on 06h - sunrise. Cyan is the pale blue-green sky before the sun clears the horizon, and its mood is Hope: the emotion that looks forward.
The east arm sits on 18h - sunset. Red-orange is the burning evening sky, and its mood is Longing: the emotion that looks back. Hope and Longing face each other across the dial as dawn faces dusk.

cures: Humility cures Pride - the center heals itself.

articles_note: articles[theme][body] = {base, variants}: base = 3-4 left-aligned paragraphs (blank-line separated) weaving the entity with its day, color, hour mood, virtue and vice, anchored in the owner's image; variants = one short paragraph per pointer/palette combination (hexa_paint, hexa_light, octa_paint, octa_light, cross, trio). religion = the basic seven (Freemasonry Sunday, Islam Monday, Buddhism Tuesday, Taoism Wednesday, Hinduism Thursday, Christianity Friday, Judaism Saturday - the 2026-07-12 remap moved Freemasonry off Tuesday onto Sunday, Islam off Friday onto Monday, Buddhism off Monday onto Tuesday and Christianity off Sunday onto Friday); religion_alt = the alternate seven, one per body (Mithraism/Sol Invictus Sunday, Druidism Monday, Zoroastrianism Tuesday, Shamanism Wednesday, Sikhism Thursday, Babylonian religion Friday, Voodoo Saturday); entries carry a 'name' field. Display location TBD by the owner.

sun.base:
[[The Sky]] The Sun is the only body on this dial that burns with its own fire, and the image hides nothing of it: a seething sphere of white-gold plasma, its surface boiling in granular cells of yellow and orange, ringed by a thin fringe of flame-red corona. Everything else on the wheel — every planet, and the Moon most of all — merely returns a borrowed portion of this light.

[[On the Dial]] That is why, on the hexa paint dial, the Sun takes no arm at all: it holds the center, and its color is white-gold — the sum of every color. The six arms are sunlight decomposed; the star itself is the prism; Sunday is the source. To pin the Sun to one hue would be to mistake a part for the whole.

[[The Lesson]] Its metal is gold, the day metal that crowns the dial's top tip at noon, and its calling is the Ruler — the one seat whose cure never leaves the center, for the royal planet carries the royal pair of faces: Humility as virtue, Pride as vice, the source that heals itself or nothing does. But the center is DOUBLE, always two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat — the dial's own yin-yang. The Sun argues it plainly: its light falls on everyone alike, which is Justice, and it serves its route without a single day of rest, until the moment that service bends into mere routine and becomes Servility. Its moods are the pair the sky itself stages — Glory at full noon and Awe when the light fails — and the cure stays home: Humility answers Pride, Justice straightens Servility.

[[The Hour]] And so Sunday rules no single hour but all of them: joy, zeal, passion, sorrow, calm and renewal all radiate from the middle, the way the day's every mood is finally some angle of the same light.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six arms take their color. No hour belongs to it because every hour does.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette nothing moves — the center stays the center, white-gold. If anything the symbol grows more literal: this is Newton's own white beam, caught one instant before the prism splits it into red, green and blue.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Sun holds the noon arm still, but the wheel now crowns it in royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, power at its zenith with the Crown for its object. The source of all light seated as the source of all rule: 'by me kings reign' (Proverbs 8:15), the throne hour wearing the throne's own metal.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth — life at its blazing height — and the Sun wears its sun-yellow (#FFE800). No body fits the zenith of a life better than the one that owns the zenith of the day: strength before the descent, the morning of a man read as the noon of the sky.
variants.cross: On the cross the arms point at the year's hinges, and the Sun holds the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with Jupiter. The summer solstice is the Sun's own festival — the day it stands highest, the noon of the year.
variants.trio: The Trinity returns the Sun to the center, white-gold, with only three arms around it — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue: the painter's three primaries. The prism reading at its simplest — white light decomposed not into six moods but into the three virtues, and Sunday still the source of them all.

faces.ruler:
The Ruler face is the Sun exactly as the sky shows it, with no myth standing between the disc and the eye — the physical body that names the whole day, since Sunday still carries dies Solis in root and shape. On this face there is no god riding it and no crown resting on it; the star simply is what it is, and that plainness is itself the claim to rule: nothing needs to be added to make it the source.

The medallion holds nothing back: a seething sphere of white-gold plasma, its surface boiling in granular cells of yellow and orange, ringed by a thin fringe of flame-red corona, painted with the same honesty a photograph would use. Every other body on the dial only returns a portion of this light; the Ruler face is the one plate that generates what it shows instead of reflecting it.

Its virtue is Justice, argued in the plainest possible terms — this light falls on every latitude and every creature alike, playing no favorites among the arms it feeds — but Justice at the center is watched over by Pride, the fault of believing that because the light is even, it is also owed. Its mood is Glory, the sky at full noon, the corona at its widest and every granule of the surface visible at once — the hour when the source is most obviously the source, and Pride's whisper is loudest.
faces.servant:
The Servant face is the same star, not a second body but the first one with its light taken — the Sun made to serve an eclipse instead of ruling one, standing in for every myth on the dial that needed a second, dimmer face without inventing a new one.

Where the Ruler plate holds nothing back, this one holds almost everything back: the Moon's pitch-black disc lies exactly over the Sun, leaving only a thin, blazing white-gold corona ring streaming irregular wisps and a pair of small pink prominence flares at the rim, one last diamond-ring glint caught at the upper edge before even that goes dark, deep space black pressing in around it.

Its virtue is Humility — the star that generates all light, admitting for these few minutes that it can be entirely covered — but the same submission tips into Servility if it is read only as absence, as a light that has stopped mattering rather than one that is briefly spent. Its mood is Awe, the sky at midnight wearing noon's own disguise, and it is the necessary far side of Glory: the same body, remembering it can be lost.

moon.base:
[[The Sky]] The Moon in the owner's image is full and silver-gray: dark seas pool across its upper face, pale cratered highlands fill the south, and near the bottom rim one bright crater throws its rays outward like a star scratched into stone. It is the most honest portrait on the dial — a world of ash and dust that shines only because the Sun is somewhere behind you.

[[On the Dial]] Its home on the hexa paint dial is the arm at 4h, deep blue, the hour before dawn — Calm. Blue is the still color, the moonlit color, the temperature of a world asleep; and 4h is the night's last watch, when darkness is at its most complete and its most peaceful at once.

[[The Lesson]] That doubleness is the Moon's whole nature. Monday's body carries Serenity as its virtue and Fear as its vice — the same unlit world, once accepted and once dreaded. Its healer hangs straight across the dial: Mars in the working afternoon, whose Courage cures Fear, while the Moon's Serenity quells Mars' Wrath in return. The Physician is its calling — the calm hand at the night sickbed against the patient's fear — and silver its metal, the night's answer to the Sun's gold, kept at the dial's bottom tip.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules 4h because that is the hour serenity is actually tested: nothing left of yesterday, nothing yet of tomorrow, only borrowed silver light. Whoever is at peace then is at peace.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 4h, deep pigment blue, Calm — the Moon presiding over the hour before dawn.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps the Moon at 4h but tunes its blue to the prism's own primary — a purer, electric blue. Calm read not as painted stillness but as the actual color of pre-dawn light.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Moon keeps its 03h post in the deep night, but the arm now wears lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object. The body already ruled by the Physician's calm finds an easy cousin here: both keep the long night watch, one reading symptoms, the other reading pages, by the same borrowed light.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the 03h arm is the Unborn, painted moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the age of ancestors and the not-yet-born together. Fittingly it falls to the Moon, whose light was never its own: a body that has always stood for what is not yet lit fits the hour that belongs to those not yet born.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette, and the Moon takes the 6h dawn arm, green for Spring, sharing it with Saturn. The spring equinox is the year's own daybreak — a fitting post for the body that ends every night.
variants.trio: The trio keeps the Moon at its own 4h, but the arm now reads Hope — blue, shared with Mercury — and its tip is now the very center of its hue: it guards the morning third from 0h to 8h. Fitting work for the body that ends every night: hope is the Moon's calm turned to face the coming sun.

mars.base:
[[The Sky]] Mars is the rust-colored one, and the owner's image shows the detail that matters: a vast dark scar torn across its equator, a canyon system long enough to stretch across a continent. The planet named for war actually looks wounded — its red is the red of iron oxide, of dried blood, of an old battlefield.

[[On the Dial]] Yet on the hexa paint dial its arm at 16h is orange, not red — because the arm colors belong to the hours, and 16h is the working afternoon, the mood of Zeal. Orange is the ember-color of effort: past noon's peak but far from evening, the hours when fire is not display but fuel. Mars, the planet of drive, is exactly the body to stoke them.

[[The Lesson]] Tuesday's planet carries one fire with two uses: Courage, the flame that defends, and Wrath, the same flame let loose on its own side. The Soldier is its profession because a soldier is precisely this pair walking. And its remedy hangs straight across the dial at 4h: the Moon's Serenity quells Wrath, while Mars sends Courage back along the axis to cure the Moon's Fear — the warrior and the night-watch healing each other.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is when work is won or lost: morning's freshness is spent, evening's rest not yet earned, and only the stubborn ember carries a task to its end.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 16h, ember orange, Zeal — Mars stoking the working afternoon.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette Mars stays at 16h but the ember flares to pure yellow — pigment's orange has no seat in the prism wheel, so the afternoon fire burns at light's brightest hue instead. Zeal, only hotter.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Mars holds its 15h afternoon arm, now dressed in working copper (#C87533) — the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object. It is a demotion the fire resents and needs in equal measure: the same drive that wins battles also counts spoils, and the hour that once meant Zeal now prices what the sword has taken.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 15h belongs to Maturity, and Mars wears it in amber (#FFB400) — past Youth's blaze, short of the Elder's rest, the hour of a life spent doing rather than dreaming. The war-planet fits this age plainly: maturity is Courage without the glory, work continued after the applause has stopped.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Mars takes the 18h dusk arm, red for Autumn, and finally wears its own color — sharing the arm with Venus, as Ares shared everything with Aphrodite. War and love on one arm, pointing at the autumn equinox, the year's long evening.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves Mars to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Venus — each arm's tip centers its third of the day, and this pair guards the evening from 16h to 24h. Mars finally wears its own red, and Love reads through it as protection: courage keeping watch so that what is loved can sleep.

mercury.base:
[[The Sky]] The owner's Mercury is a small cratered ball painted in two minds: one face warm amber-tan, the other pale ashen gray, the whole surface pocked with craters like a well-traveled road. It suits the planet perfectly — the innermost wanderer and the fastest of all — it circles the Sun in 88 days — a slow-turning world of the harshest extremes, its days scorching and its nights freezing on the very same plain.

[[On the Dial]] Its home on the hexa paint dial is the bottom arm at 0h, purple, midnight — the mood of Sorrow. Purple is the mixed color, red mourning dissolved in blue stillness, and midnight is the mind's hour: the day is dead, tomorrow unborn, and thought runs on alone. Where better for the planet of the mind than the hour when only the mind is awake?

[[The Lesson]] Wednesday's planet carries Wisdom as its virtue and Greed as its vice — the merchant's pair, for the same cleverness that understands the world can start counting it instead. Hermes was god of merchants and of roads, and his cure comes down the longest road on the dial: Jupiter's Generosity, straight above at noon, cures Greed, while Mercury's Wisdom travels back up the axis to curb Jupiter's Excess.

[[The Hour]] So Sorrow rules midnight not as despair but as depth: the hour of accounting, when the mind weighs the day honestly because no one is watching. Mercury, who guided souls across the last border, keeps that hour.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: the dial's bottom at 0h, mourning purple, Sorrow — Mercury keeping the midnight watch.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Mercury at 0h but turns its purple to magenta — the one color missing from the rainbow, existing only where red and blue light meet. A color the mind must invent: no hue could suit Mercury better.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Mercury holds its 21h evening arm, recast in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object. The trader's cunning becomes the actor's craft with barely a change of hand: the same face that priced the day's goods by lamplight now performs behind a Mask, wisdom bent toward invention instead of accounting.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 21h is Old Age, and Mercury wears it in dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — twilight's own shade, the hour after the day's business is done. Wisdom accrues like interest: the quickest of the planets, having spent a life in trade and travel, finally sits still long enough to be called wise instead of merely clever.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Mercury returns to the bottom arm at 0h, blue for Winter, and holds it alone — the only body that shares its arm with no one. The winter solstice is the year's midnight, and Mercury stands that watch solitary, as thought does.
variants.trio: The trio lifts Mercury off midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Moon, guarding the morning third from 0h to 8h. Hope is the theological shape of Wisdom's forward look: the mind that weighed the day at midnight now stands at the morning's gate, counting what comes instead of what has gone.

jupiter.base:
[[The Sky]] Jupiter is the giant in banded robes: the owner's image shows cream and caramel stripes wrapping the whole globe, whipped into curls at their borders, and low on one side the Great Red Spot — a single storm wider than the Earth that has been turning for centuries. Everything about the planet is surplus: the largest body, the loudest weather, a king's wardrobe of clouds.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa paint dial the king takes the crown position: the top arm at 12h, yellow, noon — the mood of Joy. This is no metaphor smuggled in afterward: 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter,' and the top of this dial is where the star's vertex points at true solar noon. The day's peak belongs to the planet of abundance.

[[The Lesson]] Thursday's planet gives with an open hand — Generosity is its virtue — and keeps giving past the point of sense, for Excess is its vice: the royal table overflowing. Its calling is the Priest and Teacher, the one who distributes what he has received; and its correction hangs at the far end of the dial's spine — Mercury's Wisdom at midnight curbs Excess, while Jupiter's Generosity pours down the same axis to cure Mercury's Greed. The full hand and the counting hand, balancing each other.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is the day's own generosity: the most light, the most warmth, the moment the world is given more than it asked for. Jupiter simply makes that hour a person.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: the dial's top at 12h, noon yellow, Joy — the king planet at the crown of the star.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Jupiter at the 12h crown but dresses it in green — the middle color of the spectrum, which the prism wheel seats at noon. Joy read as fullness of light rather than gold.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Jupiter holds its 09h morning arm, now in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — and the king finds he was already dressed for the part. Generosity given from a pulpit is still generosity given, and the Bell simply rings the hour the Sun no longer needs to.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 09h is Childhood, and Jupiter wears it in spring green (#7CE577) — the freshest hour matched to the freshest age. Nothing suits the planet of abundance like a life still all appetite and no restraint yet learned: Childhood is Excess before Excess has a name for itself.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Jupiter keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with the Sun. The summer solstice is the year at its most generous — the longest light, the fullest table — and the two most royal bodies preside over it together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Jupiter keeps its crown: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, shared with Saturn, and the arm centers the day's third - 8h to 16h, noon at its tip. Faith reads naturally through the king planet: generosity is faith practiced with the hands, giving before any return is proven.

venus.base:
[[The Sky]] The owner's Venus is not the pale pearl of the evening sky but the truth beneath it: a globe of molten orange and red, its surface veined with bright fire like embers seen through smoke. That is the real planet under the lovely clouds — a furnace hotter than Mercury — and it is the perfect portrait of the passion it stands for: the most beautiful light in the sky, burning at close range.

[[On the Dial]] Its home on the hexa paint dial is the arm at 20h, deep red, the evening — the mood of Passion. The placement is astronomy before it is poetry: Venus is the evening star, the first light to open over the sunset, and 20h is when the sky itself turns blood-red. Friday's planet rules the hours when work ends and wanting begins.

[[The Lesson]] Love is its virtue and Jealousy its vice — not love's opposite but love's shadow, the fear of losing what is loved. Its cure stands straight across the dial in the green morning: Saturn's Patience heals Jealousy, while Venus' Love flows back across the axis to dissolve Saturn's Envy. The Artist is Venus' calling — the old woodcuts drew the planet's children as musicians and lovers — and art is exactly this: passion given patience enough to become form.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because evening is when the day stops demanding and starts desiring: the light turns red, the work lets go, and what remains is what one actually loves.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 20h, blood red, Passion — the evening star over the evening hour.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Venus at 20h and gives its red the prism's full voltage — pure red, light's own primary. Passion undiluted.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Venus holds its 18h sunset arm, now recast in iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — and the fit is a deliberate friction: the planet of Love drawn as the planet of the blade. Yet Jealousy already IS a kind of war fought for the heart's own territory, so the Sword only names honestly what Love's vice was always capable of.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 18h is the Elder, and Venus wears it in ember (#FF6A3C) — the banked-down fire of a harvested life. It reframes the evening star gently: passion does not vanish with age, it settles into the Elder's ember glow, Love grown patient enough to warm a room instead of burning it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Venus holds the 18h arm, red for Autumn, sharing it with Mars — Aphrodite and Ares together, as in the oldest gossip of the gods. The autumn equinox is the year's dusk: the harvest of what was loved, and the first cold that makes one hold it tighter.
variants.trio: Venus does not move at all: the Trinity's red Love arm is her own 20h, and Love is already her virtue — the one word belonging to both the seven pairs and the theological three. Mars joins her there, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h: passion and courage guarding the dark third of the day.

saturn.base:
[[The Sky]] Saturn is the only body on the dial that carries its own circle: the owner's image shows the butter-gold globe tilted inside its broad rings, band upon band of cream and caramel, with the dark divisions drawn clean between them. A planet wearing a halo of orbiting dust — or, seen with this dial's eyes, a planet wearing the year itself as a ring.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa paint dial it takes the arm at 8h, green, the morning — the mood of Renewal. It seems a paradox to give the slowest wanderer the freshest hour, but that is the point: Saturn takes nearly thirty years to close one circle, so no body knows better that mornings return. Green is dew and growth, and Saturday's morning is the week's own renewal — the day of rest, whose planet in Hebrew is named Shabtai, after the Sabbath itself.

[[The Lesson]] Patience is its virtue, learned from its own pace, and Envy its vice — the bitter green of another's harvest, the oldest failure of those who wait. The Farmer is its calling: Cronus carries the harvest sickle, and farming is patience practiced in public — sow, then wait, and do not stare too long at the neighbor's field. The cure stands across the dial in the red evening: Venus' Love dissolves Envy, while Saturn's Patience travels back to heal Venus' Jealousy — the two slow arts, waiting and loving, mending each other.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules 8h because the morning is where patience pays out: whatever was buried in the dark comes up green. The week's day of rest owns that hour on behalf of all the others.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 8h, harvest green, Renewal — the slow planet keeping the morning of the day of rest.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Saturn at 8h but cools its green to cyan — morning read as sky rather than field, the pale clean light after sunrise.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Saturn holds its 06h dawn arm, and here the fit needs no argument: field green (#3E8914) — the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — is simply Saturn's own calling handed back to it. Cronus already carried the sickle; the Compass only gives the same hand its plough.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 06h is Birth, and Saturn wears it in predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the coldest, most certain hour, when night has not quite let go. It is the dial's sharpest irony made gentle: the slowest of all the wanderers, the planet of nearly thirty patient years, is set to govern the shortest and newest of the Ages.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Saturn holds the 6h arm, green for Spring, sharing it with the Moon. The spring equinox is sowing time — the farmer's own festival — patience at the exact moment it commits its seed to the ground.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Saturn from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Jupiter — the two slow giants on one arm, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads through Saturn as patience aimed upward: trusting a harvest that takes thirty years to come round.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Helios is not a god who carries the sun; he is the sun, the Titan the Greeks trusted to see everything done under the open sky — witnesses swore their oaths by him because nothing escapes the one who rides above it all. In the drawing he drives the quadriga, four winged horses hauling his chariot through a dark, star-scattered heaven; a spiked disc of gold blazes behind his head like a halo too heavy for any crown, and in his raised hand he grips a staff tipped with a small burning sun. He does not point at his element — he wields it.

[[On the Dial]] On the six-armed dial he takes no arm at all: Sunday sits in the center, colored white-gold. This is the founding sentence of the whole design — white light is the sum of all colors, so the six arm-hues around him are nothing but his own light decomposed. The star is the prism; Helios is what goes into it.

[[The Lesson]] Helios stands at the center of everything, and the one who stands there is the one most tempted to believe he deserves to — the star that gives all light must not mistake itself for the light's owner, for his virtue is Humility and his vice is Pride, the oldest pair, and his cure crosses no axis: the center heals itself. Yet that center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, and Helios lived both faces. The all-seeing eye that witnessed the theft of Persephone and testified when no other god would is Justice itself; the driver who serves the same dawn route without thanks is the servant, and Servility would be forgetting that the route is a trust, not a chore. Phaethon's day tells the rest — the chariot in a son's proud hands is Pride, the scorched sky its Awe, and Glory is the same chariot back on its arc the very next morning.

[[The Hour]] That is also why he rules no single hour. Noon's Joy is Helios overhead, evening's Passion is Helios leaving, the pre-dawn Calm is the world holding its breath for him: every mood on the wheel is the same god at a different angle.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six moods radiate. Sunday does not sit among the days — the days sit around Sunday.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette nothing about him changes — still the center, still white-gold. Every arm around him translates from pigment to beam, but white is the sum in both worlds; the source is the one point where the two readings agree.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Helios steps from the center onto the noon arm dressed now in royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, the Crown for its object. The Titan who drove the sky in a golden chariot needs no metaphor to wear a crown: 'by me kings reign' (Proverbs 8:15) fits the all-seeing charioteer as naturally as it fit any throne.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, and Helios wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) — life's blazing height matched to the god who drives the day's own height. Phaethon's brief hour behind those reins is Youth's whole warning distilled: the zenith is glorious and merciless to anyone not seasoned enough to hold it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross his arm at 12h is Summer, colored yellow, and he shares it with Zeus. The cross arms point at the solstices and equinoxes: this is the midsummer arm, the year's longest light, where Sunday's source and Thursday's king hold the peak together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Helios keeps the center, and the star around him thins to three arms — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue, the painter's own primaries. The founding sentence holds at its simplest: three virtues instead of six moods, and all of them still his one white light taken apart.

faces.ruler:
Helios is the Ruler face by the oldest right there is — not a god who carries the sun but the Titan the Greeks trusted to see everything done under the open sky, sworn by because nothing escapes the one who rides above it all.

The medallion drives him across dark, star-scattered heaven in the quadriga, four winged horses hauling his chariot, a spiked disc of gold blazing behind his head like a halo too heavy for any crown, a staff tipped with a small burning sun gripped in his raised hand — bronze-and-gold sculptural relief on cracked dark marble, the Greek-key meander running the border whole.

His virtue is Justice, the all-seeing eye that testified when Persephone was stolen and no other god would speak — light that plays no favorites among what it exposes — but that same all-seeing gaze tempts Pride, the driver forgetting he only witnesses the world and beginning to believe he owns it. His mood is Glory, the chariot back on its arc at sunrise after every fall, the quadriga running true — noon's own triumph, the wheels turning exactly where they should.
faces.servant:
Phaethon is the Servant face, not another god but Helios's own son, borrowed for one disastrous day — proof that the chariot's reins can be held by anyone the light trusts, and that trust is not the same as mastery.

The plate matches Helios's finish exactly — bronze-and-gold relief on cracked dark marble, the same Greek-key ring — but the scene has come apart: Phaethon grips the reins in white-knuckled panic as the four winged horses bolt out of control, the chariot tilting and scorching a jagged black streak across sky and earth, the corona guttering uneven behind him where it once burned steady.

His virtue is Humility, though he never reaches it in the story — the lesson the fall exists to teach every reader, that inheriting the light is not the same as commanding it — while Servility is what the myth actually shows: a boy pretending mastery he was only lent. His mood is Awe, the scorched sky where the sun's ordinary route should have run — and Glory's own promise is that the chariot rides again the very next morning, in steadier hands.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Selene is the moon as a person — not a goddess of the moon but the light itself, driving her own chariot through the night while her brother Helios sleeps. The drawing seats her in a crown horned with a crescent, her dark gown sown with stars and a crescent clasping her belt; a winged horse rears beside the great sickle moon behind her, a chariot wheel waits at her side, and in her open palm she holds the full moon itself — cratered, golden, offered like something being weighed. The Greeks told how she loved the shepherd Endymion and had him kept in eternal sleep: the one myth where watching over someone's night is the whole story.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the arm at dial 4h, painted deep blue — Monday's arm, the mood of Calm, the hour before dawn. The lower half of the dial is the night, and 4h is its stillest chamber, when even the sleepless finally go quiet. Blue was the phlegmatic Moon's color in the old humors, and silver is her metal: as gold crowns the star's noon tip, silver crowns its midnight one — day metal above, night metal below.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Serenity and her vice is Fear, and they are the same night twice: moonlit, the world is the calmest thing there is; unlit, it is the oldest terror. That is why she is the Physician — medieval medicine timed its cures by her phases, and the healer's calm hand is exactly what is set against the patient's fear. Across the dial stands Ares: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath — the soldier and the physician bound to one axis.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the hour before dawn because that is where calm is earned. Anyone can be serene at noon; the 4h watch is serenity in the dark, with no proof yet that the sun returns — the moon holding the light until it does.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 4h arm in the painter's deep blue, Monday's Calm in the hour before dawn.
variants.hexa_light: The same 4h arm, but the blue turns elemental: in the light wheel blue is one of the three primaries of light itself, not a mixture. Calm read through the prism is bedrock, not blend — the night as one of the world's fundamental colors.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Selene keeps her 03h deep-night post, now in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object. The goddess who watched over Endymion's endless sleep already knew how to keep a long, quiet vigil; the Scholar's Book only gives her watching a page to read by.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm is the Unborn, silver moonlight (#C8D7F0) — ancestors and the not-yet-born held in one hue. Selene, whose whole light is borrowed from a brother she never quite outshines, fits an age defined by what has not yet arrived, or has already passed beyond seeing.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she holds the 6h arm — Spring, green — and shares it with Cronus. The arm points at the spring equinox, the dawn of the year: the dial's two quiet teachers, Serenity and Patience, keeping the year's morning together.
variants.trio: The Trinity leaves Selene on her 4h arm but renames the post: the blue now means Hope, she shares the arm with Hermes, and with the pre-dawn hour at its very tip it guards the morning, 0h to 8h. The goddess who kept Endymion's night now guards the promise that night ends — calm turned to face the sun.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Ares is war without the parade — the Greeks gave him a planet and a weekday while keeping him at arm's length; in the Iliad, Zeus calls him the most hateful of the gods. The drawing is honest about it: he strides shouting under a crested helmet, spear in one fist and a shield embossed with a roaring lion's face in the other, while the storm behind him literally rains spears; a vulture waits at his left and a war-dog at his right — his sacred animals, which are also the battlefield's scavengers — and at his feet lie a broken wheel and someone else's fallen, eagle-blazoned shield.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the arm at dial 16h, painted orange — Tuesday's arm, the mood of Zeal, the working afternoon. Orange is fire domesticated: not the noon blaze and not the evening blood-red, but the ember that gets things done. 16h is the hour of the second wind — effort as its own mood, the soldier's hour of drill and march rather than glory.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Courage and his vice is Wrath, and the drawing refuses to separate them: the lion on the shield and the shout on his face are one fire, which defends or destroys depending only on where it is pointed. His cure hangs straight across the dial in Selene's blue: Serenity quells Wrath — and in return, Courage cures Fear. The soldier and the physician share an axis because they share a battlefield.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the working afternoon because that is where courage actually lives. Battles are rare; the long unglamorous march toward them is daily — and the ember-orange of 16h is the hour when persistence, not fury, carries the fight.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 16h arm in ember orange, Tuesday's Zeal of the working afternoon.
variants.hexa_light: The same 16h arm, but the prism has no pigment orange — the ember flares to full yellow, red and green light summed. Zeal read as light is louder than zeal read as paint: the working fire at battle brightness.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Ares keeps his 15h afternoon arm, recolored in working copper (#C87533) — the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object. It sits oddly on the god Zeus called 'most hateful' — until one remembers that war has always had its quartermasters, and even Ares' spear point has a price stamped somewhere behind it.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 15h is Maturity, amber (#FFB400) — and it suits the war-god better than any glory-hour would. Maturity is Courage with the shouting worn off: the discipline of the march, not the charge, is exactly what an army calls its most useful age.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he stands at 18h — Autumn, red — and shares the arm with Aphrodite. It points at the autumn equinox, the year's dusk; the lovers whom Hephaestus once caught in a single net are bound to a single arm again, war and love ruling the season of the falling leaf together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Ares marches to 20h and stands on Love's red arm — beside Aphrodite, as always, as the net proved. Together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h. Love reads through the war god as its garrison: the same fire that rains spears in his storm stands sentry so that what the heart keeps can sleep.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Hermes is the god of everything that moves between: messages, merchants, roads, thieves, and finally souls — the only Olympian who walks freely into the underworld and back. The drawing catches him in a rare state, seated; but the winged cap and winged sandals stay on, the caduceus with its two twined serpents stands upright in his hand, and spare serpent-staffs lean at the pedestal's base beneath an arc of small stars — a courier between errands, never a courier retired.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the arm at the very bottom of the dial, painted purple — Wednesday's arm, the mood of Sorrow, midnight. Why give the quickest god the darkest post? Because Hermes is the psychopomp, the guide of souls: midnight is the day's grave and its mystery, and someone must know the way through it. Purple is the mourning color, and the bottom tip he stands on carries silver, the night's metal.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Wisdom and his vice is Greed — the merchant's pair, for the same cleverness that understands the world can begin merely counting it. His cure stands straight overhead: Zeus at noon, whose Generosity cures Greed — while Hermes' Wisdom, in return, curbs Excess. The dial's whole vertical axis is a conversation between the open hand and the counting one.

[[The Hour]] And Sorrow suits the hour of thought. Midnight is when the mind is most itself — accounts closed, roads empty, nothing left to trade but understanding; the god of exchange keeps the one watch where wisdom is the only currency.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the midnight arm at the dial's bottom, mourning purple, Wednesday's Sorrow.
variants.hexa_light: The same midnight post, but the purple becomes magenta — the prism's impossible color, found in no rainbow, existing only where the eye joins the two far ends of the spectrum. No hue suits the boundary-crosser better: a color that is itself a crossing.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Hermes keeps his 21h evening arm, now stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object. No god wears a mask more naturally: the trickster-messenger who guided souls and stole cattle in the same myth already knew that a face is a tool, to be put on or taken off as the errand requires.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 21h is Old Age, dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — twilight's own hue for the god who twice crosses that border, guiding the dead across it and returning himself. Wisdom is what Old Age is finally allowed to keep, and no messenger has carried more of it back and forth.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he keeps the bottom of the dial — Winter, blue — and he shares the arm with no one. It points at the winter solstice, the year's own midnight: the guide of souls stands alone at that door, as he always has.
variants.trio: The Trinity calls Hermes up from midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Selene, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The psychopomp is handed the opposite door: not the guide down into the dark but the herald of what comes — hope being wisdom's forward road, and roads were always his.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Zeus is the storm made into law — the sky-father who ended the age of the Titans and then had to learn to govern the unruly family he freed. The drawing gives him the full regalia of that office: crowned and enthroned, a golden thunderbolt gripped in one fist and an eagle-topped scepter in the other, eagles carved into the throne's armrests and a live one circling above, while behind the throne rises an oak heavy with acorns — the tree of his oracle at Dodona, where the god spoke through rustling leaves.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the top of the dial, the 12h arm, painted yellow — Thursday's arm, the mood of Joy at noon, the day's peak. The language remembers it: jovial literally means 'of Jupiter.' The king takes the crown position, where the real sun pours its gold straight down onto his arm; and Thursday is Guruvar in the Vedic week, the day of Brihaspati, teacher of the gods — the king's arm is also the teacher's.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity and his vice is Excess — the royal table that feeds everyone and then cannot stop. It is one hand that never closes, whether giving or reaching for a second portion. His cure stands at the opposite tip of the dial: Hermes' midnight Wisdom curbs Excess, while Zeus' Generosity, poured straight down the same axis, cures Greed.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon hides nothing: everything stands in full light, shadows at their shortest, the world at its most given. Generosity is exactly that — the noon of the heart — and its excess is what happens when the feast refuses to admit the afternoon exists.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the noon arm at the dial's top, royal yellow, Thursday's Joy at the day's peak.
variants.hexa_light: The same crown position, but the prism turns his yellow to green — light's own primary at the top of the wheel, and the band the human eye sees most keenly. In the light reading the king wears the color of maximum sight: nothing at noon escapes him.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Zeus keeps his noon crown-arm, now alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object. It fits better than it first seems: Zeus spoke through the rustling oak at Dodona, an oracle's voice from a king's throat, and the Bell is only that same voice given a clearer instrument.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Childhood, spring green (#7CE577) — an unlikely hue for the sky-father until his own appetite is recalled: the royal table that cannot stop feeding everyone is Excess with a child's whole heart, wanting more simply because more is good.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he returns to the top: 12h, Summer, yellow, sharing the arm with Helios. It points at the summer solstice — the year's longest light — where Thursday's king and Sunday's source hold the peak together, as throne and sun have always shared a summit.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Zeus keeps the top: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, and he shares it with Cronus, the father he overthrew. Together they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads strangely and rightly through the king: the keeper of oaths stands on the arm of what every oath is made of.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Aphrodite was born of sea-foam, armed with nothing, and conquered everyone anyway. The drawing seats her in calm possession of her two emblems: the scallop shell of her birth in one hand, and in the other, raised like a verdict, the golden apple inscribed 'to the fairest' — the prize that flattered one goddess, enraged two, and burned Troy. Dove-topped staffs wreathed in roses stand around her throne, and small hearts float among the stars: the picture is all tenderness, holding the war quietly in one raised hand.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the arm at dial 20h, painted deep red — Friday's arm, the mood of Passion, the evening. Venus is the evening star, the first light to appear at dusk, and 20h is the hour the working day releases people to one another. Red is doubly hers: the sunset's blood and the heart's color, one pigment for the sky and for the pulse.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Love and her vice is Jealousy — not love's opposite but its shadow, the fear of losing what is loved; the apple in her hand is proof of how quickly 'fairest' becomes a wound. Her cure stands across the dial in Cronus' morning green: Patience heals Jealousy — and her Love, in return, dissolves Envy. She is the Artist, for the old woodcuts drew Venus' children as musicians and lovers: art is love given a form that cannot be taken away.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because passion is what the day was for. Work fills the light; what the work was in service of appears when the light goes. And Love alone also belongs to the theological trio of Faith, Hope and Love — the dial's human warmth, among people, on Friday evening.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 20h arm in deep red, Friday's Passion of the evening.
variants.hexa_light: The same evening arm, but the red goes pure — light's first primary at full strength. In the prism world passion is not a pigment mixed on a palette but an elemental beam: the one color that needs nothing added.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Aphrodite keeps her 18h dusk arm, now iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object. It is no idle assignment for the goddess who raises the golden apple like a verdict: 'to the fairest' started the Trojan War, and Love proved capable of arming a decade of soldiers with a single word.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm is the Elder, ember (#FF6A3C) — a banked, harvested warmth. The goddess born already grown, who never ages in myth, wears it as a lesson rather than a mirror: Love's fire, however it began, is asked in the end to settle into embers that still warm a room.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she stands at 18h — Autumn, red — sharing the arm with Ares. It points at the autumn equinox, and the pairing is the oldest gossip on Olympus: the lovers Hephaestus once trapped in a single net now share a single arm, love and war ruling the season of ripeness and fall.
variants.trio: Aphrodite does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red, for Love alone belongs to both sets — arm virtue and theological virtue at once. Ares joins her, and the lovers guard the evening from 16h to 24h: the third of the day when work is done and only what one loves remains.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Cronus is the hardest of the seven images, and it should be. The crowned titan glowers from a stone throne with a great scythe across his body; in his left hand he cradles a clock face with Roman numerals, gearwork showing behind it, and a toothed cog-ring circles his crown like a mechanical halo — the drawing deliberately fuses Cronus the titan with Chronos, time itself, a pun the Greeks were already making. In the upper corner floats a small winged child stained with blood: the memory of the children he swallowed. Behind him stands a bare, gnarled tree; near his feet, a young golden one — the old growth and the new.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the arm at dial 8h, painted green — Saturday's arm, the mood of Renewal, the morning. The pairing looks like a paradox — the devourer on the arm of new growth — until you see the Farmer in him: Cronus carries the harvest sickle, and in Hebrew the planet is Shabtai, named for the Sabbath itself. The slowest planet keeps the day of rest, and green is dew, growth, and the week beginning again.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Patience and his vice is Envy — the farmer's pair: he sows and waits, and eyes the neighbor's field. Envy is the bitter green of another's harvest, renewal's own color turned sour; Cronus swallowed his children because he could not endure being succeeded — envy of the future itself. His cure stands across the dial in Aphrodite's evening red: Love dissolves Envy — and his Patience, in return, heals Jealousy.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because renewal is patience paid out. The scythe cuts so the field can grow again; the night ends because it was waited through. On the day of rest the green arm says quietly: what you did not seize will grow back — and that is the whole cure.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 8h arm in harvest green, Saturday's Renewal of the morning.
variants.hexa_light: The same morning arm, but the green becomes cyan — the color of the early sky rather than the leaf. In the light reading renewal belongs to the air, not the field: the morning before anything has been planted at all.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Cronus keeps his 06h dawn arm, now field green (#3E8914) — the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — and no reassignment on the wheel needed less persuading: the harvest sickle in his stone hand already IS the Farmer's tool, myth handing the Compass its answer before it asked. Isaiah's swords beaten into plowshares (2:4) could be Cronus' own epitaph.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel dawn is Birth, predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — and it lands hardest on the titan who once swallowed his own children rather than be succeeded. The wheel's cruelest kindness: the god most afraid of what comes next is set to guard the very age that always does.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he keeps 6h — Spring, green — and shares the arm with Selene. It points at the spring equinox, the year's own dawn: Selene's Serenity and his Patience, the dial's two quiet virtues, holding the childhood of the year together.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Cronus to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Zeus — the devourer and the child who escaped him on one arm, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith is the titan's hardest lesson read forward: to trust the future he once swallowed, and wait for it with open hands.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] In the North the sun is a woman. Sol stands in full scale armor with golden hair streaming, and she does not wear the sun — she holds it up in one raised hand, a blazing disc lifted like a lamp for the world, while behind her a two-horse chariot races above the waves: Arvakr and Alsvidr, Early-Waker and All-Swift, who pull the sun across the sky with the wolf Skoll forever at their heels. Her light is not a possession; it is a duty performed at a run.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial she sits nowhere and everywhere: the center, white-gold, Sunday. The Sun is not one color among six — white light is the sum of all colors, so the six arms are her light decomposed, the star is the prism, and she is the source. The top of the dial is solar noon and carries her metal, gold, as the day's crown; but Sol herself belongs to no single hour, because every hour is lit by her.

[[The Lesson]] Sól cannot stop to be admired — pursued across the sky by the wolf, the sun that lingered to receive worship would be devoured, and so her body carries the oldest pair: Humility as virtue, Pride as vice, the only axis that folds onto itself, for the center heals the center. Yet the center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, a doubleness she wears grimly — queen of the sky and its servant at once, driven on schedule by a wolf she cannot outrun forever. Her Justice is the daylight dealt equally to every farmstead; her Servility would be to mistake the flight for the duty. And no mythology stages Glory and Awe more honestly: Skoll catches her at Ragnarok — the Awe foretold — yet the Eddas promise her daughter drives on, Glory reborn on the same road.

[[The Hour]] So her mood is not one of the six but their spring: Joy at noon, Sorrow at midnight, Calm, Zeal, Passion and Renewal are all one white ray, taken apart. Whoever reads any arm of the dial is reading a piece of her.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six arms decompose. Sunday does not ride the wheel of hours — it is the hub the wheel turns on.
variants.hexa_light: The prism reading repaints every arm, but not her: white light is white in both worlds, the one color that pigment and beam agree on. Sol alone stands unchanged at the center while the week shifts around her.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Sol steps from the center onto the noon arm, now in royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, the Crown for its object. A goddess who has never once stopped running from the wolf Skoll wears a crown of a different kind here — not leisure but rule, the hour she has earned simply by never once failing to arrive.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Youth, sun-yellow (#FFE800) — life's blazing height, and Sol's whole myth is exactly that height defended: pursued but never caught, her daughter promised to drive on after Ragnarok. Youth, this wheel says, is worth outrunning a wolf for.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: her arm at dial 12h points at the summer solstice, colored summer yellow, and she shares the arm with Thor. Sun goddess and thunder god hold the season of longest light together — the sky's two loudest voices on one arm.
variants.trio: The Trinity is the theological palette: Sol returns to the hub with three arms only — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue — all decomposed from her white-gold center. Faith radiates from her outward, and each arm fences one third of her daily run: the wolf-chased goddess remains the still point of the virtues.

faces.ruler:
Sól is the Ruler face — in the North the sun is a woman, and she does not wear the sun but holds it aloft in one raised hand, a duty performed at a run rather than a possession worn at rest.

The medallion casts her in full scale armor with golden hair streaming, the blazing disc lifted overhead, a two-horse chariot racing above the waves behind her — Arvakr and Alsvidr, Early-Waker and All-Swift — bronze relief on dark slate stone, ringed in Celtic knotwork.

Her virtue is Justice, the daylight dealt equally to every farmstead whether it has earned her favor or not, but that fairness is stalked by Pride, the temptation to believe the route is hers to slow or savor rather than a duty owed to everyone under it. Her mood is Glory, the noon sky where the wolf falls behind and the chariot outruns him — for one turn of the wheel, the pursued outrunning the pursuit.
faces.servant:
Skoll is the Servant face — not Sól herself but the wolf who has chased her since the first telling, the pursuit built into her story from the start rather than a separate character invented for the occasion.

The plate keeps Sol's own bronze relief and Celtic-knotwork ring, but the scene belongs to the wolf now: Skoll caught mid-leap against a starless dusk sky, jaws closing around the last sliver of the sun-disc, Sól's empty upraised hand and trailing golden hair just visible at his shoulder, swallowed in his shadow, the chariot and horses rearing behind in his dust.

The virtue this face carries is Humility, Sól's own admission written into the myth that the chase cannot be outrun forever — she who gave light equally must also concede it equally, even to the wolf — and Servility is the same admission gone wrong, the flight mistaken for nothing more than a losing race instead of a duty kept until the very last mile. Its mood is Awe, Ragnarok's promise fulfilled — yet the Eddas insist a daughter drives on after, Glory reborn on the same road the wolf just won.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Mani, Sol's brother, steers the moon. The relief shows him as an older god with long silver hair and a braided beard, bronze scale armor under a night-blue cloak spangled with tiny stars; his raised right hand holds a silver crescent, his left cradles a silver orb marked with its own crescent — the moon shown twice, as phase and as whole. Behind him his chariot rides above the waves, for he too is hunted, by the wolf Hati, and he too never stops: the old texts say he governs the moon's waxing and waning, and men counted their nights by him before they counted days.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), Monday's arm, the hour before dawn, and the mood is Calm. Blue is the still, moonlit hour — the phlegmatic Moon of the old physicians, whose whole art ran on his phases; the sickbed watch is kept at exactly this hour of the night, and it is kept by his profession, the Physician.

[[The Lesson]] Blue carries both his faces: Serenity, the stillness of a moonlit world, and Fear, the same darkness unlit. Mani earns the virtue honestly — serenity is not the absence of the wolf but a steady hand despite it, the healer's calm against the patient's dread. Across the dial at 16h stands Tyr: Tyr's Courage cures Mani's Fear, and Mani's Serenity quells Tyr's Wrath — the soldier and the physician healing each other along one axis.

[[The Hour]] And that is why his mood rules the pre-dawn: it is the deepest quiet of the night, when fear is nearest and calm is worth most. The dial gives the last hour of darkness to the god who has driven through it every night since the beginning.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 04h arm, deep pigment blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — Monday resting one arm below the sunrise line.
variants.hexa_light: Same arm, same hour, but the pigment gives way to the prism's pure blue (#0040FF) — Calm read as light. Of all the arms Mani's changes least between the two worlds; the moon is the moon in any physics.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Mani keeps his 03h deep-night arm, now lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object. The god who counted nights before anyone counted days already kept the ledger the Scholar now holds; Mani was reckoning time in the dark long before the Book existed to write it in.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel his arm is the Unborn, moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — ancestors and the not-yet-born in one pale hue. Hunted nightly by Hati as his sister is by Skoll, Mani fits an age that is itself always just out of reach, never quite arrived and never quite gone.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, shared with Loki. The moon-steerer stands on the sunrise arm in the season of first growth — the counter of nights lending his silver to the year's green morning.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Mani at his 04h post but changes the watchword: the blue arm (#002FFF) now stands for Hope, shared with Odin, and it guards the morning from 0h to 8h, the pre-dawn calm at its tip. A right duty for the hunted moon-steerer: he has proven every single night that dawn arrives.

mars.base:
[[The Story]] Tyr raises his right arm like a banner, and the arm ends at the wrist: a bandage-wrapped, blood-streaked stump, held high and unhidden, with a snarling wolf's head at his shoulder and a bloodied sword gripped point-down in his remaining hand, runes arcing through the field around him. The stump is the whole story — when the gods bound Fenrir with a ribbon they swore was harmless, only Tyr would lay his hand in the wolf's mouth as pledge, knowing the pledge was false and the hand was forfeit. Northern courage is not fearlessness; it is paying an honest price with open eyes.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the 16h arm in orange (#DC9600) — Tuesday, which is literally Tyr's day, Tysdag. Orange is Mars' ember, the fire of the working afternoon, and its mood is Zeal: not the flash of battle but the discipline that keeps pushing when the day is long. It is the Soldier's arm, and Tyr is the soldier's god twice over — god of war and god of justice, the sword and the oath in one figure.

[[The Lesson]] The ember has two faces, and the image shows both in one blade: Courage is the virtue, Wrath the vice — one fire that defends or destroys, the bloodied sword equally able to be either. His cure stands straight across the dial at 04h: Mani's Serenity quells Tyr's Wrath, and Tyr's Courage cures Mani's Fear. The one-handed god who did not flinch is the exact medicine for the hunted moon's dread.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the working afternoon because that is when courage is actually spent — not at dawn when strength is fresh, but at 16h, past the peak, when the day demands what a soldier has: the will to hold the line after comfort is gone.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 16h arm, ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — Tuesday's fire banked into steady heat.
variants.hexa_light: Same 16h arm, but the prism burns the ember to bright yellow (#FFFF00): zeal read as raw light instead of glowing coal — the afternoon at its most blinding.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Tyr keeps his afternoon arm, now working copper (#C87533) — the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object. It sits strangely on the one-handed oath-keeper until the object underneath is recalled: Tyr's whole myth is a bargain paid in full, a hand given honestly as the price of a promise — the Merchant's ethic of an honest debt, told here in flesh instead of coin.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Maturity, amber (#FFB400) — and no god earns it more plainly. Tyr's courage was never a young man's reckless charge; it was the mature calculation of a debt that had to be paid, eyes open, at the wolf's own mouth.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 18h points at the autumn equinox, colored autumn red, and he shares it with Freya. War and love on one sunset arm — the old Mars-and-Venus pairing in northern dress, the sword-hand and the sword-maiden holding the year's red season together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Tyr shifts to 20h and stands under Love's deep red (#B60000), sharing the arm with Freya — sword-hand and sword-maiden guarding the evening from 16h to 24h. Love reads through Tyr as the pledge: he gave his hand so the others would be safe, which is love spelled in the north's own alphabet.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Odin here is the wanderer-sage with the ledger of prices paid: an eyepatch over the eye he traded into Mimir's well for one drink of wisdom, the spear Gungnir in his fist with runes glowing faintly along the shaft — runes he won by hanging nine nights on the world-tree, a sacrifice of himself to himself — and his two ravens, Huginn and Muninn, Thought and Memory, one perched on the spear-arm and one at his shoulder, with a great bare tree rising behind him. Everything he has, he bought; nothing was given.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the bottom of the dial, the 0h arm at midnight, in mourning purple (#542E85) — Wednesday, Odin's own day, Wodnesdag. Mercury's arm is the mind itself, and midnight is where minds like his do their work: the god of the hanged, the whisperer with the dead, the seeker who goes down into the dark to bring knowledge back up.
