The arm's profession is the Merchant, and it fits him strangely well — Odin never receives, he only trades: an eye for wisdom, nine nights for the runes, everything at a price.

[[The Lesson]] Purple carries his pair: Wisdom is the virtue, Greed the vice — because a hunger for knowing, pursued without end, takes greed's exact shape even when the currency is insight rather than gold. The cure hangs straight overhead: his son Thor at noon. Generosity cures Greed, and Wisdom curbs Excess — father and son healing each other across the full height of the dial, midnight to noon.

[[The Hour]] And Sorrow rules his hour because Odin's wisdom has a specific weight: he knows how it ends. Ragnarok is not a rumor to him but a certainty carried daily; the one-eyed god sees further than anyone and mourns most of what he sees. Midnight, the day's grave and its mystery, is the only hour deep enough for him.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 0h arm at the very bottom of the dial, mourning purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of midnight — Wednesday at the day's nadir.
variants.hexa_light: Same midnight arm, but the prism electrifies the purple into magenta (#BD00BD): sorrow with a neon edge, the midnight mystery read as strange light rather than mourning cloth.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Odin keeps his 21h evening arm, now stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object. Few gods wear masks more often: the wanderer who wins runes by hanging on the tree and trades an eye for one drink of wisdom has always performed a different face for every errand, wisdom itself worn like a costume he paid for.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel 21h is Old Age, dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — and Odin, the oldest-eyed god on the dial, wears it like a debt collected early. He bought a lifetime of knowing exactly how the story ends, the specific tax Old Age charges everyone else for free.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: he keeps the 0h arm, pointed at the winter solstice and colored winter blue — and alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; Odin has no partner. The god of the longest night holds the year's longest night by himself, which is exactly how he travels.
variants.trio: The Trinity raises Odin from midnight to the 4h arm of Hope, blue (#002FFF), shared with Mani — for once the god of the longest night is not alone. Hope sits strangely on the one who knows how it all ends, and that is the point: he gathers wisdom against Ragnarok anyway. Hope is what Odin does, not what he feels.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Thor is shown at full swing: winged helmet, braided beard, both hands locked on the short haft of Mjolnir with the rune-carved hammer swept across his body, knees bent in a wide braced stance, the girdle of might at his waist and the cape flaring behind him. It is not a portrait of majesty but of labor — thunder is not something Thor has, it is something he does, and the whole relief is caught mid-blow.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the top of the dial: the 12h arm in noon yellow (#F8E600), Thursday, Thor's own day. Jupiter is the jovial planet — 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter' — and Thor is the gods' laugh made flesh, the protector everyone toasts. The arm's profession is the Priest and Teacher, and the hammer earns it: Mjolnir hallowed weddings, births and funerals — the thunder god was the blessing god, the one instrument that consecrated and defended alike.

[[The Lesson]] Yellow holds his two faces: Generosity is the virtue, Excess the vice — the open hand and the overflowing table, and no table in myth overflows like Thor's, who at Thrym's feast ate an ox and eight salmon and drank three casks of mead while disguised as a bride. He guards Midgard and asks nothing for it; he also never stops at enough. The curb hangs straight below at midnight: his father Odin, whose Wisdom curbs Thor's Excess, while Thor's Generosity cures Odin's Greed — one axis, father and son, noon and midnight.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules his hour because noon is the day's peak and the peak belongs to its defender: the hammer rests at the top of the dial where the sun stands highest, and the mood of that height is exactly what Thursday's god brings into a room — appetite, laughter, and the certainty that the roof will hold.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 12h arm crowning the dial, noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — Thursday at the top of the world.
variants.hexa_light: Same crowning arm, but the prism turns noon green (#00DC00) — the middle band of the spectrum for the middle of the day. Joy read as light: the peak hour in the color of full leaf.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Thor keeps his noon crown-arm, now alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object. Mjolnir already hallowed weddings, births and funerals in the old sagas — a hammer that consecrated as often as it struck — so the Bell only rings what the hammer was quietly doing all along.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Childhood, spring green (#7CE577) — an odd hue for a beard and a hammer until Thrym's feast is recalled: an ox, eight salmon, three casks of mead, eaten with a child's whole unashamed appetite. Excess, in Thor, has always looked exactly like Childhood grown enormous.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 12h points at the summer solstice, colored summer yellow, shared with Sol. Thunder and sun together on the summer arm — the season of the highest light and the heaviest storms, both of them his.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Thor keeps the crown at 12h in Faith's yellow (#F8E600), sharing the arm with Loki — his old road-companion made his arm-mate — as the two guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads plainest through Thor of all the gods: Midgard trusts that the hammer will come, and it always has.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Freya stands double-armed: a raised sword in one hand and a single golden falcon feather in the other — her feather-cloak, which carries its wearer between worlds — with a winged diadem on her brow, the amber-stoned Brisingamen at her throat, and at her side a boar and the prow of a longship. The image tells the truth the Roman Venus hides: the northern love goddess is also a war goddess, chooser of the slain who takes half the battle-dead before Odin picks. Love and the sword in the same two hands, neither one lowered.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000) — Friday, Freya's day, the evening hour whose mood is Passion. Red is the sunset's blood, and Venus is the evening star that rises exactly then; hers is the Artist's arm, the planet whose 'children' in the old woodcuts were musicians and lovers — the hour when the day's work ends and the day's wanting begins.

[[The Lesson]] Red carries her pair: Love is the virtue, Jealousy the vice — the fear of losing what is loved, and Freya's myths are crowded with coveting: the Brisingamen bargained for and stolen, giants naming her as the price of every ransom. Her cure stands straight across at 08h, and the dial's wit is sharp here: it is Loki, the very thief who stole her necklace. His arm's Patience heals her Jealousy, and her Love dissolves his Envy — owner and thief prescribed to each other across the whole dial.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because that is when it truly lives — not at noon among the day's duties but at 20h, when the light turns red and people turn to what, and whom, they actually want.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 20h arm, deep pigment red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — Friday glowing where the evening star rises.
variants.hexa_light: Same evening arm, but the prism saturates the red to full light (#FF0000): passion as a beam instead of an ember — the evening at maximum intensity.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Freya keeps her 18h dusk arm, now iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — and here the fit is not irony at all but plain fact: the goddess already stands with a raised sword in one hand, chooser of the slain who takes half the battle-dead before Odin ever picks. Love and the Sword were never separate hands for her.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm is the Elder, ember (#FF6A3C) — banked fire, a harvested warmth. It reads her own myth back to her: Freya wandering the world weeping tears of red gold for her lost husband Odr is passion that has outlived its object and burns on anyway, the Elder's exact condition.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: her arm at dial 18h points at the autumn equinox, colored autumn red, shared with Tyr. The sword-maiden and the one-handed soldier hold the red season together — love and war on one arm, as the North always knew they were.
variants.trio: Freya stands still: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red (#B60000), for Love alone belongs to both sets. Tyr joins her, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the chooser of the slain and the one-handed pledge-giver guarding the dark third, love with a sword in each hand.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Loki grins out of the relief — the only smiling god on the dial. Red-gold braids frame the smirk; one hand grips a spear whose head glows poison-green above green-lit runes, the other lifts a golden woven net; a serpent coils over a small fire at his feet and a wolf snarls at the lower edge. Every object is either his invention or his sentence: the net is the fisherman's tool the gods rebuilt from his own ashes to catch him as a salmon, the serpent is the one that drips venom on his bound face, the wolf is Fenrir, his son. Cleverness and punishment share one frame.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the 08h arm in green (#007E00) — Saturday. Old Norse gave that day no god at all, only laugardagr, washing day; the owner's canon seats Loki in Cronus' empty chair, the bound trickster as the northern mirror of the bound Titan. The arm's hour is morning, its mood Renewal, its profession the Farmer — and renewal is Loki's paradox: nothing in the myths changes, breaks open, or grows back without him. Even his fire clears the field.

[[The Lesson]] Green carries his pair with brutal precision: Envy is the vice — his envy of Baldr, the god everyone loved, drove the dial's darkest deed — and Patience is the virtue, taught to him the hard way: bound to the rock beneath the dripping serpent until the world's end, the trickster's sentence is nothing but waiting. His cure stands across at 20h, and it is Freya, whose necklace he stole: her Love dissolves his Envy, and his arm's Patience heals her Jealousy — thief and owner healing each other along one axis.

[[The Hour]] So Renewal rules his morning honestly: the farmer sows, waits, and eyes the neighbor's field; the week rests and restarts on Saturday; and after Ragnarok — the fire Loki himself pilots — the world comes back green. The morning arm belongs to the one who proves, at the highest price on the dial, that endings are how the story grows.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 08h arm, morning green (#007E00), the Renewal of the young day — Saturday, the week's rest and restart.
variants.hexa_light: Same morning arm, but the prism turns the green to cyan (#00DDDD) — renewal read as water-light, fitting for the god caught as a salmon on the day the North called washing day.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Loki keeps his 06h dawn arm, now field green (#3E8914) — the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object. The bound trickster makes an uneasy farmer, yet nothing in the myths grows back without first being burned or broken by his hand — even his own fire, at Ragnarok, clears the field for the green world after.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel dawn is Birth, predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the strangest grace the dial gives him, yet earned twice over: the world that ends in his fire is also the world his own fault reseeds, and Birth belongs, in the end, to whoever survives what came before it.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, shared with Mani. The bound god and the hunted moon hold the sunrise arm together — spring, the season that forgives winter everything, on the arm of the one who caused it.
variants.trio: The Trinity gives Loki the strangest promotion on the dial: the 12h Faith arm, yellow (#F8E600), beside Thor, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith on the deceiver reads as sentence and prophecy at once — the gods' broken trust bound him, yet after his own fire the world comes back green. Even Loki serves the promise.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Christianity is the faith that God entered history from below: the crucified and risen Christ, and a love that answers punishment with forgiveness rather than the reverse.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion sets a glowing white cross on black stone, the Chi-Rho monogram at its crossing and a crown of thorns lying at its foot; the silver rim weaves grapevines with tiny ichthys fish — the vine and the fishermen, the oldest signs of the church.

[[On the Dial]] It returns to the white-gold center after a detour to Friday's evening arm, and the return completes a fusion history had already made: in 321 Constantine decreed rest on 'the venerable day of the Sun,' dies Solis, fusing Sun and Church so tightly that the day still carries both names at once. No faith on this dial has a stronger claim to the throne today either — roughly 2.4 billion strong, the largest creed on Earth. And the fit runs deeper than any single date: this is a faith whose own scripture names its God as light itself — 'God is light, and in him is no darkness at all' (1 John 1:5) — and the Sun is the one body on the dial that IS light rather than something merely lit by it.

[[The Lesson]] And the center is DOUBLE, as it always is — two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat — though this time the doctrine's usual rule bends on purpose: the dial's other duals stand throne-above-and-throne-below without needing to be good against evil, but here the faith names its own opposite outright. Justice is the same measure the faith turns first on itself — 'judge not, that you be not judged' (Matthew 7:1) — and Pride is that same authority mistaking the measure for permission to escape it rather than the duty to keep it. Across the shadow face, Satanism inherits Humility only in the sense scripture never lets it escape — an accuser who needs permission before it can act at all (Job 1:12), never a sovereign in its own right, no matter how loudly Isaiah's Lucifer once boasted otherwise (14:14) — and its Servility is worship rerouted rather than abolished. Glory is Easter's stone already rolled away; Awe is the cold the shadow face is dignified enough to keep at a respectful distance.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa — yellow, red and blue mixed into orange, green and purple — Christianity holds the white-gold center. Pigment cannot mix white; it must be given, not blended, and the faith's own word for that is grace: unearned, unmanufactured by any amount of toil at the wheel's six hues, conferred rather than deserved.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world six beams sum to white at the hub, and Christianity's own scripture already claims the arithmetic: 'in him was life, and the life was the light of men' (John 1:4). Six colors converging into one radiance is a decomposition the faith would call, without much translation needed, the light of the world made visible again.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Christianity keeps its noon-center arm, now royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — and the faith already keeps a King of its own: Christ Pantocrator, crowned in glory, and 'by me kings reign' (Proverbs 8:15) reads as easily of the risen Christ as of Solomon's own throne.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Youth, sun-yellow (#FFE800) — and a faith whose God is called light itself (1 John 1:5) finds an easy home in life's blazing height: Christ's own ministry, brief and burning bright before the cross, is Youth's arc told as gospel.
variants.cross: The cross pointer marks the four seasons, and Christianity keeps the center where the arms meet — though the faith's own calendar sits close to the mark twice over: Christmas falls within days of the winter solstice, a date the fourth-century church set deliberately near Rome's old Sol Invictus feast, and Easter's own date is still reckoned from the spring equinox. Two of the cross's four arms were claimed before this dial ever drew them.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Sun keeps the center, and its theme is Faith — the vertical line toward God. Christianity does not borrow the word: 'faith, hope, love, these three; but the greatest of these is love' (1 Corinthians 13:13) is the faith's own triad, named in its own scripture long before this dial ever split white light three ways.

faces.ruler:
Christianity is the Ruler face — the faith that God entered history from below, the crucified and risen Christ, and a love that answers punishment with forgiveness rather than the reverse, now seated where history's own fusion once placed it: in 321 Constantine decreed rest on 'the venerable day of the Sun,' dies Solis, and no faith on this dial has a better claim to that day today, roughly 2.4 billion strong, the largest creed on Earth, on the one day of the week that still carries its old name, the Lord's Day.

The medallion sets a glowing white cross on black stone, the Chi-Rho monogram at its crossing and a crown of thorns lying at its foot; the silver rim weaves grapevines with tiny ichthys fish — the vine and the fishermen, the oldest signs of the church.

Its virtue is Justice, the same measure the faith turns first on itself before it turns on anyone else — 'first take the log out of your own eye' (Matthew 7:5) — but that same authority is stalked by Pride, the throne mistaking the scale of its own following for permission to judge rather than the duty to. Its mood is Glory, the stone already rolled away on the third day, the tomb found empty rather than sealed.
faces.servant:
Satanism is the Servant face — not a rival throne so much as the shape Christianity's own doctrine already drew for its opposite: the adversary, ha-satan, who needs the court's own permission before it can act at all (Job 1:12, 2:6), never once a sovereign in its own right, a shadow with no light of its own to cast.

The plate keeps the same grapevine-and-fish rim as the bright face above it, cast in dark red and turned against itself — the vines withered, the fish swimming tail-first, circling the opposite way — around a horned head rendered as a clean, logo-like emblem, eyes closed in cold repose, a small inverted pentagram traced faintly behind the crown of its horns.

Its virtue is Humility, though never once claimed as one: even Lucifer's own boast in scripture, 'I will ascend above the heights of the clouds, I will make myself like the Most High' (Isaiah 14:14), is answered in the very same passage by the fall that boast earns — a subordination the doctrine never lets this face escape, however loudly it refuses the word. Its vice is Servility, worship rerouted rather than abolished, the same bent knee pointed at a different altar. Its mood is Awe, the cold and the horns kept at a dignified distance rather than played for spectacle.

sun.name: Christianity

moon.name: Islam
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Islam is surrender to the one God - tawhid, the radical oneness of Allah - lived out in five daily prayers and in mercy invoked before every act.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion sets a golden crescent on deep lapis blue, its body filled with arabesque vinework - no image of the divine, only pattern, as the tradition insists - and a five-pointed star engraved like a rosette in the crescent's embrace.

[[On the Dial]] It moves from Friday's evening arm to Monday's arm at 4h, deep blue, the hour before dawn - and the new seat is the truer one: Islam tells time by the Moon, not the Sun. 'They ask you about the crescent moons,' the Quran says, 'say, they are timekeeping for mankind' (2:189); Ramadan opens and closes on a sighted crescent, not a fixed date, and Laylat al-Qadr, the Night of Power, is kept on one of the last odd nights the Moon's own dark end provides. Friday keeps its own honest place in the faith as Jumu'ah, the day the community stands shoulder to shoulder in prayer - but the calendar that counts the year belongs to the Moon, and so, now, does the arm.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Serenity with Fear under the Physician, and Islam answers both from the same well: 'And when My servants ask you concerning Me, indeed I am near' (2:186) is the faith's cure for fear, while the calm of salah, prayer paced five times through the day, is a serenity built into the clock rather than hoped for. Across the dial Mars answers in turn: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath - the healer and the soldier still bound to one axis, whichever faith stands on either arm today.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Moon's arm at 4h wears blue, the painter's pigment of depth and night. Ground-stone lapis is exactly what the owner's medallion is cut from, and lapis was the stone Quranic illumination itself was ground for - gold script laid over the same blue that darkens toward dawn.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the same 4h arm glows instead of covering - color as emission, not surface. A faith whose ninety-nine names for God include An-Nur, the Light, is at home in a palette built from beams rather than pigment: the pre-dawn call to Fajr kindled from within before the sky itself brightens.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Islam keeps its 03h deep-night arm, now lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — and few faiths fit a Book more exactly: Islam is, at root, the religion of a recited text, its whole civilization built on scholarship kept by lamplight through the deep hours the arm already governs.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is the Unborn, moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — ancestors and the not-yet-born together, and a faith that tells time by the crescent moon (Quran 2:189) already reads its calendar from a light that is itself always half-arrived, always becoming.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Moon body holds the spring arm, green, shared with Saturn - though Islam's own calendar would resist the label: eleven days shorter than the solar year, it lets Ramadan drift through every season in a thirty-three-year circuit, spring included one year in three. The dial's spring is not a fixed claim but exactly the freedom a pure lunar count was built for.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the Moon's body on its own 4h arm but renames the post Hope, sharing the blue with Mercury and Taoism. Islam already keeps this hour under its own name - Fajr, the dawn prayer said in the last dark before the sky lightens - hope timed to precisely the watch this arm was already keeping.

mars.name: Buddhism
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Buddhism is less a creed about gods than a diagnosis of suffering and a path out of it: all things pass, clinging hurts, and the clinging can be set down.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is lapis-blue stone under a golden eight-spoked Dharma wheel - one spoke per fold of the Eightfold Path - with a silver Buddha seated in meditation at its hub, and a rim of silver lotus blossoms rising, as lotuses do, clean out of the mud.

[[On the Dial]] It moves from Monday's calm arm to Tuesday's, the ember-orange 16h hour of Zeal, and the change trades a lunar footnote for the tradition's actual central scene: the night under the Bodhi tree was a battle. Mara's armies - fear, desire, doubt - massed against the Buddha before dawn, and he met them seated, unmoved, touching the earth itself to witness his right to stay. 'Though one may conquer a thousand times a thousand men in battle, one who conquers himself is the greatest of all conquerors,' the Dhammapada says (103) - and Tuesday's own canon virtue is Courage, its vice Wrath. A tradition whose founding victory was fought without a single weapon is exactly the one that disarms wrath and still takes the war-day.

[[The Lesson]] The arm carries Courage and Wrath under the Soldier, and Buddhism reads both as the same fire differently held. Wrath is craving turned outward, striking to relieve a pain it cannot name; Courage is the same energy kept and aimed inward instead, the discipline that faced Mara's whole army sitting perfectly still. The Soldier's oak-leaf endurance and the Buddha's cross-legged stillness are, in this light, one and the same battle stance.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Mars body wears orange at 16h - the pigment world's tempered ember, yellow's brightness folded into red's heat. Buddhism reads it as the balance the Buddha found after abandoning both a prince's luxury and an ascetic's starvation: the Middle Way, effort neither raw nor extinguished, wearing the color its own metaphor would choose.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the 16h arm burns as emission rather than pigment, and Buddhism's own image for awakened mind is luminosity - 'this mind is luminous, but it is defiled by adventitious defilements,' runs one recorded teaching. The ember read as light draws that claim as a dial arm: the glow was always there, effort only clears what covers it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Buddhism keeps its 15h afternoon arm, now working copper (#C87533) — the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object. It sits at a slant to a faith built on non-attachment, yet the Buddha himself was born a prince of a trading kingdom, and the Coin only names plainly what the path asks every merchant, and every mind, to finally set down.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Maturity, amber (#FFB400) — and no age fits the doctrine better. The Buddha's night under the Bodhi tree, meeting Mara's whole army seated and unmoved, is Maturity's exact discipline: 'one who conquers himself is the greatest of all conquerors' (Dhammapada 103).
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mars body stands with the autumn arm, shared with Venus. Buddhism keeps its own autumn rite on this exact spoke: Kathina, the robe-offering that closes Vassa, the three-month rains retreat, falls in this same season - the harvest reading of Zeal is a monastic year's harvest too, effort gathered in and formally laid down.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mars body sits on the red arm of Love, guarding the night third and sharing it with Venus and Christianity. Buddhism's own reading of Love is metta, loving-kindness extended without exception to every being, friend or enemy - a guard that needs no sword, only a mind held open through the watch.

mercury.name: Taoism
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Taoism follows the Way that cannot be named: water's way, yielding yet unbeatable, the low place all streams obey. The sage acts by not forcing - wu wei - and wins by yielding.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion cuts a taijitu of gold and dark metal into near-black marble: the bright half engraved with scrolling clouds, the dark half with mountains and waves - heaven turning inside earth. Around the rim eight bagua trigrams alternate with small yin-yangs.

[[On the Dial]] It keeps Wednesday because in the East Asian week Wednesday IS the water-star day, and water is the Tao's central metaphor. Its arm hangs at 24h, the purple midnight bottom - and the Tao has always preferred the dark, the valley, the root below.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Wisdom with Greed under the Merchant - a joke the Tao Te Ching enjoys: 'he who knows he has enough is rich.' Across the axis its wisdom curbs Jupiter's excess; at home it simply wants less.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Mercury body wears purple at the midnight bottom - the deepest mix on the painter's wheel, red and blue folded together until they nearly vanish. The Tao would approve of a color made by descent: the dial's lowest arm carrying its most inward pigment.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the midnight arm glows out of the dark, and that is a thoroughly Taoist image: the valley spirit that never dies, light held low. Where pigment absorbs, the luminous arm gives back - darkness used not as absence but as the fertile ground of shining.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Taoism keeps its midnight arm, now stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object. Wu wei, acting without forcing, is itself a kind of mask worn loosely: the sage who yields like water plays every part without ever seeming to perform one.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Old Age, dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — and the Tao Te Ching already prizes exactly this hour: 'he who knows he has enough is rich,' the wisdom that only comes once wanting less has finally become easier than wanting more.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mercury body holds the winter arm, the year's midnight - and winter is the Tao's home season: sap withdrawn to the root, water resting as ice, strength stored in stillness. What other faiths endure as bleakness, Taoism reads as the return that makes spring possible.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mercury body sits on the blue arm of Hope, guarding the morning third beside the Moon and Buddhism. Taoist hope is trust without grasping: the empty vessel confident of filling, the winter root sure of spring - hope as patience with the Way's own timing.

jupiter.name: Hinduism
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Hinduism is the oldest living river of faith: one Brahman behind countless faces, dharma as the order that holds the world, and moksha - release - as the far shore.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion raises a golden Om on amber stone, its curves filled with filigree, the crescent and bindu floating above like the last dot of the sound; the silver rim strings flower rosettes with small Om glyphs set at the quarters.

[[On the Dial]] It keeps Thursday because Thursday is Guruvar - the day of Brihaspati, guru of the gods, who IS Jupiter in Vedic astrology; no mapping on this dial is more literal. Its arm stands at the yellow 12h top, and yellow is what Thursday's devotees actually wear.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Generosity with Excess under the Priest-Teacher, the guru's own calling. Dana, giving, is a pillar of dharma; excess is abundance unmeasured, and the Gita's cure is measure itself - in food, in sleep, in joy.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Jupiter body wears yellow at the noon top - a primary pigment, needing no mixture, like the syllable Om needing no translation. Turmeric and marigold yellow is what Thursday's devotees wear in living practice; the painter's wheel simply agrees with the temple.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the noon arm shines at the dial's crown, one step from the white source. Hindu thought has a name for that adjacency: the medallion's Om is pranava, the sound-form of Brahman - first vibration out from the absolute, as this arm is the first station under the light.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Hinduism keeps its noon crown-arm, now alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a placement that needs no bending: Thursday is already Guruvar, the day of Brihaspati, guru of the gods, and the Bell simply rings what the guru's office already was.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Childhood, spring green (#7CE577) — and dharma's own cure for Excess is measure, in food, in sleep, in joy, the very discipline a faith teaches every child before it teaches anything else about the far shore.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Jupiter body takes the summer arm, the year's noon - fullness matching its Joy. Festival processions, open temple courts, the feeding of pilgrims by the thousand: abundance is the season's gift, and abundance is exactly what this body's Generosity exists to spend.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Jupiter body sits on the yellow arm of Faith, guarding midday into evening beside Saturn and Judaism. Faith here is shraddha - the trust a student places in a guru - so the teacher's planet carrying the Faith arm is Guruvar stated twice over.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Sikhism, founded by Guru Nanak in fifteenth-century Punjab, teaches one formless God, equality of all, honest work and sharing — ten Gurus completed by the scripture itself, enthroned as the living Guru.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion raises the golden Khanda against a starry midnight field: the double-edged sword upright in the chakkar's ring, two curved kirpans crossed behind it, in a silver rim of alternating lotus flowers and khanda emblems.

[[On the Dial]] It moves from Thursday's Guruvar pun to Friday's evening arm, deep red at 20h, and the new seat trades a calendar coincidence for the faith's own center of gravity: seva, service without payment or preference, is Sikhism's word for love in practice, and its clearest form is langar, the free kitchen every gurdwara keeps running — prince and laborer seated in the same row on the floor, pangat, eating the same food from the same kitchen. The five Ks worn on the body — kesh, kangha, kara, kacchera, kirpan — keep the same lesson stitched into the everyday; the kirpan especially is a weapon of last resort, not conquest, love armed enough to bleed for someone else's freedom. Friday's own Passion has a Sikh martyr's face for exactly that reason: Guru Tegh Bahadur was beheaded in Delhi in 1675 defending a faith that was never his own, the right of Kashmiri Hindus to worship freely.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Love with Jealousy under the Artist, and Sikhism answers jealousy with geometry as much as doctrine: langar seats everyone in the same row on the floor, so there is no higher place left at the table to envy. Ahankar, ego measured against a neighbor, is named among the Five Thieves the Gurus taught every Sikh to guard against, alongside lust, anger, greed and attachment — and the free kitchen is that discipline made edible, one plate, no rank. Across the dial Saturn answers in turn: Patience heals Jealousy, and this arm's own Love dissolves Envy before it is ever seated.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Venus body wears deep red at 20h — langar's own color in a diet of dal and roti, but Sikh memory keeps a deeper red on the same hour: Chandni Chowk in Delhi, where Guru Tegh Bahadur's blood was given in 1675 for a faith that was never his own. Passion, feeling and suffering at once, was already the evening arm's oldest word before Sikhism ever stood on it.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the same evening arm goes to pure red, light's own primary. The Sikh calendar keeps its own reddest days for exactly this reason: Shaheedi Diwas remembers Guru Arjan, the first Sikh martyr, tortured to death in 1606, and Guru Tegh Bahadur after him — the evening arm's color already spoken for twice over before this dial ever painted it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Sikhism keeps its evening arm, now iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — and the fit needs no irony at all: the Khanda already crosses two kirpans behind its blade, love armed enough to bleed for someone else's freedom, exactly as Guru Tegh Bahadur did in 1675 for a faith that was never his own.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is the Elder, ember (#FF6A3C) — banked, communal warmth, and langar's free kitchen is precisely that: a lifetime of seva cooling into a shared table where prince and laborer sit the same row, love grown patient enough to feed rather than only burn.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Venus body stands with the autumn arm, shared with Mars — and Sikhism keeps its own autumn homecoming on this same spoke: Bandi Chhor Divas, when Guru Hargobind returned free from Gwalior Fort in 1619 and freed fifty-two princes alongside him by insisting each hold a hem of his own cloak. Autumn's harvest, for once, is a harvest of prisoners let go.
variants.trio: Venus does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is its own 20h in its own red, and seva was always Sikhism's word for it too — langar needs no scripture to prove it, only a queue that never asks who is standing in it. Mars joins it there, sharing the red with Buddhism, and metta and seva turn out to be two tongues for the same instruction: feed the person in front of you now.

venus.name: Sikhism

saturn.name: Judaism
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Judaism is the covenant faith: one God, a people bound to Him by law and promise, and time itself made holy - its first sanctuary was not a building but a day.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion weaves a golden Star of David from two interlocked triangles on dark slate, the bands inscribed edge to edge with minute Hebrew script - a symbol literally built of text, for a people of the book; silver leaves and small stars circle the rim.

[[On the Dial]] It keeps Saturday because Saturday IS Shabbat: in Hebrew, Saturn is Shabtai, named for the Sabbath itself. Its arm is the green 8h arm of Renewal, and Shabbat is exactly that - the renewal the work-week exists to reach.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Patience with Envy under the Farmer - and Torah farms in sabbaths too: every shmita year the land itself rests as the people do. Patience is covenant kept through long exile; envy is what the tenth commandment names, and forbids.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Saturn body wears green at 8h - a mixed pigment, yellow worked into blue, joy tempered with depth. The mixing suits a tradition of argument and interpretation: nothing left simple, everything blended through generations of reading, and still unmistakably itself.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the 8h morning arm cools from green to cyan (#00DDDD), the clear early sky. Judaism keeps its lights literal - Shabbat candles at dusk, the ner tamid never allowed to go out, Hanukkah's eight flames - so a dial where color is kindled rather than painted speaks its native language.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Judaism keeps its dawn arm, now field green (#3E8914) — the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — a covenant that already farms in sabbaths: every shmita year the land itself rests as the people do, the Plough set down on schedule as an act of faith, not neglect.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel dawn is Birth, predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — and the covenant's whole logic is renewal answering exile: Shabbat itself is a weekly rehearsal of beginning again, and Birth is the age that rehearsal has always pointed toward.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Saturn body stands with the spring arm, and spring is where the Jewish year takes its bearings: Nisan, the month of Passover, is counted first, because liberation is the founding memory. Green Renewal on the spring arm is Exodus retold as a season.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Saturn body sits on the yellow arm of Faith, guarding midday into evening beside Jupiter and Hinduism. Faith here is emunah - steadfastness more than belief - and an arm that holds from noon until candle-lighting at dusk is emunah drawn as geometry.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] The Ruler sits enthroned in bronze: a crowned, bearded king with a tall scepter upright in his right hand and a small cross-topped orb resting open in his left palm, an eagle-blazoned shield leaning at the throne's side and imperial eagles engraved into the stone behind him. He is the Sun's child and Sunday's figure — the royal planet's calling, the profession the old woodcuts always drew closest to the light.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial he holds no arm at all: his home is the center, colored white-gold, the sum of every hue on the wheel. That is the star's whole political philosophy in one placement — a ruler is not one calling among six but the point the six radiate from, just as white light is not one color but all of them undivided. The crown works only if every arm can find itself in it.

[[The Lesson]] The canon makes him DOUBLE — the servant-king, black and white in one figure, the dial's own yin-yang. As ruler he practices Justice and is stalked by Pride; as servant he practices Humility and is stalked by Servility, the bent spine that serves the throne instead of the truth. The medallion argues the axis by itself: the world he rules is that little orb, small enough to sit in one open hand — held, not gripped. And since nothing stands opposite the center, the cure never leaves it: Humility answers Pride, Justice straightens Servility — the one calling on the dial that is both its own disease and its own medicine, in Glory at noon and in Awe when the light fails.

[[The Hour]] That is also why he has no hour. Every mood of the day — noon's Joy, midnight's Sorrow, the Calm before dawn — passes through the throne, and the ruler's discipline is to sit under all of them without belonging to any. The shield rests beside the throne, not on his arm: power at rest, light at its source.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel the Ruler keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are his light decomposed, every mood radiating from the throne. This is his home reading — the source, not a spoke.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around him but cannot change him: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world it is literally true that white is the sum of all colors. Newton's reading only proves what the throne already claimed.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Ruler keeps his center-turned-noon arm, now royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — and no relabeling was needed at all: the Ruler already IS the King the wheel now names outright, 'by me kings reign' (Proverbs 8:15) simply spoken over a throne that was always his.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Youth, sun-yellow (#FFE800) — and the small cross-topped orb resting in his open palm reads differently in this light: a reign is always someone's Youth once, the world held lightly before the years of Justice and Servility wear the grip in.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Ruler stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice — the sun's own festival. He shares the arm with Jupiter: throne and altar on one spoke, king and teacher holding the year's longest light together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Ruler keeps the center, and the court around him narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue, the painter's primaries. It is the throne's oldest reading made plain: a crown holds only what radiates from it, and all three virtues are his white-gold light taken apart.

faces.ruler:
The Ruler is the Sun's child and Sunday's own calling — the profession the old woodcuts always drew closest to the light, a crowned figure whose authority is meant to radiate rather than accumulate.

The medallion sits him enthroned in bronze: a crowned, bearded king with a tall scepter upright in his right hand and a small cross-topped orb resting open in his left palm, an eagle-blazoned shield leaning at the throne's side, imperial eagles engraved into the stone behind him.

His virtue is Justice, argued by the very orb he holds — the world he rules small enough to sit in one open hand, held rather than gripped — but that same authority is stalked by Pride, the throne mistaking the crown for something owed rather than lent. His mood is Glory, the shield resting beside the throne rather than raised on his arm: power fully at rest, light fully at its source.
faces.servant:
The Servant is the Ruler's own other face — not a separate calling but the same king with the crown lifted from his head, proof the canon has always called this figure double rather than inventing a new title for it.

The plate matches the Ruler's bronze relief and cracked-slate stone exactly, the same imperial eagles ghosted behind him, wings folded low instead of raised: the king stands before his own empty throne, crown lifted from his head and held low in both hands, eyes downcast, the ermine-trimmed robe replaced by a plain rope-belted tunic, one bare foot resting in a bronze basin of water where the eagle-blazoned shield stood in the Ruler plate.

His virtue is Humility, the crown physically surrendered rather than merely spoken of — but that same posture is stalked by Servility, the bent spine that serves the throne instead of the truth, obedience without the will behind it. His mood is Awe, the light failing at exactly the hour the crown comes off — and Glory is simply the next plate over: the same king, throne and crown both his again.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] The Physician stands mid-diagnosis: a cloaked, bearded man with a lancet raised in one hand and a mortar and pestle cradled in the other, an anatomy book lying open on the lectern beside him, a winged heart and two microscopes engraved into the stone — and the whole scene ringed by a border of serpent and laurel woven into a wreath — the single snake of Asclepius' rod, the healer's oldest animal. He is the Moon's child and Monday's figure: medieval medicine ran on the lunar calendar, timing its bloodletting and its cures by the phases.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn. No color fits a healer better: blue is the still, moonlit hour, and the pre-dawn watch is the physician's native shift, the long quiet stretch at the sickbed when the fever decides which way to break.

[[The Lesson]] The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear — the night's two faces, the same darkness lit or unlit — and the image holds both at once: the raised blade heals only if the hand holding it is steady. Straight across the dial at 16h stands the Soldier, and the two professions cure each other: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath. The surgeon must borrow the soldier's nerve; the soldier must borrow the surgeon's calm.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because that is when calm is hardest and worth most: the darkest stretch of the night, when the patient is most afraid and the watcher must not be.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — the physician's night watch, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps him at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's muted night becomes light's electric one. Same watch, same Calm; the lamp over the sickbed simply burns brighter.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Physician keeps his 03h arm, now lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — and his own anatomy text lying open on the lectern already makes the case: every healer is first a reader, the Book the Physician's oldest instrument before the lancet ever touches skin.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is the Unborn, moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — ancestors and the not-yet-born together, and no calling sits closer to that border than the Physician's: the hand at the sickbed decides, more often than any other profession, which side of that hue a life lands on.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Physician moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with Saturn, the Farmer. Healer and grower on one spoke, and rightly: both tend living things, and both must wait for them.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the Physician at 04h but renames his watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with the Merchant, and it guards the morning from 0h to 8h. No calling earns the word more honestly — hope is the physician's true instrument, the thing he keeps alive at the bedside until the fever breaks.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] The Soldier stands square in scale armor, a spear held slantwise in his right hand and a round shield stamped with an eagle on his left arm; crossed spears and axes rise behind his shoulders, a spectacled helm and a battle-axe are engraved in the stone beside him, and the whole ring is wreathed in oak leaves — the tree of endurance, not of triumph. He is Mars' child and Tuesday's figure, the red planet's oldest calling.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon. The placement is deliberately unromantic: war in this canon is not the dawn charge but the long grind of the afternoon, fire banked down to a working heat. Orange is Mars' ember — hot enough to forge with, not yet the evening's open flame.

[[The Lesson]] His two hands carry his two faces: the shield is Courage and the spear is Wrath — one fire that defends or destroys, the warrior's virtue and vice being the same heat pointed differently. Across the dial at 04h waits the Physician, and each is the other's medicine: Serenity quells Wrath, Courage cures Fear. The calm hand cools the burning one; the burning heart steadies the frightened one.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is the soldier's true hour: past noon's glory, before evening's passion — the discipline of continuing after the excitement is gone. Anyone can start a battle at dawn; the soldier is whoever is still holding the line at four.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the banked fire of the base article.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — in light, red and green fused. The working fire glows hotter in Newton's reading, though the hour and its Zeal stand unchanged.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Soldier keeps his 15h arm, now working copper (#C87533) — the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — a deliberate friction: the spear that once meant Zeal now prices what it has won. Even the oak-leaf wreath around him, endurance rather than triumph, already knew war has its ledgers as much as its glories.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Maturity, amber (#FFB400) — and it suits him exactly: Maturity is Courage with the shouting worn off, the discipline of still holding the line at four in the afternoon, long past any dawn charge's glamour.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Soldier stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Venus, the Artist. Mars beside Venus, the oldest pairing in the sky: autumn's red belongs to them both, blood and rose in one color.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Soldier moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside the Artist, and the pair guard the evening from 16h to 24h. It is the arm that explains the profession: courage is love wearing armor — the soldier stands the night watch not for the fight's sake but for the sake of what sleeps behind him.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] The Merchant sits at his counting table in a fur-trimmed coat and peaked cap, a hand-balance raised in his right fist and a tall staff gripped in his left — Hermes' rod still standing in the trader's hand — with stacks of coins and an open ledger before him and sacks spilling gold at both sides; the border around him is minted from coins, gold and silver, with balance scales set at the quarters. He is Mercury's child and Wednesday's figure: Hermes was the god of merchants and roads.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in deep purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. The color is history's private joke on him: Tyrian purple was the costliest pigment of the ancient world, worth more than the gold in his sacks — a color that was itself a trade empire. And midnight is the accounting hour: the day is over, the market closed, and what remains is to weigh what it was worth.

[[The Lesson]] The scale in his raised hand is the whole ethics of the arm: Wisdom is knowing the true weight of things; Greed is wanting the balance to tip. Straight up the axis at noon stands the Priest, and the two correct each other across the dial: Generosity cures Greed, and Wisdom curbs Excess — the open hand teaching the closed one, the careful scale steadying the overflowing table.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because every honest ledger closes in it: the day's grave, where profit and loss are finally told apart. The merchant is the one calling that must look at the bottom of the wheel nightly — and wisdom begins exactly there.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of the closed ledger — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for Mercury, the mind itself: at midnight he wears a color that exists only in thought.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Merchant keeps his 21h arm, now stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — and the hand-balance in his fist finally makes sense of it: a fair scale is itself a kind of performance, honesty acted out in public so the counterparty believes the weight. The ledger becomes a stage; the Mask was always half the trade.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Old Age, dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — and no calling accrues it more literally: wisdom compounds like the coins in his sacks, and the Merchant who has weighed a lifetime of trades finally trades in judgment instead of gold.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Merchant keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and he alone shares it with no one. The year's midnight belongs to a single figure: the one calling that can sit at the bottom of the wheel, alone, and still balance the books.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the Merchant off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Physician, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names the trade's true engine: every venture is hope with numbers on it — the ledger closed in sorrow at midnight reopens at the morning's gate, credit extended to the day.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] The Priest — the Teacher — stands robed to the ground, his right hand lifting a staff crowned with a radiant sun above his own head, his left holding an open book turned outward for others to read; a small stone altar stands beside him, a bare tree and whole flocks of birds are cut into the stone behind him, and open books repeat around the border. He is Jupiter's child and Thursday's figure — in the Vedic week Thursday is Guruvar, the day of Brihaspati, who IS Jupiter: the guru of the gods.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter'. The image explains the placement by itself: he raises a sun on a staff because that is what teaching is — lifting the light higher than your own head so that it falls on someone else. Noon is the hour when light is given most freely, and yellow is that gift as a color.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity — the book held open and outward, knowledge given away — and his vice is Excess, the royal table overflowing, the blessing that becomes a flood. Straight down the axis at midnight sits the Merchant, and each cures the other: Wisdom curbs Excess, and Generosity cures Greed — the scale below teaching measure to the open hand above.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is the one hour that needs no lamp: the day's peak, when the sun — like the teacher — asks nothing back for its light.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. The teacher's hour keeps its Joy; light simply measures the peak instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Priest keeps his noon arm, now alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the estate of the Priest itself, the Bell for its object — the one seat on the whole wheel that needed no argument at all: he already lifts a radiant sun on his own staff, and the Bell only rings the hour he was already teaching.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Childhood, spring green (#7CE577) — and the open book turned outward in his hand reads as exactly that: a lesson pitched to a mind still new enough to take it whole, generosity given before Excess has had time to spoil it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Priest stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with the Sun itself. Teacher beside king on the year's highest spoke: the light and the one who lifts it, on the same arm at last.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Priest keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, his own working material, and he shares the arm with the Farmer as they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. The placement reads itself: faith is the sun he lifts on the staff, raised higher than his own head so others can see by it.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] The Artist is the only figure on the whole dial who carries actual color: in a world of engraved bronze, the palette in his raised hand bears real dabs of red, blue, yellow and green. A young, long-haired figure in an embroidered doublet and flowing cape, he touches a brush to the palette while a small hammer waits in his lowered hand and a scroll with a quill is tucked at his belt; behind him the comedy and tragedy masks — one laughing, one weeping — hang beside an engraved lyre. He is Venus' child and Friday's figure: in the old woodcuts the planet's children are the musicians and the lovers.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, when Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour fits the calling exactly: evening is when work releases the heart, when the theatre lights come up, when the day's material world softens into its emotional one. Red is that hour made pigment.

[[The Lesson]] The two masks behind him are his virtue and his vice wearing faces: Love, and Jealousy — the fear of losing what is loved, love's own shadow rather than its opposite. Across the dial at 08h stands the Farmer, and the two heal each other: Patience heals Jealousy, and Love dissolves Envy — the sower teaches the lover to wait, and the lover teaches the sower to cherish his own field rather than covet another's.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because passion is what the daylight was for: the day earns, the evening feels. And the palette explains why this figure alone receives color — love, like art, is the moment the world stops being bronze.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the evening star's own hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is not a pigment but a wavelength, and the evening burns in it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Artist keeps his 18h arm, now iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — a hard trade for the one figure on the dial who carries real color, yet the comedy and tragedy masks already hanging behind him prove he has always fought his battles with a different weapon: Jealousy is love's own war, and the Sword only makes the metaphor literal.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is the Elder, ember (#FF6A3C) — a banked, settled warmth, and it flatters the calling more than it costs it: half of art is memory, and the Elder's ember is exactly the hour a life's passions are finally recorded rather than merely felt.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Artist stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Mars, the Soldier. Venus beside Mars, as in every myth: autumn's red is harvest and blood and rose at once, and it takes both figures to say so.
variants.trio: The Artist does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is his own 20h in his own red, for love is his virtue in both sets at once. The Soldier joins him, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — guard and poet of the same dark hours, one keeping what is loved safe, the other keeping it in color.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] The Farmer stands in rough, fringed cloth with a net slung across his shoulders, a sickle and a bundle of wheat ears raised in his right hand and a shepherd's crook in his left; a plough and an axe lie at his feet, a ram and an ox rest behind him, and the whole scene is wreathed in a border braided from wheat. He is Saturn's child and Saturday's figure — and the sickle in his fist is Cronus' own blade, the harvest god's tool handed down to the harvest man.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning. The hour is his by right of habit: the farmer is the calling that rises with it, and green is dew and growth, the week renewing itself on the day of rest.

[[The Lesson]] His color holds his virtue and his vice in a single hue: green is growth — Patience, the sower who waits a season for what he planted — and green is also envy, the bitter green of the neighbor's better harvest. One color, two faces, exactly like the arm itself. Across the dial at 20h stands the Artist, and each cures the other: Love dissolves Envy, and Patience heals Jealousy — the lover teaches the farmer to love his own field, the farmer teaches the lover that what is worth having is worth waiting for.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because the farmer's whole faith lives there: every morning the field is slightly different, and every seed is a wager that waiting works.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than of the field. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world the farmer looks up before he looks down.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Farmer keeps his 06h dawn arm, now field green (#3E8914) — the estate of the Farmer itself, the Plough for its object — the sickle already in his fist needing only its partner tool returned to the other hand: no seat on the whole wheel required less persuading.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel dawn is Birth, predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — and it is the Farmer's whole faith made an hour: every seed already a wager that a beginning comes, and Birth is simply that wager finally paid.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Farmer stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: sowing season, his own festival. He shares the spoke with the Moon, the Physician — grower and healer together, the two patient professions that tend life and wait for it.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes the Farmer from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with the Priest, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The pairing is the oldest sermon there is — sowing is faith performed with the hands, every seed a creed: buried, waited on, and believed in before it is seen.

moon.name: Druidism
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Druidism is the old faith of the Celtic lands and its modern rebirth: a religion of groves, wells and turning seasons, whose scripture is the living land itself. The druid was priest, healer, judge, keeper of memory.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion shows that trust: a golden Tree of Life whose roots braid downward as its branches climb, a green light glowing in the trunk's heart, ringed by silver Celtic knotwork threaded with red-capped fly agaric mushrooms.

[[On the Dial]] It keeps Monday because druids counted time by the moon - nights before days, months before years; Pliny set the mistletoe rite on the sixth day of the moon. It rides the Moon's blue arm at hour 4, the still hour before dawn.

[[The Lesson]] The arm's pair is the grove's own lesson: Fear is the dark wood seen from outside, Serenity the same wood known from within as kin - fitting for the Physician's arm, whose druid emblem was mistletoe, the all-heal.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's hexagram the Moon body wears blue, one of the three pigment primaries - unmixed, original, a color no palette can compose. Pigment is druidry's world: woad from leaves, ochre from earth, the sacred ground by hand from the land itself.
variants.hexa_light: In Newton's world the Moon keeps blue, for blue is a primary in both wheels - pigment and beam agree on it. Yet here it is no longer woad crushed from leaves but moonlight itself: cold, direct, the very light by which druids counted their nights, their months and their tides.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Druidism keeps its 03h arm, now lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — fittingly for a faith whose only scripture was the living land itself and whose druids were, among all else, the keepers of memory: the Book here is simply the grove finally given pages.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is the Unborn, moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — ancestors and the not-yet-born together, and a faith that counted nights before days already lived by that same pale, half-arrived light.
variants.cross: The cross pointer marks the year's four hinges, and the Moon body at hour 4 flanks the dawn arm - the spring equinox side, shared with Saturn's body. For druids this is Alban Eilir, the Light of the Earth: day and night in equal measure, the balance the whole grove-faith keeps.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Moon body stands on the Hope arm, deep blue, guarding the morning third from 0h to 8h and sharing the arm with Mercury. Hope is the druid's horizon virtue: the moon always returns and the wheel always turns - night, in this faith, is a promise rather than an end.

mars.name: Zoroastrianism
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Zoroastrianism is among the oldest prophetic faiths: Zarathustra taught that the world is a battlefield between asha - truth, order, light - and the lie, with everyone enlisted through good thoughts, good words, good deeds.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion sets the golden Faravahar on night-dark stone: a crowned, bearded figure rising from a winged disc, one hand raised, the other holding the ring of covenant, inside a silver rim engraved with small flames.

[[On the Dial]] It takes Tuesday because fire is its altar: Atar burns on the fiery planet's day, on the orange arm at hour 16 beside Ares, Tyr and the Soldier - a soldierly faith, every believer a volunteer in truth's war.

[[The Lesson]] Its arm's Courage is asha itself, the nerve to tell the truth; its Wrath is fire's own vice, the tended flame turned wildfire. A religion that has kept single temple fires alight for a thousand years knows the difference exactly.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Mars body burns orange, the painter's fire: red passion folded into yellow joy to make ember. Paint suits a religion of tended flame - color as something material, kept and fed, like the temple fires priests have nourished with sandalwood for centuries.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world colors are not mixed but emitted, and the Mars body glows rather than smolders - fire as radiance instead of ember. That is the Zoroastrian reading of flame: Atar is not burning matter but the visible presence of truth, the beam by which asha is seen.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Zoroastrianism keeps its 15h arm, now working copper (#C87533) — the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — an odd metal for a fire-faith until asha itself is recalled: truth kept honestly, like a fair weight, is the same discipline whether counted in coin or kept burning in a temple for a thousand years.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Maturity, amber (#FFB400) — the color of a tended flame rather than a wild one, and Zoroastrianism's whole ethic of good thoughts, good words, good deeds is exactly Maturity: courage grown disciplined enough to keep a single fire alight for centuries.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mars body at hour 16 flanks the dusk arm - the autumn equinox side, shared with Venus. That is Mehregan, the feast of Mithra, lord of covenant and judge of oaths: a season of reckoning fit for a faith where every soul must cross the bridge of its own deeds.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mars body stands on the Love arm, deep red, guarding the evening from 16h to 24h beside Venus. In Zoroastrian terms that love is devotion, Spenta Armaiti - the fire fed through the dark watches; choosing the good again each night is how this faith loves.

mercury.name: Shamanism
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Shamanism is humanity's oldest religious craft: by drum, fast or vision the shaman leaves the body, travels the world-tree and bargains with spirits for healing and lost souls. The word is Evenki; the practice, worldwide.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion glows like a fire in a dark tent: a totem of thunderbird, wolf and bear stacked atop a round hide drum laced with crossed cords, ember light rising beneath, ringed in pewter feathers and antlers.

[[On the Dial]] It takes Wednesday, the messenger's day: the shaman is Mercury's kind of traveler. Odin, this arm's god, hung nine nights on the tree to seize the runes - a shamanic initiation. The purple arm at hour 24 marks midnight, when the drum opens the door.

[[The Lesson]] The arm's Wisdom is bought with ordeal and only real when given away; its Greed is the shaman's corruption - hoarding the spirits' gifts, selling the crossing. Fittingly, the Merchant works the same arm.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Mercury body wears purple, red and blue married into a between-color - the shaman's exact address, neither this world nor the other. In paint the hue is earthly and deep, pigment smeared on a drumhead or blown against a cave wall ten thousand years ago.
variants.hexa_light: In the world of light the purple reach of the wheel grows stranger: where red and blue beams meet lies a color no rainbow contains, a hue the eye invents at the spectrum's closed seam. A fitting banner for the walker between worlds - the shaman stands where the visible loops back on itself.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Shamanism keeps its midnight arm, now stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — and no calling wears a truer mask: the shaman who leaves the body by drum or trance already performs a face the spirits will recognize, art and ritual sharing the same painted door.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is Old Age, dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — and it is earned the hard way, exactly as Odin's nine nights on the tree earned his runes: wisdom bought by ordeal, real only once it is given away rather than hoarded.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mercury body stands at hour 24, on the winter solstice arm itself - the longest night, silver-tipped at the dial's foot. Winter is the shaman's season: the world sleeps, the tent closes, the drum begins, and the deepest journeys are made while the sun is furthest away.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mercury body stands on the Hope arm, deep blue, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h beside the Moon. Hope is the shaman's entire errand: to go down after the lost and return with them by dawn. Every journey wagers that something can be brought back.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] The Eleusinian Mysteries were antiquity's most closely kept secret: rites held near Athens for roughly two thousand years around the story of Demeter, goddess of grain, and her daughter Persephone (Kore), carried into the underworld and returned to the light each spring — death answered not with an argument but with a season.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion raises a golden sheaf of wheat beside a wide silver kykeon cup, a torchlit procession of hooded figures winding away into the dark behind them, and two silhouettes standing just beyond the torches as if mother and daughter reunited — one crowned in wheat, one in narcissus flowers. The rim alternates silver torches with small poppy-pod roundels, flames caught mid-flicker.

[[On the Dial]] It takes Thursday because Thursday's arm belongs to the Priest-Teacher, and Eleusis produced antiquity's own template for that very office: the Hierophant, 'the one who shows the sacred,' a title held hereditarily by the Eumolpidae clan for two thousand years — the word history has borrowed ever since for any priest of a mystery. Its arm stands at the yellow 12h top, in noon's own harvest-gold, Demeter's gift before it is ever bread.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Generosity with Excess under the Priest-Teacher, and no mystery cult of the ancient world gave more freely: the kykeon cup was offered to any Greek-speaker who walked the Sacred Way from Athens to Eleusis and had not killed — slave beside citizen, woman beside man, on the same processional road. Excess is the same rite profaned: in 415 BCE Alcibiades and his circle were accused of performing the Mysteries as a drunken party trick outside the sanctuary, the scandal that drove them into exile — the one crime this generosity could never forgive.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Jupiter body wears yellow, a painter's primary — and the wheat sheaf at the medallion's own heart already wears it, threshed and given rather than kept. Demeter's oldest title, Sito, 'she of the grain,' names the same color the wheel does before either one was ever asked to.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the same noon arm turns pure green (#00DC00), one of the three primary beams that sum to white. The Telesterion held thousands of initiates under one roof for a single shared rite, no separate fire kept for any one of them alone — a green built from joined beams reads the same way: no single lamp, one witnessed hall.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Eleusinian Mysteries keep their noon arm, now alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — and the Hierophant, 'the one who shows the sacred,' already held that very office for two thousand years before the word was ever borrowed for anyone else's priesthood.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Childhood, spring green (#7CE577) — and the epopteia, the beholding, the moment initiates were finally shown the light none could ever describe again, is Childhood's own wonder given back to grown initiates: a first sight, kept sacred by never being repeated in words.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Jupiter body takes the summer arm, the year's noon. The Mysteries' own rite fell in autumn, but the Attic wheat harvest itself does not wait that long — cut and threshed in the weeks either side of the solstice, months before Demeter's own festival, this arm keeps the grain's actual season even though her rite keeps a later one.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Jupiter body carries the Faith arm itself, bright yellow, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h beside Saturn. Faith here is the initiate's own word for it: Cicero, who had walked the Sacred Way himself, wrote that the rites taught a mortal 'not only to live with joy, but to die with a better hope' — trust professed once, at the one initiation an entire city kept silent about forever after.

jupiter.name: Eleusinian Mysteries

saturn.name: Voodoo
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Vodou was born in Haiti when West African religion survived the slave ships, wearing Catholic saints as masks: one creator, Bondye, served through the lwa, spirits who ride their people in ceremony, and the ancestors.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion restores dignity to the emblem outsiders know best: a burlap doll with a copper skull face, chest bound in an X of twine, cowries ringing the silver rim, and a copper belly plate engraved with a veve, the sign that calls a spirit down.

[[On the Dial]] It rules Saturday through Baron Samedi - 'Baron Saturday' - keeper of the crossroads and the dead, at home on Saturn's green arm at hour 8, the Farmer's arm: the Ghede govern graves and fertility alike.

[[The Lesson]] The arm's Patience is the ancestor-faith itself, debts honored over generations; its Envy is the sorcery Vodou is slandered with and its ethics condemn. Green Renewal is its truth: nothing sprouts that was not buried.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Saturn body wears green, the painter's mix of yellow and blue - growth out of dark ground. Pigment is Vodou's own medium: veve traced in cornmeal and ash on the temple floor, color poured onto earth to open a way. This green is graveyard grass, life fed by the dead.
variants.hexa_light: In the world of light the arm cools from green to cyan (#00DDDD), the pale sky just before sunrise. There is a quiet justice in that: a faith of the enslaved, long dismissed as darkness, wears the dawn's own color in the brighter wheel. Cyan is the Ghede's paradox made visible - out of the tomb, the first light of morning.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Vodou keeps its dawn arm, now field green (#3E8914) — the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — and Baron Samedi, Baron Saturday, already governs graves and fertility together: the Ghede's whole office is proof that nothing sprouts that was not first buried.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel dawn is Birth, predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — and an ancestor-faith built on debts honored over generations finds its truest hour here: the dead are kept close precisely so that Birth, when it comes, arrives already known and already welcomed.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Saturn body at hour 8 flanks the dawn arm - the spring equinox side, shared with the Moon. Spring is this faith's deepest teaching made season: the Ghede who rule the grave rule fertility too, and nothing sprouts that was not first buried. Dawn out of the cemetery is Vodou's whole hope.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Saturn body stands on the Faith arm, bright yellow, guarding 8h to 16h beside Jupiter. Vodou's faith is service, sevis lwa - a reciprocal bond: feed the spirits and they hold your world steady. Even the Baron, joking at death's gate, is trusted utterly to open it.

sun.name: Mithraism (Sol Invictus)
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Mithraism was Rome's best-kept secret: a mystery religion of soldiers, merchants and customs officers that spread from Britain to the Euphrates between the first and fourth centuries, worshipping in windowless cave-temples called mithraea. Its whole architecture was the week's: seven grades of initiation, each under one of the seven planets — Corax the Raven under Mercury, Nymphus the Bridegroom under Venus, Miles the Soldier under Mars, Leo the Lion under Jupiter, Perses the Persian under the Moon, Heliodromus the Sun-Runner under the Sun, Pater the Father under Saturn. A Mithraist did not merely keep the planetary week; he climbed it.
