[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is the tauroctony, the cult's one universal icon, struck in bronze on a starred lapis field: Mithras in his Phrygian cap kneels astride the white bull and draws the dagger across its flank, the raven alighting on his billowing cloak, the dog and the serpent leaping for the wound, the scorpion clamped at the bull's loins — and above the cave's stone arch the radiate face of Sol blazes in a sunburst. The rim weaves ravens, hounds, scorpions and stars between Mithraic monograms, gold on silver over deep blue.

[[On the Dial]] It guards Sunday by the shortest possible chain of title: this IS the cult of dies Solis. In 274 Aurelian made Sol Invictus, the Unconquered Sun, the empire's official god; in 321 Constantine decreed rest on 'the venerable day of the Sun' — the very Sunday this dial still keeps. And the tauroctony itself is a star map: bull Taurus, dog Canis, serpent Hydra, raven Corvus, scorpion Scorpius — the constellations strung along the celestial equator, the turning sky mastered at a stroke. The white-gold CENTER, the source the six colors decompose from, is exactly where Rome put its unconquered source of light.

[[The Lesson]] Senators crawled blindfolded through initiation as Ravens and served at table long before rising toward Pater, while the radiate crown of Sol became the very costume emperors reached for when Pride wanted one — so the center pairs Humility against Pride, and even Sol does not command Mithras but shakes his hand, the dexiosis. Yet the center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, and the mysteries drilled that doubleness into every initiate: whoever rose to Pater had first crawled as a Corax and served the banquet — the king in the cult was its former servant, on purpose. Justice was the grade ladder itself, the same seven steps for senator and slave; Servility its corruption, serving rank instead of light. Sol INVICTUS names the mood pair outright: the Unconquered Sun is Glory precisely because the Awe never holds it.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa Mithraism holds the unmixable white-gold center, and its founding image agrees: Mithras petrogenitus, born out of solid rock with the torch already lit. Light out of stone — given from elsewhere, never mixed on a palette — is the paint wheel's doctrine of the center told as a nativity.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world six beams sum to white at the hub, and the cult built exactly that room: a mithraeum had no windows, so the only sun in the cave was the one the mysteries carried in — Sol's radiate face over the bull, emission in the dark, white light assembled where no daylight reaches.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Mithraism keeps its noon-center arm, now royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — and Rome had already crowned this exact light: Aurelian's Sol Invictus, the Unconquered Sun, wearing the radiate crown emperors later reached for whenever Pride wanted one.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is Youth, sun-yellow (#FFE800) — and the cult kept a uniformed office for precisely this hour: Heliodromus, the Sun-Runner, sixth of the seven grades, whose whole duty was to walk the sun's road at its freshest and fastest, before any grade's slower wisdom set in.
variants.cross: The seasons cross points its four arms at solstices and equinoxes — and that is the tauroctony's deepest reading: bull Taurus and scorpion Scorpius are the old equinoctial pair, and the slaying has been read as the precession itself, the spring point wrestled out of Taurus. A god shown turning the wheel of the year stands naturally at the crossing where the year's four hinges meet.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Sun keeps the center and the yellow arm of Faith rises vertically from it — Mithraism's native virtue, sworn rather than felt: initiates bound themselves by sacramentum, the soldier's oath, and the handshake of Mithras and Sol above the bull is Roman fides cast in bronze. Faith as a contract kept in the dark and paid at dawn, with Hope at 4h and Love at 20h fanning from the same disc.

faces.ruler:
Mithraism is the Ruler face — Rome's best-kept secret, a mystery religion of soldiers and merchants worshipping in windowless cave-temples, its whole architecture built on seven grades of initiation, one for each planet.

The medallion is the tauroctony, the cult's one universal icon, struck in bronze on a starred lapis field: Mithras in his Phrygian cap kneels astride the white bull and draws the dagger across its flank, raven on his cloak, dog and serpent leaping for the wound, scorpion at the bull's loins, the radiate face of Sol blazing above the cave's stone arch, ravens, hounds, scorpions and stars woven round the rim.

Its virtue is Justice, the same seven-grade ladder climbed by senator and slave alike, no rank exempted from the same steps — but that ladder is stalked by Pride, the radiate crown of Sol becoming exactly the costume emperors reached for when they wanted to look unconquerable rather than earn it. Its mood is Glory, Sol Invictus itself, the Unconquered Sun blazing over the cave precisely because no eclipse ever holds it for long.
faces.servant:
Corax is the Servant face — the Raven, the first and lowest of the seven Mithraic grades, standing in for every initiate before he has climbed a single rung toward Pater.

The plate keeps the bronze-and-gold relief and raven-hound-scorpion-star border of the tauroctony, but the scene has moved indoors to the grade-banquet: Pater, the cult's highest grade, sits in a Phrygian cap at the head of a stone table lit by the same radiate Sol blazing through the cave arch behind him, while a raven-masked Corax initiate kneels low, serving him a cup and plate at the table's foot, a raven perched on his shoulder, the tauroctony carved faintly into the wall behind them both.

Its virtue is Humility, the plain fact the cult built into its own ranks on purpose — that whoever would rise to Pater first crawled as a Corax and served the banquet — while Servility is the grade gone wrong, rank served instead of the light the rank exists to carry toward. Its mood is Awe, the initiate's own light kept deliberately low at the cave's foot — and Glory is the same figure's whole future, climbing grade by grade toward the seat now serving him his cup.

venus.name: Babylonian religion (Babylon)
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Babylon's faith made the planets gods and the gods mathematics: its priests watched Venus from ziggurat tops, pressed her risings into clay, and named the seven days after the seven wanderers - the very week this clock still keeps was Babylon's invention.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is Ishtar entire: her eight-pointed star in burning copper, cuneiform running down the rays, the sign of Venus set in its heart; beneath it strides her lion - the same beast that paced the Processional Way in glazed brick - and the silver rim parades lions' heads between palm fronds on a starry lapis night.

[[On the Dial]] It guards Friday by identity, not analogy: Ishtar IS Venus, morning and evening star, and Friday is Venus' day in every tongue that kept the planetary week. The red 20h arm, Passion's hour, is her exact address - the evening star lighting the first hour of the night.

[[The Lesson]] The arm pairs Love with Jealousy, and no goddess carried both more openly: goddess of love AND war in one body, who, refused by Gilgamesh, loosed the Bull of Heaven on his city. The Artist shares her arm - and Babylon's artists answered her with the most beautiful bricks ever fired.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa Babylon wears the deep red of Venus' 20h arm as pigment - kiln-fired, like the city itself: glazed brick reds and golds burned permanent in the Ishtar Gate. Love as a material you can build walls from, jealousy as the same fire left untended.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the red arm becomes emission - and Ishtar was always light first: the evening star, the one lamp lit before all others in the darkening sky. Her medallion star glows copper against lapis night exactly as Venus does at dusk.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Ishtar keeps her 18h arm, now iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — and no goddess needs less persuading: she was already war and love in one body, and the Bull of Heaven loosed on Gilgamesh's city after his refusal proves Love's Jealousy could field an actual army.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that arm is the Elder, ember (#FF6A3C) — banked fire over centuries of watching, and it suits the priests who tracked her risings from ziggurat tops for generations: a light young at every dawn, but read, over such long study, with an Elder's patience.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Venus' body shares the autumn arm with Mars - love and war side by side, which for Babylon is no pairing at all but one goddess: Ishtar owned both. Autumn suits her too - the harvest festivals when the evening star stood longest over the city.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer her body stands on the red arm of Love, paired with Mars - and Babylon would call that arm simply 'Ishtar', love and war in one body, guarding the night third where the evening star reigns. Love as the theology of the first hour of darkness.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Ra is not a god who rules the sun; he is the sun. Every day he crosses the sky in the Mandjet, the day barque, growing old as the light reddens; every night he dies into the Duat aboard the night barque and fights his way through the serpent-haunted dark to be reborn at dawn. Three thousand years of Egyptian faith turned on that circuit — temples faced his risings, pharaohs ruled as his sons, and the other gods took their greatness by joining their names to his.

[[The Plate]] The medallion gives him in polished gold as Ra-Horakhty, the falcon-headed form of the risen sun: the blazing disc burns above the falcon's brow with the cobra Wadjet coiled around it, her hooded head rearing beside the fire; the striped royal headdress falls to a broad collar, with columns of hieroglyphs engraved down the golden chest; and the rim braids ankhs — the key of life — with Eyes of Horus, gold on silver over deep lapis blue, life and protection circling the god without a break.

[[On the Dial]] He belongs to Sunday because Sunday is his in the oldest sense: the planetary week names this day for the Sun, and no pantheon fused sun, king and temple more completely than Egypt. On this dial the Sun takes no arm at all — it holds the white-gold center, the source the six arm colors decompose from — and Egypt anticipated exactly that geometry: Amun-Ra, Ra-Horakhty, Atum-Ra — every great god was some hue of him, flowing into and out of the one disc the way the arms flow from the hub.

[[The Lesson]] Kings called themselves sons of Ra, yet every heart, royal or common, ended on the scales against the feather of Ma'at — so the center pairs Humility with Pride, the one axis whose cure comes not from across the dial but from itself. And that center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, which is simply Ra's day written as doctrine: the day barque in Glory, the night barque through the Duat in Awe, one god in two boats. His Justice is Ma'at riding at the prow, the feather every heart is weighed against, pharaoh's included; his servant face is the oarsman of his own route, aging by dusk to be reborn by dawn, and Servility is that oarsman forgetting the route is sacred. Humility answers Pride, Justice straightens Servility — the cycle heals itself nightly.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa Ra takes no arm: he holds the white-gold center, the source from which all six pigment arms take their color. Egypt drew him the same way — Amun-Ra, Ra-Horakhty, every god a hue of the one disc — and no hour belongs to him because his barque is under way through all twenty-four.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette nothing moves for Ra — the center stays white-gold, and the reading only sharpens: the disc above the falcon's brow is not a painted surface but emission itself, Newton's white beam an instant before the prism breaks it into red, green and blue — Ra at dawn pouring out every color the day will spend.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the falcon-headed Ra takes the noon arm robed in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of the King, the Crown his object — and no seat needs less argument: every pharaoh already called himself Ra's son, and the striped headdress beneath his sun-disc was a crown before it was a costume. 'By me kings reign' could have been carved at Karnak as easily as in Proverbs; the god who crosses the sky each day simply steps onto the arm built for the throne he already keeps.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm belongs to Youth, and Ra wears the wheel's own sun-yellow (#FFE800) — a fitting recursion for a god who is reborn every single dawn and never actually grows old, dying each dusk only to rise young again each morning. Three thousand years of temples faced this risen, ever-young light; the Ages wheel simply names what the Mandjet already performed daily.
variants.cross: On the cross the arms point at the year's hinges, and Ra holds the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with Jupiter — a pairing Egypt made literal: Jupiter's day belongs to Amun, and the two were fused as Amun-Ra, king of gods and sun in one name. The summer solstice was Ra's own hinge as well, Sirius rising beside it to call the flood and open the Egyptian year.
variants.trio: The Trinity returns Ra to the white-gold center with three arms around him — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue, the painter's three primaries — and their thirds retell his voyage: Faith holds 8h–16h, the day barque's high crossing; Love takes 16h–24h, the western descent where Hathor, lady of the west, receives the aging sun; Hope keeps 0h–8h, the night passage through the Duat whose whole meaning is the certainty of dawn.

faces.ruler:
Ra is the Ruler face because Ra is not a god who rules the sun; he is the sun — three thousand years of Egyptian faith turned on his daily circuit, temples facing his risings, pharaohs ruling as his sons.

The medallion gives him in polished gold as Ra-Horakhty, the falcon-headed form of the risen sun, the blazing disc burning above the falcon's brow with the cobra Wadjet coiled and hooded beside the fire, the striped royal headdress falling to a hieroglyph-inscribed collar, ankhs and Eyes of Horus braided round the rim, gold on silver over deep lapis blue.

His virtue is Justice, Ma'at herself riding at the prow of the day barque, the feather every heart is weighed against, pharaoh's included — but that same authority is stalked by Pride, the son of Ra forgetting he is only weighed like everyone else. His mood is Glory, the Mandjet under full sail at noon, the god at the height of his daily strength before the long descent into dusk begins.
faces.servant:
Afu-Ra is the Servant face — "the flesh of Ra," the god's own aged, ram-headed night form, not a rival but the same Ra after the sun has set, towed rather than rowing.

The plate keeps Ra-Horakhty's gold-and-silver relief and lapis-blue field, ankh-and-Eye border unbroken, but the scene has gone underground: Ra stands ram-horned in the night barque as it is towed through the serpent-haunted dark of the Duat, the sun-disc dimmed to a dull ember behind curved horns, the crown-cobra now coiled low and watchful while the great serpent Apophis strikes at the prow and spears drive it back.

His virtue is Humility, the same god who ruled at noon reduced to cargo in his own boat, fighting to be reborn rather than commanding the day outright — and Servility is the failure the myth warns against, the oarsman forgetting that this humbling voyage is sacred and not mere transit. His mood is Awe, the night barque's whole ordeal — and Glory is simply tomorrow's dawn, the same god surfacing at the eastern horizon exactly on schedule.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Khonsu is "the traveller" — the moon god of Thebes who crosses the night sky as the moon itself, son of Amun and Mut in the great Theban triad. His name comes from the verb to cross: he is the wanderer whose road is the dark, and because the moon was Egypt's first calendar he is also a reckoner of time, the god who counts out the months and measures the lifespans of kings.

[[The Plate]] Egypt drew him young and bound: a beardless boy with the sidelock of youth falling over one shoulder, his body wrapped close in mummiform linen, and on his head the moon whole — the full silver disk cradled inside the crescent. From the wrappings his hands come free to hold the crook and flail of kingship crossed with the was-scepter of power: a child swaddled like the dead, carrying the regalia of eternity.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial he keeps Monday, the Moon's day, riding the deep blue arm at 4h — Calm, the hour before dawn, the last and stillest watch of his nightly crossing. Blue is moonlight's own temperature, and no god earned it more literally: while Ra dies each dusk and sails the underworld, it is Khonsu who holds the visible sky, the lamp of the world between sunset and sunrise. Silver, the Moon's metal at the dial's bottom tip, is his by the same right.

[[The Lesson]] His two faces are exactly the arm's pair of Serenity and Fear. The oldest texts knew Khonsu fierce — a devourer in the Pyramid Texts' hymns — but Thebes came to know him as the great healer whose statue was sent abroad to cure a foreign princess: the night accepted becomes medicine, the night dreaded becomes terror. The Physician's arm fits no god better. And his cure stands straight across the dial at 16h: Montu, the falcon war god on Mars' orange arm, whose Courage cures the Moon's Fear — while Khonsu's Serenity, in return, is the cool hand that quells Montu's Wrath.

variants.hexa_paint: The home post: the 04h arm in deep pigment blue (#002FFF), Calm, the hour before dawn. Khonsu rides one arm below the sunrise line, on the last stretch of his crossing, and the paint reads like Egypt's own night pigment — the ground lapis the texts gave the gods for hair.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps him at 04h and sharpens the blue to #0040FF — the pure primary, Calm read as light instead of paint. It suits him best of all the arms: the moon makes no color of its own, it only carries the sun's light across the dark, and Khonsu's electric blue is exactly that — borrowed radiance, delivered nightly.
variants.octa_paint: The 'traveller' god settles onto the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the Walk of the Scholar and his Book — a seat Khonsu earns outright, since the moon was Egypt's first calendar and he its reckoner, the god who counted out months and measured the lifespans of kings. The healer who cured a foreign princess by his priests' own written rites now sits where the deep-night hours belong to study rather than fear.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel the same deep-night arm turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the Age of the Unborn, ancestors and the not-yet-born held in one hue. Khonsu, wrapped mummiform like the dead yet crowned with the crook and flail of eternity, was always a god who belonged as much to what has not happened as to what already has; the silver simply names the space he was already standing in.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, and he shares it with Osiris. Moon god and grain god on one spoke is Egypt's whole calendar in miniature — Khonsu counts the months, Osiris dies and sprouts to count the years, and the equinox dawn belongs to both: the night's traveller handing the sky to the season of returning life.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Khonsu at his 04h post but renames the watch: the blue arm (#002FFF) now stands for Hope, and he shares it with Thoth — Egypt's two moon-touched gods together, the reckoner beside the traveller. The arm guards the morning third from 0h to 8h, and no one holds better title to it: Khonsu has proven on every crossing since Egypt began that the dark ends and the dawn arrives.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Montu is the falcon-headed war god of Thebes — the raised arm of Egypt's armies. Before Amun rose to rule the city, Montu WAS Thebes: four temples ringed his ground at Karnak, Armant, Medamud and Tod, and the Middle Kingdom's warrior kings wore his name as their own — Mentuhotep, "Montu is satisfied." When Egypt wanted to say a pharaoh fought perfectly, this god's name was the whole sentence: Thutmose III was praised as a Montu on the battlefield, and Ramses II at Kadesh raged "like Montu, lord of Thebes."

[[The Plate]] On the medallion, know him by the marks every temple gave him: the falcon head, kin to Ra's — for he fused with the sun as Montu-Ra — but crowned with the solar disk that rears TWO uraeus cobras where Ra's carries one, beneath a pair of tall twin plumes, the war-crown that names him at a glance; his weapon is the khopesh, the bronze sickle-sword Egypt's soldiers actually swung. Even his sacred beast is martial: the Buchis bull of Armant, white-bodied and black-faced, kept as his living herald.

[[On the Dial]] He stands on Tuesday because he is Egypt's exact word for the red planet: dies Martis, Mars' burning hour, and Montu is the war god without rival for it. The hexa arm at 16h burns orange — Zeal, the mood of the working afternoon, the ember hours when a campaign is won by refusing to stop. That is Montu's native tempo: not the parade after victory but the march that earns it — the Soldier is this arm's profession, and Montu is the soldier's god, the pharaoh's sword-arm deified.

[[The Lesson]] Mars carries Courage and Wrath, one fire with two uses, and Egypt drew the doctrine straight into this god: Montu is force under the king's command — the khopesh raised FOR Egypt — while berserk rage the Egyptians gave to Sekhmet's rampage, not to him. His remedy stands straight across the dial at 4h, and in this theme it is a neighbor from home: Khonsu, Thebes' own moon god, healer and marker of time, whose Serenity quells Wrath — while Montu sends Courage back along the axis to cure the Moon's Fear. Two Thebans holding opposite ends of the day, the war god and the night-healer keeping each other whole.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 16h, ember orange, Zeal — Montu's campaign hour, the working afternoon when an army either pushes on or breaks; the falcon rides the day's stubborn fire.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette Montu keeps 16h but the ember flares to pure yellow — pigment's orange has no seat in the prism wheel, so the afternoon burns at light's brightest hue. For this god the flare is doctrine: he fused with the sun as Montu-Ra, and the war-arm blazing solar yellow is that fusion drawn in light.
variants.octa_paint: Montu, the falcon war god whose name alone once meant 'fought like a god,' now shares his afternoon arm with copper (#C87533) — the Walk of the Merchant, the Coin his object, a seat that reads at first like defeat for a god of the khopesh. But Thebes never separated the sword from the treasury: war paid for temples, tribute followed conquest, and every campaign Thutmose III or Ramses II fought 'like Montu' filled Egypt's granaries as surely as its armories — the Merchant's Coin was always the war god's other harvest.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the afternoon arm is Maturity, and Montu takes its amber (#FFB400) as the mid-life register of his own doctrine: not the raw Wrath of a young berserker but Courage seasoned into command, the campaign fought with judgment rather than fury. A warrior-king praised 'like Montu on the battlefield' was never praised as reckless — amber is the color of strength that has already learned restraint.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Montu takes the 18h dusk arm, red for Autumn, pointing at the autumn equinox — and shares it with Hathor, as Ares shared everything with Aphrodite. Egypt smiles at the pairing: the war god beside the lady of the west at the very hour the sun goes west — war and love on one arm, the year's long evening.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves Montu to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Hathor — each arm's tip centers its third of the day, and this pair guards the evening from 16h to 24h, with Faith holding 8h to 16h and Hope 0h to 8h. Montu finally wears his own red, and Love reads through him as protection: the garrison on the wall so that Thebes can sleep — courage keeping watch over what is loved.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Thoth is the scribe of the gods — inventor of hieroglyphs, reckoner of years and moons, clerk of the divine court. Egypt fixed his image for three thousand years: a man with the head of an ibis, the long beak curving like the crescent he wears — a full lunar disk cupped in a silver crescent above his brow — the scribe's palette in one hand and the reed pen poised in the other, with the ankh, the key of life, close by; for in Egypt to write a name was to keep its owner alive.

[[The Plate]] He belongs to Wednesday the way water belongs to the river. The day is Mercury's — Hermes' day, the messenger's day — and the Greeks who met Thoth did not compare the two gods, they merged them: Hermes Trismegistus, the thrice-greatest, is Thoth wearing Greek. Both count, both write, both guide souls across the last border. And Thoth is this dial's own patron besides: when the year ran five days short, he gambled with the Moon and won a sliver of its light to build the five extra days that made the calendar come out true — the reckoner of time literally repaired the clock.

[[On the Dial]] His arm is the bottom of the dial, 0h, midnight, in mourning purple — the mood of Sorrow. Midnight is the mind's hour, and Egypt gave Thoth exactly that shift: in the Hall of Two Truths, deep in the night of the Duat, the heart is weighed against Ma'at's feather, and it is Thoth who stands beside the scales with pen ready, recording the verdict without mercy and without malice. Sorrow rules his hour not as despair but as exactness — the hour of accounting, when the day is weighed honestly because no one is watching except the one whose job is to write it down.

[[The Lesson]] Wednesday's pair is Wisdom and Greed — the same cleverness that understands the world can start counting it instead, and even a hunger for knowing takes greed's shape when pursued without end. The cure comes down the dial's longest road: straight above at noon stands Amun, the hidden king, Jupiter's crown in Egyptian dress — his Generosity cures Greed, while Thoth's Wisdom travels back up the axis to curb Amun's Excess. The scribe and the king heal each other across the full height of the day, midnight to noon.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: the dial's very bottom at 0h, mourning purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of midnight — Thoth beside the scales in the Hall of Two Truths, keeping the night's record on Wednesday's arm.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 0h but turns the purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue no rainbow contains, invented by the eye where the spectrum's two ends meet. The inventor of writing wears the invented color: a sign standing for something that is not there, which is what every hieroglyph is.
variants.octa_paint: The ibis-headed scribe takes the late-evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of the Artist, the Mask its object — an unlikely seat for the god of exact accounting until one remembers Thoth is also Hermes Trismegistus, thrice-great and endlessly disguised across a thousand years of syncretism, one god performing as many masks as cultures needed him to wear. The reed pen that records every verdict in the Hall of Two Truths is, after all, the truest performance in the whole temple.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel the same hour deepens to dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — Old Age, the wisdom that comes only after long counting. No god fits it more naturally than the one who reckoned every year of the calendar and gambled with the Moon to win the five days that made the count come out true; Old Age here is not decline but the accumulated exactness of a god who has already written down everything that came before.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Thoth returns to the bottom arm at 0h, blue for Winter, pointed at the winter solstice — and he holds it alone, the only body that shares its arm with no one. It is the right solitude: the god who added the five days that made the year come out true stands at the year's own midnight, keeping its longest night the way he keeps every record — by himself.
variants.trio: The trio lifts Thoth off midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm (#002FFF) beside the Moon, guarding the morning third from 0h to 8h — and the pairing is an old joke between them, for it was from the Moon that Thoth won the light that lengthened the year. Hope is the theological shape of the scribe's trust in his own ledger: the record is kept at midnight precisely so that morning has something to read.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Amun is the Hidden One — his name in Egyptian means exactly that, the unseen — a god of air and breath from Thebes who rose to king of the gods, absorbed the sun itself as Amun-Ra, and ruled from Karnak, the largest temple complex the ancient world ever raised. The Greeks did not merely compare him to Zeus; they equated him: Zeus Ammon, whose desert oracle at Siwa Alexander crossed the sands to consult. Thursday is Jupiter's day and Jupiter is Zeus — so the king of Egypt's gods takes the king planet's medallion by the ancients' own identification, not ours.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion seats him wholly in gold: the enthroned king wearing his signature crown of two tall plumes — a doubled noon ray rising from the striped royal headdress — with an ankh in one crossed hand and the curved heqa crook in the other, life in one fist and rule in the other. Behind him the field is deep royal purple, and the rim weaves lotus and papyrus blossoms in gold and silver, studded with small amethysts: the king's metal framed in the king's color.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa paint dial his arm is the crown itself: the top vertex at 12h, yellow, noon — the mood of Joy, at the very tip the star points at true solar noon. 'Jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter,' and the medallion says the same in metal: its gold is noon yellow made solid, and the twin plumes point exactly where the arm points — straight up, at the day's peak.

[[The Lesson]] Thursday's virtue is Generosity and its vice is Excess, and Amun lived both. Ramesside prayers call him the vizier of the poor, the one who comes at the voice of the humble — generosity from a hidden hand; yet his priesthood at Karnak grew so rich in land and grain that it came to rival the throne itself — the royal table overflowing into an estate. The cure hangs across the dial at midnight: Mercury's Wisdom — on this Egyptian dial Thoth, the scribe who counts — curbs Amun's Excess, while Amun's open hand pours down the same axis to cure the counting god's Greed. The full hand and the counting hand, balancing each other.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm is the crown of the star: the dial's top at 12h, noon yellow, the mood of Joy — and Amun's medallion was drawn for that seat, the twin plumes of his crown pointing exactly where the top vertex points, at true solar noon. The king of the gods in gold, on the arm whose color is the day's gold.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Amun at the 12h crown but dresses the arm in green — the middle color of the spectrum, which the prism wheel seats at noon. A fitting robe for the Hidden One: like the god whose name means unseen, green stands at the exact center of the visible band — Joy read as fullness of light rather than gold leaf.
variants.octa_paint: Amun the Hidden One, king of Karnak's priesthood, takes the mid-morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of the Priest, the Bell his object — the one seat on the wheel that needed no argument at all, since Amun's own temple was the largest the ancient world raised and his clergy all but ran the state's treasury of grain and land. The vizier-of-the-poor who answers 'the voice of the humble' and the priest who rings the temple bell are, in Amun's case, simply the same open hand.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel his morning arm turns spring green (#7CE577) — Childhood, the wheel's freshest hour — and Amun, breath and air made visible, invisible and everywhere, wears it as the youngest register of his own vast patience: the temple day of giving beginning again, small and green, before Karnak's centuries of accumulated gold had piled up around him.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Amun keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with the Sun — which in Egyptian dress is simply Amun-Ra spelled out: the hidden king and the visible disc presiding together. The summer solstice is the year at its most generous — the longest light, the season the Nile began to rise and fill the land.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Amun keeps his crown: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, shared with Saturn (Osiris on this dial), and the arm centers the day's third from 8h to 16h with noon at its tip. Faith is the Hidden One's native virtue: Egypt's personal piety prayed to a god no eye could see and trusted that he comes at the voice of the poor — belief in the unseen, practiced daily.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Hathor is Egypt's great goddess of love, beauty, music and joy — her name, Hwt-Hr, means "House of Horus": she is the sky itself as a golden house for the falcon sun. Mistress of dance and of the festival of drunkenness at Dendera, mother and lover in one figure, she is also the Lady of the West, who stands at the sunset horizon to receive the dying sun — and the dead — into the evening land. The Greeks who met her knew exactly whom they were looking at and called her Aphrodite without hesitation.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion gives her in pale gold on deep turquoise — and the stone is no decorator's whim: Egypt named her Mistress of Turquoise, patroness of the Sinai mines where the sky's own blue-green was dug. The sun disc rides between tall cow horns on her brow, a cow's ears flank the human face beneath the striped headdress, and her crossed arms hold her two instruments: the arched sistrum, the rattle whose shiver called her into the temple, and the ankh, the key of life. A broad beaded collar covers her shoulders, and the rim weaves gold and silver tendrils through lotus blossoms — her flower of rebirth.

[[On the Dial]] She belongs to Friday because Friday belongs to the evening star. Venus opens over the western horizon just after sunset — precisely the sky Hathor rules as Lady of the West — so goddess and planet literally share a station: the most beautiful light in the heavens standing over the gate of the dead. On the hexa paint dial her arm is 20h, deep red, the mood of Passion — the hour when work ends and wanting begins, when the sunset bleeds and the music starts at Dendera.

[[The Lesson]] Love is her virtue and Jealousy her vice — love's shadow, not its opposite. Egypt told that shadow plainly: as the Eye of Ra she withdrew from the land in bitterness and had to be coaxed home with music and dance, and once, unleashed, only beer dyed red as blood could still her — love turned resentful until joy calls it back. The cure stands straight across the dial at 8h, on the green morning arm this theme gives to Osiris: Patience heals Jealousy, and Hathor's Love flows back across the axis to dissolve Envy. It is the dial's most Egyptian axis — the Lady of the West delivers the dead to the lord of rebirth, and love and patience have passed between those two since the first sunset.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 20h, blood red, Passion — the Lady of the West on the arm of the evening she rules, the sistrum shaking as the sun goes down into her country.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Hathor at 20h and turns her red to the prism's full voltage — pure red, light's own primary. Passion undiluted: the goddess of the drunkenness festival poured the strongest red that light itself can mix.
variants.octa_paint: Hathor, the Lady of the West who receives the dying sun into evening, now shares the sunset arm with iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of the Soldier, the Sword its object, an estate her gentle sistrum never asked for. Yet Egypt knew her fiercer face too: unleashed as the Eye of Ra she rampaged until only blood-red beer could still her, and a goddess capable of that fury wears a Soldier's crimson more honestly than her turquoise medallion first suggests.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her sunset arm turns ember (#FF6A3C) — the Elder, the harvest of a life — and no goddess sits it more fittingly: Hathor stands at the horizon precisely to gather in the dying sun and the dying alike, the evening's harvest of a whole day's light collected into her own hands before the dark takes it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Hathor holds the 18h arm, red for Autumn, sharing it with Montu — love and war on one arm, as Egypt itself told it: the lady of joy and the Eye of Ra are one goddess in two moods, and Thebes worshipped her beside its falcon of battle. The autumn equinox is the year's dusk — the harvest of what was loved, carried west.
variants.trio: Hathor does not move at all: the Trinity's red Love arm is her own 20h, and Love is already her whole estate — the one virtue standing in both the seven pairs and the theological three. Montu joins her there, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h: music on one side of the door and a raised arm on the other, passion and courage keeping the dark third of the day.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Before he was lord of the dead, Osiris was the king who taught Egypt to grow grain — the myth remembers him as the first civilizer, murdered and cut into pieces by his brother Set, who envied the throne. Isis gathered him, love reassembling what envy had scattered, and the risen god took the throne of the underworld instead: judge of hearts, first of the westerners, the one every Egyptian hoped to become at death — the funerary texts promote each soul into 'an Osiris'. His death and return were read into everything that cycles: the Nile flood, the buried seed, the grain that stands again.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion holds him in bronze against a field of glowing malachite green: mummiform, wrapped in banded linen, wearing the tall atef crown — the pale cone of Upper Egypt between two ribbed ostrich plumes — with the crook and flail crossed at his chest, the crook striped in lapis blue and gold, and an ankh knotted at his belt: the key of life worn by the lord of the dead. The rim answers him in kind, wheat sheaves braided with lotus and ankhs around the whole border — a harvest woven into metal.

[[On the Dial]] He rules Saturday because Osiris is Saturn's patience made a god. Cronus carries the harvest sickle; Osiris owned the harvest first, and the flail on his chest is a threshing tool as much as a scepter. Saturn takes nearly thirty years to close one circle, and no figure in any pantheon out-waits Osiris — he waits out death itself and comes back green. On the day of rest, whose planet in Hebrew is Shabtai after the Sabbath, Egypt's god rests in the earth the way seed does: temporarily.

[[The Lesson]] The arm gives him green at 8h, the morning, the mood of Renewal — and Egypt agreed millennia in advance, painting Osiris's skin green as new growth, exactly the field his medallion glows with. Patience is the arm's virtue: the seed's whole art is waiting in the dark. Envy is its vice, and Osiris knows it from the receiving end — Set's envy is the engine of the entire myth. The cure stands across the dial on the red evening arm: Venus' Love — Hathor's, in this theme — dissolves Envy, just as Isis's love regathered the dismembered king; and Saturn's Patience travels back to heal Venus' Jealousy — the waiting art and the loving art mending each other.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 8h, morning green (#007E00), Renewal — Saturday, the week's rest and restart. Paint green is Osiris's own pigment: tomb painters gave him skin this color to mean rebirth, and the medallion's malachite field simply widens it to fill the arm.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps him at 8h but turns the green to cyan (#00DDDD) — morning read as water-light rather than field. For Osiris that is the Nile's color: the flood that buried the fields each year and left them fertile was called his coming.
variants.octa_paint: Osiris, the king who first taught Egypt to grow grain, takes the dawn arm in field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of the Farmer, the Plough its object — the one seat on this whole wheel that asks no translation whatsoever. The crook and flail crossed on his mummiform chest were farming tools before they were royal regalia, and a god whose green skin already meant new growth simply steps into the estate he founded.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and Osiris wears its predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the hour Ra's barque leaves the underworld, and the exact threshold Osiris himself crosses every time a soul is 'made an Osiris' at death and promised rebirth. No god on the dial has out-waited death more thoroughly; Birth here is simply his oldest and most literal specialty, dressed in the color of the hour just before the light returns.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Osiris holds the 6h arm, green for Spring, pointed at the vernal equinox and shared with the Moon — Khonsu in this theme. Sowing season is his festival in the most literal sense: Egyptians planted 'Osiris beds', seed grain in god-shaped molds, and watched the deity himself sprout.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes him from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow (#F8E600), shared with Jupiter — Amun here — guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads through Osiris as the oldest resurrection promise there is: every tomb in Egypt was built on trust that what goes into the earth comes back — the harvest creed spoken toward the sky.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Dažbog is the giving sun — his very name binds dati, to give, to bog, god: the god who gives. He is the son of Svarog the sky-smith, and he rides the daylight across the heavens in a chariot, dealing out warmth and grain and fortune to whoever stands beneath him. The Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the Slavs themselves Dažbog's grandchildren, vnutsi Dazhbozhi — a whole people descended from the sun, warmed by an ancestor who asks nothing and pours out everything.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion paints him as a radiant youth of silver skin crowned with a golden winged helm, an eagle spreading its wings above his brow, and behind his head a corona of long golden rays like a second sun. His cloak is beaten gold and stamped with sun-discs; in the crook of one arm he cradles a horn of plenty spilling silver coins and ripe wheat, and in the other hand he lifts a scepter capped with a blazing sun-star. Flames of amber and gold fill the field behind him, and the border prowls with wolves and glowing runes.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Sunday — nedelja in the Slavic tongue, the 'no-work' day, the day the week rests in the light before it begins again. So Dažbog does not take an arm at all: he holds the CENTER of the dial, white-gold, the still hub from which the six colored arms radiate. He is the source the whole spectrum decomposes from — white light that only looks empty because it already contains every color the other gods will wear.

[[The Lesson]] Dažbog gives all his light away and keeps none for himself — that endless open-handed giving is the deepest Humility, and its shadow is the Pride that would hoard the blaze and call the warmth its own, the one axis whose cure sits in the center it came from. Yet the center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, and the GIVING god's very name splits the same way: he is king and servant at once, the ancestor-lord of the Slavs who nevertheless hands out the day like bread, every morning, to everyone. That even-handed giving is his Justice; giving reduced to bare obligation would be his Servility. His Glory is the high summer he pours from the horn — and his Awe the winter dimming, when the grandfather of the people walks his road old and gray, trusting the spring to crown him again.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette the center is the one color no brush can reach: pigments mix by subtraction, and no blending of pigments ever yields white. So Dažbog's white-gold hub is the unmixable center — the source every colored arm is a piece of, yet none of them, mixed, can climb back up to. He can give the colors out; they can never give him back.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the logic reverses: the six colored beams of the arms, summed at the hub, add up to exactly white. Here Dažbog's white-gold center is not a paint that cannot be mixed but a radiance that is the sum of all six — the giving sun proven by arithmetic, every arm's color returning to make the light whole again.
variants.octa_paint: Dažbog, the giving sun whose name means simply 'the god who gives,' steps from the dial's center onto the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of the King, the Crown his object — a seat this ancestor-lord already half-occupies, since The Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the Slavs his own grandchildren, warmed and ruled by one radiant ancestor. The horn of plenty in his medallion hand was always a king's largesse before it was a Compass arm's harvest.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, and Dažbog wears its sun-yellow (#FFE800) as the blazing register of his own myth: the light he pours out endlessly, high summer poured from the horn, strength before any descent. A god who rides the daylight chariot at full strength each day was already living at the zenith the Ages wheel now simply names.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps a true crossing — four arms meeting at one point — and Dažbog keeps that meeting-place, the hub where the year's four quarters intersect. The giving sun holds the still center around which spring, summer, autumn and winter wheel, the fixed white-gold heart of the turning year.
variants.trio: In the trio — Faith, Hope, Love — the sun keeps the center, and from it the yellow Faith arm rises straight up the 12h vertical (Faith at top, Hope at 4h, Love at 20h). Dažbog is the white-gold source, and Faith is the first thing that climbs out of him toward noon: the giving light made into the upward-pointing virtue.

faces.ruler:
Young Dažbog is the Ruler face — the giving sun, his very name binding dati, to give, to bog, god, son of Svarog the sky-smith, ancestor of a people the old chronicles call his own grandchildren.

The medallion paints him a radiant youth of silver skin under a golden winged helm, an eagle spreading its wings above his brow, a corona of long golden rays like a second sun behind his head, a horn of plenty cradled in one arm spilling silver coins and ripe wheat, a scepter capped with a blazing sun-star lifted in the other, flames of amber and gold filling the field.

His virtue is Justice, the day handed out like bread to everyone beneath him without exception, king and peasant warmed by the same beam — but that even-handedness is stalked by Pride, the temptation to believe the horn empties because he chooses to be generous rather than because giving is simply what a sun does. His mood is Glory, high summer poured freely from the horn, the corona at its widest and the wheat at its ripest.
faces.servant:
Old Dažbog is the Servant face — not a second god but the same giving sun grown gray, the ancestor of the Slavs walking his road in winter instead of riding it in high summer.

The plate keeps the silver-and-bronze relief and wolf-and-rune knotwork of the young god's border, but the field has turned cold winter-gray: a bent, silver-bearded elder wrapped in a thin gray cloak stamped with faded sun-discs, his winged helm dulled, walking a snow-dusted road with a near-empty horn of plenty at his side and his scepter reduced to a plain traveler's staff, a pale banked corona of cold silver-gold light behind him where the young god's blaze once stood.

His virtue is Humility, the giving god admitting the horn runs low and trusting the spring to refill it rather than pretending the bounty is endless — and Servility is what the winter reduces him to if that trust curdles into mere plodding, the ancestor walking only because the road demands it. His mood is Awe, the winter dimming the whole people wait out — and Glory is the same grandfather crowned again every spring, young once more.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Hors is the light of the night — the cold luminary that rules the sky when the sun is gone. His name may carry the old Iranian root for glow or sun-disc, and he moves as a wheel of pale fire across the dark. The Tale of Igor's Campaign remembers him in one unforgettable line: prince Vseslav the sorcerer, running as a wolf through the night, 'crossed the path of great Hors' before the cockcrow — a werewolf racing the moon's own track and cutting across it in the last dark before dawn.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion shows a youth of silver-blue skin crowned with a horned crescent that cradles a full moon between its tips, the disc glowing with soft light above his brow. His cloak is silvered and stamped with a running row of moon-phases, from new to full and back again; in his hand he lifts a staff capped with a crescent. Behind him is deep starry night, and the whole border is crowded with crescents, silver stars and runes, one full moon riding at the top.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Monday — ponedeljak, the day 'after nedelja,' the first step out of the day of rest and still half in the dark. Hors takes the 4h arm, low on the left of the dial, in deep blue — the hour just before dawn, when the night's light is thinnest and the world is most still. His mood is Calm: the held breath of the last dark hour before the sun returns.

[[The Lesson]] His axis is Serenity against Fear. The dark is where fear breeds — every shape a threat, every sound a wolf — and Hors answers it with the Serenity of a steady night-light that does not flinch. His cure lies across the dial in the mars arm: the Courage of Svetovid is what dissolves the Fear that the moonlit dark invents. Calm holds the night; courage ends it.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Hors holds the 4h arm in a deep blue (#002FFF) — the pigment of the pre-dawn hour, night thinned but not yet broken. It is the mixing-blue of the darkest part of the wheel, the color the world wears in the last cold hour before Dažbog climbs back over the rim.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the same 4h arm burns as pure primary blue (#0040FF) — one of the three beams the white hub decomposes into. Hors is no longer a mixed pigment but a fundamental ray: the blue that light itself is built from, the night's own frequency standing clean in the prism wheel.
variants.octa_paint: Hors, the cold luminary who crosses the night as a wheel of pale fire, settles onto the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of the Scholar, the Book his object. The Tale of Igor's Campaign already reads him as a measured track that even a sorcerer-prince must 'cross,' a fixed and countable path across the dark — the Scholar's arm simply gives that path its proper shelf and pen.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel his arm turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the Unborn, ancestors and not-yet-born together — a fitting hue for a wheel of pale fire whose phases wax from nothing and wane back to nothing every month, the same nightly disappearance and return that Hors performs while Vseslav the werewolf races across his track before the cockcrow.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Hors to the 6h arm, colored spring green and aimed straight at the vernal equinox — and here he shares the arm with Morana, the saturn body. The night-light and the winter-death stand on the same spring arm: for the dawn to come and the year to turn, the long dark and the frost must both give way at once.
variants.trio: In the trio Hors keeps the 4h arm, but it is now the Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF) — and he shares it with Veles, the mercury body. The last dark hour becomes the hour of Hope: the moon-light that outlasts the night is the promise that the sun will rise, blue leaning toward the coming gold.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Svetovid was the great war-god of the western Slavs, worshipped at the temple of Arkona on the island of Rügen until the Danes threw it down in 1168. His idol had FOUR faces, one gazing to each of the four directions, so that nothing on any horizon escaped him. In his hand he held a horn of mead that the priests refilled every year and read as an omen: a full horn promised a fat harvest, a low one warned of famine. And in the temple he kept a sacred WHITE HORSE that only the priest could ride — before any campaign they led it over crossed spears, and the foot it stepped with first told the army whether the god blessed the war.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion gives him whole: the four-faced god of Arkona under one crested helm — the carved faces watching their quarters like the Zbruch idol — in a coat of scale mail, the mead horn raised in one hand (the horn his priests read next year's harvest from), a bow in the other, the great sword at his belt and the spear beside him carrying the sacred WHITE HORSE on its banner. Two mailed guardsmen kneel at the pedestal, and the border rings him with horse heads among oak leaves — the stud of Arkona circling its god.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Tuesday — utorak, simply 'the second,' the second day of labor. Svetovid takes the 16h arm on the lower right of the dial, burning ember orange, and his mood is Zeal — the hard driving heat of the working afternoon, the hour when the day's fight is fully joined and the arms swing without pause.

[[The Lesson]] His axis is Courage against Wrath. The war-god is Courage itself — the nerve to lead the white horse over the spears and march — but his shadow is Wrath, the battle-heat that stops being brave and starts being blind. His cure waits across the dial in the moon arm: the Serenity of Hors is what quells the Wrath, the cool night-calm laid over the ember of the fight.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Svetovid holds the 16h arm in ember orange — a mixed pigment, red warmed with yellow, the exact color of iron pulled glowing from the forge. It is the afternoon at its hottest, the war-heat given a hue you can only reach by blending, a fighting color mixed from blood and fire.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette orange has no seat — the prism wheel knows no orange among its beams — so Svetovid's arm resolves to pure yellow instead. The war-god, denied his ember, stands in the raw yellow of open daylight: the Zeal is the same, but the color is stripped back to a ray the prism actually contains.
variants.octa_paint: Svetovid, the four-faced war-god of Arkona, shares his working-afternoon arm with copper (#C87533) — the Walk of the Merchant, the Coin its object — a strange seat for a god who led the sacred white horse to war, until his own temple's practice is remembered: the mead-horn his priests read each year was itself an economic omen, a full horn promising harvest, a low one warning of scarcity. The war-god who blessed campaigns also kept, quite literally, the treasury's forecast in his own hand.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel his arm turns amber (#FFB400) — Maturity, strength seasoned rather than raw — and Svetovid wears it as the four-faced god who watches every horizon at once, a readiness that has outgrown any single reckless charge. The white horse only stepped over the crossed spears once the priests had judged the year mature enough to risk it.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Svetovid to the 18h dusk arm, colored red and aimed at the autumn equinox — and he shares it with Mokoš, the venus body. The war-god and the earth-mother stand together on the arm of harvest's end: autumn is the season armies march and the season the fields are cut, dusk-red for both the blade and the sickle.
variants.trio: In the trio Svetovid moves to the 20h Love arm, colored red, standing beside Mokoš and guarding the evening third of the dial from 16h to 24h. The war-god posted on the Love arm is the guardian's paradox — the sword that stands watch so the evening can be loved in peace, Courage set to keep the hours it does not spend fighting.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Veles — Volos in the old treaties — is the horned god of everything that lives low and moves in the dark: cattle and wealth, trade and cunning, music and magic, and the underworld of the dead. He is the 'cattle-god,' skotiy bog, and when the Rus' princes swore peace with Byzantium they swore it 'by Perun and by Volos' — the thunderer for their weapons, the cattle-god for their goods. He is the serpent coiled at the roots of the world-tree while Perun holds its crown, and he is the patron of poets: the Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the great bard Boyan 'Veles's grandson.'

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion seats him as a horned, long-bearded god on a throne of living tree-roots, wrapped in a shaggy beast-pelt. In one hand he holds a shepherd's crook; in the other he cradles a lyre, for he is the god of song as well as of herds. Cattle and sheep graze around his knees, and a great serpent coils through the roots at his feet — the snake at the bottom of the world. The whole field glows underworld teal-green, and the border writhes with knotted serpents and horned animal skulls.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Wednesday — sreda, 'the middle,' the hinge of the week. Veles takes the very BOTTOM of the dial, the 0h/24h arm at midnight, in mourning purple — the day's grave, the deepest dark, the mystery under everything. His mood is Sorrow. And his position is the myth itself: Veles at the bottom and Perun at the crown of the star is the Perun–Veles axis made literal — the serpent at the roots, the thunderer at the top, the two poles of the vertical the whole dial hangs from.

[[The Lesson]] His axis is Wisdom against Greed. The underworld-god is Wisdom — the deep, buried knowing of the dead, the cunning of trade, the poet's craft — but the same appetite that gathers cattle and coin curdles into Greed, the hoard kept for its own sake. His cure is across the dial: the Generosity of the jupiter arm, the open hand of the thunder-god's rain, is what dissolves the serpent's Greed. Wisdom knows what to keep; generosity knows what to give away.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Veles holds the 0h arm in mourning purple (#542E85) — a mixed, bruised color, red and blue ground together into the hue of grief and midnight. It is the pigment of the day's grave, the deepest, most blended dark on the wheel, the color of the underworld the cattle-god keeps.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette his purple resolves to magenta (#BD00BD) — the strange color the prism itself cannot make, that only appears where the spectrum's two ends are bent around to touch. Fitting for the god of the boundary between living and dead: Veles wears the one hue that lives outside the rainbow, at the bottom where the ends meet.
variants.octa_paint: Veles, the horned cattle-god and patron of poets — Boyan the bard was called 'Veles's grandson' — takes the late-evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the Walk of the Artist and its Mask, a seat his own lyre had already half-earned. The serpent coiled at the world-tree's root and the singer coiled around a verse are, in Veles, one and the same cunning performance.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the arm deepens to dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — Old Age — and Veles, god of the underworld and of the dead's own deep knowing, wears it as the accumulated wisdom of the buried: not decline but the cunning of trade and song that only comes from having watched every generation of cattle, coin, and poet pass beneath his roots.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Veles on the 0h arm, aimed at the winter solstice and colored winter blue — and alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; the cattle-god shares none. The god of the longest night holds the year's longest night by himself, the serpent at the bottom of the year as well as of the dial, unpartnered in the deepest dark.
variants.trio: In the trio Veles climbs to the 4h Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF), and shares it with Hors, the moon body. The underworld-god rises out of the grave to stand on the arm of Hope beside the night-light — the buried Wisdom leaning toward dawn, the serpent lifting its head at the promise of morning.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Perun is the thunder-god, the axe-bearer, the chief of the old Slavic sky. His tree is the oak, his weapon the axe and the hurled thunderstone, and his sign is the six-petalled thunder-mark — the gromovnik rosette that Slavs carved into their roof-beams to turn the lightning away, a six-armed star the clock's own hexagram pointer quietly echoes. His great myth is the storm itself: the serpent Veles steals the cattle and dams up the waters, and Perun hunts him across the sky and strikes him with the bolt — and the RAIN that bursts loose is the sky's generosity, the withheld water finally given back to the earth.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion shows him as a broad, bearded warrior in ornate armor and a winged helm, hurling a bolt of brilliant blue lightning from his raised right hand while a great battle-axe hangs ready in his left. Storm-clouds boil around him, mountains stand below, and forked lightning splits the sky. The border is woven from oak leaves and acorns — his sacred tree — set with lightning-bolts and runes, and a bull's head glowers from his belt.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Thursday — četvrtak, 'the fourth' — and on the dial he takes the TOP, the 12h arm, the crown of the star, in noon yellow. His mood is Joy: the high, ringing gladness of the sky at its brightest, the thunder-god at the zenith. That the six-armed hexagram crowns him at the top is no accident — the pointer is his own thunder-mark, and he sits at the head of it.

[[The Lesson]] His axis is Generosity against Excess. The rain he releases is pure Generosity — the sky opening its hand and pouring the hoarded water down onto the fields — but the same storm can drown what it means to feed, and that is Excess, the flood that gives too hard. His cure is across the dial in the mercury arm: the Wisdom of Veles, the serpent he strikes, is exactly what curbs the Excess. The old enemies balance each other — the thunderer's giving needs the serpent's knowing-when-to-stop.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Perun crowns the 12h arm in noon yellow — the pure high pigment of midday, the brightest color the wheel puts at the top. The thunder-mark's crown-petal glows the yellow of the sky at noon, Joy given the boldest hue on the dial, straight up where the light is highest.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the top arm resolves to green — because green sits at the exact middle of the visible spectrum, and the middle of the spectrum belongs at the middle of the crown. Perun's noon becomes green here: the thunder-god wearing the balance-point of all light, the center wavelength raised to the zenith.
variants.octa_paint: Perun, the thunder-god whose six-petalled gromovnik already echoes the clock's own hexagram, takes the mid-morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of the Priest, the Bell his object — and the fit runs deeper than costume: the thunder-mark was carved into roof-beams as a ward, a household rite as devout as any bell rung in a nave. The axe that strikes Veles free from his dam is, seen from the Priest's arm, simply the storm performing its own liturgy.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel his arm turns spring green (#7CE577) — Childhood — and Perun wears it as the freshest register of the rain he releases: the fields fed newly each spring, the storm's Generosity read as growth just beginning rather than the flood of Excess it can also become.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Perun at the 12h arm, colored yellow and aimed at the summer solstice — and he shares it with Dažbog, the sun body. The thunder-god and the giving sun stand together at the top of the year: high summer is the season of both the ripening light and the ripening storm, the sun and the rain crowning the dial as one.
variants.trio: In the trio Perun keeps the crown at 12h, now the Faith arm in Faith yellow, shared with Morana, the saturn body — and he guards the day third of the dial from 8h to 16h. The thunder-god at the top of the vertical holds the arm of Faith over the bright working hours, the storm-crown turned into the keystone of the day.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Mokoš is the great goddess — the only goddess Prince Vladimir set among the idols of his Kyiv pantheon, standing alone among the gods. She is the spinner: the flax and the wool, the distaff and the thread, and by extension the thread of fate itself, measured out and cut. And she is Moist Mother Earth, Mati Syra Zemlya, the damp fertile ground her very name seems to hold — mok-, the wet. When Christianity came she did not die but changed her name to St Paraskeva-Pyatnitsa, 'Friday,' venerated on the day that was already hers, where it stayed forbidden to spin.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion seats her as a veiled woman in a copper-bronze robe, a shawl over her long hair, haloed in a soft earth-green glow. In her raised hand she turns a distaff wound with pale flax; across her lap lies a curved harvest-sickle; and to either side of her stand full sheaves of ripe wheat. A serpent coils at her feet along the ground, and the whole field is the deep brown of turned earth — the goddess grown straight out of the soil she is.

[[On the Dial]] Her day is Friday — petak in the Slavic count, 'the fifth,' but her true name for it is Pyatnitsa, the day of St Paraskeva, when no woman spun for fear of her. On the dial she takes the 20h arm, deep in the evening, in blood red, and her mood is Passion — the warm red heat of the evening hours, the fire of love and of the hearth after the day's work is done.

[[The Lesson]] Her axis is Love against Jealousy. Mokoš is Love itself — the fertile, binding, life-spinning love of the earth-mother — but its shadow is Jealousy, the love that clenches into possession and fear of loss. Her cure is across the dial in the saturn arm: the Patience of Morana, the long slow patience of the ground that waits through winter, is what heals the Jealousy. Love that can wait does not need to grasp.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Mokoš holds the 20h arm in blood red — the deep mixed crimson of the evening, of the hearth-fire and the ripe fruit and the blood of birth. It is the warmest pigment on the lower dial, the color of Passion given body, the evening turned the red of love.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette her arm resolves to pure red — one of the three primary beams the white hub breaks into. Mokoš's evening becomes a fundamental ray: not the mixed crimson of the pigment but the clean elemental red that light itself is built from, Passion stripped to its root frequency.
variants.octa_paint: Mokoš, the spinner of fate and Moist Mother Earth — the only goddess Vladimir set among his idols — shares her evening arm with iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the Walk of the Soldier, the Sword its object. It reads against her gentle distaff until the ground itself is remembered as the guarded thing: earth is what every army has ever marched to keep, and the goddess who is the soil holds the Sword the way a mother holds the door.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm turns ember (#FF6A3C) — the Elder, the harvest of a life — and Mokoš wears it plainly: she is Moist Mother Earth, and no figure on the dial is more literally the harvest itself, the sheaves at her side already the gathered work of a whole season's growing.
variants.cross: The seasons cross sets Mokoš on the 18h dusk arm, colored red and aimed at the autumn equinox — shared with Svetovid, the mars body. The earth-mother and the war-god stand together on the harvest arm: autumn is when the wheat she spins beside is cut and when the armies march, the sickle and the sword sharing one red dusk.
variants.trio: In the trio Mokoš keeps the 20h arm — and here the red Love arm is truly hers, the virtue that is her whole nature made into a direction. She shares it with Svetovid, the mars body: the earth-mother's Love and the war-god's guard on the same evening arm, the thing worth loving and the sword that keeps it.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Morana is the goddess of winter and of death — Marzanna, Mara, Morena, her name rooted in the old word for death itself. She is the cold that kills the year, and every spring the Slavs kill her back: they build her as a straw effigy, dress her, carry her out of the village in procession, and drown or burn her at the vernal equinox to end the winter and let the spring in. The custom is still alive across the Slavic lands. She is death not as an end but as a hinge — the dying the world has to pass through before it can be born again.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion paints her as a gaunt, veiled figure on a winter throne, her death's face half-hidden, her tattered gray robes hung with icicles and rimed with frost. In one hand she lifts a staff crowned with a crescent moon, a small bound doll hanging from it; in the other she holds a poppy seed-pod, the sleep that shades into death. Bare dead trees and snow fill the field, a serpent lies coiled in the frost at her feet, and small straw effigies stand at her side — the very poppets that will be drowned for her. The border bristles with thistles, thorns and skulls.

[[On the Dial]] Her day is Saturday — subota, the old Sabbath, the week's edge and its rest. Yet on the dial she takes the 8h arm in morning green, and her mood is Renewal — which for the death-goddess is the whole point. She sits at the hour of the week's rest and restart, the green of new morning, because the one who ends the year is also the one who clears the ground for it to begin again.

[[The Lesson]] Her axis is Patience against Envy. Morana is Patience — the deep, frozen, waiting patience of winter and of the buried dead, the long stillness that outlasts everything — and her shadow is Envy, the cold resentment of the living and the warm. Her cure is across the dial in the venus arm: the Love of Mokoš, the warm earth-mother, is what dissolves the Envy. Winter's patience and spring's love are the same hinge seen from its two sides.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Morana holds the 8h arm in morning green (#007E00) — a mixed pigment, blue cooled into yellow, the green of new grass under the last frost. It is the strangest color on her: the death-goddess given the hue of first growth, Renewal painted in the green of the morning her winter finally releases.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette her arm resolves to cyan (#00DDDD) — the cold bright blue-green of ice and thaw-water. Morana's morning turns to cyan here, the exact color of meltwater and winter light, the death-goddess wearing the hue of the very thaw that drowns her effigy.
variants.octa_paint: Morana, the winter goddess whose straw effigy is drowned each spring to end the cold, takes the dawn arm in field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of the Farmer, the Plough its object — the one seat that needs no translation. She is death as a hinge, not an end, and the Slavs who burn her at the equinox are performing exactly what a farmer performs each fallow season: clearing the old growth so the plough can turn new ground.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel her arm turns predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — Birth — and no goddess on the dial earns the paradox more honestly: Morana is winter and death made flesh, yet her whole ritual exists to be ended, drowned each spring so the year can be born again. Birth is simply her death read forward instead of back.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Morana to the 6h arm, colored spring green and aimed straight at the vernal equinox — shared with Hors, the moon body. For her this is the most literal arm on the dial: the vernal equinox is the exact day her straw effigy is drowned. The death-goddess pointed at the very moment of her own drowning, standing on the hinge where winter dies into spring.
variants.trio: In the trio Morana is promoted all the way to the 12h crown, the Faith arm in Faith yellow (#F8E600), shared with Perun, the jupiter body. The death-goddess raised to the top of the star holds the arm of Faith beside the thunder-god — because the deepest Faith is the one that trusts the dying: that winter drowned is spring promised, the effigy in the river the year reborn.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Gold is the sun brought to earth: the one metal that never rusts, never tarnishes, never dulls — incorruptible, and therefore, to the alchemists, perfect. It is the goal of the whole Great Work, the magnum opus that climbs from black nigredo through white albedo and yellow citrinitas to red rubedo, and the sign for gold and the sign for the Sun are the same circle with a central point. Soft enough to beat into leaf a few atoms thick, dense enough to sink in any river, gold was money, crown, and reliquary at once — "as above, so below," the sun's own light made solid.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion casts the shared still life in pure gold: stacked ingots, a scatter of raw nuggets, and a coil of drawn wire, all resting inside a blazing solar corona that flares out behind them. The rim is a ring of sun rosettes — radiant starbursts and little sun-faces set in medallion circles — the emblem of the Sun repeated all the way round. It is the same wealth every one of the seven plates displays; here it is gold, the state the other six metals are always trying to become.

[[On the Dial]] Sunday is the Sun's day in plain sight — Sunday, Sonntag, dies Solis — so gold takes the day and the very center of the dial. On the hexagram, the cross, and the trio it sits at the hub, the white-gold source from which all six colors of the star decompose, the way a prism unfolds one white beam into a spectrum. Only on the octa does it leave home, stepping up to the topmost 12h arm to blaze as noon itself.

[[The Lesson]] Gold sits at the heart of everything yet asks for nothing — Pride is the false gold, all glare and no warmth, while Humility is the true, the white light that gives itself to every color and keeps none, answering Pride not by dimming but by shining evenly on all. Yet the center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, a doubleness assayers know by trade: gold is the standard every metal is measured against, which is Justice with scales in its hand, and at the same time the most obedient servant in the shop — coined, alloyed, beaten to leaf a breath thick. Servility is gold spent as mere money; Pride is gold worshiped as more than metal. Its Glory is the incorruptible shine no age can tarnish — and its Awe the same metal buried nameless in the ore, waiting for the Work to call it out.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, gold holds the unmixable center. No two paints ever blend into white — white is where paint runs out — so the goldsmith does not mix it at all: he lays it on as leaf, beaten to gossamer and pressed whole onto the surface. The six colored arms radiate from a hub that can only be gilded, never ground. It is Humility as a technique — the one color you receive rather than make.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the trick reverses. Here white is not the absence of color but the sum of all of it: the six colored beams run inward and meet at the hub, and where they overlap they add to pure white-gold light. The Sun is what the whole star adds up to. Gold, in light, is Humility rewarded — every arm gives its hue, and their gift, combined, is the source shining back.
variants.octa_paint: Gold, the metal that never tarnishes, takes the noon arm and needs no new coat of paint at all — the wheel's royal gold (#F0C420) for the Walk of the King and his Crown IS gold's own color, the Great Work's own goal made literal. No metal on the dial sits its Walk more exactly: the incorruptible metal was already money, crown, and reliquary before any wheel assigned it an estate.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, and gold wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) as the blazing target of the whole magnum opus — the rubedo's promised endpoint, matter perfected into the one substance time cannot age. Youth suits it exactly: gold is the metal alchemy calls incorruptible, unaging by definition.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette, four arms for the two solstices and two equinoxes — and gold keeps the crossing at their heart, the still point where all four seasons meet. The year turns around it: summer up, winter down, the equinoxes left and right, and the Sun at the axis that never moves. It is Humility as geometry — the center that lets everything else revolve, and takes no season for its own.
variants.trio: The trio is Faith, Hope and Love — three arms from a single root — and gold keeps the center where that root is anchored. From it the yellow Faith arm rises straight up the 12h line, with Hope at 4h and Love at 20h fanning below. The Sun is the source all three virtues draw from; Faith is simply gold pointing at noon. Humility holds the hub so that Faith, Hope and Love can each reach outward.

faces.ruler:
Gold is the Ruler face — the sun brought to earth, the one metal that never rusts, tarnishes or dulls, the incorruptible goal the whole Great Work climbs toward from black nigredo to red rubedo.

The medallion casts it as a still life in pure gold — stacked ingots, a scatter of raw nuggets, a coil of drawn wire, all resting inside a blazing solar corona flaring out behind them, a ring of sun rosettes, radiant starbursts and little sun-faces, running the border.

Its virtue is Justice, the standard every other metal is measured against, scales in hand, showing no favor to lead or to silver — but that same standing is stalked by Pride, gold worshiped as more than metal, glare mistaken for warmth. Its mood is Glory, the incorruptible shine no age can tarnish, the corona at full blaze around ingots that will still look this way in a thousand years.
faces.servant:
Raw Ore is the Servant face — not a different metal but the same gold before the Work has called it out, still buried, still nameless, waiting in the rock rather than resting on a shelf.

The plate keeps gold's polished border, the same ring of sun rosettes, but the center has gone dark and unpolished: a jagged vein of dull, unrefined gold ore embedded in a broken chunk of dark quartz stone, matte and unglinting, resting in shadow with only the faintest corona of gold light bleeding from within the rock, as if the blazing corona of the finished metal were trapped and waiting under the surface.
