Its virtue is Humility, gold content simply to be gold without yet being praised for it, asking nothing while it waits in the dark — and Servility is what the ore becomes if it is spent as mere rock, never assayed, never called forward into the Work at all. Its mood is Awe, the corona buried and unseen — and Glory is exactly what the Work promises: the same vein, one day refined, shining evenly on everyone who looks at it.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Silver is the moon's metal, the white counterpart to gold's yellow, and the alchemists gave it the albedo — the whitening, the second stage of the Work where the blackened prima materia is washed pure. Its sign is a crescent. Silver is the brightest reflector known, which is why every good mirror and every telescope's coating is silver; it throws back the light it is given rather than making its own, exactly as the Moon returns the Sun's. And silver tarnishes — it blackens in the air — so the metal itself keeps the moon's own habit of waxing bright and waning dark.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion renders the shared hoard in cool silver: the stacked bars, the raw nuggets, the coil of wire, lit by a soft lunar glow instead of a solar blaze. Around the rim runs a full sequence of crescent moons and phases — waxing and waning sickles set between star motifs — the Moon's emblem circling the plate the way it circles the month. Same still life, second metal: the wealth of gold shown again in its silver reflection.

[[On the Dial]] Monday is the Moon's day and says so — Monday, Montag, lundi, lunes — so silver holds it. On the hexagram the Moon takes the 4h arm, deep blue, the last cold hour before dawn when the sky is darkest and the mood is Calm. The blue is the moon's night: not the fear of the dark but the quiet inside it. Of all the arms this is the one that waits — the hour that knows morning is coming and does not hurry it.

[[The Lesson]] Silver's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear, the two faces of the dark. The same night that steadies one mind terrifies another; the mirror shows you whatever you bring to it. Fear is cured from across the dial by Courage on the Mars arm — the iron nerve that meets the ember afternoon — while Serenity is Fear already answered, the still blue that has made its peace with the hour before dawn. Silver keeps Calm by reflecting, never by resisting.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, silver keeps the 4h arm in deep blue (#002FFF) — the darkest ground of the wheel, the hour before dawn mixed as a heavy night-blue. Paint reaches its coldest here; there is no bluer blue on the plate. It is Serenity ground into color: the calm of the last dark hour, laid thick where the star runs closest to midnight.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 4h arm burns as pure primary blue (#0040FF) — one of the three lights the whole spectrum is built from. In pigment blue is a mood; in light it is a foundation. The Moon becomes elemental here, a clean cold beam that the other hues need before they can make anything. Serenity, in light, is not dimness but a primary — the calm the rest of the star is composed from.
variants.octa_paint: Silver, the moon's own metal and the alchemists' albedo, takes the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of the Scholar, the Book his object — a fitting turn for the metal of every mirror and telescope lens, the reflective surface through which knowledge is literally gathered. What silver returns of borrowed light, the Scholar returns as recorded understanding.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel the same arm turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the Unborn — and here the metal and the hue nearly merge into one: silver was already the whitening stage of the Work, the washed-pure state the black prima materia passes through before it can become anything else, matter suspended exactly between what it was and what it is not yet.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons, and it moves the Moon to the 6h arm — the spring equinox, colored spring green — and gives her a partner: she shares the arm with Lead, the Saturn metal. Silver and lead at the vernal balance, the brightest metal beside the dullest, the moon of the albedo standing where the year turns from dark to light. Serenity here is the calm of equinox, day and night in perfect equal measure.
variants.trio: On the trio the Moon holds her 4h arm, but it is now the Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF) — and she shares it with Quicksilver, the Mercury metal. Silver and quicksilver together on the arm of Hope: the two lunar-bright metals, one solid and one liquid, pointing the same blue at the coming dawn. Serenity and Hope are the same gesture here — the calm that waits is also the calm that expects.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Iron is the soldier's metal and the planet Mars made solid — the blade, the ploughshare, the nail, and the armor. It is the most useful metal humankind ever tamed: the Iron Age is named for it because working it changed the world. And Mars runs, quite literally, in our blood — the iron at the heart of every hemoglobin molecule is what binds oxygen and turns blood red, so the war-god's metal is the one we each carry, breath by breath, in our veins. Its sign is the shield-and-spear ♂, the same glyph the alchemists used for the metal and the astronomers for the red planet.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion beats the shared still life out of dark iron, and it is the only plate that arms itself: among the stacked bars, the raw ore and the coil of wire lie a long straight sword and a battered war-helmet, lit from behind by a smoldering red forge-glow. The rim is a soldier's rack — shields, crossed spears, swords, a helm, and the ♂ of Mars set at the crown. Same hoard as all the others, but here the wealth has been forged into weapons.

[[On the Dial]] Tuesday is Mars' day — you hear it plainly in French mardi, from dies Martis — so iron takes it. On the hexagram Mars holds the 16h arm in ember orange, the working afternoon when the day is hottest at its labor and the mood is Zeal. It is the hour of effort, the anvil-hour, iron at the forge; orange is the color of the metal glowing just short of the point where it can be shaped. The afternoon belongs to the metal that gets things done.

[[The Lesson]] Iron's virtue is Courage and its vice is Wrath — the sword that defends and the sword that murders, one blade, two hands. Zeal is the heat between them, and it can temper or it can burn. Wrath is quelled from across the dial by Serenity on the Moon's calm arm; Courage is Wrath with its edge turned outward, toward what must be faced rather than what one wants to destroy. Iron is only as good as the hand that holds it — the same metal for the shield and the axe.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Mars keeps the 16h arm in ember orange — iron in the forge, glowing at the exact heat where it yields to the hammer. Orange is the working color, the afternoon at its hottest labor, and in paint it sits warm and heavy between red and yellow. Zeal made visible: the arm of effort, the hour the metal is actually being made into something.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, orange has no seat — the light-wheel runs on primaries and their pure mixes — so Mars burns as pure yellow instead. It is the whetstone spark, the yellow-white flash of steel struck on stone. In light the ember cools to a blade's gleam; Zeal keeps its heat but trades the forge-glow for the edge's shine. The working metal, lit as the finished tool.
variants.octa_paint: Iron, the soldier's metal that runs quite literally in every vein as the core of hemoglobin, shares its working-afternoon arm with copper (#C87533) — the Walk of the Merchant, the Coin its object, an odd assignment for a blade until the Iron Age itself is remembered: the metal that armed every army also priced every trade, the nail and the ploughshare both stamped from the same ore that filled treasuries as readily as armories.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel iron's arm turns amber (#FFB400) — Maturity — the metal cooling from the forge's raw orange toward a harder, worked gold, exactly the difference between a blade fresh-quenched and one that has already seen a campaign. Courage tempered by use, not courage still glowing.
variants.cross: The cross gives Mars the 18h dusk arm — autumn, the autumnal equinox, colored dusk red — and he shares it with Copper, the Venus metal. Iron and copper on one arm: the two oldest working metals, the pair that Bronze-Age memory is built from, tools and blades from one and mirrors and coin from the other. Zeal at the equinox is the harvest's effort, the hardest labor of the year done in the reddening light.
variants.trio: On the trio Mars moves to the 20h arm — the red Love arm — standing beside Copper again and guarding the evening third of the day, the hours from 16h to 24h. Iron on the arm of Love is Courage in its oldest sense: to fight for something, not against it. Zeal turned toward what one protects rather than what one would break — the warrior keeping the evening watch.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Quicksilver is the strangest of the seven — the only metal that is liquid at room temperature, a mirror you can pour, a silver that runs. The alchemists called it Mercurius and made it one of the tria prima, the three first principles, the very spirit of transformation: the mediator that dissolves gold and silver into itself and carries every metal from one state to another. It is the messenger between conditions, solid and liquid at once, and its sign ☿ — the crescent, circle and cross stacked together — marks the metal, the planet, and the quick god who runs between worlds.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion pours the shared still life in bright quicksilver, and it alone breaks the still: above the stacked bars, the nuggets and the coil of wire, a ribbon of liquid metal curls and beads across the top of the plate, refusing to stay solid. The whole surface has a pearled, mother-of-pearl iridescence, as if the metal could not decide on a single color. Round the rim run caducei, winged sandals, and the ☿ of Mercury — the emblems of the messenger, wings and serpents circling the one metal that will not hold a shape.

[[On the Dial]] Wednesday is Mercury's day — French mercredi, dies Mercurii, says it outright — so quicksilver takes it, and takes the strangest seat on the dial: the bottom 0h/24h arm, midnight, mourning purple, mood Sorrow. It is the day's grave and its mystery, the deepest point of the clock where one day dies and the next is born. The messenger belongs at the threshold; the mediator between states sits exactly where midnight mediates between yesterday and tomorrow, in the purple of the deepest hour.

[[The Lesson]] Quicksilver's virtue is Wisdom and its vice is Greed — the knowledge that transmutes and the hunger that hoards. Mercurius understands every metal because he can become each one; that is wisdom, the fluid mind that meets a thing on its own terms. Greed is cured from across the dial by Generosity on the Jupiter arm — the tin that gives its ring away — while Wisdom is Greed dissolved, the grip loosened into flow. The metal that will not be held teaches the mind not to clutch.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Quicksilver keeps the 0h arm at the very bottom, in mourning purple (#542E85) — the color of midnight and of the grave, mixed deep and cool. It is the lowest, quietest point of the star, the pigment of Sorrow. The messenger rests at the bottom of the dial where the day turns over, purple pooling at the hour that belongs to no day at all.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 0h arm lifts into magenta (#BD00BD) — the impossible color, the one hue with no single wavelength, made only where the spectrum's two ends bend around and meet. It is the perfect light for the mediator: a color that exists only as a bridge. Sorrow, in light, becomes the magenta seam where the ends of the rainbow are joined — quicksilver closing the circle at midnight.
variants.octa_paint: Quicksilver, the one metal that runs liquid at room temperature, takes the late-evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of the Artist, the Mask its object — the single most natural seat on the whole wheel for the tria prima's own mediator, the metal that dissolves gold and silver alike and never holds one shape. Mercurius wears every disguise because he can become any of the metals he touches; the Mask was always his medallion before it was his arm.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel that same hour deepens to dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — Old Age — and quicksilver wears it as accumulated fluency rather than decline: the mediator who has already passed through every other metal's state, the wisdom of a substance that has been solid, liquid, and vapor all in one long career of transformation.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons, and Quicksilver keeps the 0h arm — the winter solstice, colored winter blue — and keeps it alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; the messenger has no partner. The metal that mediates between all the others holds the year's longest night entirely by itself, the single unshared season, the mercury that touches everything and belongs to no one. Sorrow, at the solstice, is solitude — and it suits him.
variants.trio: On the trio Quicksilver rises from the bottom to the 4h Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF), and shares it with Silver, the Moon metal. The two mirror-metals meet on the arm of Hope — one moon that reflects, one mercury that pours — both pointing their quicksilver at the coming dawn. The messenger leaves the grave of midnight for the hour before morning; Wisdom, on the Hope arm, is Sorrow that has learned to expect the light.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Tin is Jupiter's metal, the benevolent one, and its gifts are mostly gifts of sound and shine. Soft, silvery, and cheap enough to plate everything, tin is what makes bronze when you add it to copper and what makes bell-metal when you add more — the alloy that lets a cast bell ring true and an organ pipe hold its note. Bend a bar of pure tin and it complains: the famous "tin cry," the faint shriek of its crystals rearranging, a metal that literally speaks when you work it. Its sign ♃ is the same glyph for the metal, the planet, and the sky-father's thunderbolt.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion casts the shared still life in pale, soft-white tin — the stacked bars, the nuggets, the coil of wire, all in the mild pewter-bright tone that gives tin its modesty. The rim is Jupiter's own regalia: crowns and oak leaves alternating with sprays of lightning, and the ♃ glyph — drawn like a rounded "24" — set in medallions all the way round. The king's emblems around the humblest-looking metal: same wealth as the gold plate, dressed as tin, crowned all the same.

[[On the Dial]] Thursday is Jupiter's day — French jeudi, dies Iovis — so tin takes it, and takes the crown of the star: the top 12h arm, noon yellow, mood Joy. It is the high point of the dial, midday at its brightest, and Jupiter the greater benefic sits there in gold-adjacent yellow, the most fortunate hour on the most fortunate arm. Where gold holds the center as source, tin holds the summit as celebration — the noon of the week, ringing like a struck bell.

[[The Lesson]] Tin's virtue is Generosity and its vice is Excess — the open hand and the hand that never closes. Jupiter is expansion itself, and expansion is a blessing until it becomes bloat; the greater benefic gives, but too much of a good thing is still too much. Excess is curbed from across the dial by Wisdom on the Mercury arm — the quick mind that knows when to stop — while Generosity is Excess kept in measure, abundance that pours without flooding. Tin makes the bell ring; Wisdom keeps it from cracking.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Tin holds the 12h arm at the top in noon yellow — the crown of the star, midday laid down in warm pigment. It is the highest, brightest arm of the wheel, the color of the sun at its peak painted onto Jupiter's own summit. Joy made visible: the noon-yellow crest where the day is fullest and the metal rings its brightest. Generosity at the top of the dial, giving the whole afternoon its light.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 12h arm turns green — because green is the middle of the spectrum, and the middle belongs at the top, at noon. The most balanced light seats itself on the most fortunate arm. Joy, in light, is not the yellow of paint but the green at the spectrum's heart, the restful center-color crowning the star. Tin lit green is Generosity in equilibrium — abundance that has found its balance point.
variants.octa_paint: Tin, the metal that rings true in bell-metal and gives the organ pipe its note, takes the mid-morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of the Priest, the Bell his very object, a seat this soft benevolent metal was practically forged for. The famous 'tin cry' — the shriek of its crystals rearranging — is the metal's own voice; the Priest's Bell simply gives that voice a nave to ring in.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel tin's arm turns spring green (#7CE577) — Childhood — the freshest register of Jupiter's own generosity, the bell not yet cracked, the note still new. Tin is soft and cheap and everywhere useful, an open hand young enough that Excess has not yet had time to set in.
variants.cross: The cross gives Tin the 12h arm — summer, the summer solstice, colored high yellow — and it shares that arm with Gold, the Sun metal. Tin beside gold at the year's peak: and this is exactly tin's genius, for in the alloys of bells and bronze, tin is what makes gold's neighbors ring. The humble metal stands at the summit beside the perfect one, not outshining it but making it sound. Joy at the solstice is the longest day, struck like a bell.
variants.trio: On the trio Tin keeps the 12h arm, now the Faith arm in Faith yellow, and shares it with Lead, the Saturn metal — the lightest useful metal beside the heaviest, both guarding the day's bright third from 8h to 16h. Tin and lead on the arm of Faith: the ringing metal and the silent one, holding noon together. Generosity here is the faith that gives before it is asked — the bell that rings for the whole working day.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Copper is the metal of Venus, and the connection is old and literal: the Romans got most of their copper from Cyprus, Venus' own island, and called it aes cyprium — "the metal of Cyprus" — which wore down over the centuries into cuprum, and then copper. The love-goddess's metal is the one named after her sea. Warm, red-gold, and endlessly workable, copper was the first metal humanity smelted and the mirror the ancient world polished to see itself; the very sign of Venus, ♀, is drawn as a hand-mirror, the circle of the glass above the cross of the handle.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion pours the shared still life in warm red-copper — the stacked bars, the nuggets, the coil of wire glowing in the metal's own rosy light — and adds one thing the other plates lack: a scatter of green gems tucked among the ore, copper's green heart showing through, the patina and the emerald both. The rim is entirely Venus: ♀ hand-mirrors, doves in flight, and scallop shells — the sea-shell she was born from — circling the plate. Same wealth, cast in the metal named for her island.

[[On the Dial]] Friday is Venus' day — French vendredi, dies Veneris, keeps her name — so copper takes it. On the hexagram Venus holds the 20h arm in blood red, the evening, mood Passion. It is the warm hour after the work is done, the red of firelight and of the blood under the skin, the color the whole body wears when it is moved. Copper is the metal that blushes; the evening arm is where the day, its labor finished, finally feels.

[[The Lesson]] Copper's virtue is Love and its vice is Jealousy — the open heart and the clutching one. Venus gives freely, but the same warmth that opens can also grasp; the mirror that shows the beloved can show a rival just as fast. Jealousy is healed from across the dial by Patience on the Saturn arm — the slow metal that can wait — while Love is Jealousy let go, the warmth that wants the other's joy more than its own possession. Copper greens with age, but the red beneath is still the metal that first learned to love.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Venus keeps the 20h arm in blood red — the evening laid down warm and deep, the color of firelight and of the blood the heart moves. Red is the most bodily pigment, and here it sits at the hour the day finally rests and feels. Passion made visible: the red arm of the evening, copper glowing at the temperature of the living pulse.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 20h arm burns pure red — the primary, the longest wavelength the eye can catch, the light that reaches furthest through the dusk. In pigment red is blood; in light it is the last color of the sunset, the one that lingers. Passion, in light, is that lingering red — Venus as the final warm beam before the dark, copper's evening fire made of pure emission.
variants.octa_paint: Copper, named for Venus's own island of Cyprus, shares its evening arm with iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of the Soldier, the Sword its object, a hard turn for the mirror-metal of love until its harder history surfaces: copper was the first metal humanity ever smelted into a blade, the mirror and the weapon cast from the very same warm red ore. The love-goddess's metal armed as readily as it flattered.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel copper's arm turns ember (#FF6A3C) — the Elder, the harvest of a life — and the metal wears it honestly: copper greens with age exactly as a life does, its red warmth going patinaed and dark, yet the ember beneath, like the love beneath the tarnish, is still the same metal that first learned to shine.
variants.cross: The cross gives Venus the 18h arm — autumn, the autumnal equinox, colored dusk red — shared with Iron, the Mars metal. Copper beside iron at the equinox: the two Bronze-Age metals, the soft one and the hard one, mirror and blade, sharing the arm where the year tips into dark. Passion at the autumn balance is love in the reddening light, the warmth held steady as the days begin to shorten.
variants.trio: On the trio Venus keeps her 20h arm, and now it is the Love arm outright — colored Love red, and hers by right, though she shares it again with Iron. The evening arm belongs to the love-goddess; that Mars stands on it too only means Courage and Love keep the same watch. Passion and Love are one gesture here — copper on its own red arm, the metal named for Venus holding the hour named for what she is.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Lead is Saturn's metal — the heaviest of the seven the alchemists knew, the dullest, the slowest to change, and therefore the beginning of everything. It is the prima materia, the base matter the whole Work starts from: black, dense, saturnine, the lead that alchemy promises to turn into gold. That single transformation — lead into gold, the lowest metal into the highest — is the entire dream of the art. Modern physics keeps the old symbolism honest: lead is so dense it stops radiation, the heavy metal that shields us from the invisible, still guarding as its planet always did. Its sign ♄ marks the metal, the ringed planet, and old Father Time.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion casts the shared still life in dark blue-grey lead — the stacked bars rough and unpolished, the nuggets, the coil of wire, and one heavy round lead disc, all under a cold dim light with none of gold's blaze. The rim tells you exactly whose metal this is: ♄ glyphs set in medallions and, between them, lengths of heavy chain linking the plate all the way round — Saturn's chains, the weight and the binding of the slowest planet. Same wealth as every other plate, shown in the metal it all begins as.

[[On the Dial]] Saturday is Saturn's day and wears his name unmistakably — Saturday, dies Saturni — so lead takes it, the last day of the week and its rest. On the hexagram Saturn holds the 8h arm in morning green, mood Renewal — the week's rest and its restart, the quiet green hour when one cycle ends and the next has not yet begun. Lead is the metal of endings that are also beginnings; the prima materia waiting on Saturn's day for the Work to start again, green as the first growth after the fallow.

[[The Lesson]] Lead's virtue is Patience and its vice is Envy — the slow trust and the sour comparison. Saturn is the planet of time, and time rewards patience and rots into envy; the same slowness that lets lead wait forever can curdle into resenting what came faster. Envy is dissolved from across the dial by Love on the Venus arm — the copper warmth that wants another's good — while Patience is Envy outlasted, the heavy metal content to be the base that gold is made from. Lead does not envy gold; lead knows it is where gold begins.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Lead keeps the 8h arm in morning green (#007E00) — the quiet green of the week's restart, the fallow ground ready to grow. Green is the color of renewal in paint, and here it sits at the slow morning hour when the cycle turns over. Renewal made visible: the base metal at rest, green as the first shoot, patient on Saturn's own arm.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 8h arm turns cyan (#00DDDD) — green cooled toward blue, the clear light of an early morning that has not yet warmed. In light, lead's renewal is not the green of growth but the cyan of dawn air, the hour before the day commits. Patience, lit, is that held breath of first light — the heavy metal waiting, cool and clear, for its turn to change.
variants.octa_paint: Lead, the heaviest and dullest of the seven metals, takes the dawn arm in field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of the Farmer, the Plough its object — an apt turn for the prima materia, the base matter the whole Work tills before anything is grown from it. Every harvest of gold the alchemists ever dreamed of started in this same dark, patient ground.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel lead's arm turns predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — Birth — the exact hour before the Work begins, and no metal waits at that threshold more literally: lead is the beginning matter every transmutation starts from, base and unregarded until the first light of the Work calls it toward becoming something else entirely.
variants.cross: The cross gives Lead the 6h arm — spring, the vernal equinox, colored spring green — shared with Silver, the Moon metal. Lead beside silver at the year's balance: the heaviest metal and the brightest, the prima materia and the albedo, standing together where the year turns toward light. Renewal at the equinox is the true restart, day and night made equal and the slow metal ready, green, for its long climb toward gold.
variants.trio: On the trio Lead is promoted all the way to the 12h Faith arm in Faith yellow (#F8E600), sharing the summit with Tin, the Jupiter metal — the heaviest metal raised to the top of the dial. It is the alchemist's whole hope drawn as geometry: lead lifted to noon, standing where gold's light is brightest, on the arm of Faith. Patience here is faith itself — the base matter trusting, at the crown of the star, that it will one day be gold.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Nichiyobi, 日曜日, is the Sun's own day, and its first character 日 nichi simply means 'sun' — the same glyph that begins the name of the country, Nippon, the land where the sun rises. Sunday belongs to Amaterasu-omikami, the sun goddess from whom the Imperial line claims descent, the deity who once hid in the cave of Ama-no-Iwato and left the world in darkness until she was coaxed back. Her messenger is Yatagarasu, the three-legged crow who guided the first emperor through the mountains — a black bird carried on the solar disc itself.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is a blaze of gold: a rising-sun fan of cream-and-amber rays spreads behind a glossy crimson sun-orb, and across that orb the black Yatagarasu stands with wings flung wide. Beneath the disc a vermilion torii gate marks the threshold of the sacred. The border alternates chrysanthemum crests — the Imperial kiku — with red roundels each stamped 日 in gold, so the sun's own kanji rings the whole piece.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial the Sun holds the CENTER, and no argument is needed beyond the name: the day is called sun-day, so the sun sits at the hub where the hours turn. It is the white-gold source from which all six arm colors decompose — every hue on the face is the sun's own light split apart, the way a prism unfolds noon into a rainbow, and the way Amaterasu's light, withdrawn, unmakes the world.

[[The Lesson]] Amaterasu's Pride sent her into the cave, and her return was an act of Humility — drawn out by laughter and by her own reflection in a mirror, the source of all light remembering it is not the whole, the oldest pair whose cure never leaves the center. Yet the center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, and Japan owns the canon's Glory-and-Awe pair outright: when she shut herself in the Ama-no-Iwato cave the world's light went out — the Awe told as a story — and her coaxed return, mirror-first, is Glory itself. Her Justice is the sun as the measure of the realm, the line of rulers answerable to her light; her servant face is the daily faithfulness of sunrise, and Servility is that faithfulness curdled into mere routine. Humility answers Pride, Justice straightens Servility — the cave opens from the inside.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment the Sun keeps the center and stays empty of color — because paint cannot mix white. Pile every pigment together and you get mud, not light; the one shade a painter cannot reach by blending is the white-gold at the hub. So the six arms carry their colors outward and the center holds the one thing they can never compound, the unmixable source. Humility suits it: the middle claims no hue of its own.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light the logic inverts and the Sun is vindicated: six colored beams thrown inward SUM to white at the hub. What pigment could never mix, light does effortlessly — add all the arm colors as emission and they rebuild the white-gold blaze at the center. Here the Sun is literally the sum of its dial, Amaterasu reassembled from her own scattered spectrum, and Pride would be forgivable if Humility did not become her better.
variants.octa_paint: Amaterasu-omikami takes the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of the King, the Crown its object — and no goddess needs the seat argued: the Imperial line claims descent from her light alone, and the rising-sun fan behind her medallion was already a crown before any wheel drew one. Nippon, 'the land where the sun rises,' is itself her Crown's own name.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her noon arm is Youth, and Amaterasu wears the wheel's sun-yellow (#FFE800) as the light restored — the same blaze the world lost entirely when she withdrew into the Ama-no-Iwato cave, and won back the instant laughter and her own mirrored reflection coaxed her out. Youth is the world re-lit, not merely the world at noon.
variants.cross: The seasons cross has only four arms, one per solstice and equinox, and the Sun keeps the crossing where all four meet. It does not take a season — it is the pivot the seasons turn around, the still point at the center of the year. Summer climbs to 12h, winter sinks to 0h, spring and autumn reach left and right, and the sun-day holds the axis they all pass through, humble and unmoving at the heart of the wheel.
variants.trio: On the trio — Faith, Hope, Love — the Sun keeps the center while the yellow Faith arm rises straight up the 12h line, Hope reaching to 4h and Love to 20h. The three virtues fan from the hub the sun anchors, and Faith takes the vertical noon path as the sun's own gold climbs to the top. The source stays central; the virtues are its light given three directions.

faces.ruler:
Amaterasu is the Ruler face — the sun goddess from whom the Imperial line claims descent, her name folded into the day itself, since Nichiyobi's first character, 日, simply means sun.

The medallion blazes gold: a rising-sun fan of cream-and-amber rays spreads behind a glossy crimson sun-orb, the black three-legged Yatagarasu standing across it with wings flung wide, a vermilion torii marking the sacred threshold beneath, chrysanthemum crests and gold-stamped 日 roundels ringing the border.

Her virtue is Justice, the sun as the measure of the realm, the line of rulers answerable to her light rather than owning it outright — but that same standing is stalked by Pride, the very fault that once sent her into the cave rather than share the sky's praise. Her mood is Glory, her coaxed return mirror-first, laughter drawing her back out into the rising-sun fan at full blaze.
faces.servant:
Ama-no-Iwato is the Servant face — not a second deity but the cave itself, the place Amaterasu withdrew to, standing in for the goddess during the one stretch of the story when she refused to be seen at all.

The plate keeps the gold relief, sunburst rays and chrysanthemum-and-kanji border of Amaterasu's own plate, but the field has gone dark: the sealed boulder fills the frame in near-total darkness, the gold rays snuffed to cold gray-black behind it, the sacred mirror Yata no Kagami propped against the rock catching one last thin glint of light, a sakaki branch hung with jewels beside it, the torii barely visible — one sliver of warm gold light just beginning to leak from where the boulder has not quite sealed.

Its virtue is Humility, the goddess withdrawing rather than demanding the world keep praising her — the world going dark being the honest cost of that withdrawal — while Servility is the danger the myth actually warns of, the light habituating itself to absence instead of straining toward the mirror's glint. Its mood is Awe, the world's light gone out entirely — and Glory is the instant just after this plate: the boulder rolled back, the mirror's reflection mistaken by the goddess for a rival, and curiosity enough to step back out.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Getsuyobi, 月曜日, is Moon-day, and its lead character 月 — read getsu here, tsuki on its own — is the plain word for the moon. The day belongs to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto, the moon deity born from the right eye of Izanagi as Amaterasu was born from his left; brother and sister, sun and moon, they were set to rule day and night and have kept apart ever since, which is why the moon follows the sun across the sky but never meets her. Japan reads its own figure in the lunar face: not a man but tsuki no usagi, the moon rabbit, standing at a mortar and pounding mochi through the night.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is worked in silver over a deep navy field strewn with stars. At its heart a bright lunar orb carries the rabbit in silhouette, mallet raised above the tub, caught mid-pound; silver cloud-scrolls drift past, and the outer border cycles the moon through all its phases — waxing crescent, half, full, waning — alternating with dark roundels each bearing 月.

[[On the Dial]] On the 24-hour face the Moon takes the 4h arm, and the name settles the claim before it is made: a day called moon-day seats the moon on the dial without dispute. Four o'clock is the deep-blue hour just before dawn, the last watch of true night, and the Moon holds it — the coolest, stillest blue on the wheel, when the world sleeps and only the rabbit is awake at his mortar.

[[The Lesson]] The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear. Night is where dread lives, and the small hours before dawn are where the frightened mind wakes; the Moon answers with the calm of a settled surface, its mood Calm. Its cure lies straight across the dial: the Courage burning on the 16h fire-arm reaches over the center to quell the Fear that pools at 4h — day's nerve steadying night's unease.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment the Moon holds the 4h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the pre-dawn spoke pointing down and to the left. It is a mixed, weighty blue — pigment blue, the color of the last dark before morning — and it anchors the lower-left of the wheel where the night is thickest. Serenity is a painted stillness here, the calm of a color that does not move.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light the same 4h arm sharpens to a pure primary blue (#0040FF) — no longer mixed pigment but blue as a beam, one of the three lights everything else is built from. The Moon becomes elemental: not a shade among shades but a root color of the spectrum, throwing clean blue down the pre-dawn spoke. Calm, distilled to its wavelength.
variants.octa_paint: Tsukuyomi, born from Izanagi's right eye as Amaterasu was born from his left, takes the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of the Scholar, the Book its object. A god who keeps to a fixed nightly course, never once meeting his sister sun, is already the dial's most literal reckoner — a path followed and recorded, never wandered.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel his arm turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the Unborn — and Japan already reads it: the moon-rabbit pounding mochi through the night works toward a dawn it never quite reaches, an endless preparation for something not yet arrived, exactly the hue's own ancestors-and-not-yet-born held in one silver light.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves the Moon to the 6h arm — due left, the spring equinox — and colors it spring green, and here the Moon does not stand alone: it SHARES the equinox spoke with Doyobi, the earth-day. Moon and soil hold the balance-point of the year together, the hinge where light and dark come even. The night-body lends its calm to the greening of the world.
variants.trio: On the trio the Moon keeps its 4h arm and it becomes the Hope spoke, blue (#002FFF), reaching down-left from the hub. And it is shared: Suiyobi, the water-day, joins the Moon on the Hope arm, two night-bodies leaning into the same virtue. Moon and water — reflection and tide — carry Hope together through the small hours, Serenity turned toward the dawn it trusts is coming.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Kayobi, 火曜日, is Fire-day, and the character 火 — ka here, hi alone — is fire itself, the pictograph of a flame with sparks flying off. Its planet is written 火星 Kasei, the 'fire star,' the name Japanese astronomy gives to Mars, so the martial red planet and the element of fire share a single word on this day. Fire in the old telling is dangerous kin: Kagutsuchi, the fire god, burned his mother Izanami to death being born, and his father Izanagi beheaded him in grief — from that spilled blood came new gods. Fire is birth and war at once, and Kayobi carries the soldier's edge of it.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is forged in dark bronze under a red glow: a samurai kabuto, its wide crescent horns curving up, sits over two crossed katana, and orange flames wreath the whole arrangement against a black-and-crimson field. The border rings it with tomoe — the comma-shaped swirl of motion and thunder — each roundel stamped 火, with tongues of flame set between them.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial Mars takes the 16h arm, and the naming is decisive: the day is Kasei's day, the fire-star's day, so the fire-star burns on the fire spoke. Sixteen o'clock is the working afternoon, the ember hour when the day's labor is hottest, and Mars holds it in ember orange, its mood Zeal — the heat of effort, the forge still roaring past midday.

[[The Lesson]] Mars's virtue is Courage and its vice is Wrath. Fire is the element of both: the flame that tempers steel and the flame that burns the house down are the same flame, differing only in whether they are governed. Courage is fire mastered; Wrath is fire loosed. Its cure sits straight across the dial at 4h, where the Moon's Serenity reaches over the center to quell the Wrath smoldering at 16h — night's calm banking the afternoon's fire.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment Mars holds the 16h arm in ember orange, the spoke pointing up and to the right into the afternoon. Orange is a mixed pigment — red and yellow beaten together — which suits the fire-day: a worked, kindled color, not a raw primary. Zeal glows here as heat you can see, the amber of a blade left in the coals.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light Mars cannot stay orange — the prism has no seat for it, so the 16h arm resolves to pure yellow, the nearest true wavelength the spectrum offers. Fire trades its pigment-warmth for a clean yellow beam; the fire-star burns brighter and simpler in light than in paint. Courage, refined to the one color the prism will grant it.
variants.octa_paint: The fire-star Kasei, armored in kabuto and crossed katana, shares its working-afternoon arm with copper (#C87533) — the Walk of the Merchant, the Coin its object, a turn against the blade until the samurai's own economy is remembered: swords were commissioned, forged, and traded as prized goods, a warrior's katana as much a merchant's masterpiece as a soldier's tool. Kagutsuchi's fire, after all, is also the forge-fire that turns raw ore into coin as readily as into steel.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the fire-star's arm turns amber (#FFB400) — Maturity — bronze cooling from the ember's raw orange into a harder, worked gold, the difference between a blade fresh from the fire and one already carried through a season of campaigns.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Mars to the 18h arm — due right, the autumn equinox — and colors it red, the fire of falling leaves. Here Mars shares: Kin'yobi, the metal-day, holds the same dusk spoke. Fire and metal at the west edge of the year, war-red and evening-red together, two heats meeting where the sun goes down. Courage in company at last.
variants.trio: On the trio Mars leaves the afternoon and drops to the 20h arm, the red Love spoke, guarding the evening hours from 16h to 24h beside Kin'yobi. Fire becomes ardor: the same red that meant war now means passion, and Mars stands with the metal-day on the Love arm. Courage bends toward the heart — the warrior's fire warming, not burning.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Suiyobi, 水曜日, is Water-day, and the glyph 水 — sui here, mizu alone — draws water as a running stream. Its planet is 水星 Suisei, the 'water star,' Japanese for Mercury, so the swift innermost planet and the element of flow are named alike on this day. The sea belongs to Ryujin, the dragon king who keeps the coral palace Ryugu-jo on the ocean floor and rules the tides with two jewels, the kanju and manju, that draw the water in and drive it out. Water is the element of communication and passage — messengers, currents, the way news and rivers both run.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is worked in silver and blue-green: the Great Wave rears and breaks, and rising from its curl a coiled dragon lifts a glowing white tide-jewel in its claws, teal water all around. The border is seigaiha, the scalloped blue wave-pattern, with koi swimming between roundels each stamped 水 — fish, waves and water-kanji circling the sea-dragon at the heart.

[[On the Dial]] On the 24-hour dial Mercury holds the BOTTOM arm, 0h and 24h at once — the deepest seat on the wheel, and no seat suits water better. The day is the water-star's day, so water takes the spoke, and water always seeks the lowest place: midnight is the floor of the day and the sea-floor of the clock, held in mourning purple, its mood Sorrow — the grave and mystery at the bottom of the night.

[[The Lesson]] Mercury's virtue is Wisdom and its vice is Greed. There is an old, very Japanese reading here: water is wisest exactly where it sinks lowest, for the sea gains its greatness by lying beneath every stream and asking nothing. Greed grasps upward; Wisdom, like water, settles and receives. The cure comes from straight overhead — the Generosity crowning the 12h arm pours down the whole height of the dial to answer the Greed pooled at the bottom, the giving top curing the grasping floor.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment Mercury holds the bottom 0h arm in mourning purple (#542E85), the single spoke pointing straight down to midnight. Purple is the deep-mixed pigment, red and blue drowned together, and it colors the lowest, gravest point of the wheel. Sorrow sits at the floor of the clock — water gathered in the dark, wisest where it lies deepest.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light the bottom arm lifts from purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the glowing edge-of-spectrum color that light makes but no single wavelength holds, a color of pure mixture. Mercury's midnight spoke burns magenta at the base of the dial, Sorrow turned luminous. Even at the deepest point, water catches light and gives it back.
variants.octa_paint: Suisei, the water-star of the dragon-king Ryujin and his tide-jewels, shares its late-evening arm with stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of the Artist, the Mask its object. Water that can be wave, current, or still coral palace floor by turns is already a shape-shifter's medium; the dragon who rules the tide with a jewel in each claw wears the Artist's Mask as naturally as the Great Wave wears its own curling foam.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel that hour deepens to dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — Old Age — and Ryujin's sea wears it as the ocean's own accumulated depth: water gains its wisdom exactly by lying beneath every stream for longest, the coral palace ancient before any current that feeds it.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Mercury at the 0h arm — straight down, the winter solstice — colored winter blue, and it is the only body that stands alone. Every other season spoke is shared, but the water-day holds the year's longest night by itself, the frozen floor of the calendar. Water at the bottom, winter at the bottom, midnight at the bottom: one solitary spoke gathering all the depths of the wheel.
variants.trio: On the trio Mercury rises from the floor to the 4h Hope arm, blue, and shares it with Getsuyobi, the moon-day. Water and moon — tide and its mistress — lean into Hope together in the pre-dawn dark. The water-star leaves its midnight grave and reaches, with the Moon, toward the coming light; Wisdom learns to hope. Two night-blues on one spoke, waiting for dawn.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Mokuyobi, 木曜日, is Wood-day, and the character 木 — moku here, ki alone — is a tree, roots and branches drawn from a single stroke. Its planet is 木星 Mokusei, the 'wood star,' Japan's name for Jupiter, the largest of the wanderers, so the giant planet and the element of growing things are one word on this day. Wood is the living element, the only one that grows: the great sugi cedars and sacred pines that Shinto marks as shinboku, kami-trees, girdled with rope to show that a god dwells in the timber. Some, like Yakushima's ancient cedar, have grown for thousands of years — wood as patience made visible.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion carries a green patina over bronze: an ancient pine, gnarled and wind-bent, grips a mossy rock, and around its trunk runs a shimenawa rope hung with white zigzag shide streamers, marking it sacred. The border is a grove in metal — bamboo stalks, maple leaves and pine-sprays — set with roundels each stamped 木, so the wood-kanji ripens around the tree.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial Jupiter takes the TOP 12h arm, the crown of the wheel, and the name crowns it: the day is the wood-star's day, so the wood-star climbs to the highest point. Twelve o'clock is noon, the sun's own summit, and the growing element takes it in noon yellow, its mood Joy — the exuberance of a tree in full light, reaching as high as the dial allows.

[[The Lesson]] Jupiter's virtue is Generosity and its vice is Excess. Wood is the element of both: a tree gives shade, fruit and timber freely — pure Generosity — but wood also grows without stopping, and unchecked growth is Excess, the branch that shades out its own roots. The cure rises from the opposite pole: the Wisdom sunk at the 0h bottom curbs the Excess crowning the top, the deep water-star teaching the high wood-star when to stop reaching.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment Jupiter crowns the 12h arm in noon yellow, the spoke standing straight up at the top of the dial. Yellow is nearly a primary, the brightest pigment on the wheel, and it suits the crown — the wood-star at the summit of noon, Joy painted as the highest, warmest light. The tree reaches its peak and holds it.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light the 12h crown turns green — and for the wood-day this is a gift. The spectrum's middle wavelength seats itself at noon, so the tallest arm glows the color of leaves; the wood-star, high on its spoke, wears the green of the very foliage it stands for. Growth crowned in green light, Joy in the exact hue of a living canopy — no other body's light-color fits its element so perfectly.
variants.octa_paint: Mokusei, the wood-star wrapped in Shinto's own shimenawa rope and paper shide, takes the mid-morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of the Priest, the Bell its object, the one seat that asks nothing of translation: a shinboku kami-tree, girdled to mark a god's dwelling, is already a shrine before any wheel names it one. The rope that hangs shide from an ancient cedar is the Bell's own rite, tied straight onto living wood.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the wood-star's arm turns spring green (#7CE577) — Childhood — and no element on the dial wears youth more honestly: wood is the one element that grows, and even the thousand-year cedars of Yakushima began as this same green first leaf, patience's whole career starting from Childhood's own color.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Jupiter at the 12h crown, colored summer yellow, and here it shares the top with Nichiyobi, the sun-day. The wood-star and the sun stand together at the summer solstice, the year's high noon — growth and light on one spoke, the season when everything green is at its fullest. Generosity doubled at the peak of the year.
variants.trio: On the trio Jupiter keeps the 12h crown as the Faith arm, yellow, and shares it with Doyobi, the earth-day, the two of them guarding the daylight hours from 8h to 16h. The wood-star holds Faith at the top of the dial — the tree's upward reach read as trust — with the patient earth beside it. Growth and ground keeping Faith together at high noon.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Kin'yobi, 金曜日, is Metal-day — gold-day — and the character 金, kin or kane, means metal and gold at once, the most refined of the five elements. Its planet is 金星 Kinsei, the 'gold star,' Japan's name for Venus, the brightest point in the sky, so the evening star and the precious metal share one word on this day. Venus's emblem the world over is the mirror, and Japan's most sacred mirror is Yata no Kagami, one of the Three Imperial Regalia beside the sword and the jewel — the very glass held up outside the cave so that Amaterasu, seeing her own light, would step back into the world.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is warm gold throughout: a round polished mirror stands upright on a lotus-blossom pedestal, and golden ginkgo leaves drift down around it. The border runs ginkgo scrollwork between koban coins — the old oval gold pieces, and round coins with square holes — each roundel stamped 金, so metal money and metal-kanji frame the sacred glass.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial Venus takes the 20h arm, and the naming decides it: the day is Kinsei's day, the gold-star's day, so the gold-star burns on the evening spoke. Twenty o'clock is deep evening, the hour the love-planet has always owned, and Venus holds it in blood red, its mood Passion — the warm dark when the evening star hangs brightest and the heart is least guarded.

[[The Lesson]] Venus's virtue is Love and its vice is Jealousy. The mirror is the sign of both: it is the instrument of beauty and self-knowledge, but a mirror is also where comparison begins, and comparison is the seed of Jealousy. Metal refines and metal cuts. The cure crosses the dial from the 8h morning arm, where Saturn's Patience reaches over to heal the Jealousy that flares at 20h — the earth-star's slow steadiness cooling the gold-star's evening fever.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment Venus holds the 20h arm in blood red, the spoke reaching down and to the left into the evening. Red is a primary here, deep and unmixed, and it colors the love-planet's hour — the warm red of the evening star at its brightest. Passion painted at full strength, the gold-star wearing the reddest pigment on the wheel.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light the 20h arm becomes pure red, red as a beam rather than a pigment — one of the three root colors the whole spectrum is built from. Venus's evening spoke burns clean and primary, the love-star reduced to its most elemental heat. Love as a single wavelength, unmixed and unmistakable.
variants.octa_paint: Kinsei, the gold-star whose sacred mirror once coaxed Amaterasu from her cave, shares its evening arm with iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of the Soldier, the Sword its object, an unlikely turn for the Imperial Regalia's gentlest treasure until its company is remembered: the mirror stands beside the sword itself among the Three Regalia, beauty and blade granted equal rank in the same royal trust. The gold-star's brightness has always kept martial company.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel Kinsei's arm turns ember (#FF6A3C) — the Elder, the harvest of a life — and the gold-star wears it as the mirror's own long service: Yata no Kagami has been carried, guarded, and venerated across the entire span of the Imperial line, a beauty aged into an heirloom rather than a fleeting shine.
variants.cross: The seasons cross sets Venus on the 18h arm — due right, the autumn equinox — colored red, and she shares it with Kayobi, the fire-day. Metal and fire meet at the west edge of the year, evening-red and war-red on one spoke, the season when the leaves themselves turn the color of both. Love and Courage side by side at the sunset of the calendar.
variants.trio: On the trio Venus keeps the 20h arm, and it is the Love spoke itself — red, reaching into the evening — shared with Kayobi, the fire-day. Of all bodies the gold-star is most at home here: the love-planet on the Love arm, its own virtue named for it. Fire stands beside her, war turned to ardor, and the two guard the evening together. Love holds its own hour.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Doyobi, 土曜日, is Earth-day — soil-day — and the character 土, do or tsuchi, is the ground itself, a mound of earth on the land. Its planet is 土星 Dosei, the 'earth star,' Japan's name for Saturn, the slowest and most distant of the visible wanderers, so the far, patient planet and the element of soil share one word on this day. Earth is the element of endurance and rest: the mountains that do not move, the paddies worked the same way for a thousand years, and the week's own seventh day, when labor stops and the ground is let lie fallow.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion is aged iron and silver over an earthen brown field: Mount Fuji rises snow-capped behind terraced rice paddies walled with fitted stone, and in the foreground stands an ishidoro, a stone lantern, patient and weathered. The border is the harvest in metal — heavy rice-ears bowing with grain and asanoha hemp-leaf stars — set with roundels each stamped 土, so the earth-kanji rings the mountain and the fields.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial Saturn takes the 8h arm, and the name grounds it: the day is the earth-star's day, so the earth-star holds the morning soil-spoke. Eight o'clock is mid-morning, the hour the rested week begins again — Saturday as the pause that restarts everything — and Saturn holds it in morning green (#007E00), its mood Renewal, the fresh green of ground turned over for a new planting.

[[The Lesson]] Saturn's virtue is Patience and its vice is Envy. Earth teaches both: the soil waits out every season without hurry, pure Patience, but ground is also the thing most coveted — one field always looks richer than another, and that coveting is Envy. The cure comes from across the dial at 20h, where Venus's Love dissolves the Envy rooted at 8h — the evening gold-star's warmth loosening the morning earth-star's grip on what the neighbor has.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment Saturn holds the 8h arm in morning green (#007E00), the spoke pointing up and to the left into mid-morning. Green is the color of new growth on turned soil, and the earth-star wears it as Renewal — a deep, grounded green, the hue of a paddy in spring. The slowest planet keeps the freshest color, patience about to bud.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in light Saturn's 8h arm brightens from green to cyan (#00DDDD), the cool blue-green the prism makes of the growing hues. The earth-star cools toward the sky, morning soil seen through clear early light. Renewal in a crisper key — the green of the fields answered by the azure of the dawn air above them.
variants.octa_paint: Dosei, the earth-star of terraced paddies and the ishidoro's patient stone, takes the dawn arm in field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of the Farmer, the Plough its object, the single most exact match on the whole dial. A thousand years of paddies worked the same way already IS the Farmer's estate; the earth-star simply steps onto ground it has held since Doyobi was first named for it.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the earth-star's arm turns predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — Birth — and Dosei wears it as the fallow field's own promise: soil let lie on the week's rest day is never dead ground, only ground waiting for the next planting, the mist before dawn standing in for the moment before any new season truly starts.
variants.cross: The seasons cross sets Saturn on the 6h arm — due left, the spring equinox — colored spring green, and it shares the spoke with Getsuyobi, the moon-day. Earth and moon hold the balance-point of the year together, soil and tide at the hinge where day and night come even. The patient ground greens at the equinox with the Moon beside it, Renewal and Serenity on one arm.
variants.trio: On the trio Saturn is promoted from the morning to the very crown: it takes the 12h Faith arm, yellow (#F8E600), shared with Mokuyobi, the wood-day. The lowest, slowest element rises to the top of the dial and holds Faith — earth's endurance read as the steadiest trust of all — with the growing wood-star beside it. Ground and tree keep Faith together at high noon.

sun.name: Queen · Cleaner
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] The Queen lies at the living center of the comb, long-bodied and regal, laying an egg into an open hexagonal cell while her retinue rings her on every side, all faces turned inward, antennae reaching toward her like rays of a corona that breathes. Behind her a round patch of comb has been worked into a gilded sun disc of perfect hexagons — the hive's own image of its own maker. She is the Sun's child and Sunday's figure: the one bee among tens of thousands who was never assigned a task, because she is the task every other task exists to serve.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial she holds no arm at all — her home is the center, colored white-gold, the sum of every hue the six workers wear. The hive gives this literal proof no other theme can: colonies raised without a queen do not merely lose a leader, they lose their nervous system, their common purpose, brood-rearing, foraging, defense, all of it drifting and failing within weeks. She is not one calling radiating outward from six; she is the reason the six exist to be arranged around anything at all.

Every other bee on this dial ages through a career, cleaner to nurse to builder to guard to forager, her whole life a six-week week; the Queen alone does not walk that ladder. She hatches from the same kind of egg as any worker, fed nothing but royal jelly instead of the mixed diet that turns her sisters into laborers, and the diet alone rewrites her: an ovary instead of a pollen basket, two to five years instead of six weeks, an egg-laying life instead of a task-graded death. The week's whole clock is measured against her stillness — she is the one bee for whom Sunday is not a stage in a life story but the entire story, told every day.

[[The Lesson]] The canon's double survives the translation whole, and the hive even supplies its own eclipse: as ruler she practices Justice — the medallion argues it plainly, an egg laid into whatever open cell presents itself, favoring no daughter over another, fecundity apportioned rather than chosen — and she is stalked by Pride, a stillness so total it could be mistaken for a court that exists to flatter rather than to feed her. Her dual is the Cleaner, the day-one daughter found with her head and forelegs still deep in the very cell she hatched from, practicing Humility before she has earned any other virtue, and stalked by Servility, obedience performed by instinct with no will yet behind it to call it a virtue. Where the Queen's disc is gilded comb standing in for the sun at Glory, the Cleaner's plate carries one dark, empty cell rimmed in a thin ring of fresh gilded wax — the hive's own eclipse, the one hour the light fails and only its rim still catches gold.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel the Queen keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the comb's six castes decompose around her like light through a prism made of wax, every one of them radiating outward from a body that itself does no radiating labor at all. This is her home reading — the source, not a cell.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around her but cannot change her: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world it is literally true that white is every color summed. A colony without her loses the sum and keeps only the scattered parts — light decomposed with nothing left standing in the middle to recompose it.
variants.octa_paint: The Queen takes the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of the King, the Crown its object — the one Walk on the whole wheel that asks no metaphor at all. She is the hive's sole monarch, the single bee a colony simply cannot function without, and the gilded hexagon disc behind her in her own medallion was already a crown in wax before the wheel drew one in gold.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her noon arm is Youth, and the Queen wears the wheel's sun-yellow (#FFE800) as the one true flight of her entire life: her single nuptial climb into open light, taken once, young, before she ever laid an egg and never left the dark comb again.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Queen stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the solstice, sharing the spoke with the Builder. Mother and architect on one arm: the longest light of the year is also the colony's busiest building season, comb raised fastest exactly when she is laying fastest, the two of them keeping the same summer clock without either one commanding the other.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Queen keeps the center, and the court narrows to three arms around her: Faith yellow at the Builder and Forager's spoke, Love red at the Guard and Drone's, Hope blue at the Nurse and Scout's. It is the hive's oldest fact made a diagram — every caste's labor is, in the end, hers taken apart and handed out, white-gold light returned to the six colors that make it.

faces.ruler:
The Queen is the Sun's child and Sunday's own calling, mother of every bee the dial's other six castes belong to — not a ruler who commands from a distance but one whose entire biology is the command: without her eggs there is no Nurse to feed, no Guard to defend, no Forager to feed the field, nothing at all for the other six days to be about.

The medallion holds her at the living center of the comb, long-bodied and regal, laying an egg into an open hexagonal cell while her retinue rings her on every side, faces turned inward, antennae reaching toward her like rays of a corona; behind her a round patch of comb worked into a gilded sun disc of perfect hexagons. The border runs bronze honeycomb, some cells open and some capped, tiny worker bees crawling the lattice, four small roundels bearing the Sun glyph.

Her virtue is Justice, argued by the medallion itself: an egg laid into whatever open cell presents itself next, no daughter favored over another, fecundity apportioned rather than chosen. That same stillness is stalked by Pride, a court so total in its devotion it could mistake itself for the reason she reigns rather than the reason she can. Her mood is Glory, the corona of antennae at its fullest, comb worked into a gilded disc standing in for the very sun — the height of a season when she may lay two thousand eggs in a single day and every one of them finds an open cell waiting.
faces.servant:
The Cleaner is the hive's own answer to the servant-king, though the canon's usual trick — one figure with two faces — will not quite fit a colony: no bee is ever queen twice, so the theme casts the double across two bodies instead of one, mother above and day-one daughter below, the doubled self read across a generation rather than within a single skin.

The plate matches the Queen's bronze relief and cracked-slate stone exactly, the same style, the same border language: a just-hatched young worker, fuzzy and soft, wings still crumpled from the cell, head and forelegs deep inside an open hexagonal cell polishing its walls — cleaning the very cell she was born in — while the comb stretches away toward a distant warm glow where the Queen's retinue circles, small and far. Directly above her stands one dark, empty cell rimmed with a thin gilded ring of fresh wax. Her posture is lowly; her work is exact.

Her virtue is Humility, the crown's opposite performed without ever having worn one — the humblest labor in the whole hive done first, before any bee has earned the right to anything grander. She is stalked by Servility, the same posture curdled: an instinct followed with no will yet behind it to call it a choice. Her mood is Awe, and the hive writes it more literally than any sky could — the one dark cell above her, empty where a sister should be, circled by the only bright metal on her whole plate: the light failed there, and only its rim still catches the gold.

moon.name: Nurse
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] The Nurse bows head-down over an open brood cell, feeding a curled translucent larva a glistening drop of royal jelly, while around her the capped brood cells swell smooth and pale as a field of small full moons set into the dark comb. She is the Moon's child and Monday's figure: a young worker only days out of her own cell, whose entire assignment is the hive's youngest and most helpless — the larvae who cannot yet feed themselves, cannot yet see, cannot yet do anything but wait to be fed.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn. The hive keeps this shift true to its own architecture: brood-rearing happens in the darkest, most central cells of the comb, sealed away from the light and the weather both, and a nurse's whole working world is that windowless dark, lit only by the small chemical signal of larval pheromone telling her which cell is hungry next. No hour fits the calling better than the one before any sun has risen at all.

[[The Lesson]] The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear, the same darkness lit or unlit, and a nurse's hands hold both at once: her feeding rhythm must stay steady through hundreds of cells a day, because larvae fed erratically starve as surely as larvae not fed at all. Straight across the dial at 16h stands the Guard, and the two professions cure each other exactly as the canon promises — Courage cures Fear, Serenity quells Wrath — the sting-bearer lending her nerve to the one who has none to spare, the nurse lending her calm to the one whose whole day is adrenaline.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because a nurse's fear, if she had any leisure to feel it, would live there: hive science calls hers the second stage of temporal polyethism, the bee who has aged out of cell-cleaning and into something that can be trusted with a life not her own. She will not do this work forever — in another week or two her glands will fail and her body will reassign her to the builder's chain — but for now, in the dark before dawn, she is the whole colony's only mother that is not the Queen.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — the nurse's dark, windowless shift among the brood, exactly as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps her at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's muted pre-dawn becomes light's electric one. Same shift, same Calm; the larval cells she tends simply glow a colder white in the retelling.
variants.octa_paint: The Nurse, bowed over the brood cells feeding curled larvae in the comb's darkest heart, takes the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of the Scholar, the Book its object. Her whole calling is a study kept by touch and chemical signal rather than sight — hundreds of cells read and answered correctly every day — the Scholar's patient record-keeping performed blind, in wax instead of ink.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel her arm turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the Unborn — and no bee on the dial sits it more exactly: a Nurse's whole shift is spent among larvae that cannot yet see or feed themselves, life suspended precisely between egg and worker, held in her care until it is ready to become anything at all.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Nurse moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with the Forager. Feeder and grower on one spoke, and rightly: spring is when the queen's laying and the field's flowering both peak together, and a colony's whole season depends on both keeping pace.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the Nurse at 04h but renames her watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with the Scout, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. No pairing earns the word more honestly — hope is the larva raised in the dark trusting a flower it has never seen, danced into being by a sister it will also never see fly.

mars.name: Guard
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] The Guard plants herself wide-legged on the hive's landing board, wings half-raised, mandibles open, abdomen curled forward to bare her sting, facing down the huge looming shape of a hornet three times her size at the plate's edge, its shadow already falling across her. She does not step back. She is Mars' child and Tuesday's figure, and of every bee on this dial she is the only one whose weapon kills its owner: the barbed sting tears loose in the strike, and she dies within minutes of using it.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon, fire banked to a working heat rather than a blaze. The placement suits her exactly as it suited the theme's older soldier: guard duty is not a single dramatic charge but hour after hour on the board, checking every bee that lands against the colony's own scent, an unglamorous grind that only occasionally erupts into the one fight she cannot walk away from.

[[The Lesson]] Her stance carries her two faces at once: the sting offered, whatever the cost, is Courage; the mandibles already open before the threat is even confirmed are Wrath, the same readiness pointed at friend and stranger both until she has checked. Straight across the dial at 04h waits the Nurse, and each cures the other — Serenity quells Wrath, Courage cures Fear — the bee with nothing to lose lending her nerve to the one cradling everything the colony has yet to become.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is a guard's true shift, past morning's quiet traffic, into the hours when robbers and hornets actually come hunting; she reached this post only after cleaning, nursing and building behind her, the career ladder promoting her to violence last of all, once nothing but experience is left to give the colony. She will not survive every fight she starts, and the hive built her career order so that only a bee who has already given everything else is finally asked to give that.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the board she stands all day, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps her at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00). The working fire glows hotter in Newton's reading, though the hour and the traffic she checks stand unchanged.
variants.octa_paint: The Guard, sting bared against a hornet three times her size, shares her working-afternoon arm with copper (#C87533) — the Walk of the Merchant, the Coin its object, a strange turn for a bee whose whole trade is violence until the real shift is remembered: guard duty is mostly a toll-booth, checking every arriving forager's scent against the colony's own, an economy of who is let in and who is turned away long before it ever becomes a fight.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm turns amber (#FFB400) — Maturity — and the hive's own career ladder proves the hue: a Guard reaches this post last of all, after cleaning, nursing, and building behind her, promoted to violence only once nothing but seasoned experience is left to give.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Guard stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Drone. Both give their bodies for the colony in a single irreversible act, one in defense and one in love, and autumn's red is exactly the color of a debt paid in full.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Guard moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside the Drone, and the pair guard the evening from 16h to 24h together. It reads the profession honestly: courage is love wearing a sting, the last bee at the door standing watch for the sake of everything sleeping behind her.

mercury.name: Scout
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] The Scout dances mid-figure-eight on the vertical face of the comb, her path worn into the wax as a trail, body tilted at a precise angle while a ring of foragers crowd close, antennae extended, reading her. At the comb's very top a small engraved sun mark stands, and the angle between her waggle run and that mark upward is, in truth as much as in this medallion, the entire message. She is Mercury's child and Wednesday's figure — the one bee whose whole profession is translation.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in deep purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. The placement is the theme's cleverest borrowing: a scout dances in the pitch-dark interior of the hive, blind, communicating a sunlit meadow she alone has seen to sisters who never will, purple's old costliness — the rarest pigment paid for in labor rather than coin — matching a message that cost her a whole flight to earn and will cost her audience nothing but attention.

[[The Lesson]] Mercury's virtue is Wisdom, its vice Greed, and the swarm's own science supplies the cleanest illustration on the whole dial: when a colony must choose a new home, several scouts dance for several different sites at once, each dancing harder for her own find — until, cell by cell, the hive's collective attention tips to whichever site earns the most dancers, and the losing scouts simply stop dancing and follow the consensus. Wisdom is the whole colony deciding together; Greed would be the scout who keeps dancing for her own site after the swarm has already chosen, mistaking loyalty to her discovery for loyalty to the hive.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because midnight is also where the real waggle dance lives — inside a completely lightless hive, communicating a direction that only makes sense outside in the sun. A dial whose own hexagram tip points at true solar noon owes its clearest tribute to the bee that already invented that idea: a compass built from angle-to-the-sun, run in the dark, trusted completely by bees who will never see the sun it points to.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of a message danced in complete darkness — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns her purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — a hue found in no rainbow, invented by the eye where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for a scout: she communicates a direction the dance itself cannot see, trusted completely though neither end of the message is visible at once.
variants.octa_paint: The Scout, mid-figure-eight on the vertical comb, shares her midnight arm with stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of the Artist, the Mask its object, an estate she earns bee for bee: her waggle dance is pure performance, body tilted at a precise angle, translating a sunlit meadow she alone has seen into a language her sisters read by touch alone in the pitch dark.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel the same hour deepens to dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — Old Age — and the Scout wears it as hard-earned fluency: when several scouts dance for several different sites, the wisest of them yield their own dance and follow the swarm's consensus, an elder's judgment about when a discovery is worth surrendering.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Scout keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and she alone shares it with no one. Winter is when foraging mostly stops and there is little left to dance for; the year's midnight belongs to a single figure with nothing left to report.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the Scout off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Nurse, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names her trade's true engine: every dance is hope with an angle attached, a whole colony's faith in a meadow only one bee has actually seen.

jupiter.name: Builder
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Builder bees hang from each other in a living chain — the festoon — suspended from the comb's growing upper edge, the lowest bee shaping a fresh hexagonal cell with her mandibles while thin scales of new wax pass hand to hand down the chain above her. Below them the finished comb spreads in perfect crystalline hexagons, the newest cells still pale and bright. He is Jupiter's child and Thursday's figure, and his medallion is the one plate on this entire dial that draws the dial's own shape being built.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and the hexagon he raises cell by cell is the same geometry the hexagram pointer wears at that very hour. No other theme's profession makes its instrument with its own hands: the comb's six-sided cell is provably the most wax-efficient shape possible for storing the most honey with the least material, joy proven as engineering before it is felt as a mood.
