[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity — wax scales passed down a living chain to hands that will never sign their own name to the finished comb — and his vice is Excess, and the bees' own chemistry confirms it exactly: a colony must eat six to eight pounds of honey, all of it already gathered and stored by other bees, to secrete a single pound of wax. Generosity and Excess are, for a builder, literally the same appetite: the abundance a colony must first overspend before it can build anything to hold the next harvest.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because building only happens when the colony can afford it — a strong hive with a full honey flow raises new comb fast, a weak one raises none at all — so a Builder in Joy is also a colony announcing, cell by cell, that this is a good year. Straight down the axis at midnight waits the Scout, and the two correct each other as the canon requires: Wisdom curbs Excess, Generosity cures Greed, the honest report of what is out there tempering how much comb gets raised to hold it.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — the festoon's own working hour, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the very summit of human sight. The building hour keeps its Joy; light simply measures the hexagon's own efficiency instead of painting it gold.
variants.octa_paint: The Builder, hand-lowered down the living festoon chain, takes the mid-morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of the Priest, the Bell its object, and the comb rising beneath her hands is the closest thing a hive has to a temple: the hexagon she shapes cell by cell is proven the most efficient shape possible for storing what the colony will need to survive the year, a liturgy built in wax rather than chanted.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm turns spring green (#7CE577) — Childhood — and no color fits new comb better: freshly built cells are always paler than old ones, the youngest wax on the whole frame, growth made structural before it is even filled.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Builder stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the solstice — and shares it with the Queen herself. Architect beside mother on the year's highest spoke: the comb she lays eggs into and the comb he raises to hold them, built and filled on the very same clock.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Builder keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, and he shares it with the Forager, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The placement reads itself: comb built today is faith that a harvest is coming to fill it, raised on credit against a season not yet flown.

venus.name: Drone
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] The Drone rises in full vertical flight, stocky and broad, huge wraparound eyes meeting at the top of his head, wings a blur climbing toward a high bright gleam where a young queen flies far above him near the sun's own edge. Small at the plate's bottom, tiny and already distant, the hive entrance he will never re-enter. He is Venus' child and Friday's figure, and he is also the rarest thing on the whole dial: one of the very few males, on the one day given over entirely to love.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, Venus' own hour as the evening star. A drone does no work at all inside the hive; he is fed, groomed, tolerated for exactly one purpose, and every hour of his short life outside the mating season is simply waiting for the afternoon flight when the drone congregation gathers in open air and a queen, once, arrives among them.

[[The Lesson]] His whole existence resolves into Love the instant he catches her — mating itself kills him, his body torn away by the act, his one contribution to the colony's future paid in full and at once — and Jealousy is what surrounds that single moment rather than what follows it: hundreds of drones from a dozen hives converge on the same congregation area, all racing the same queen, and only one, ever, succeeds. The rest fly home unspent, or do not fly home at all: worker bees drive the unmated drones out of the hive at the season's end, stinging and starving what love could not use.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because that is when the queen mates and the season ends alike, the day's warmth spent before the year turns cold — and the drone's whole story argues something the other six professions only imply: that on this dial, giving everything for one perfect thing is not a tragedy the theme is embarrassed by, but the closest a bee ever comes to choosing her own life's meaning, even a bee who never had a choice.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the hour the mating flight actually happens.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is not a pigment but a wavelength, and a single flight burns in it completely.
variants.octa_paint: The Drone, climbing in full vertical flight toward a queen near the sun's edge, shares his evening arm with iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of the Soldier, the Sword its object, an odd estate for a bee with no sting at all, until the fight itself is remembered: hundreds of drones from a dozen hives race the same queen in the same congregation area, and only one, ever, wins her — a battle fought entirely with wings and altitude instead of blades.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel his arm turns ember (#FF6A3C) — the Elder, the harvest of a life — and the Drone wears it as his whole existence compressed into one instant: mating kills him outright, his single contribution paid in full and at once, a harvest of a life gathered in one afternoon rather than a season.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Drone stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the equinox — sharing the spoke with the Guard. Love beside war, as in every myth: autumn's red is both the season the mating flights taper off and the season the last robbing fights are fought at the door, harvest and blood and rose in one color.
variants.trio: The Drone does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is his own 20h in his own red, for love is his one and only virtue in both readings at once. The Guard joins him, and together they hold the evening from 16h to 24h — the lover and the defender of the same hive, each spending his whole body on it once.

saturn.name: Forager
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] The old Forager lands heavy on the hive's board in low slanting light, her wing edges visibly frayed and torn from a thousand flights, both hind legs loaded with full pollen baskets, her body worn dark and bald along the back where fuzz has rubbed away against a thousand flowers. Her long shadow stretches across the board toward the dark entrance. She is Saturn's child and Saturday's figure — and the oldest bee on the entire dial, in every sense the word allows.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning, the hour a forager rises with by habit rather than instruction, since nothing tells her to fly except the sun coming up and the flowers opening with it. Green fits a body that has spent its whole last career stage among growing things, pollen and nectar both harvested from a field that renews itself daily whether or not she is there to see it.

[[The Lesson]] Her color carries her virtue and her vice in one hue exactly as the canon requires: Patience is a forager who may fly miles for a single load and fly it a hundred times a season, waiting on flowers that open and close by their own clock, not hers; Envy is the sharper, uglier fact hive science calls robbing — when a dearth comes and a colony's own stores run thin, its foragers will find and raid a weaker neighbor's hive outright, guards fighting guards at the entrance over honey neither built. One color, two harvests, exactly like the arm itself.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because a forager's whole faith lives there, one more sunrise, one more flight — and the week's own shape closes on her: she is the last stage of the ladder that began with the Cleaner's first hour in her own birth-cell, and she will very likely die exactly as she worked, wings finally failing her mid-flight, in a field, having never once returned unspent. The week that opened with the queen's newest daughter closes with her oldest working sister, birth and death sharing the same seven days the way they share every single hive.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning flight — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns her morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than the field below it. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world the forager looks up at the weather before she looks down at the flowers.
variants.octa_paint: The old Forager, wings frayed from a thousand flights, takes the dawn arm in field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of the Farmer, the Plough its object, the one Walk on the wheel that fits her without a single translation: pollen and nectar gathered flower by flower across a whole last career stage already IS the Farmer's own estate, tilled with wings instead of a plough.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm turns predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — Birth — and the pairing closes the week's whole circle: the oldest bee on the dial takes the hue of its very beginning, a forager who rises each dawn on the same faith as any first flight, one more sunrise, one more chance to gather what the hive will need to raise its newest brood.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Forager stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox, sowing season and her own festival too, the flowers she has worked for weeks finally opening in earnest. She shares the spoke with the Nurse, grower and feeder together, the two patient castes that tend living things and wait on them.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes the Forager from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with the Builder, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. It is the oldest sermon a hive ever preaches without words: every flight out is faith performed with wings, gathered without knowing yet whether the colony will need it before winter closes the door.

sun.name: Matriarch · Memory
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] The Matriarch strides at the head of her family in weathered bronze relief, trunk raised high, ears spread wide like a crown, a gilded sun disc of rays low in relief directly behind her head; the whole column bends after her — cows, young ones, a small calf gripping its mother's tail with its own trunk. She is the Sun's child and Sunday's figure, and the fit needs no forcing: of the three animal societies on this dial, only the herd is ruled by MEMORY rather than muscle or caste — the leader is simply the one who remembers where the water was, thirty years ago, in a drought the others were not yet born to survive.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial she holds no arm at all, exactly as a ruler never does: her home is the center, colored white-gold, the sum of every hue the six arms carry. The column behind her makes in ivory and hide the same argument the star makes in geometry — six lives filed in behind one, bending to a point they did not choose but trust completely. A matriarch is not the strongest cow in the herd, nor the biggest; she is the one the file bends itself around, the way six colored arms only ever describe the white light they were split from.

[[The Lesson]] Her calling carries a virtue that argues with itself: Justice, the discipline of a leader who must choose the herd's one route through a landscape with no second chances, and Pride, the same certainty gone hard — an old matriarch's confidence that she remembers correctly, even the one time she does not. The gilded disc behind her head is glory made literal, the noon of a long authority; but glory earned over decades is also the likeliest pride to calcify, because it has so rarely been wrong before.

And so the center doubles, here more literally than anywhere else on the dial. Elephants return to the bones of their dead and touch the skulls with their trunks — documented behavior, not folklore — so the Sunday pair is not one face turned two ways but a living matriarch and the grave of the one before her: Memory, the herd standing in a still half-circle under an eclipsed sun while the young matriarch rests her trunk on the brow of the teacher who taught her the map. Glory at noon behind the living Matriarch's head, Awe over the bones of Memory — the throne and the grave of the throne, exactly as old as the herd's own habit of grief.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour — the herd's six lessons filing in behind the one figure who remembers all of them at once. This is the Matriarch's native reading: not a spoke but the point the spokes radiate from, the file's whole memory held in a single skull.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel redraws all six arms around her and cannot touch the center: it stays white-gold, because in the light world white genuinely is every color summed. The Matriarch's authority proves the same point in both worlds — she is not one wisdom among six, she is what the six add up to.
variants.octa_paint: The Matriarch steps from the dial's center onto the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of the King, the Crown its object, and the herd's whole file behind her already performs a Crown's own authority: six generations bending to the one who remembers where the water was found thirty years before any of them were born. The gilded sun disc behind her bronze head was a crown in relief before the wheel ever made it literal.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her noon arm is Youth, and the Matriarch wears the wheel's sun-yellow (#FFE800) — a curious hue for the herd's oldest authority until her own decades are remembered: a matriarch's confidence was built young, on a first crisis survived, and Youth here names the original memory her whole later Glory was built from.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Matriarch stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice — the year's longest light, the season a herd's memory is tested least, water everywhere. She shares the arm with the Mentor: the dial's two teaching elephants, matriarch and old tusker, holding the year's brightest spoke together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Matriarch keeps the center, and the court around her narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue. It is the oldest thing memory does: it is faith practiced without proof, carried forward on nothing but the conviction that the water will be where it was before.

faces.ruler:
The Ruler here wears the name Matriarch — the Sun's own calling, and the only profession on this dial's cousin themes that fits her without translation: she leads not by force but by accumulated knowing, thirty wet and dry seasons carried in one skull.

The bronze plate holds her mid-stride at the head of the file: trunk raised high, ears spread wide like a crown, a gilded sun disc low in relief directly behind her head, the herd bending into single column behind her — cows, young ones, a calf holding its mother's tail with its own small trunk, every line of the column obedient to her lead.

Her virtue is Justice, argued by the column itself — a route chosen for six lives at once, with the drought-year memory to back it. Her vice is Pride, the same authority hardened past the point of doubt; her mood is Glory, the gilded disc at the height of a long and unbroken correctness, noon for an elephant who has been right about the water for longer than anyone now living can remember being wrong.
faces.servant:
The Servant here is not a lesser twin of the Matriarch but her own history made visible: Memory, the remembered ruler, the matriarch who led before this one and taught her the map before dying of it — old ivory, worn molars, a life spent walking toward water only she recalled.

The bronze plate gathers the herd in a still half-circle around a weathered skull and long bones lying in the grass; the young matriarch stands at the center, resting the tip of her trunk gently on the skull's brow, and the others reach their own trunks toward the bones, touching lightly. High above, a thin eclipsed sun hangs in the dark sky — a black disc rimmed by a slender gilded corona, the mark carried by every dual plate on this dial. Nothing in the scene moves.

Her virtue is Humility, argued by the stillness itself — an entire living herd pausing its migration to stand over what is only bone. Her vice is Servility, the risk in any inherited authority: deferring to what the old matriarch would have done long after her actual knowledge has stopped applying. Her mood is Awe, the sun's light failed at exactly the hour a leader is lost — and Glory is simply the next plate over, carried now by the trunk resting on the skull instead of the head that once wore it.

moon.name: Allomother
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] The Allomother stands curved around a sleeping calf in weathered bronze relief, her body a wall between it and the night, her trunk draped loosely across its back; a full moon worked as a smooth polished disc rises above them, the rest of the herd reduced to dark sleeping shapes at the plate's edge. She is the Moon's child and Monday's figure, and the herd hands her the job without dispute — a young female not yet a mother of her own, standing the night watch over someone else's calf, precisely the care that gives 'allomothering' its name and its oldest home in this theme's whole claim: memory is a job the whole family shares, not just the one at its head.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn. No hour suits a night-watcher better: it is the stillest, darkest stretch, when the calf is most defenseless and the watcher must be steadiest, moonlight the only argument against the dark.

[[The Lesson]] The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear — the same night lit or unlit, and the image holds both: a young female standing guard alone in open country, where lions hunt, must feel exactly the fear she is not permitted to show. Straight across the dial at 16h stands the Musth, and the two cure each other: Courage cures Fear, Serenity quells Wrath — the calm watcher borrows the bull's nerve when the dark turns dangerous, and the raging bull, on his better days, borrows her steadiness.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because that is when it is hardest and worth the most: the last watch before light, when a tired young aunt must hold her post exactly as long as the moon does.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — the Allomother's night watch, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps her at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's hushed moonlight becomes light's electric one. The watch is unchanged; only the disc above the sleeping calf burns a little brighter.
variants.octa_paint: The Allomother, curved around a sleeping calf that is not her own, takes the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of the Scholar, the Book its object. Allomothering is itself an apprenticeship, a young female learning the herd's whole craft of care years before she raises a calf of her own — the Scholar's patient study, performed as a night-watch instead of a page.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel her arm turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the Unborn — and no figure on the dial sits it more plainly: she guards a calf that is not hers, practicing tomorrow's motherhood tonight, the Unborn's ancestors-and-not-yet held quite literally in her own curved trunk.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Allomother moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with the Elder. Watcher and rememberer on one spoke, and rightly: the young aunt who guards the calf tonight is the old cow who will remember the water forty years from now.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the Allomother at 04h but renames her watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with the Caller, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. No calling earns the word more plainly — the whole night watch is hope with a body wrapped around it, holding on until the sky changes.

mars.name: Musth
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] The Musth stands mid-fury in weathered bronze relief: head lowered, ears flared forward, dark temporin streaming in wet trails from the glands at both temples down his cheeks, one forefoot mid-stamp with dust exploding beneath it, trunk curled back over a raised tusk. He is Mars' child and Tuesday's figure, and the theme gives Mars its most literal body on the whole dial — musth is not a mood but a chemistry, hormone levels spiking many times their baseline, the most dangerous animal on the savanna running on a fury he did not choose and cannot fully steer.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon. The hour is right for him precisely because musth is not a single charge but weeks of sustained heat — the discipline, if it can be called that, of staying furious for a working season rather than a single dawn.

[[The Lesson]] His two faces sit in the one gland: temporin is Wrath made visible, streaming down a face that in every other season is placid; but musth is also the surest sign of a bull in his prime — the confidence to challenge, to breed, to hold ground — which is Courage's own chemistry, differently spent. Straight across the dial at 04h waits the Allomother, and the two cure each other: Serenity quells Wrath, Courage cures Fear — her steadiness is the only thing in the herd a musth bull will sometimes, briefly, still respect.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is exactly musth's own season — not a dawn's outburst but weeks of banked, working fire, the discipline of a fury that must be carried rather than merely felt.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the banked fire of the base article, weeks of musth rather than one furious hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — in light, red and green fused. The temporin still streams; Newton's reading only makes the working fire glow hotter.
variants.octa_paint: The Musth bull, temporin streaming from his glands mid-fury, shares his working-afternoon arm with copper (#C87533) — the Walk of the Merchant, the Coin its object, the hardest turn on the whole dial for a body running on pure hormonal war. Yet musth is also precisely what raises a bull's standing to breed and hold ground — status the herd trades in as surely as any coin, dominance itself the currency the Coin's Walk was always describing.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel his arm turns amber (#FFB400) — Maturity — and the hue names musth exactly: not a young bull's single tantrum but weeks of banked, working fury, the confidence of a bull finally old enough to challenge, breed, and hold his ground.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Musth stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Reunion. Bull and greeting-cow on the same arm, the oldest pairing on the dial reread: autumn's red is blood and rose in one color, temporin and the trunk-embrace both belonging to it.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Musth moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside the Reunion, and the pair guard the evening from 16h to 24h. It explains the fury better than fear does: musth exists because reproduction matters enough to run this hot for — courage wearing a hormone instead of armor.

mercury.name: Caller
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] The Caller stands utterly still in weathered bronze relief, ears spread to their fullest, trunk hanging to the ground, one forefoot lifted and pressed flat — listening through her feet. From beneath her, engraved concentric rings radiate outward through the cracked earth to the plate's far edge, where a distant tiny herd on the horizon lifts its head as the message arrives. She is Mercury's child and Wednesday's figure, and the theme rewrites Hermes without losing him: the messenger god's word no longer rides winged sandals, it rides the GROUND itself, infrasound too low for the human ear traveling for kilometers through bone and earth alike.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. The hour fits the physics as much as the myth: the ground carries a rumble farthest when the world above it has gone still and cool, the same quiet that makes midnight the hour honest news travels — a call for help, a warning about poachers, word that the far waterhole finally failed.

[[The Lesson]] The listening foot and the spread ears are the whole ethics of the arm at once: Wisdom is knowing which rumble matters and which to let pass unanswered; Greed is the temptation to keep a voice this powerful for one herd only — to call the good grazing toward your own family and let word of a rival's drought go unspoken. Straight up the axis at noon stands the Mentor, and the two correct each other: Generosity cures Greed, Wisdom curbs Excess — the old tusker's open teaching answers the closed ear, the careful listener steadies the overgenerous table.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because the ground's own honesty rules it too: the hour the world is quiet enough to hear the truth, whatever the truth happens to be that night.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of a call that travels farthest when the world goes still — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns her purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, invented by the eye where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for a message human senses cannot hear at all: at midnight the Caller wears a color that, like her rumble, exists just past where ordinary perception reaches.
variants.octa_paint: The Caller, ears spread and one forefoot pressed to the ground, shares her midnight arm with stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of the Artist, the Mask its object. Infrasound too low for any human ear is its own performance, a message composed entirely in vibration and sent through bone and earth alike to a herd on a horizon she cannot see — Hermes's winged word, restaged as a rumble underfoot.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel that hour deepens to dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — Old Age — and the Caller wears it as long practice: knowing which rumble is worth sending and which to let pass unanswered is a wisdom built only after years of listening through the ground with her own feet.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Caller keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and she alone shares it with no one. The year's midnight belongs to a single figure: the one voice that can stand at the bottom of the wheel, entirely alone, and still be heard for kilometers.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the Caller off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Allomother, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names what the call actually is: every rumble sent into the ground is hope with a destination, sound thrown toward a herd that may or may not still be listening.

jupiter.name: Mentor
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] The Mentor stands immovable in weathered bronze relief, a huge old bull with long weathered tusks laying his trunk firmly across the shoulders of a rearing adolescent bull, pressing him back down to earth — the lesson, not the punishment — while two more young bulls watch subdued at his side. He is Jupiter's child and Thursday's figure: Guruvar, the day of Brihaspati who IS Jupiter, the guru of the gods, and the bachelor herd's own school is the closest thing the savanna has to a classroom.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter'. The image argues the placement without needing help: an old bull's bulk alone teaches half the lesson, making the youngsters at his side look half-grown before he has said or done anything at all — presence as the first and oldest form of instruction.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity — years of accumulated calm laid across a stranger's calf rather than spent defending his own status — and his vice is Excess, the same bulk that teaches so easily also apt to dominate rather than mentor, discipline curdling into simple size winning by intimidation. Straight down the axis at midnight sits the Caller, and each cures the other: Wisdom curbs Excess, and Generosity cures Greed — the far-carrying rumble below teaches proportion to the mentor's overwhelming scale.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is the one hour that needs no lamp: the bachelor school in full daylight, an old bull's patience on full display, asking nothing back for the lesson it gives.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — the base reading, the bachelor school in full daylight.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. The lesson keeps its Joy; light simply measures the old bull's presence instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: The Mentor, laying his trunk across a rearing adolescent's shoulders, takes the mid-morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of the Priest, the Bell its object, a seat his own bulk had already half-built: an old bull's mere presence teaches half the lesson before he says or does anything, the bachelor herd's whole schoolyard run on a patience close to liturgy.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel his arm turns spring green (#7CE577) — Childhood — and the Mentor wears it not for himself but for his pupils: the young bulls pressed gently back to earth are the actual green growth his whole calm patience exists to raise.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mentor stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with the Matriarch. Teacher beside leader on the year's highest spoke: the herd's two memories, one for the file and one for the bachelor school, holding the brightest light together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Mentor keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, his own working material, and he shares the arm with the Elder as they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. The placement reads itself: teaching a rearing bull to stand down is faith that the lesson outlasts the fury, taught over and over until it does.

venus.name: Reunion
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Two elephant cows meet mid-rush in weathered bronze relief: heads high, foreheads nearly touching, trunks wound tightly around each other, ears flapping wide, mouths open mid-rumble, their two families surging together around them, calves squeezing between legs. She is Venus' child and Friday's figure, and the greeting ceremony is the herd's own love letter — documented in the field for as long as elephants have been watched: bonded relatives separated for weeks or months, running to close the last distance, thundering and entwining as if the parting had been a wound.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, when Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour suits the ceremony exactly: reunions on the savanna cluster at day's soft edges, the heat broken, the families closing ground toward each other as the light turns warm.

[[The Lesson]] The entwined trunks are her virtue and her vice in one gesture: Love, argued by every line of the composition, and Jealousy — the fear, real in elephant families, that a bond this strong could be crowded out by a new calf, a new ally, a rival matriarch's family merging too close. Across the dial at 08h stands the Elder, and the two heal each other: Patience heals Jealousy, and Love dissolves Envy — the old cow teaches the reuniting family to wait out a slight, and the reunion teaches the elder that what she has is still hers to celebrate.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because that is when the herd's emotional accounts come due: the day's walking and foraging behind them, nothing left to do at dusk but recognize who has come home.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the trunks winding tight as the light turns warm.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps her at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. The rumble and the entwined trunks stay exactly as loud; Newton's reading only sharpens the color of the joy.
variants.octa_paint: The reuniting cows, trunks wound tight and families surging together, share their evening arm with iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of the Soldier, the Sword its object, an unlikely turn for an embrace until the ceremony's own intensity is weighed: two families thundering together after weeks apart is fought for with exactly the urgency of a charge, relief and reunion closing distance the way an army closes a line.
variants.octa_light: On the Ages wheel their arm turns ember (#FF6A3C) — the Elder, the harvest of a life — and the reunion wears it honestly: what closes in that embrace is the gathered value of a whole relationship, months of separation harvested back into one thundering, trunk-wound moment.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Reunion stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Musth. Greeting-cow beside raging bull, the oldest pairing on the dial reread through fur and temporin: autumn's red is harvest and blood and rose at once, and it takes both figures to say the whole of it.
variants.trio: The Reunion does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red, for love is her virtue in both sets at once. The Musth joins her, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the two most physically demonstrative bodies on the dial, one embracing, one raging, both simply love with the volume turned all the way up.

saturn.name: Elder
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] The Elder kneels in a bone-dry riverbed in weathered bronze relief: sunken temples, hollowed cheeks, one tusk broken short, digging a water hole with her trunk and forefoot — and finding it, a dark gleam of water rising in the sand exactly where she alone remembered it would be, the thirsty herd waiting behind her in a patient line, a leafless dead tree at the plate's edge. She is Saturn's child and Saturday's figure, and no image on the whole dial states the theme's founding claim more plainly: an elephant's life is measured in six sets of molars, and when the last set wears down, she dies — the teeth are her own hourglass, and the old ones know where the water sleeps precisely because they have run out of time to be wrong about it.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning. The color argues past its own irony: green belongs to growing things, and an Elder digging in a colorless drought is renewal's driest possible test, proof that patience outlasts even a landscape that has stopped rewarding it.

[[The Lesson]] Her one color holds her virtue and her vice at once, exactly as it does for every body on this arm: green is growth — Patience, decades spent carrying a memory that a wet season always eventually pays out — and green is envy's own bitter shade, the temptation of an old cow who has earned the herd's trust to resent how easily the young ones now spend it, following her without ever having doubted the water would come. Across the dial at 20h stands the Reunion, and each cures the other: Love dissolves Envy, and Patience heals Jealousy — the reuniting family reminds the elder that being trusted is still a form of being loved, and the elder teaches the reunion that anything worth having was worth this long a wait.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because that is when a dug well is finally judged: the water either rises with first light or it does not, and either way the Elder's whole authority — the teeth worn down for it — was staked on remembering correctly.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning — the base reading, water rising in the sand exactly where memory said it would.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns her morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than of the cracked field. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world the Elder looks up at the sky she is reading for rain before she looks down at the ground she is digging.
variants.octa_paint: The Elder, kneeling to dig a water hole she alone remembers, takes the dawn arm in field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of the Farmer, the Plough its object, the plainest match on the whole dial: she tills dry sand exactly as a Farmer tills dry ground, both trusting a memory of rain that has not yet arrived.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel her arm turns predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — Birth — and no elder on the dial earns it more sharply: the water either rises with the first light or it does not, her whole worn authority staked, at the very hour of Birth, on a memory that must still be right.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Elder stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: sowing season, water finally easy again. She shares the spoke with the Allomother — the herd's two patient callings, one tending a sleeping calf through the night, one tending the whole family's memory through the drought.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes the Elder from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with the Mentor, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The pairing is the oldest sermon the herd has: digging for water you cannot see yet is faith performed with a trunk, believed in decades before it is proven right again.

sun.name: Alpha · Omega
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] The Alpha stands where every real wolf pack's founding pair stands — not a tyrant crowned by force but the parent who leads because the pack's whole structure runs through him: den, hunt, and the order pups are fed. The medallion casts him exactly so: weathered bronze relief on cracked dark stone, the alpha wolf braced atop a high rock outcrop, head lifted, chest out, tail raised like a banner over the pack below — and behind his head the rising sun is worked as a gilded disc of low-relief rays, a crown he did not ask for and cannot set down. Beneath the outcrop the rest of the pack tilts its muzzles upward, watching. He is the Sun's creature and Sunday's figure — the one body on the dial that needed no invented crown, only the one the morning already wears.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial he takes no arm at all, the same as every other reading of the center: his color is white-gold, belonging to no single hour because every hour of the pack's day answers to him eventually — the hunt he calls, the rest he permits, the den he guards. It is worth naming, here, the wolf the internet invented and ethology never did: the Sigma, a wolf supposedly equal to the Alpha but standing outside the hierarchy altogether, a ruler without a throne. No pack has ever produced one and no plate was carved for him — he belongs to modern myth-making, not to the forest, and the dial leaves him exactly where he belongs: unrepresented, a rumor the Alpha's own medallion outlives.

[[The Lesson]] Real wolf biology is gentler than the old dominance stories: an alpha is usually just a parent, and the authority he carries is closer to Justice than to conquest — the fair order of the den, the hunt shared so the whole pack eats, the pups defended without exception. That is the virtue the crown of sun-rays argues for. But the very word 'alpha' was corrupted once by captive-wolf studies into a myth of constant combat for rank, and that myth is Pride's exact shape: the throne mistaken for something won and defended rather than a duty simply carried. The rock outcrop he stands on is real height, not borrowed — but height still tempts whoever stands on it to believe the ground below owes him the view.

The center is DOUBLE, as it is on every dial theme, and here the doubling writes itself onto the clock face more plainly than anywhere else: the ring letters already spell it, M at noon and Ω at midnight, Alpha's own initial handed the sunlit seat and Omega's the dark one. The Omega plate answers the Alpha's precisely — body lowered, ears folded, tail curled into a faint echo of his own Greek letter, carrying a bone to two pups at the den mouth while the rest of the pack feeds, unwatched, on the kill he left them first. High over his lowered head hangs a thin eclipsed sun, a black disc rimmed in a slender gilded corona — the same crown the Alpha wears, gone dark. His virtue is Humility, the crown surrendered before it was ever claimed; his vice, should the servant's calm curdle, is Servility, service without the dignity real omega wolves keep even at the bottom of the order — for the pups love him best, and his eyes in the plate stay level, not cowed. Justice straightens Servility and Humility cures Pride, the axis crossing inside this one seat exactly as it crosses on every other Sunday reading — Glory at noon when the rayed sun crowns him, Awe at midnight when the same disc goes dark and the corona is all that is left of it.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel the Alpha keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are his light decomposed, every hour's mood a piece of what the pack's leader answers for. This is his home reading — the source, not a spoke, the rock outcrop rather than any one arm of the trail.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around him but cannot change him: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world it is literally true that white is the sum of all colors. Newton's reading only proves what the rock outcrop already claimed — the crown of rays was never one hue among six.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Alpha holds the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420) — the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — and no seat fits him more plainly: the medallion already crowned him in the sun's own gilded rays, the leader who rules not by force but because the whole pack's order runs through him. 'By me kings reign' could be the outcrop's own motto, the throne hour finally wearing the throne's own metal instead of borrowing it from the sunrise behind him.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, the zenith of a life, and the Alpha wears the wheel's own sun-yellow (#FFE800). No wolf in the pack fits the blazing height of Youth better than the one whose whole authority is a young pack's daily engine — strength at its peak, spent generously rather than hoarded, before any thought of the descent the Elder already knows.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Alpha stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice — the year's longest daylight, the height any lookout could ask for. He shares the arm with Beta: leader and teacher on one spoke, the pack's first rank and its second holding the year's brightest hour together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Alpha keeps the center, and the pack around him narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue, the painter's primaries. It is the oldest reading of leadership made plain: a leader holds only what radiates outward from him, and all three virtues are his white-gold light taken apart.

faces.ruler:
Alpha is the Ruler face by the oldest right there is in a pack — not a crown handed down but a crown that is simply where the den's authority happens to sit, carried by whichever parent the pack's survival currently runs through.

The medallion sets him atop a high rock outcrop in weathered bronze relief on dark cracked stone, head lifted, chest out, tail raised like a banner, the rising sun worked as a gilded disc of rays in low relief directly behind his head like a crown he did not choose to wear; below the outcrop the rest of the pack lifts its muzzles toward him. The border runs its circling trail of wolf paw-prints and pine sprigs, four small roundels stamped with the Sun glyph.

His virtue is Justice — the fair order of a real pack's hunt and den, argued by the pack gazing up at him rather than cowering from him, authority as something practiced rather than merely held. That same height tempts Pride, the rock outcrop's real elevation mistaken for something he is owed rather than a duty he happens to be standing over. His mood is Glory, the sun's rayed crown blazing whole behind his head — noon's own triumph, the pack fed and watching, nothing yet asked of him that he has not already given.
faces.servant:
Omega is the Ruler's own other face — not a rival wolf but the pack's own lowest member, the same hierarchy read from its opposite end, proof the canon has always called this figure double rather than needing a second name for it. Real ethology gives the servant wolf real dignity: the omega often keeps the peace, defuses the pack's tension, and is loved by the pups precisely because he never postures.

The plate matches Alpha's bronze relief and cracked-slate stone exactly, the same running trail of paw-prints and pine sprigs at the border, four roundels still bearing the Sun glyph — but the scene has come down off the rock: Omega's body is lowered, ears folded back, tail curled close enough that its sweep traces a faint omega curve, a bone carried in his jaws to two pups waiting at the den mouth, one already clambering over his shoulder, the rest of the pack small and distant, feeding at the kill he left them first. High over his bowed head hangs a thin eclipsed sun, a black disc rimmed by a slender gilded corona — the only bright metal left on the whole darkened bronze.

His virtue is Humility, the crown never claimed and the kill never touched until the pups and the pack have eaten — but that same lowered posture is stalked by Servility, obedience with the dignity bled out of it, the omega reduced to nothing but the pack's cushion instead of its quiet keeper. His mood is Awe, the crowned sun gone dark overhead — and Glory is simply the next plate over: the same crown, the same rock, waiting for whichever wolf the den's order asks to carry it next.

moon.name: Luna
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Luna is the cleanest link this theme makes, and the plate does not hedge it: weathered bronze relief on cracked stone, a she-wolf utterly alone on a cliff edge — no pack anywhere on the medallion, a deliberate emptiness — muzzle lifted to the sky mid-howl, her breath rising as a faint mist worked into the relief, and above her the full moon cut as a large, smooth, polished disc with no rays at all, the quiet opposite of Alpha's crowned sun. Pine tops fall away far below the cliff. She is the Moon's creature and Monday's figure, and the border keeps its running trail of paw-prints and pine sprigs even here, four small roundels stamped with the crescent glyph.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 04h in deep pigment blue (#002FFF), the mood the wheel calls Calm — the still, moonlit hour before dawn. No wolf image earns a color assignment more honestly: this is the hour real wolves actually howl in, the pre-dawn stillness when a lone voice carries farthest and costs the least to answer.

The howling lone wolf is folklore's oldest wolf cliché, and this theme does not apologize for using it — it simply corrects it. A howling wolf on a cliff is not baying at grief; she is locating her pack, or being located by it, across miles of country where scent runs out and sound does not. Solitude, for a real wolf, is a working condition, not a wound — dispersers travel alone for months finding new territory, and the howl is how alone stays in contact with together. The moon she calls to gives nothing back but light; the pack she is really calling does.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Serenity — the stillness of a creature entirely sure of her own voice, needing no chorus to be heard — and her vice is Fear, the shadow side of every solitary hour: the cliff edge is real, the miles between her and the den are real, and not every howl is answered. Straight across the dial at 16h stands the Hunter, and the two cure each other exactly as the canon requires: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath — the calm voice on the cliff steadies the burning one at the kill, and the hunter's nerve is what lets the lone wolf's howl go out unafraid.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — the cliff-edge howl, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps her at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's muted night becomes light's electric one. Same cliff, same Calm; the moon above her simply burns colder and clearer.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Luna's cliff-edge arm turns lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — an unlikely seat for a howling wolf until the hour is weighed: hers is the deep-night voice that reads the wind and the miles the way a scholar reads a page, patient study rather than blind cry.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the same deep-night arm is the Unborn, ancestors and the not-yet-born together, and Luna's coat turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — fitting for a howl that answers no living voice at all, only the pack that might be out there and the pack that once was, both held in the same silvered call across the dark.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Luna moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with the Elder. The lone howler and the old watcher on one spoke, and rightly: both keep a vigil the rest of the pack sleeps through, and both are proven right only by morning.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Luna at 04h but renames her watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with the Scout, and it guards the morning from 0h to 8h. No wolf on the dial earns the word more honestly — hope is what a howl actually is, a voice sent out believing the pack is close enough to hear it.

mars.name: Hunter
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] The Hunter is the pack's warrior at the one moment that matters — not a march or a howl but the leap itself: weathered bronze relief on cracked stone, a powerful wolf at full stretch mid-air through deep snow, jaws open, every muscle taut, closing on a great elk that has turned at bay, antlers lowered to meet him, snow bursting from both their strides in the same instant. He is Mars' creature and Tuesday's figure, the red planet's oldest calling handed to the pack member whose work is danger. The border keeps its paw-print trail and pine sprigs, four roundels stamped with the Mars glyph, and the only bright accent on the whole dark bronze is a single ember-red gleam worked into the hunter's eye.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon, fire banked to a heat you can actually hunt by. The placement is exact rather than romantic: this is not the dawn ambush of legend but the long grinding pursuit that real wolf hunts actually are, run down over miles before the final closing leap the medallion freezes.

An elk turning at bay is not a helpless animal — a set of antlers and a well-placed hoof kill wolves every season, and real wolf packs choose their target with exactly the caution the plate's held breath suggests: not the strongest animal in the herd, but the one whose weakness has already been read. The Hunter's courage is calculated, not reckless, which is the whole difference between a warrior and a brawler.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Courage — the leap taken anyway, against an animal that can kill him — and his vice is Wrath, the same taut muscle and open jaw with the calculation burned out of it, aggression for its own sake rather than the pack's next meal. Straight across the dial at 04h waits Luna, and the two heal each other: Serenity quells Wrath, and Courage cures Fear — the hunter's nerve is what lets the lone howl go out unafraid, and the cliff's calm is what keeps the leap from becoming a rampage.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the banked fire of the chase, the base article's own leap.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the Hunter stays at 16h but the ember flares to pure yellow — pigment's orange has no seat in the prism wheel, so the afternoon chase burns at light's brightest hue instead. Zeal, only hotter.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Hunter's leap lands on the 15h arm in copper (#C87533), the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — a strange trade for a warrior until the hunt is counted honestly: every calculated leap is itself a transaction, an elk's life weighed against the pack's fed winter, the coin here paid in meat rather than metal.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same afternoon arm is Maturity, and the Hunter wears the wheel's own amber (#FFB400) — the color fits him better than any other body could claim it: this is no dawn cub's ambush but a grown wolf's calculated strength, courage seasoned by miles already run and elk already read correctly.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Hunter takes the 18h dusk arm, red for Autumn, and finally wears his own color — sharing the arm with the Mate, as the pack's warrior and its bonded pair have always shared the same protection. Chase and devotion on one arm, pointing at the autumn equinox, the year's long evening.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves the Hunter to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside the Mate — each arm's tip centers its third of the day, and this pair guards the evening from 16h to 24h. The Hunter finally wears his own red, and Love reads through him as protection: courage keeping watch so that what is loved can sleep.

mercury.name: Scout
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] The Scout works alone and ahead, and the plate holds nothing but him: weathered bronze relief on cracked stone, a lean, long-legged wolf alone on a windswept ridge, one forepaw raised mid-step, nose lifted high into the wind, ears pricked forward. A winding line of his own paw tracks trails behind him in low relief, and far below the ridge, tiny with distance, the rest of the pack sits waiting for whatever signal he decides to give. He is Mercury's creature and Wednesday's figure — messenger and pathfinder, the exact job description Hermes has always carried, handed here to the pack member whose legs are built for it.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at the dial's bottom, midnight, 0h, in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. It is a stranger fit than it first looks: midnight is the accounting hour, and the scout is the one who has actually walked the ground the pack will trust by morning — his information is the currency the whole night's decision runs on, weighed out alone in the dark before anyone else has to.

Real dispersing wolves and scouting pack members read country the way a merchant reads a market — wind for scent, snow for tracks, silence for danger — and the intelligence gathered on a solitary ridge is worth exactly what it is honestly reported. A scout who exaggerates the good ground or hides the bad one is not protecting the pack; he is hoarding what was never his to keep.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Wisdom — reading the wind and the tracks for what they actually say, not what would flatter him to report — and his vice is Greed, the temptation to keep the good find to himself a little longer than the pack needs him to. Straight up the axis at 12h stands Beta, and the two correct each other: Generosity cures Greed, and Wisdom curbs Excess — the teacher's open hand answers the scout's closed one, and the scout's careful reading of the ground keeps the teacher's table from ever promising more than the land can give.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of the day's ground fully walked and fully reported — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for the pathfinder: at midnight he wears a color that exists only in the reading he alone did, wind and track turned to knowledge no one else walked far enough to gather.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Scout's windswept ridge turns to the 21h evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — an odd trade for a creature who works entirely alone, except that every scout's reading of the wind is itself a kind of performance staged for an audience of one pack waiting below, the mask being the scout's own unreadable face while he decides what signal to give.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the same evening arm is Old Age, and the Scout's coat turns dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — a fitting hue for the pack member whose whole worth is accumulated experience, the miles of wind and track already read, wisdom carried the way an old wolf carries scars rather than youth's raw legs.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Scout keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and he alone shares it with no one. The year's midnight belongs to a single figure: the one wolf on the dial built to walk alone in the dark and still bring back a true report.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the Scout off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Luna, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names the pathfinder's true engine: every scouting run is hope with a direction on it — the ground read alone in sorrow at midnight becomes, by morning, the road the whole pack trusts.

jupiter.name: Beta
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Beta is the pack's second seat, and the plate reads exactly like the role: weathered bronze relief on cracked stone, a broad-chested wolf standing calm and immovable, his head bowed without menace over a juvenile rolled belly-up in play-discipline beneath him — the correction of a teacher, not the punishment of a tyrant. Two more yearlings sit in an orderly file to the side, watching the lesson rather than fearing it. He is Jupiter's creature and Thursday's figure — the second-in-rank, teacher and regent, exactly the king-teacher role Brihaspati holds among the Vedic gods on this same day.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright noon yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter'. The color explains the composition: discipline given generously, without cruelty, is itself a kind of gift, and the yearling on the ground already looks more amused than afraid — correction as instruction, not as fear.

A real Beta wolf is often the one who actually raises the litter closest, second only to the breeding pair, patient with young that test every boundary they can find. That patience under provocation is the whole art of the second rank: enough authority to correct, never so much that correction turns into cruelty.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity — the file of yearlings taught rather than merely dominated, discipline handed down as a gift — and his vice is Excess, the second-in-command's own oldest temptation: the teacher's authority curdling into the tyrant's, correction overused until it stops teaching anything. Straight down the axis at 0h sits the Scout, and each cures the other: Wisdom curbs Excess, and Generosity cures Greed — the scout's careful reading of what the pack actually needs keeps Beta's discipline from overreaching, and Beta's open-handed teaching keeps the scout's private knowledge from staying private.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — the teacher's own hour, the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. Beta's hour keeps its Joy; light simply measures the lesson's peak instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Beta's teaching arm turns alb ivory (#EDEDE0), the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a natural seat: the second-in-rank who corrects a yearling without cruelty is already the pack's own clergy, ringing discipline the way a bell calls a congregation rather than driving it.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same morning arm is Childhood, and Beta wears the wheel's spring green (#7CE577) — exact fitness rather than irony: no wolf on the dial stands closer to the pack's actual children than the one whose whole calling is raising the litter, patient with a Childhood that tests every boundary it can find.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Beta stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with the Alpha himself. Teacher beside leader on the year's highest spoke: the pack's first rank and its second, holding the brightest hour together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Beta keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, his own working material, and he shares the arm with the Elder as they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. The placement reads itself: faith is what a teacher hands down to yearlings who cannot yet see why the lesson matters.

venus.name: Mate
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] The Mate carries the theme's one image with no hierarchy in it at all: weathered bronze relief on cracked stone, two wolves standing chest to chest, necks crossed, each head resting along the other's shoulder, eyes closed, tails wrapped around one another — no ornament, no myth, only the crossed-neck embrace on a quiet rise of stone. He is Venus' creature and Friday's figure, and the plate earns the day honestly: wolves bond for life, and this theme draws that fact without the kitsch a lesser telling would reach for.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, Venus' own hour as the evening star. The composition fits without strain: this is the hour the day's working structure softens into its private one, and a real breeding pair's bond is the structural fact the whole pack's order is quietly built on.

Wolf monogamy is not sentiment layered on top of biology — the breeding pair's stability is the pack's actual foundation, the den the whole hierarchy protects, the reason an Alpha leads at all. Two wolves standing still, doing nothing but leaning on each other, are doing the most structurally important thing in the entire pack.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Love — the crossed necks, the closed eyes, trust total enough to stop watching the horizon — and his vice is Jealousy, love's own shadow rather than its opposite: the mate-guarding aggression a real bonded pair can turn on any wolf, even their own, who strays too near the den. Straight across the dial at 08h stands the Elder, and the two heal each other: Patience heals Jealousy, and Love dissolves Envy — the old wolf's stillness teaches the bonded pair to trust the waiting, and their devotion teaches the old wolf that what he built is still being tended.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the crossed-neck embrace's own hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is not a pigment but a wavelength, and the bonded pair's evening burns in it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Mate's crossed-neck embrace shifts to the 18h sunset arm in iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — an edge this gentle image does not obviously wear, until the mate-guarding is remembered: a bonded pair defends its den and its partner with exactly a soldier's ferocity, the crimson naming the jealousy their love can turn to blade.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that sunset arm belongs to the Elder, the harvest of a life, and the Mate's coat deepens to ember (#FF6A3C) — fitting, since two wolves who have crossed necks year after year are themselves a harvest, the years of devotion gathered into the one bond a pack's whole structure was quietly built to protect.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mate stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Hunter. Devotion beside courage, as in every real pack: autumn's red is harvest and hunt and hearth at once, and it takes both figures to say so.
variants.trio: The Mate does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is his own 20h in his own red, for love is his virtue in both sets at once. The Hunter joins him, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the den's devotion and the kill's courage over the same dark hours, one keeping what is loved fed, the other keeping it safe.

saturn.name: Elder
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] The Elder sits apart, and the plate does not soften the distance: weathered bronze relief on cracked stone, an old wolf, muzzle frosted white, one ear torn by ancient scars, seated alone on a low flat stone beneath a bare winter tree, perfectly still, watching the distant pack at play — long shadows stretching from their feet toward him across the snow. He is Saturn's creature and Saturday's figure, the old gray wolf whose calling is patience and time, watched from just outside the game he can no longer join.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 08h in harvest green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning, the day of rest. It is a hard color for a figure this still to earn, and the medallion earns it anyway: renewal belongs to the pack playing in the distance, not to him directly, and his stillness is what makes room for it.

Real wolf packs age their elders out of the hunt long before the hunt is done with them — an old wolf's teeth wear down, his stride slows, and the pack's survival increasingly does not include him at the kill. What remains is a kind of watching that asks nothing back: the shadows reach him, but he does not reach for them.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Patience — decades folded into one still posture on a cold stone, no urgency left to perform — and his vice is Envy, green in its bitterest key: the temptation, watching a game he can no longer play, to begrudge the pack the very youth he once had and can no longer claim. Straight across the dial at 20h stands the Mate, and each is the other's cure: Love dissolves Envy, and Patience heals Jealousy — the devotion of the bonded pair is what an old wolf's stillness was always protecting, and his patience is the proof that what they built outlasts either of them.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning — the pack at play he watches from the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than of the field. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world the old wolf looks up at the sky before he looks down at the game he can no longer join.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Elder's stone seat turns to the dawn arm, 06h, in field green (#3E8914), the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — a fitting trade for stillness: a Farmer's whole work is patience sown into ground he may not live to harvest, precisely the discipline of an old wolf who tends a pack's future from the sidelines rather than the game.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and the Elder wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — a pointed irony rather than a comfort: the oldest body on the dial seated at the youngest hour of a life, his own frosted muzzle a reminder that every Birth the pack celebrates is itself the beginning of the same long arc he is nearly done walking.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Elder stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: the season the pack's next litter is born. He shares the spoke with Luna — the old watcher and the lone howler together, the two patient figures who keep a vigil the rest of the pack sleeps through.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes the Elder from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Beta, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The pairing is the oldest lesson in the pack — patience is faith performed with stillness, every season endured a wager that what was built outlasts the one who built it.

sun.name: Ancient of Days · Son
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] The seat is doubled the way scripture itself doubles it: Daniel's Ancient of Days, robed white as snow with hair like pure wool, seated on a throne of fire — and John's Christ at the same supper table, on his knees with a towel at his waist, washing a disciple's feet. One throne, two testaments, and the dial keeps them as one seat because the Bible already tells the story that way: the king who is also the servant, majesty answered by its own kneeling.

[[The Plate]] The plate reads them side by side. The Ancient of Days sits enthroned in white-gold glass, hair and robe wool-white, the throne itself blazing fire-glass on wheels of burning flame, streams of golden light pouring outward over countless small ministering figures at his feet, an open book of amber glass laid across his knees. Its answering plate is deliberately quiet: the same throne stands empty and unlit behind a kneeling Christ in plain ivory glass, an outer garment folded aside, a disciple's bare foot resting in a simple silver basin, a folded towel over one arm, eyes lowered — the crown never shown at all, because in this plate it has already been set down.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial this figure holds no arm: home is the center, white-gold, the sum of every hue the six spokes carry. As the Ancient of Days he practices Justice, argued by the small book held open on his knee rather than clutched shut — but that same throne is stalked by Pride, the crown mistaking itself for the light rather than its seat. As the Son he practices Humility, the crown set down before it was ever contested — but the same kneeling is stalked by Servility, obedience with no will behind it. The canon's own scale of self runs between two named extremes on either side of this seat, and scripture puts a third point at its exact zero: not the man who hates himself into the pit, but the one who gives himself away out of love while never once denying who he is — the same hands that could have held a scepter instead hold a basin.

[[The Hour]] That is also why this figure keeps no hour. Every mood of the day passes through the throne without settling into it: Glory at noon, when the fire-glass throne blazes whole, and Awe when the light fails and the same disc goes dark over a bowed head — the crown always the next plate over, waiting to be worn again.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel this seat keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are the throne's own light taken apart, each mood — Zeal, Sorrow, Joy, Passion, Renewal, Calm — a piece of the glory the throne holds whole. This is the home reading: the fire at the center of the window, not a lancet along its rim.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around the throne but cannot change it: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world white is, quite literally, every color at once. Newton's spectrum only proves in the laboratory what Daniel's vision already saw in fire — a light too whole to be one hue among six.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the throne steps onto the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420), the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — Daniel's Ancient of Days needs no fresh argument for this seat, robed already in the color the wheel now names outright: 'by me kings reign' (Proverbs 8:15), the throne hour finally wearing its own metal.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, the zenith of a life, and the same seat wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) — fitting less for the ancient robe than for the kneeling Christ across the same figure, an eternal life read at its blazing height rather than its ending, strength offered rather than spent before any thought of descent.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross this seat stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice. It shares the arm with Solomon — throne and temple on one spoke, the Ancient of Days and the wisest of kings holding the year's longest light together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the throne keeps the center, and the court around it narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue. It is the oldest reading of this seat made plain: a throne that gives its light away holds nothing but what radiates outward from it, and all three virtues are the Ancient of Days' own white-gold light, taken apart.

faces.ruler:
The Ancient of Days is Sunday's own seat before the dial ever asked it to share the throne — Daniel's vision of him seated, 'his clothing was white as snow, the hair of his head like pure wool, his throne was fiery flames' (Daniel 7:9), the oldest image scripture has of authority that does not need to raise its voice.

The plate sets him exactly so: enthroned in white-gold glass on a throne of blazing fire-glass, wheels of burning flame beneath it, streams of golden light pouring outward over countless small ministering figures rendered tiny at his feet, an open book of glowing amber glass laid across his knees.

His virtue is Justice, argued by that same open book — held out to be read rather than sealed shut, a judgment made in the light rather than behind it — but the same throne is stalked by Pride, majesty forgetting it is meant to radiate rather than to be simply admired. His mood is Glory, the fire-glass throne blazing whole behind him: noon's own triumph, nothing yet asked of him that the flame has not already given freely.
faces.servant:
The Son as Servant is the Ancient of Days' own other face, not a separate throne but the same authority read at its lowest point — John's supper-table scene, where the teacher and lord 'rose from supper... laid aside his outer garments, and taking a towel, tied it around himself' (John 13:4) before washing his own disciples' feet.

The plate matches the Ruler's tracery and golden oculus exactly, but the light inside reads low and quiet instead of blazing: Christ kneels in a plain ivory-glass robe, the outer garment folded aside, washing a disciple's bare foot in a simple silver basin, a folded linen towel over one arm, eyes lowered — and behind him, the same throne from the other plate stands empty, its fire-glass gone cold and clear.

His virtue is Humility, the crown never even shown in this plate because it has already been set down before the scene begins — but that same kneeling is stalked by Servility, obedience with no will left inside it, the bent back that serves out of fear rather than love. His mood is Awe, the light failing at exactly the hour the towel comes out — and Glory is simply the next plate over: the same throne, waiting, its fire not put out but merely banked.

moon.name: Mary
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Mary is the Moon's own figure before the dial ever counted the phases — not by resemblance but by scripture's own naming: 'a great sign appeared in heaven: a woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars' (Revelation 12:1). No other figure in this set has a planet named as their own attribute this directly; Monday's blue was always going to be hers.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds her in deep Marian blue, standing on a pale crescent moon of frosted white glass, a mandorla of golden sun-rays radiating behind her exactly as Revelation describes it, a crown of twelve small star-points above her head, her hands folded low in a stillness the whole window is built around.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the same hour the dial already calls Calm. The color needed no invention: this is the blue the Church has painted her in for a thousand years, and the dial's own pre-dawn hour turns out to be her hour too — the night not yet given over to morning, the hush before an answer is asked for. Her virtue is Serenity, spoken in one line — 'let it be to me according to your word' (Luke 1:38) — but it begins as Fear: 'she was greatly troubled at the saying' (Luke 1:29), until Gabriel's own words dissolve it, 'do not be afraid, Mary' (Luke 1:30). Straight across the dial at 16h waits David, and the two mend each other the way this axis always has: his Courage cures her Fear, her Serenity quiets his Zeal down into something aimed rather than raging.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the hour before dawn because that is when the angel comes: not at noon's crowd or midnight's dread, but in the private, unwitnessed dark where the whole answer has to be given alone. Every other mood of the week is public; hers is the one mood earned in a room with no one else in it.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the dial's own Calm answering the hush before Gabriel speaks — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism sharpens her blue to #0040FF — pigment's quiet night blue becomes light's electric one. The hour and the Calm hold; the mandorla behind her simply burns a little brighter.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Mary's crescent arm turns lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — an unexpected trade for a figure known by silence rather than study, except that her whole answer to Gabriel was itself a reading: 'let it be to me according to your word' (Luke 1:38), scripture's plainest scholar of obedience.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that deep-night arm is the Unborn, ancestors and the not-yet-born together, and Mary's mantle turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the exact hue of her own calling, the one body in the set who carried the not-yet-born quite literally, the crescent already resting beneath her feet in Revelation's own vision (Revelation 12:1).
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Mary moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with Joseph the Farmer. The Visitation always did read as a springtime scene, two women carrying new life meeting in a hill country in bloom.
