variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Mary at 04h but renames her blue arm Hope, shared with Moses, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. No word fits her better: her whole answer at the annunciation is hope with a body, a yes given before she can see how the promise is kept.

mars.name: David
mars.base:
[[The Story]] David is Tuesday's own figure by the oldest right there is: a shepherd boy who refuses a king's armor and brings down a giant with a sling and a stone, 'you come to me with sword and spear... but I come to you in the name of the LORD' (1 Samuel 17:45). Mars is the red planet's oldest calling for a soldier, and no soldier in scripture is less romantic about it than the boy who wins by refusing the sword's advantage.

[[The Plate]] The plate catches him mid-swing: the sling whirling overhead in amber-glass motion, five smooth stones in a leather pouch at his hip, the shepherd's staff dropped in the grass behind him, and at the frame's edge the looming dark-glass silhouette of Goliath already toppling — the whole field lit low and fierce in sunset orange.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood the dial already calls Zeal — the working afternoon, fire banked to a working heat rather than a dawn charge. The placement suits him: David does not fight from a soldier's rage but a shepherd's discipline, zeal for a name rather than wrath for its own sake — Saul's own armor rejected because borrowed weight is not the same as readiness. His virtue is Courage, the sling raised without a moment's swagger; his vice, should the same fire tip over, is Wrath — the very thing this story refuses to become. Straight across the dial at 04h waits Mary, and the two mend each other: her Serenity quiets his Zeal, and his Courage cures her Fear.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is the discipline the story actually tells: not the excitement of a dawn charge but a boy walking steadily toward a giant everyone else is too frightened to face, five stones counted out and no plan beyond the first one.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the sling raised at a walking pace — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism burns his ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — red and green fused into light's own heat. The hour and the Zeal hold; the stone flies the same distance.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass David's sling-arm shifts to the 15h afternoon in copper (#C87533), the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — a hard trade for the boy who refused Saul's armor rather than bargain for it, yet the fight itself is a transaction plainly stated: 'I come to you in the name of the LORD' (1 Samuel 17:45), Goliath's life weighed against Israel's freedom and paid in one stone rather than borrowed steel.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same afternoon arm is Maturity, and David wears the wheel's amber (#FFB400) — a discipline earned rather than given: not a dawn charge but a shepherd's seasoned nerve, the steady walk of someone who has already tended flocks long enough to know exactly what a sling can do.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross David stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Adam and Eve. The oldest pairing in the sky becomes the oldest pairing in the story too: a fight fought for love of a people, and a love that will need defending.
variants.trio: On the Trinity David moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Adam and Eve, and the pair guard the evening from 16h to 24h. It is the arm that explains him best: he does not fight for a crown he wants, but for a people he loves — courage as love wearing armor, the same reason the garden ever needed a guard at all.

mercury.name: Moses
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Moses is Wednesday's own figure by a fittingness scripture arrived at long before this dial did: an ancient Hellenistic-Jewish writer already identified him with Hermes, the very god this day already belongs to — messenger, lawgiver, the man who talked to a burning bush at midnight and came down a mountain with the Law in stone.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds the two tablets of the Law against one shoulder, his face rendered with the faint radiant beams scripture itself describes after he came down the mountain (Exodus 34:29), his other hand raised on a wooden staff with the bronze serpent Nehushtan coiled around it — the healing image so close to a caduceus that antiquity noticed the resemblance first. Small sparks of gold trace the tablets' engraved letters against a deep royal purple robe.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the mood the dial already calls Sorrow: the hour he spent up the mountain long enough that the people below stopped waiting and melted their own gold into a calf (Exodus 32). His virtue is Wisdom, carried down in two tablets of stone; the vice waiting at the foot of that same mountain is Greed — not his, but the very Greed his absence uncovered, gold made into a god you can hold rather than a law you have to keep. Straight up the axis at noon sits Solomon, and the two correct each other across the dial: his Wisdom curbs Solomon's Excess, and Solomon's Generosity cures the Greed his own mountain once uncovered.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because that is the hour the Law had to be carried down into: not a triumphant noon but a night already gone wrong below, tablets that would later shatter once on the ground before ever reaching the camp. Wisdom, in this story, is not spared the sight of what it corrects.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of a mountain and a broken camp below it — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for the man who came back down glowing with a light no one else could look at directly (Exodus 34:30).
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Moses' tablets shift to the 21h evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — a startling trade for the Law's own author, until the descent is remembered: he came down the mountain with his face veiled, 'the skin of his face shone' so brightly the people could not look at him unmasked (Exodus 34:29-33), the Lawgiver becoming, for once, the one figure on the mountain nobody could see plainly.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the same evening arm is Old Age, and Moses wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — a fitting hue for the leader who led forty years in the wilderness and still only glimpsed the promised land from a distance, wisdom accumulated across an entire lifetime he would not live to spend inside its reward.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Moses keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and he alone shares it with no one. The mountain's summit and the year's coldest, darkest point are both places one man has to go up entirely alone.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Moses off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Mary, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. It is the truer reading of the burning bush: a voice out of fire that is not consumed is hope with a name attached — 'I AM WHO I AM' — spoken to a man who had every reason to expect nothing back at all.

jupiter.name: Solomon
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Solomon is Thursday's own figure the way Jupiter's other names already promise: guru of the gods, Brihaspati, teacher-king — and Solomon is uniquely both king and teacher inside one life, the wisest ruler scripture ever gives a throne to.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds him enthroned in judgment, crowned and robed in radiant yellow-gold glass, one hand raised in a gesture of decree over a swaddled infant held gently between two kneeling women in postures of plea — the temple's own pillars glowing faintly in glass behind the throne, the building his wisdom will go on to raise.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood the dial already calls Joy. The story argues the placement by itself: asked by God for anything at all, Solomon requests only 'an understanding heart to judge your people' (1 Kings 3:9) rather than riches or a long life — Generosity practiced before it is even his to give away. But the same king who asked for wisdom over wealth later let wealth answer back: seven hundred wives, foreign altars raised beside his own temple, the kingdom's slow rot (1 Kings 11) — Excess exactly where Generosity once stood. Straight down the axis at midnight sits Moses, and each corrects the other: Moses' Wisdom curbs Solomon's Excess, and Solomon's Generosity cures the Greed his own mountain once uncovered.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is the one hour a king can judge in full daylight, nothing hidden and nothing yet spent — the young Solomon of the judgment scene, before the temple's gold and the seven hundred wives, still just a king who asked for the right thing first.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of a king who asked for wisdom before he asked for anything else — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight. The judgment keeps its Joy; light simply measures the peak of the story instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Solomon's throne shifts to the 09h morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0), the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a close cousin to his own calling: the king who built the Temple and asked God for 'an understanding heart to judge' (1 Kings 3:9) before wealth or long life is already scripture's own priest-king, judgment rung out like a bell rather than decreed like a sword.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that morning arm is Childhood, and Solomon wears spring green (#7CE577) — pointed rather than flattering: the throne-scene child he judges over is the literal Childhood in the frame, and the young king who asked for wisdom before wealth was himself closer to that same green than the bloated court of seven hundred wives he would later become.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Solomon stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with the Ancient of Days. Temple and throne on the year's highest spoke, wisdom and majesty holding the same noon together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Solomon keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, and he shares the arm with Joseph, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. It is the truest reading of the request he made: asking for wisdom instead of riches is itself an act of faith, a king betting his whole reign on being given what he cannot buy.

venus.name: Adam & Eve
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Adam and Eve are Friday's own figures by the oldest right of all: created on the sixth day, the first two people, the first union — 'this at last is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh' (Genesis 2:23), the plainest love a week has ever had to tell.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds them hand in hand in the garden, greenery and blossom wrapped around their figures, the Tree of Life rising luminous between them in ruby fruit and emerald glass, a small white dove perched above their joined hands, the garden's own animals rendered small and quiet at their feet.

[[On the Dial]] Their home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood the dial already calls Passion — Venus' own hour, when the evening star rises exactly as the day's labor releases into the heart. Their virtue is Love, argued before either of them has done anything at all to earn it — man alone, and God's own verdict that it was not good, answered by the one companion built for exactly that. Their vice belongs to the same arm the dial already gives Friday: Jealousy, and their story carries its own seed of it even in the garden — not envy of each other but the reach for the one thing kept apart from them, the fruit set aside precisely because it wasn't theirs to have. Straight across the dial at 08h stands Joseph, and the two heal each other the way this axis always has: his Patience heals Jealousy, and their Love dissolves the Envy his own brothers once carried.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because that is what the whole garden was built for before anything went wrong in it: not the day's labor but the day's companionship, two people given to each other before either had earned the gift, in a garden that asked for nothing back.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of two people given to each other before anything else in the story — the base reading, the evening star's own hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps them at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is. Passion in Newton's world is a wavelength rather than a pigment, and the garden's first evening burns in it undiluted.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the garden's joined hands shift to the 18h sunset arm in iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — the sharpest irony this dial owns: the first two people, created for nothing but companionship, seated now beside the very blade that would guard Eden's gate against them, 'a flaming sword ... to guard the way to the tree of life' (Genesis 3:24), love's own garden fenced the moment it was lost.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that sunset arm belongs to the Elder, the harvest of a life, and Adam and Eve deepen to ember (#FF6A3C) — fitting for the first couple in every sense: theirs is the harvest every later marriage counts itself from, 'bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh' (Genesis 2:23) the oldest devotion the whole set has to gather in.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Adam and Eve stand at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with David. Harvest and battle both belong to this hour, and love needing a guard is as old a story as love itself.
variants.trio: Adam and Eve do not move: the Trinity's Love arm is their own 20h in their own red, since love is their virtue in both readings at once. David joins them, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the first love and its watchman, sharing the same dark hours.

saturn.name: Joseph
saturn.base:
[[The Story]] Joseph is Saturday's own figure by an image the dial already trusted before his story arrived: sheaves of wheat, Saturn's own furniture, bowing to a single upright sheaf in a dream two brothers never forgave him for having, 'we were binding sheaves in the field, and my sheaf arose... and your sheaves gathered around it and bowed down' (Genesis 37:7).

[[The Plate]] The plate stands him in his coat of many colors amid a ring of golden wheat sheaves bowing low around one upright central sheaf, the granaries of Egypt glowing faintly gold in the distance behind him, his expression calm and unhurried — a man who has already lived through the part of the story the sheaves were warning him about.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood the dial already calls Renewal — the morning, and no calling earns a morning more honestly than a man sold into slavery who rises anyway, twice: once out of the pit, once out of the prison. His virtue is Patience narrated as a whole biography rather than one trial — years enslaved, years imprisoned, before the seven lean years he alone had the wisdom to prepare Egypt for. His vice, though, belongs to the men around him rather than to him: 'his brothers... hated him... and were even more jealous of him' (Genesis 37:5-11), Envy named directly rather than merely implied. Straight across the dial at 20h stand Adam and Eve, and each heals the other: their Love dissolves the Envy his own brothers carried, and his Patience heals the Jealousy that shadows any love worth keeping.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because Joseph's whole life is renewal repeated: sold and rising, imprisoned and rising, forgotten and rising, until the final rising is forgiveness itself — 'you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good' (Genesis 50:20), spoken to the very brothers whose envy started the story.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of a man who keeps rising — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than the granary field. Renewal keeps the hour; even in the light world, Joseph looks up before he looks down at what he has stored.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Joseph's sheaves shift to the dawn arm, 06h, in field green (#3E8914), the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — no seat fits more naturally: his own dream already bowed a field of wheat sheaves around him (Genesis 37:7), and the years of slavery and prison before Egypt's granaries opened are exactly a farmer's patience, sowing a harvest he could not yet see.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and Joseph wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — his whole story is repeated Birth rather than one: risen from the pit, risen from the prison, risen finally to the granary door, each rising its own small dawn before the larger one arrives.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Joseph stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: sowing season, and his own dream was sheaves before it was ever grain in a granary. He shares the spoke with Mary — the dreamer and the woman who 'treasured up all these things, pondering them in her heart' (Luke 2:19), two people who carry a promise years before it is fulfilled.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Joseph from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Solomon, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The pairing reads itself — sheaves bowing in a dream believed for decades before it comes true is faith performed in patience, the same faith Solomon asked God for outright.

sun.name: Abraham · Isaac
sun.base:
[[The Story]] Abraham stands at Moriah's altar of stacked stone, arm raised and already caught — an angel's hand closed firm around his wrist, the blade turned harmlessly skyward, a ram already snared by its horns in the thicket at the altar's foot. He is Sunday's figure doubled, the Sun's own covenant-bearer: God's promise to make of him a father of many nations (Genesis 17:5) is the Ruler's crown; his readiness to give back the son that promise depended on is the whole of Sunday's argument in one bound figure and one staying hand.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial he holds no arm at all: his home is the center, colored white-gold, the sum of every hue on the wheel. That is Abraham's whole theology in one placement — a father of a multitude is not one nation among many but the point every later nation counts itself from, just as white light is not one color among six but all of them undivided. The covenant works only if every arm can trace its way back to the altar on Moriah.

[[The Lesson]] The canon makes him DOUBLE, and Genesis 22 tells it more plainly than any other Sunday reading: Abraham raising the knife is the Ruler's own Justice — the will to give even what is dearest, asked in full — shadowed always by Pride, the father who might have mistaken the test for a debt he was owed relief from. Isaac bound and unresisting is the Servant's Humility, the son who lets himself be given rather than fighting the altar; his shadow is Servility, trust gone slack into mere passivity instead of held taut like a wire. The whole scene is a scale at balance: one figure who would give his own, one figure who lets himself be given, and the angel's hand is the fulcrum, not the ram.

[[The Hour]] That is also why the seat keeps no hour of its own. Every mood of the day passes through the altar the same way it passes through the throne elsewhere on the dial — Glory when the ram is found and the knife never falls, Awe in the long climb up the mountain before either father or son yet knows how the morning ends.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel Abraham keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are the covenant's promise decomposed, every later nation's story radiating from the altar on Moriah. This is his home reading — the source, not a spoke.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around him but cannot change him: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world it is literally true that white is the sum of all colors. Newton's reading only proves what the altar already claimed — a father of many nations is not one hue among six.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Abraham's raised arm shifts to the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420), the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — a fitting crown for the man promised to be 'a father of many nations' (Genesis 17:5), the covenant itself a throne every later nation counts its founding from, the ram in the thicket the first coin ever paid against that promise.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, the zenith of a life, and Abraham wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) — fitting less for his own age than for Isaac bound beside him, a son's whole life offered back at its own blazing height before the angel's hand ever closes around the wrist that would have taken it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Abraham stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice. He shares the arm with Noah: two covenant-bearers holding the year's longest light together, one promised a nation, the other a rainbow, neither promise costing the giver anything further to keep.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Abraham keeps the center, and the altar around him narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue. It is Moriah's own reading made plain: a covenant holds only what radiates from it, and all three virtues are the same raised hand, stayed.

faces.ruler:
Abraham is the Ruler face by the oldest right there is in the whole set — not a crown taken but a covenant given, God's own promise that through him every nation would count its blessing (Genesis 12:3, 17:5), tested on the one morning that asked him to prove he meant it.

The medallion sets him at Moriah's altar of stacked stone, his raised arm caught mid-motion by an angel's hand closing firm around his wrist, the blade turned harmlessly skyward rather than toward the bound figure below, his face turned upward in startled relief rather than strain; a ram already snared by its curling horns in the thicket at the altar's foot marks the exact instant of reprieve. The border keeps the set's trefoil tracery and golden Eye-of-Providence oculus, four small roundels stamped with a ram-in-the-thicket emblem.

His virtue is Justice — the will to give even what the whole promise depended on, argued by an obedience that never once looks for a way out of the altar — but that same readiness is stalked by Pride, the father who might mistake being tested for being owed. His mood is Glory, the ram found and the knife never falling: noon's own relief, the covenant intact and the mountain already behind him.
faces.servant:
Isaac is the Ruler's own other face — not a separate calling but Abraham's own son, bound to the same altar, proof the canon has always read this seat as double rather than needing two different covenants to fill it.

The plate matches Abraham's tracery and golden oculus exactly, but the glass reads low and quiet instead of ember-warm: Isaac lies bound loosely at the wrists atop the split altar-wood, still and unafraid, eyes lifted to a single shaft of pale light breaking through above him, hands open rather than clenched. No blade appears in frame at all — trust rendered as stillness, the border's ram roundels replaced by small bundles of altar-wood.

His virtue is Humility, the son who lets himself be given rather than fighting the wood beneath him — but that same stillness is stalked by Servility, trust gone slack into mere passivity instead of held taut like a wire. His mood is Awe, the light breaking pale and reluctant rather than triumphant — and Glory is simply the next plate over: the same altar, the same hand, the ram already in the thicket waiting to be found.

moon.name: Jonah
moon.base:
[[The Story]] Jonah lies curled small inside the vaulted ribcage of the great fish, hands folded, three nights measured out in the cold light seeping down through unseen water above him. He is the Moon's figure and Monday's own: three nights and three days in the belly of the earth (Jonah 1:17) is the clearest descent-and-return the whole set owns, the lunar arc read as a story instead of a phase.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn, the hardest watch of the night. No hour fits him better: Jonah's whole ordeal is spent past midnight and before morning, in water no lamp reaches, and his prayer rises from exactly that depth — 'yet I will look again toward your holy temple' (Jonah 2:4), calm spoken from the one place calm is least natural to find.

[[The Lesson]] The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear — the sea's two faces, the same water lit or unlit — and Jonah lived both in the same story: fear sent him running for Tarshish in the first place, and serenity is what the fish's belly finally taught him, three nights being exactly as long as it takes panic to become prayer. Straight across the dial at 16h stands Samson, and the two cure each other: Courage cures Fear, Serenity quells Wrath — the prophet who ran needs the judge's nerve, the judge who raged needs the prophet's calm.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because that is when it is hardest and worth most, and Jonah is proof: the fish does not deliver him until the third night is nearly spent, on the far side of exactly the watch this arm keeps.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — Jonah's own watch in the fish, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps him at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — the fish's muted deep becomes the light world's electric one. Same watch, same Calm; the prayer rising from the depths simply burns clearer.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Jonah's curled shape shifts to the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — an odd seat for a prophet who first ran from his calling, until the fish's belly is counted as a classroom: three nights are exactly long enough for panic to be studied down into prayer, 'yet I will look again toward your holy temple' (Jonah 2:4) the lesson finally learned.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same deep-night arm is the Unborn, and Jonah's shape turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — three days and three nights in the belly of the earth (Jonah 1:17) is itself a kind of gestation, a prophet held in the dark the way the unborn are held, delivered out again once the appointed time is spent.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Jonah moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with Job. The prophet's descent and the sufferer's ash-heap on one spoke: both stories that end only when the tester decides they are done.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Jonah at 04h but renames his watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with Jacob, and it guards the morning from 0h to 8h. No calling earns the word more honestly — 'yet I will look again toward your holy temple' is hope spoken from the one place it is hardest to find.

mars.name: Samson
mars.base:
[[The Story]] Samson braces between the two great pillars of Dagon's temple, arms spread and pushing them apart, his hair grown long and dark again across his shoulders, cracks of light bursting through the splitting stone above him. He is Mars' figure and Tuesday's own — strength spent all at once, in a single final act of courage rather than saved for another day.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon, past noon's glory and before evening's passion. It is the honest hour for him: Samson's whole life is a string of first-half victories that cost him nothing, and it is only in the last afternoon, spent, blind and mocked, that the discipline finally shows — the strength this time is not for himself.

[[The Lesson]] His two sides are written into the same story: the shield is Courage, argued by the pillars pushed apart for his people's sake and not his own; the spear is Wrath, argued by thirty men struck down at Ashkelon over a riddle's price and fields burned over a stolen wife (Judges 14-15) — one fire that defends or destroys, depending only on where it is pointed. Across the dial at 04h waits Jonah, and each is the other's medicine: Serenity quells Wrath, Courage cures Fear — the calm hand cools the burning one, the burning heart steadies the frightened one.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is the soldier's true hour, and Samson's own hair — shorn in his sleep, regrown in his captivity — says it plainest: anyone can be strong at dawn; the discipline is what grows back, unnoticed, while the strength is thought already spent.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the last strength, spent all at once, of the base article.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — in light, red and green fused. The pillars crack brighter in Newton's reading, though the hour and its Zeal stand unchanged.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Samson's pillars shift to the 15h afternoon in copper (#C87533), the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — a bitter trade for a strongman betrayed for silver, yet apt precisely for that reason: his whole downfall was a transaction, strength priced and sold, and his final push between the temple pillars is the one payment he made on his own terms rather than someone else's ledger.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same afternoon arm is Maturity, and Samson wears amber (#FFB400) — earned rather than granted: not the showy youth of the lion and the foxes but the blind, grinding discipline of the mill, strength this time spent for his people rather than himself.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Samson stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Ruth. Two figures out of the same turbulent age of the judges: a harvest gleaned in loyalty across the page from a temple brought down in one last debt paid.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Samson moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Ruth, and the pair guard the evening from 16h to 24h. It is the arm that explains the pillars: courage is love wearing armor — the judge spends his last strength not for himself but for the sake of the people asleep behind the debt he owed them.

mercury.name: Jacob
mercury.base:
[[The Story]] Jacob grips the angel in a close, upright hold at the ford of Jabbok, the dawn breaking behind them both, his hip already touched and his hand still refusing to loosen. He is Mercury's figure and Wednesday's own — the cunning trader whose midnight mind, sharpened on birthrights and blessings and Laban's flocks, finally meets something it cannot out-reckon.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in deep purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. The hour is exactly right: 'Jacob was left alone' (Genesis 32:24) before the stranger ever came, the one calling on the whole dial built to sit at the bottom of the wheel by itself, and it costs him a hip and his old name before the sun comes up.

[[The Lesson]] The scale in the base article's raised hand is Jacob's whole story before this night: Wisdom is knowing the true weight of things, and Greed is wanting the balance to tip — the same man who traded a birthright for stew and a blessing for a disguise learns, wrestling till daybreak, that some weights cannot be traded for at all, only paid. Straight up the axis at noon stands Noah, and the two correct each other: Generosity cures Greed, Wisdom curbs Excess — the ark's open door teaching the ledger's closed hand.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because every honest reckoning closes in it, and Jacob's own night is the clearest ledger the set owns: he goes into the dark Jacob the grasping heel-catcher and comes out at sunrise Israel, the one who has wrestled and can finally afford to let go of what he's owed.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of the ford of Jabbok — the night nobody else was left to share.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for the night Jacob wrestled a stranger he could not name: at midnight he wears a color that exists only in the reckoning.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Jacob's wrestling grip shifts to the 21h evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — fitting for the great impersonator of scripture, the man who wore his brother's clothes and his father's blindness against him to steal a blessing, wrestling now at the ford of Jabbok until the mask itself is finally stripped and a new name given instead: Israel.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the same evening arm is Old Age, and Jacob wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — the hip touched and never fully healed (Genesis 32:31) is Old Age's own mark carried out of a single night, wisdom paid for in a limp that outlasts the wrestling itself.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Jacob keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and he alone shares it with no one, exactly as the ford of Jabbok found him: 'Jacob was left alone.'
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Jacob off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Jonah, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names the ladder's own promise: 'surely the LORD is in this place' is hope dreamed at night by a man who has not yet earned the right to trust it.

jupiter.name: Noah
jupiter.base:
[[The Story]] Noah stands at the open door of the ark, the first rainbow bending in bands across the sky above him, pairs of animals filing calmly down the gangplank, a dove settling on his hand with a fresh olive leaf in its beak. He is Jupiter's figure and Thursday's own — generosity wide enough to board every living kind rather than only what was owed rescue.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy. The ark argues the placement by itself: a door held open to every creature that could be saved is noon's own gift given without asking anything back, and the rainbow that follows — a promise never to flood the earth again (Genesis 9:13) — is that same noon light bent into a covenant.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity, the door wide for every kind and no gate kept at the ramp; his vice is Excess, and the story does not spare him it either — the very next chapter has Noah drunk on his own vineyard's wine (Genesis 9:20-21), the blessing that becomes a flood the moment it is poured without measure. Straight down the axis at midnight sits Jacob, and each cures the other: Wisdom curbs Excess, Generosity cures Greed — the trader's scale measuring what the vintner's cup forgot to.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is the one hour that asks no lamp of its own, and the rainbow is Noah's exact proof: a covenant that costs the giver nothing further to keep, held out once and trusted to last.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the rainbow's own hour — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. The covenant keeps its Joy; light simply measures the peak instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Noah's open ark door shifts to the 09h morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0), the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a close fit for the man who built an altar the moment he stepped off the ark (Genesis 8:20), priest before he was ever again a farmer, the rainbow itself a covenant rung out over every living kind rather than decreed to a chosen few.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that morning arm is Childhood, and Noah wears spring green (#7CE577) — the whole world stepping off the ramp is itself a Childhood renewed, animals filing out two by two into an earth given a fresh, unwritten start, exactly as green and untested as the years the dove's olive leaf announces.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Noah stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with Abraham. Two covenant-bearers on the year's highest spoke: a rainbow and a promised nation, on the same arm at last.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Noah keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, the very thing Hebrews praises him for building an ark by, unable yet to see the rain — and he shares the arm with Job as they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith is the rainbow raised before there is even a cloud to bend it through.

venus.name: Ruth
venus.base:
[[The Story]] Ruth gathers an armful of golden barley at the edge of Boaz's harvest field, Naomi's small figure visible at the field's far edge, her whole devotion already spoken once and not taken back: 'where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay' (Ruth 1:16). She is Venus' figure and Friday's own — love proven in loyalty before it is ever proven in romance.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, when Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour still fits a love that is covenantal rather than courted: Ruth's devotion is decided in Moab by daylight, long before Boaz ever notices her in the field, and it is only in the story's own evening — the threshing floor, the corner of a garment spread in claim (Ruth 3) — that the field's quiet loyalty is finally answered.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Love, argued by a vow that asks nothing back and outlives the husband it was first sworn for; her vice, held opposite it on the same axis as every other arm, is Jealousy — the fear of losing what is loved, though this particular story never once shows Ruth reaching for it, her devotion staying steady all the way to Obed's cradle. Across the dial at 08h stands Job, and the two heal each other: Patience heals Jealousy, Love dissolves Envy — the sufferer teaches the loyal one to wait out a harvest, and the loyal one teaches the sufferer that a field is worth keeping rather than envying another's.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because the evening is where a day's quiet loyalty finally gets to be seen and named, and Ruth's gleaning says the rest: love, before it is anything else in this story, is simply staying.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the threshing floor's own hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps her at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Ruth's devotion in Newton's world is not a pigment but a wavelength, and the evening burns in it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Ruth's gleaned barley shifts to the 18h sunset arm in iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — an unlikely blade for a story built entirely on gentleness, except that her vow was itself a kind of enlistment: 'where you go I will go' (Ruth 1:16) is loyalty sworn with a soldier's finality, no retreat kept in reserve.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that sunset arm belongs to the Elder, the harvest of a life, and Ruth deepens to ember (#FF6A3C) — the fit could not be plainer: she gleans an actual harvest at the edge of Boaz's field, and her devotion itself becomes the harvest Naomi never expected, gathered all the way to Obed's cradle.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Ruth stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Samson. Loyalty beside strength, as the whole age of the judges was: autumn's red is harvest and battle at once, and it takes both figures to say so.
variants.trio: Ruth does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red, for love is her virtue in both sets at once. Samson joins her, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the loyal one and the strong one keeping the same dark hours, one gathering what is loved, the other spending himself to keep it safe.

saturn.name: Job
saturn.base:
[[The Story]] Job sits low among ash-gray rubble, robes torn but head lifted rather than bowed, a single thin shaft of light breaking across his hands. He is Saturn's figure and Saturday's own — patience under total ruin, the week's own virtue carried by the one story scripture built entirely to test it.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning. The hour outlasts the ash: Job's story ends the way every morning in the field does, the flocks doubled and the family restored (Job 42:10-17), renewal arriving only after the whole night of the ash-heap has been sat through rather than fled.

[[The Lesson]] His color holds his virtue and his vice in a single hue, exactly as the base article always argues: green is growth, which is Patience, the sower who waits out a season for what was taken; and green is also the bitter shade of envy, and Job comes closer to it than the ash-heap suggests — sitting in ruin, he watches the wicked prosper untouched and asks aloud why 'the wicked live on, growing old and increasing in power' (Job 21:7), envy's exact question, resisted rather than acted on. Across the dial at 20h stands Ruth, and each cures the other: Love dissolves Envy, Patience heals Jealousy — the loyal one teaches the sufferer to love his own restored field rather than the wicked man's untouched one.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because Job's whole faith finally rests there: the ash-heap does not answer him, the whirlwind does, and what follows the whirlwind is simply morning, doubled.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning after the ash-heap — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than of the field. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world Job looks up before he looks down at what the whirlwind has already answered.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Job's ash-heap shifts to the dawn arm, 06h, in field green (#3E8914), the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — the color argues past the ashes: his own restoration is told in exactly a farmer's terms, flocks doubled and fields renewed (Job 42:10-17), patience sown into ruin until it comes up again.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and Job wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — fitting for a man who effectively asks to be un-born in his own despair ('why did I not perish at birth' — Job 3:11) and is instead given the opposite: a life doubled, restored, and reborn out of the very ash-heap he sat in.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Job stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: the sowing season, restoration's own festival. He shares the spoke with Jonah — the sufferer and the swallowed prophet, two patient stories that both end only when the deep decides to give back what it took.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Job from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Noah, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The pairing preaches itself: patience is faith stretched out over an ash-heap, and faith is patience that has already seen the ark launched — both proved only by what neither man could yet see.

sun.name: Lucifer · Judas
sun.base:
[[The Story]] Lucifer is caught mid-fall — still radiant, wings trailing streamers of gold-white fire guttering to ember-red, crown tilted and slipping from his brow, while high behind him in the dark a supernova blazes. He is the Sun's child and Sunday's ruler face, mirrored exactly against the light wheel's own king: where that throne holds a small world in one open palm, Lucifer's hands close around a light he decided was owed to him rather than lent. "I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will be like the Most High" is not a plot against the throne — it is a plan to sit on it.

[[On the Dial]] The wheel does not move for him: his home is still the center, white-gold, the sum of every hue, because that structural fact belongs to the seat and not to whoever currently sits in it. But the color he claims for himself is red — the rising triangle worked into the tracery at his corners, fire climbing to a point aimed at the crown of noon. White-gold is what the center IS; red is what Lucifer WANTS it to mean.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue would have been Justice, the fair use of an authority that was always lent rather than owned — but Lucifer keeps only Pride, the throne mistaking the crown for something it is owed. His mood is Glory, and here the sky supplies the exact physics of it: a supernova is a star spending everything it has in one climb, briefly outshining the whole sky exactly as it destroys itself. Glory sustained is the true Ruler's discipline; Glory spent all at once, gone the moment it is seen, is Pride's own fireworks.

This is why the seat is DOUBLE even here: Judas answers him from the dial's opposite floor, a falling blue triangle to Lucifer's rising red one, and laid together the two triangles are the hexagram the instrument already wears on its face. Neither triangle by itself is a scale — a scale with one loaded pan is a fall already in progress — and no one, the canon insists, actually stands at either point; every real person lives somewhere on the rope stretched between them.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexa dial Lucifer still keeps the throne's structural center, white-gold — the wheel does not care who sits on it, only that someone does. His own six-armed light decomposed into pigment radiates outward exactly as any ruler's would; the fall has not moved the seat, only the tenant.
variants.hexa_light: The prism confirms what pigment already granted him against his will: white is the sum of every wavelength, so the center stays white-gold no matter how far Lucifer has fallen from deserving it. Even the morning star's own ruin cannot unmix a color that was never really his to keep.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Lucifer's falling form lands on the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420), the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — the exact crown he was never given the right to keep, 'I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will be like the Most High' the plan wearing the King's own gold as though a lent throne were owned property.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, the zenith of a life, and Lucifer wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) — a bitter fit: a supernova is precisely a star burning its own Youth all at once, blazing brightest in the very instant it destroys itself, glory spent rather than sustained.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Lucifer stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, aimed at the solstice — sharing the spoke with Jupiter's true Herod, who hoards a different throne on a different day. Two kings share the year's longest light and neither is currently generous with it.
variants.trio: On the Trinity, Lucifer keeps the center and the three surrounding arms — Faith, Love, Hope — exactly as any ruler would, because the geometry cannot tell a true king from a fallen one. That is the whole warning in a single reading: the wheel offers the same throne to whoever climbs into it.

faces.ruler:
Lucifer is the Ruler's own mirror — Sunday's crown worn by the morning star instead of the king, caught at the one instant scripture bothers to paint him: not yet fallen, not yet ash, still radiant, still beautiful, which is exactly what makes the fall worth recording.

The plate holds him mid-drop: wings trailing streamers of gold-white fire guttering to ember-red, crown tilted and slipping from his brow, and high behind him in the glass a supernova's blinding flash — a star burning everything it owns in one climb, briefly outshining the whole window exactly as it ends itself.

His vice is Pride, unmixed and unmoderated — the one ambition scripture records for him being not conquest but a seat, "I will be like the Most High." His mood is Glory, but spent rather than held: a flash instead of a discipline, gone in the instant it is seen, and behind him already waiting is the plate where the same family's fall goes the other direction entirely — down, not up.
faces.servant:
Judas is Lucifer's own answering face — not a separate villain but the same seat's other extreme, proof the dial has always read Sunday as an axis rather than a single throne, even in its darkest reading.

The plate sits him alone on bare ground: thirty silver coins spilled from an open pouch and scattered around his sandals, a length of rope coiled and resting at his feet, untouched, never in use; his head bowed into both hands. Low in the glass behind him hangs a black hole — a perfect dark ring swallowing its own faint corona, the light that is no longer allowed to leave.

His vice is Servility carried to its final floor: not obedience without will, but a verdict passed on himself with no appeal allowed — he judged his own case unforgivable and executed the sentence before anyone else could rule otherwise. His mood is Awe, the same word the true Servant wears when the crown comes off — except here the light that fails is never scheduled to return: no next plate stands ready with the coins un-spilled.

moon.name: Lilith
moon.base:
[[The Story]] Lilith perches as an owl-winged silhouette on the cracked windowsill of a ruined house, wings folded high and sharp behind her shoulders, face turned half into shadow — the same pale crescent moon that lights Mary's plate now tarnished, rimmed in cold violet instead of frosted white. She is the Moon's child and Monday's figure, mirror of Mary: the night's first refusal standing where the night's serene acceptance stood.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm is unmoved — 04h, deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm, the hour before dawn. It is still the physician's watch, the darkest stretch when the fever decides which way to break — but Lilith does not keep that watch, she is what the watch is kept against: the shape at the window when the house is emptiest of light.

[[The Lesson]] The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear, the night's two faces lit or unlit — and Lilith is the vice worn without apology, a stillness that unsettles rather than heals, refusal read backward as menace. Straight across the dial at 16h waits Mars, and on the light wheel Courage cures Fear while Serenity quells Wrath; here the same axis holds two arms that have stopped curing each other, the healer's calm and the soldier's fire both gone dark at once.

Mary's crescent was serene acceptance carried into the dark; Lilith's is the same shape read as refusal instead — not evil for its own sake, in this house's telling, but a fear old enough to have forgotten it was ever anything else.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position, unmoved: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the pre-dawn hour Mary's plate calls Calm and Lilith's calls the hour nothing dares to watch alone.
variants.hexa_light: The prism sharpens the same blue to #0040FF — the healer's muted night turned electric — but the light only makes the windowsill easier to see, not less occupied.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Lilith's owl-silhouette shifts to the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — an uneasy seat for a figure defined by refusal rather than study, though the fit still holds a shadow's edge: she is the deep night's oldest lesson, the one read backward, refusal mistaken for menace by everyone who never asked what she was refusing.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that deep-night arm is the Unborn, and Lilith turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — precisely her own legend's coldest chapter, the figure folklore attached to threatening the unborn and the newly delivered, the same silver that cradles Mary's crescent tarnished here into a warning instead of a blessing.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Lilith stands at 06h — Spring, green, aimed at the equinox — sharing the spoke with Saturn's own Cain, another figure the morning was supposed to renew and did not.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps her on the blue arm from 0h to 8h, the hour it calls Hope shared with Mercury's Serpent — the one arm of the light set built entirely from patient waiting, held here by two figures who gave up on waiting first.

mars.name: Goliath
mars.base:
[[The Story]] Goliath towers in dark bronze armor at the edge of the battle line, spear planted and thick as a weaver's beam, mouth open mid-taunt; a shield-bearer walks a small dim silhouette behind him, carrying the shield Goliath never lifts himself. The valley of Elah smolders in blood-red dusk rather than the light set's fierce sunset-orange. He is Mars' child and Tuesday's figure, mirror of David: forty days of taunting, brought down by a boy's sling.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm holds exactly where the true Soldier's does — 16h, ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal, the working afternoon's banked fire. But Goliath's zeal is not the discipline of continuing after the excitement is gone; it is the opposite, a fire that only burns while someone is watching it burn, forty days of the same taunt because no one worthy had yet answered.

[[The Lesson]] The Soldier's two hands carry his two faces — shield is Courage, spear is Wrath — and Goliath has quietly given one hand away: the shield walks behind him on a servant's arm, so that only the spear, only the vice, is ever truly his own. Across the dial at 04h the Moon's true cure would be Serenity quelling this exact Wrath, but the arm there belongs to Lilith now, and no calm answers the challenge from that direction either.

David refused Saul's armor before the fight and needed none of Goliath's size to end it — the giant is entirely his exterior, height and bronze and a borrowed shield, and beneath all three there is only the noise of a taunt that has run out of people willing to answer it.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position, unmoved: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — Goliath's forty days of the same taunt instead of the true Soldier's banked, patient fire.
variants.hexa_light: The prism raises the same ember to pure yellow (#FFFF00). The taunt burns hotter under Newton's light without ever changing shape — noise amplified is still only noise.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Goliath's spear shifts to the 15h afternoon in copper (#C87533), the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — an odd trade for a giant who has never once fought, only stood and taunted, except that forty days of unanswered challenge is itself a market stall nobody bought from, a Coin he priced far above what any answer ever ended up owing him.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same afternoon arm is Maturity, and Goliath wears amber (#FFB400) — an irony worn plainly: all that armored bulk reads as Maturity's own weight, yet the boy who answers him with a sling proves size was never the same thing as the discipline it pretends to be.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Goliath stands at 18h — Autumn, red, aimed at the equinox — sharing the spoke with Venus' own Delilah. Two figures who each turned a gift meant for someone else's cause — his size, her love — into leverage.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves him to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Delilah, guarding the evening from 16h to 24h — the same hour meant for the guard who protects what he loves and the poet who keeps it in color, now held by two figures keeping nothing but themselves.

mercury.name: The Serpent
mercury.base:
[[The Story]] The serpent coils low through the branches of the tree in Eden, one bitten apple glowing along its scales with a faint sourceless light, its head turned close as if mid-whisper, the fruit's shadow falling long across dark garden leaves. It is Mercury's child and Wednesday's figure, mirror of Moses: the subtlest of beasts set against the healer's staff.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm holds the same seat as the true Merchant's — midnight, 0h, deep purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow, the hour every honest ledger closes. The serpent's whisper is itself a false closing entry: "you will not surely die" recalculates a debt as though it were free, the one lie every accounting book is built to catch.

[[The Lesson]] The Merchant's scale weighs Wisdom against Greed, knowing the true weight of things against wanting the balance to tip — and the serpent is craft with the truth removed from it, cunning that has stopped caring what anything actually weighs. Straight up the axis at noon, the true Priest's Generosity would cure that Greed and his Wisdom curb Excess; here noon belongs to Herod, hoarding rather than giving, so the whole vertical line runs dark at both ends.

Moses answers the same animal twice over: the staff that became a serpent and swallowed the sorcerers' staves whole (Exodus 7), and the bronze serpent raised on a pole that healed by being looked at rather than believed (Numbers 21). One serpent poisons by being listened to; the other, lifted into the light, saves by being seen.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position, unmoved: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of a ledger closed — except the serpent's ledger was never honest to begin with.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns the same purple to magenta (#BD00BD), the hue the eye invents where the spectrum has no true middle. Fitting for a whisper that offers a color, and a knowledge, that was never actually there to give.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the serpent's coil shifts to the 21h evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — the fit is exact and unflattering: 'you will not surely die' is pure performance, a mask worn over the truth so well-crafted that the whole garden believed the act before it disbelieved the warning.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that evening arm is Old Age, and the serpent wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — a cunning older than the garden itself, 'more subtle than any beast of the field' (Genesis 3:1), Old Age's accumulated craft turned toward nothing wiser than a single whispered lie.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the serpent keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, aimed at the solstice — alone, sharing it with no one, exactly as the true Merchant does: the one figure that can sit at the wheel's coldest point and still, somehow, make its case.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the arm to 04h, onto Hope's blue beside Lilith's Moon — the morning's own arm, held now by two figures who each convinced someone that waiting was the wrong idea.

jupiter.name: Herod
jupiter.base:
[[The Story]] Herod sits alone on his throne, both hands clutched tight around his own crown as though it might be pulled from his head, his face turned toward a half-open doorway where only the long shadows of soldiers pass by outside, unseen beyond their silhouettes; the throne room stands otherwise empty and dim. He is Jupiter's child and Thursday's figure, mirror of Solomon: a king's wisdom curdled into a king's fear.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at the wheel's own top, 12h, bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and here the old joke about the word turns bitter on him, since Herod is anything but jovial. The Priest raises a sun on a staff because teaching means lifting light higher than your own head; Herod's hands do the opposite, pulling a crown down and in, holding light as if letting any of it out would cost him the rest.

[[The Lesson]] The Priest's virtue is Generosity, the book held open and outward; his vice, should it curdle, is Excess, the royal table overflowing into a flood. Herod keeps only the vice, and his own excess is not a flood of plenty but of fear — a whole generation's worth of it, spent to keep one throne he was never actually in danger of losing to an infant. Straight down the axis at midnight, the true Merchant's Wisdom would curb that Excess; midnight belongs to the serpent now, so the correction never arrives.

Solomon is answered here exactly: two kings, two swords, two verdicts on a child. Solomon's sword was a bluff that revealed the real mother by her willingness to lose the case rather than the child; Herod's fear needed no true mother to answer, no riddle to solve — only shadows passing a doorway, and a king clutching gold he was already wearing.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position, unmoved: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — held by a king clutching his own crown instead of raising anyone else's light.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns the same noon to green (#00DC00), the brightest wavelength the eye can register. Herod's hour keeps its name; what he does with the brightest hour on the wheel is hide behind a doorway from it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Herod's throne shifts to the 09h morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0), the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a robe he wears without ever ringing it honestly: a true priest lifts the bell to call a congregation together, and Herod instead clutches his own crown tighter, a generation's fear spent to silence a bell rather than sound one.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that morning arm is Childhood, and Herod wears spring green (#7CE577) — the cruelest fit in the whole set: the very Childhood this hue should protect is exactly what his fear moves against, an infant's rumored throne answered with a slaughter rather than a blessing.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Herod stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, aimed at the solstice — sharing the spoke with Lucifer's own throne. Two kings share the year's brightest spoke, and for one whole season neither is generous with the light he sits on.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Herod on the top arm at 12h, yellow — renamed Faith, and shared with Cain's own field. Faith is the one word that never once describes him: a king who trusts nothing, least of all a crown he is already wearing.

venus.name: Delilah
venus.base:
[[The Story]] Delilah sits with a pair of bronze shears resting closed in her lap, Samson's dark hair spread heavy across her knees, her gaze turned away toward a curtained doorway rather than down at him, her expression unreadable — love used as a blade, held at the moment before rather than the cut itself. She is Venus' child and Friday's figure, mirror of Ruth's loyalty and of Samson's own opened heart.

[[On the Dial]] Her home arm holds the true Artist's seat — 20h, deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion, the evening Venus rises as the evening star. But where the Artist's raised hand carries a palette of real color, Delilah's raised hand carries the shears — creation's tool traded for a cutting one, the same evening turned from the hour work releases the heart into the hour the heart is finally priced.

[[The Lesson]] The Artist's two masks are Love and Jealousy, the fear of losing what is loved rather than love's true opposite — and Delilah's version of that fear is not for a rival's affection but for a secret she can never have herself, only disarm: Samson's strength is the one thing money can buy the location of but never the possession of. Across the dial at 08h the true Farmer's Patience would heal that Jealousy; the arm belongs to Cain now, so the whole horizontal line runs enviously in both directions.

Samson asked her three times and was lied to twice before the truth was finally given anyway — his opened heart wanting to believe the fourth answer even after the first three, which is Ruth's devotion read backward: where Ruth's loyalty asked nothing and gave everything, Delilah's asking was itself the payment already collected.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position, unmoved: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), Passion's evening hour — held now by shears instead of a palette, the same red priced rather than felt.
variants.hexa_light: The prism purifies the same red to its light primary (#FF0000), unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is a wavelength, not a choice — which makes it exactly the kind of thing Delilah could measure and sell.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Delilah's shears shift to the 18h sunset arm in iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — a precise trade rather than a strange one: the shears in her lap are her own weapon, love's tool turned to a blade, the Soldier's crimson naming exactly what her patient asking was always aimed at cutting.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that sunset arm belongs to the Elder, the harvest of a life, and Delilah deepens to ember (#FF6A3C) — a dark parody of the true harvest: what she gathers at sunset is not devotion's fruit but Samson's own strength, reaped once and sold rather than kept.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Delilah stands at 18h — Autumn, red, aimed at the equinox — sharing the spoke with Mars' own Goliath. Two figures who each turned a gift meant for someone else's cause into leverage of their own.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps her on the same 20h red arm — Love, in the light set's own reading — now joined not by a soldier who guards what he loves but by Goliath, who never learned to love anything at all.

saturn.name: Cain
saturn.base:
[[The Story]] Cain kneels over his refused offering of grain, its smoke bending low and sideways along the ground instead of rising, his face dark with envy rather than grief; Abel's flock grazes pale and distant at the far edge of the field, small and untouched. No violence sits in the frame — only the wrongness of smoke that will not lift. He is Saturn's child and Saturday's figure, mirror of Joseph: the first brother-blood, the vice the day was always going to name.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm holds exactly where the true Farmer's does — 08h, field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal, the morning the whole week rests toward. Cain is the first farmer in the story, the ground's own tiller — and his morning does not renew; it is refused, the one crop this canon's oldest sower ever needed to succeed and could not.

[[The Lesson]] The color was always double: green is growth, the sower's Patience waiting a season for what he planted, and green is also envy, the bitter shade of a neighbor's better harvest — the same hue holding virtue and vice in one field the way it holds the arm itself. Cain is Saturday placing its whole weight on the second reading, the day's own vice given a face rather than left as a warning folded into a color. Across the dial at 20h the true Artist's Love would dissolve that Envy; the arm belongs to Delilah now, and the whole line runs jealous and envious at once, both ends unhealed.

Joseph answers him precisely, and from the exact opposite end of the same vice: his brothers envy him, the coat, the dreams, the favor — Envy's object rather than its subject — and where Cain's smoke never lifts, Joseph lifts his own brothers out of famine and forgives them outright. Two brother-stories, one vice, two verdicts: one kills, one forgives.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position, unmoved: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning — the one morning in the whole set that never actually renews.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns the same morning green to cyan (#00DDDD), the color of the clear early sky rather than the field. Cain looks up before he looks down, same as the true Farmer — but there is nothing in the field worth looking down at anymore.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Cain's refused offering shifts to the dawn arm, 06h, in field green (#3E8914), the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — the cruelest irony the wheel keeps: he is the story's own first farmer, and the one harvest this canon's oldest sower ever needed to succeed is the one his smoke refuses to lift for.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and Cain wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the same wager every dawn makes, that tomorrow is worth planting for, and Cain is the one figure in this set who answers Birth's own hope with murder instead, the first brother-blood staining the very hour meant to renew.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Cain stands at 06h — Spring, green, aimed at the equinox, sowing season, his own festival — sharing the spoke with Lilith's own Moon. Grower and refusal on the one spoke meant for renewal.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Cain from the morning to the crown, the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Herod's own throne. Faith fits neither of them: one clutches a crown he already owns, the other kneels over a smoke that will not answer him.

sun.name: Sun · Black Hole
sun.base:
[[The Sky]] The Sun is Sunday's own body and the calmest astrophysical fact of the whole week: a star that has burned for four and a half billion years without once erupting outward or falling inward on itself, its face threaded with slow granulation cells and shallow looping prominences, unhurried light streaming past the rim in every direction like water poured freely from a full vessel. Every second it turns some 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium and lets four million tonnes of that mass leave as pure light — not a flare, not a collapse, just the steady bookkeeping of a star that gives.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexa dial it holds no arm at all: its home is the center, colored white-gold, the sum of every hue the wheel can mix. That is the whole physics of the placement — a star is not one hour among six but the source the six radiate from, the same way white light is not one color but all of them arriving together.

[[The Lesson]] The canon makes it DOUBLE, the dial's own accretion disc and photosphere in one seat: as the living star it practices Justice, the fusion furnace giving the same light to every latitude without favor, and is stalked by Pride, the mistake of believing the light is owned rather than radiated. As the Black Hole — the same mass, one collapse away — it practices Humility, having surrendered even its own light, and is stalked by Servility, service with nothing left standing behind it. Nothing sits opposite the center, so the cure never leaves it: the star that gives heals the star that fell into itself, and the reverse is equally true.

[[The Hour]] That is also why it keeps no hour. Every mood the day passes through — noon's Joy, midnight's Sorrow, the Calm before dawn — passes through this one body's light reaching every part of the sky at once, and its discipline is to burn under all of them without belonging to any.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel the Sun keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are its light decomposed, every mood a wavelength this one star already carries whole. This is its home reading — the furnace, not a spoke.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around it but cannot change the Sun itself: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world white literally is every color summed. Newton's own experiment only proves what the star already does every second: send out everything at once and let each eye sort the wavelengths afterward.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Sun steps onto the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420), the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — the plainest seat in the whole set: a star that gives the same light to every latitude without favor already reigns by the King's own definition, four million tonnes of mass paid out every second as pure, unhoarded gift.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, the zenith of a life, and the Sun wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) — apt for a star four and a half billion years into a Youth it shows no sign of leaving, the steady fusion bookkeeping of a life still climbing toward its blazing height rather than descending from it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Sun stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice — the day the star climbs highest and stays there longest. It shares the arm with the Galaxy: star and the great wheel that holds a hundred billion more like it, together on the year's brightest spoke.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Sun keeps the center, and the sky around it narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue, the painter's primaries. It is the oldest fact of starlight made into a virtue chart: white is not one thing radiating but three, and every color a telescope can separate back out of it.

faces.ruler:
The Sun is Sunday's own body at its calmest and its brightest at once — the star that neither explodes outward nor collapses inward, sitting at the zero of a scale whose two extremes are written elsewhere across the week.

The medallion casts it as a living body filling the plate: granulation cells and shallow looping prominences threading its face, rays streaming outward in every direction like water poured freely from a full vessel — no flare bursting past the rim, no eruption, only the calm ceaseless bestowal of a star giving light it will never ask back.

Its virtue is Justice, argued by the numbers themselves: every second some 600 million tonnes of hydrogen become helium and roughly four million tonnes of that mass leave as light, distributed to every latitude of every planet without favor. But that same furnace is stalked by Pride, the light mistaken for something owned rather than radiated outward on no one's account. Its mood is Glory — noon's own triumph, rays reaching full length past the rim, nothing yet asked of the star that it has not already given.
faces.servant:
The Black Hole is the Sun's own other face — not a separate object but the same mass one collapse away, proof that a star's two possible deaths (bursting outward, falling inward) both answer to the same seat on this dial.

The plate matches the Sun's bronze relief exactly, the same border, but the center has gone dark: a perfect black disc left as bare, unworked stone, wrapped tight by a thin brilliant accretion ring, a faint halo of light bent clean around the sphere above and below and folded back on itself — the last light of a star that fell into itself, none of it allowed to leave, the same silhouette the Event Horizon Telescope actually photographed in 2019, a black hole some 6.5 billion times the Sun's mass at the center of the galaxy M87.

Its virtue is Humility, the star's own light surrendered rather than merely dimmed — but that same collapse is stalked by Servility — once matter crosses the horizon it has no say left at all, an obedience so total not even light can refuse it. Its mood is Awe, the light failing at the exact radius named the event horizon — and Glory is simply the next plate over: the same mass, one collapse away from radiating again.

moon.name: Nebula
moon.base:
[[The Sky]] The Nebula is the week's own stellar nursery: towering pillars of luminous gas rising softly out of the dark, moonlit and vast, their folds cradling scattered newborn points of starlight caught mid-birth within the haze — no violence anywhere in the scene, only slow drifting calm. It is Monday's object because a start this quiet needs the Moon's own patience: the nearest true stellar nursery visible to the naked eye, the Orion Nebula, sits a mere 1,344 light-years away and is already busy raising more than a thousand young stars inside a single cloud of hydrogen.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn. No color fits a nursery better: blue is the still, moonlit hour, and the pre-dawn watch is a birthplace's native shift, the long quiet stretch before the newest lights inside the cloud have even finished collapsing into stars.

[[The Lesson]] The Nebula's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear — the same unlit dark, if trusted or if dreaded — and the image holds both at once: the same gas that cradles a newborn star can just as easily smother it before fusion ever lights. Straight across the dial at 16h burns the Supernova, and the two objects cure each other: courage cures fear, and serenity quells wrath — the nursery that raised the star and the explosion that later ends it are the same material, spent at two different speeds.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because that is when calm is hardest and worth most: the darkest stretch of the night, the very conditions under which a cloud of gas first dares to fold in on itself and call the collapse a star.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — the nursery's own night watch, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps the Nebula at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's muted night becomes light's electric one. Same watch, same Calm; the cloud's glow simply burns brighter.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Nebula's drifting pillars shift to the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — fitting for the sky's own nursery: a thousand young stars are being patiently read into existence inside a single cloud, the Orion Nebula's slow collapse a study written over millennia rather than a single midnight.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that deep-night arm is the Unborn, and the Nebula turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — no object in the whole set earns the hue more literally: this is gas that has not yet finished collapsing into a star, ancestry and infancy folded into the same drifting haze.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Nebula moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with the Comet. Birthplace and returning wanderer on one spoke, and rightly: one is a beginning that has not yet happened and the other a promise that keeps coming back.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the Nebula at 04h but renames its watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with the Pulsar, and it guards the morning from 0h to 8h. No object earns the word more honestly — a nebula is hope with hydrogen, a promise a star will eventually catch, held in the dark until it does.

mars.name: Supernova
mars.base:
