[[The Sky]] The Supernova is a star at the exact instant of detonation: blinding light climbing outward in one convulsive burst, concentric shockwave shells expanding through the dark, the old star's collapsed core barely visible at the heart of its own conflagration. It is Tuesday's object and Mars' oldest calling — the same violence the red planet has always carried since it was first named for a god of war. A star this violent needs real numbers to believe: in 1054 CE Chinese and Japanese astronomers recorded a 'guest star' bright enough to read by at night and visible in broad daylight for over three weeks — the supernova that left behind the Crab Nebula, still expanding nine and a half centuries later.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon. The placement is deliberately unromantic: an explosion this large is not the dawn charge but the long grind of a star's whole life spent in one climb, fire banked down to a working heat until the instant it lets go. Orange is the ember before the flare — hot enough to forge with, not yet the full detonation.

[[The Lesson]] Its two natures are its two faces: the outward blast is Courage and the same blast is Wrath — one fire that lights up a galaxy or destroys everything that made it, the star's virtue and vice being the identical heat pointed at the identical instant. Across the dial at 04h waits the Nebula, and each is the other's medicine: serenity quells wrath, courage cures fear. The calm cloud cools the burning star; the burning star reminds the calm cloud what it is eventually for.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is a star's true working hour: past its youth's quiet glory, before its final evening flare — the long fusion grind of holding a shape together against its own gravity. Any cloud can begin to shine; a star is whoever is still holding the line when the fuel finally runs out.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the banked fire of the base article.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps the Supernova at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — in light, red and green fused. The blast glows hotter in Newton's reading, though the hour and its Zeal stand unchanged.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Supernova's shockwave shifts to the 15h afternoon in copper (#C87533), the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — an odd trade for a detonation, except the physics itself is a transaction: a star spends its entire remaining fuel in one payment, every element heavier than iron minted in that single violent instant and scattered outward as the universe's own coinage.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same afternoon arm is Maturity, and the Supernova wears amber (#FFB400) — precisely earned: this is no young star's tantrum but the final grinding maturity of one that has held its shape against gravity for millions of years before finally letting go.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Supernova stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Binary Stars. Solitary detonation beside orbiting pair, the oldest company in the sky: autumn's red belongs to both, a single death and a shared life on the same arm.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Supernova moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside the Binary Stars, and the pair guard the evening from 16h to 24h. It is the arm that explains the object: an explosion this total is love wearing no armor at all — a star gives away everything it has, all at once, and calls the giving a virtue rather than a loss.

mercury.name: Pulsar
mercury.base:
[[The Sky]] The Pulsar is the cosmic clock: a city-sized star spinning in place, no wider than 20 kilometers across yet weighing more than the Sun, two narrow lighthouse beams swept out from its poles and sweeping the dark with a regularity some millisecond pulsars hold as steady as an atomic clock over years at a time. It is Wednesday's object and Mercury's own calling — the winged messenger was already the god of clocks and roads before anyone had built either well, and a pulsar is simply his instrument found in the sky: when the first one was discovered in 1967, its ticking was so exact the discoverers half-joked it might be an artificial beacon, nicknaming the signal LGM-1, 'Little Green Men,' before the astrophysics caught up with the coincidence.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in deep purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. The color is history's private joke on this messenger: Tyrian purple was the costliest pigment of the ancient world, worth more than any coin a courier ever carried — a color that was itself a trade empire. And midnight is the counting hour for a clock this precise: every tick since the last one is closed, weighed, filed, and the next one has already started before the filing is done.

[[The Lesson]] The beam in its raised hand is the whole ethics of the arm: Wisdom is trusting a rhythm precise enough to build a calendar on; Greed is wanting the tick to arrive faster than physics allows. Straight up the axis at noon shines the Galaxy, and the two correct each other across the dial: Generosity cures Greed, and Wisdom curbs Excess — the galaxy's whole hundred billion stars teaching the single spinning one that scale is not the same as haste.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because every honest clock closes its ledger there: the day's grave, where the last tick and the first tick of tomorrow meet without a pause between them. The Pulsar is the one object on the dial that would keep this appointment even if nobody were left to read it — and wisdom begins exactly there.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of the closed ledger — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns its purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for the Pulsar, the sky's own metronome: at midnight it wears a color that exists only in the mind reading it, the same way a tick only becomes a clock once someone is counting.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Pulsar's lighthouse beam shifts to the 21h evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — a performer's fit after all: a millisecond pulsar's beam sweeps the dark with such theatrical regularity that its 1967 discoverers half-suspected a staged signal, nicknaming it LGM-1 before the astrophysics unmasked the act.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that evening arm is Old Age, and the Pulsar wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — fitting for a stellar corpse rather than a living star: this is the collapsed core left behind after a supernova's death, a city-sized remnant still ticking with the discipline of something ancient outliving the body it came from.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Pulsar keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and it alone shares it with no one. The year's midnight belongs to a single object: the one clock precise enough to sit at the bottom of the wheel, alone, and never lose the beat.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the Pulsar off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Nebula, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names the object's true gift: a rhythm this steady is hope with a stopwatch — the ledger closed in sorrow at midnight reopens at the morning's gate, timed by the one star that never misses a beat.

jupiter.name: Galaxy
jupiter.base:
[[The Sky]] The Galaxy is the great wheel holding hundreds of billions of stars at once, its spiral arms carved from countless individual points of light, a dense bright core at the hub holding the whole structure together like a congregation gathered around its center. It is Thursday's object and Jupiter's own calling — in the Vedic week Thursday is Guruvar, the day of Brihaspati, who IS Jupiter, the guru of the gods, and a spiral galaxy is generosity built at the largest scale astronomy has a name for: the Milky Way alone is home to somewhere between one and four hundred billion stars, every one of them held on its own orbit by gravity shared rather than gravity hoarded.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter.' The arms themselves explain the placement: a spiral galaxy gives away light in every direction from its core, an entire wheel's worth of stars catching and passing on that same gravity and glow. Noon is the hour when light is given most freely, and yellow is that gift as a color.

[[The Lesson]] Its virtue is Generosity — the light shared across a hundred billion suns rather than kept at the core — and its vice is Excess, the same core grown so massive it swallows everything nearby, the gift that becomes a black hole at the center of its own galaxy. Straight down the axis at midnight ticks the Pulsar, and each cures the other: Wisdom curbs Excess, and Generosity cures Greed — the precise single beat below teaching scale to the sprawling congregation above.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is the one hour a wheel this size does not need to hoard: the day's peak, when starlight — like the galaxy's own arms — asks nothing back for what it gives away.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns its noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. The Galaxy's hour keeps its Joy; light simply measures the peak instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Galaxy's spiral arms shift to the 09h morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0), the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a scaled-up cousin of its own noon calling: a hundred billion stars gathered around one hub is already a congregation, gravity shared rather than hoarded, the same generosity simply rung out at galactic size.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that morning arm is Childhood, and the Galaxy wears spring green (#7CE577) — fitting for the arms where new stars are still being born, a structure that never stops assembling itself, forever raising fresh light along its spiral the way Childhood keeps arriving generation after generation.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Galaxy stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with the Sun itself. The single star and the hundred billion it belongs to, together on the year's highest spoke: one light and the wheel that holds it.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Galaxy keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, and it shares the arm with the Comet as they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. The placement reads itself: faith is trusting a structure too large to see all at once, the same trust a hundred billion orbits already keep without a single star checking the others' math.

venus.name: Binary Stars
venus.base:
[[The Sky]] The Binary Stars are two suns of comparable weight, locked in a slow mutual orbit around a shared point of balance between them, a bridge of streaming light and matter drawn taut wherever their gravities meet — two bodies bound by nothing but the pull they exert on each other. It is Friday's object and Venus' own calling: in the old astrology the planet's children were always the musicians and the lovers, and a binary system is the sky's most literal picture of a pair, closer to the truth of the galaxy than any single star standing alone — by most counts roughly half of all Sun-like stars keep a companion of some kind, so that a lone star like ours is closer to the exception than the rule.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, when Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour fits the object exactly: evening is when two things that have spent all day apart finally meet in the same sky, and a binary pair's whole nature is that mutual gravity never lets them out of each other's reach — the nearest star system to our own Sun, Alpha Centauri, is itself a binary pair completing one full orbit of each other roughly every eighty years.

[[The Lesson]] Its virtue and vice share the same bridge of light: Love is the gravity holding the pair together, and Jealousy is the fear that the bridge might one day pull matter from one star to the other and leave the giver dimmer for it — real physics for close binaries, where one star can in fact strip gas from its companion. Across the dial at 08h waits the Comet, and the two heal each other: Patience heals Jealousy, and Love dissolves Envy — the returning wanderer teaches the pair to wait, and the pair teaches the wanderer that even a solitary orbit is shaped by a gravity it shares with something else.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because passion is what the daylight was for: the day is spent separately, the evening is when two orbits finally cross. And this is the one object in the whole set built from two bodies rather than one — love, like binary stars, is the moment a system stops describing a single point and starts describing two.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the evening star's own hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps the Binary Stars at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is not a pigment but a wavelength, and the evening burns in it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Binary Stars' shared bridge of light shifts to the 18h sunset arm in iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — a tense trade for a pair bound only by mutual gravity, yet true to the physics: in close binaries one star can strip matter from its companion, the crimson naming the edge where devotion and violence share the very same bridge.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that sunset arm belongs to the Elder, the harvest of a life, and the pair deepens to ember (#FF6A3C) — fitting for a system like Alpha Centauri, already gathering decades into a single shared orbit, roughly eighty years spent completing one full circuit around each other, a harvest of time no lone star ever has to keep.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Binary Stars stand at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Supernova. Orbiting pair beside solitary blast, as old a contrast as the sky allows: autumn's red is a shared life and a single death in the same color.
variants.trio: The Binary Stars do not move: the Trinity's Love arm is their own 20h in their own red, for love is their virtue in both sets at once. The Supernova joins them, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — a pair kept alive by gravity and a single star spent all at once, sharing the same watch over the dark.

saturn.name: Comet
saturn.base:
[[The Sky]] The Comet is the returning wanderer: a small icy body arcing in from the outer dark, a long tail streaming behind it, and at the far edge of its orbit a distant Sun it is only now approaching again after decades away — the whole shape of its path reading as one long patient loop. It is Saturday's object and Saturn's own calling, and the sickle-shaped tail is not a coincidence of the metaphor: Halley's Comet, the best-known of them all, returns on a schedule almost exactly one human lifetime long — last seen in 1986, not due again until 2061 — patience measured out in a single number anyone can hold in their head.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning. The hour is the Comet's by right of habit: a comet nucleus is often described as a dirty snowball, an old mix of ice and rock and dust that has carried the same frozen material since the solar system's own beginning, and every close pass to the Sun renews it — boils a little more of that ancient ice into the tail and lets it shine again, however briefly, before the long dark orbit resumes.

[[The Lesson]] Its color holds its virtue and its vice in a single hue: green is growth and renewal, Patience, the wanderer that returns exactly when it says it will — and green is also envy, the tail streaming out behind it always pointed away from the Sun rather than toward it, the one direction it can never choose for itself. Across the dial at 20h shines the Binary Stars, and each cures the other: Love dissolves Envy, and Patience heals Jealousy — the paired stars teach the comet that closeness is possible, the comet teaches the pair that even the longest absence can still keep its appointment.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because the Comet's whole nature lives there: every return is slightly changed, a little more of the ancient ice boiled away, and every orbit is a wager that coming back is still worth what it costs to make the trip.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns its morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than of frost and ice. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world the Comet is measured by the sky it crosses rather than the tail it leaves.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Comet's long tail shifts to the dawn arm, 06h, in field green (#3E8914), the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — an apt trade for the wanderer whose whole nature is a slow, patient loop: Halley's own orbit is a single lifetime long, ancient ice sown into a furrow that will not come back around for decades.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and the Comet wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — fitting for a body that is quite literally reborn on every close pass, ancient ice boiled fresh into a shining tail each time it nears the Sun, dawn's own wager renewed once a human lifetime.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Comet stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: the season of returns, its own festival. It shares the spoke with the Nebula — wanderer and nursery together, the two patient objects that promise a light not yet arrived.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes the Comet from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with the Galaxy, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The pairing is the oldest sermon a comet's orbit could preach — faith is a return trusted before it is seen, every decades-long absence a promise kept on a schedule no one alive gets to check twice.

sun.name: Justice · Humility
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Sunday is the only day the wheel doubles: Justice rules it by daylight and Humility rules it by dusk, the same throne worn two ways. Where the other six days each carry one virtue against one vice on a single arm, the center carries two of each in one seat — and that doubling is not an exception to this theme, it is the theme stated plainly. Every virtue on this dial answers a vice somewhere else on the dial: Serenity quells Wrath, Courage cures Fear, Wisdom curbs Excess, Generosity cures Greed, Love dissolves Envy, Patience heals Jealousy. Only the center cures itself — Humility answers Pride, Justice straightens Servility — proof that the whole week is one instrument built to heal itself, spoke curing spoke, virtue by virtue, all the way around.

[[The Plate]] The two medallions are cut from the same gold cameo family that crowns this whole set — an engraved sunburst field, a polished gold ring wreathed in laurel — but they light it from opposite ends of the day. Justice sits enthroned in blinding white-gold under a noon halo, a balanced golden scale in one hand and a sheathed sword kept upright, never drawn, in the other. Humility is the same king with the crown lifted from his own head and held low in both hands, the sunburst behind him gone to dusk, one bare foot set in a basin of water where the throne's shield used to rest. One plate blazes; the other kneels. Both are gold.

[[On the Dial]] On the hexagram the center holds no arm and no hour at all — its home is the hub, white-gold, the sum of every hue the six arms carry. That is Justice's argument made geometry: a ruler is not one calling among six, but the point they radiate from, the way white light is not one color but every color undivided. Only the octa star moves it, lending the crown noon's own 12h arm; on the cross and the trio it keeps the same undivided center, the still axis the seasons and the theological three both turn around.

[[The Lesson]] Justice and Humility are not two virtues so much as one virtue seen from both sides of the same coin: the ruler who judges fairly and the ruler who serves without being asked are the identical discipline, aimed once outward and once inward. Pride is what happens when the crown believes the light is its own; Servility is what happens when the bent spine stops believing the light is anyone's at all — and each cures the other because the throne is close enough to itself to reach across. The mood swings from Glory at noon to Awe at dusk, but the medicine never leaves the hub: the one calling on the whole dial that carries its own disease and its own cure in the same open hand.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment the center takes no arm — it is the hub the six colors decompose from, white-gold because paint cannot mix a white, only receive it whole, as gold leaf laid rather than ground. That is Humility as a technique before it is a word: the one hue on the wheel that must be given rather than made. And it is Justice as geometry: the hub that plays no favorites among the six colors it feeds.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel makes the same claim from the opposite direction: here white is not the paint's blank but light's total sum, the six colored beams running inward to add back into white-gold at the hub. Humility rewarded — the center receives exactly what it gave the arms, returned and combined — and Justice confirmed: no single beam outweighs another in the addition.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Sunday's throne steps onto the noon arm in royal gold (#F0C420), the estate of the King, the Crown for its object — no borrowed seat: Justice already rules by daylight, the balanced scale and the sheathed sword both proof of a King who judges rather than merely reigns, the Crown for once worn rather than only implied by the empty hub.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the noon arm is Youth, the zenith of a life, and the throne wears sun-yellow (#FFE800) — Justice's own blazing register, judgment rendered at full strength before Humility's dusk ever asks the same crown to be set back down.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the center holds the crossing itself, the still point where all four arms — two solstices, two equinoxes — meet and turn. Summer, Winter, and both equinoxes revolve around a hub that claims no season for its own: Humility as geometry, Justice as the one measure all four seasons are held to equally.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the center anchors Faith's yellow arm at noon, Hope's blue at 4h, Love's red at 20h — three virtues fanning out from one root. Justice and Humility both live in that root: the hub gives each arm its color and takes no credit for what grows from it, holding the three so they can each reach outward without the center ever reaching back.

faces.ruler:
Justice is Sunday's bright face — the virtue a ruler must practice rather than merely wear, argued in every old telling from Solomon dividing the child to Ma'at's feather weighed against the heart: light and law that fall on everyone alike, without favorites. Because Sunday is the week's one double day, Justice is only half the throne's calling — the crown that judges must also answer for how it judges.

The medallion sets him enthroned in radiant white-gold under a blinding noon halo, a perfectly balanced golden scale steady in one hand, a sword sheathed and kept upright — judgment without cruelty — in the other, the field around him an engraved sunburst of fine golden rays blazing straight up from a zenith sun, the polished gold ring at the border wreathed in laurel.

His virtue is proven by the very orb his profession-face holds elsewhere on this dial: a world small enough to sit in one open hand, held rather than gripped. But that same authority is stalked by Pride, the throne mistaking a crown for something owed rather than lent — and Justice's cure runs backward across the center's own inner axis, straightening the Servant's Servility, the bent spine that needs exactly what a fair law offers it. His mood is Glory: the shield resting beside the throne rather than raised on the arm, power fully at rest, light fully at its source.
faces.servant:
Humility is Sunday's dark face — not a separate calling but Justice's own throne with the crown taken off, proof the canon has always read this figure as one king in two postures rather than two kings. The one who stands at the center of everything is the one most tempted to believe the light is his; Humility is the source remembering it is a light among lights, not the whole of it.

The badge keeps the Ruler's gold cameo and engraved sunburst exactly, laurel wreath unchanged at the border — but the field has gone to dusk and the king inside it has changed posture entirely: the same crowned figure, crown lifted from his own head and held low in both hands, eyes downcast, plain rope-belted cloth replacing the ermine, one bare foot resting in a bronze basin of water where the throne's shield once leaned.

His virtue is proven the same way Justice's was — by the object surrendered rather than the object held — but where the scale measured others, the basin measures only himself. Servility is what this same posture becomes if the spine bends without the will behind it, service that has forgotten what it is serving. Humility's cure crosses the same inner axis back the other way, answering the Ruler's own Pride — the throne's one seat that is both its own disease and its own medicine. His mood is Awe, the light failing at exactly the hour the crown comes off; Glory is simply the next plate over, the same king with the crown restored.

moon.name: Serenity
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Monday is Serenity's day — the still lake at the hour before dawn, a calm settled deep enough to hold a reflection without disturbing it. Where Tuesday burns with Wrath, Monday answers from clear across the dial: Serenity is the discipline that quells it, the night's stillness reaching over the hub to cool the day's hottest fire. That reach is this whole theme's method in miniature — no virtue stays home; each is a cure sent somewhere else on the wheel.

[[The Plate]] The medallion sits a robed figure cross-legged beside water so still the crescent moon rests unbroken on its surface, eyes closed, hands open in the lap rather than folded. The engraved sunburst field behind him is tinted serene moonlit blue rather than gold's usual blaze; the polished gold ring at the border still carries its laurel wreath, but the four enamel roundels set into it are blue, each stamped with a tiny silver crescent.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 4h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn, when nothing is left of yesterday and nothing yet of tomorrow. No hour suits Serenity better: it is the one stretch of the day that asks nothing of the mind but to sit still inside it, borrowed silver light standing in for a sun not yet risen.

[[The Lesson]] Serenity and Fear share one arm and one color, the night's two faces lit or unlit by exactly the same darkness — the difference is entirely in the mind that meets it. Straight across the dial at 16h waits Mars, and the cure runs both ways: Serenity quells Tuesday's Wrath, and Courage, in turn, cures Monday's own Fear — the calm hand steadying the burning one, the burning heart lending its nerve to the frightened one.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 4h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), Serenity's own Calm at the hour before dawn — the still lake of the base article, silver light standing in for a sun not yet risen.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps Serenity at 4h but sharpens the blue to an electric #0040FF — pigment's muted hush becomes light's brighter one. The lake stays just as still; only the moon on it burns colder and clearer.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Serenity's still lake shifts to the deep-night arm in lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8), the estate of the Scholar, the Book for its object — a natural extension of the virtue: the mind still enough to hold a reflection without disturbing it is already reading the dark the way a scholar reads a page, patient study standing in for panic.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that deep-night arm is the Unborn, and Serenity turns moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — fitting for the one virtue built on holding still: the Unborn ask nothing of the mind but patience, and Serenity is precisely the discipline of sitting inside that silvered, unproven dark without disturbing it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Serenity moves to the 6h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — sharing the spoke with Saturn's Patience. Two of the dial's quietest virtues on one arm: the stillness that waits out the night and the patience that waits out the season, tending what cannot be rushed.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Serenity at 4h but renames the arm Hope, shared with Mercury's Wisdom, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. It is the truer name for what stillness at that hour is actually doing: a calm mind before dawn is not empty, it is waiting — hope practiced quietly enough to look like rest.

mars.name: Courage
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Tuesday is Courage's day — the raised shield before the gathering storm, the nerve to stand square rather than to flee or to swing first. Its cure travels straight across the dial to Monday: Courage answers Fear, the soldier's steadiness reaching over the hub to still the healer's dread at the sickbed. It is the oldest of this theme's cross-cure pairs, the one every child's story already half-knows — courage and fear as two readings of the same raised pulse.

[[The Plate]] The badge stands a lone figure firm before a boiling sky, round shield raised high and catching ember-orange light, a spear planted upright in the ground rather than leveled at anything — a promise kept, not a threat made. The engraved sunburst field behind him is tinted ember orange; the gold ring at the border keeps its laurel wreath, its four enamel roundels orange, each bearing a tiny raised shield.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon, fire banked down to a working heat rather than a battle's opening blaze. The hour is deliberately unromantic: Courage in this canon is not the dawn charge but the discipline of still holding the line once the excitement of morning is long spent.

[[The Lesson]] Courage and Wrath are one fire pointed two ways — the shield that defends and the spear that destroys, the same heat aimed outward at what must be faced or inward at whatever the temper decides to break. Straight across the dial at 4h waits the Moon, and the cure is mutual: Courage cures Monday's Fear, and Serenity, in turn, quells Tuesday's own Wrath — the burning heart steadying the frightened one, the calm hand cooling the one that burns too hot.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), Courage's own Zeal at the working afternoon — the banked fire of the base article, hot enough to hold a line with, not yet a battle's opening blaze.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps Courage at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — red and green fused into light's hottest reading. The working fire glows brighter in Newton's world, though the hour and its Zeal stand exactly where the base article leaves them.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Courage's raised shield shifts to the 15h afternoon in copper (#C87533), the estate of the Merchant, the Coin for its object — an unlikely trade for a soldier's virtue, yet true to the anvil hour: holding the line once morning's excitement is spent is itself a currency, nerve paid out steadily rather than spent all at once in a single dramatic charge.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that same afternoon arm is Maturity, and Courage wears amber (#FFB400) — the exact fit: this canon's own Courage is never the dawn's raw nerve but the discipline of a soldier grown into the grind, holding the shield up long after the battle's first thrill has worn off.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Courage stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Venus' Love. Shield beside rose, the oldest pairing in the sky: autumn's red is harvest and blood at once, and it takes both virtues standing together to say so honestly.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Courage moves to 20h, onto Love's own red arm beside Venus, guarding the evening from 16h to 24h. It is the arm that explains the virtue at its root: courage is love wearing armor — the shield stands the night watch not for the fight's sake but for the sake of what sleeps behind it.

mercury.name: Wisdom
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Wednesday is Wisdom's day — the mind that weighs the day honestly because no one else is watching, the owl that needs no lantern and lights one anyway. Its cure runs straight up the dial's spine: Wisdom curbs Thursday's Excess overhead, the careful midnight reckoning teaching the noon feast when enough has already arrived.

[[The Plate]] The medallion perches a wise owl on a book already glowing with its own twilight script, a small lantern held aloft beside it though the page needs none of its light to be read. The engraved sunburst field is tinted deep twilight purple; the gold ring at the border keeps its laurel wreath, its four enamel roundels purple, each stamped with a tiny owl.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. Midnight is the accounting hour: the day is over, the market closed, and what is left is to weigh, honestly, what the day was actually worth. No color suits that reckoning better than the one dye that was once worth more than gold.

[[The Lesson]] Wisdom and Greed share the same arm because they share the same instrument — the scale. Wisdom is knowing the true weight of things; Greed is wanting the balance to tip. Straight up the axis at noon sits Jupiter, and the two virtues heal in both directions: Wisdom curbs Thursday's Excess, and Generosity, in turn, cures Wednesday's own Greed — the careful scale steadying the overflowing table, the open hand teaching the closed one what it is holding.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), Wisdom's own Sorrow at the closed ledger — the base article's honest reckoning, the day weighed at its grave.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns Wisdom's purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, invented by the eye where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for the mind's own virtue: at midnight it wears a color that exists only in thought, not in light itself.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Wisdom's scale shifts to the 21h evening arm in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E), the estate of the Artist, the Mask for its object — an unlikely seat for the virtue of honest weighing, yet the owl's own night-sight is itself a kind of performance done without an audience, the mask here being the composure Wisdom keeps while the rest of the day has gone to bed.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that evening arm is Old Age, and Wisdom wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — the truest fit in the set: Wisdom is accumulated exactly the way Old Age is, weighed out slowly across a whole life of honest reckonings rather than arriving all at once.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Wisdom keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and shares it with no one. The year's midnight belongs to a single virtue: the one that can sit at the bottom of the wheel, alone, and still balance the books honestly.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Wisdom off midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Serenity, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names Wisdom's quieter engine: the mind that weighs things honestly at night is the same mind that dares to hope by morning — the ledger closed in Sorrow reopens as credit extended to the day.

jupiter.name: Generosity
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Thursday is Generosity's day — the open hand pouring before any return is proven, faith practiced with the hands rather than merely professed. Its cure runs straight down the dial's spine: Generosity cures Wednesday's Greed below it, the noon table's overflow answering the midnight scale's grasping fist.

[[The Plate]] The medallion pours an open golden hand endlessly into a ring of cupped hands below it, coins and ripe wheat streaming down together, warm noon-yellow light flooding the field behind. The engraved sunburst is tinted joyful sun-yellow; the gold ring at the border keeps its laurel wreath, its four enamel roundels yellow, each bearing a tiny cornucopia.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter'. Noon is the one hour that needs no lamp of its own, the day's peak, when light is given most freely and asks nothing back for it — which is Generosity's whole ethic, stated as an hour rather than a sermon.

[[The Lesson]] Generosity and Excess share one arm because giving, left unchecked, becomes flooding — the same open royal hand that feeds a table can also drown it. Straight down the axis at midnight sits Mercury, and the cure runs in both directions: Generosity cures Wednesday's Greed, and Wisdom, in turn, curbs Thursday's own Excess — the open hand teaching the closed one to give, the careful scale teaching the open one when to stop.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), Generosity's own Joy at the day's peak — the base reading, the hour light is given away most freely.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns Generosity's noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the very summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. The virtue keeps its Joy; light simply measures the peak it gives from instead of painting it gold.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Generosity's overflowing hand shifts to the 09h morning arm in alb ivory (#EDEDE0), the estate of the Priest, the Bell for its object — a natural kinship: the open hand that pours before any return is proven is already the Priest's own discipline, faith rung out in gifts rather than merely preached.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that morning arm is Childhood, and Generosity wears spring green (#7CE577) — fitting for the virtue that gives without keeping score, the same unguarded openness a Childhood has before the world teaches it to count what it hands away.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Generosity stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — sharing it with Justice, Sunday's own bright face. The open hand and the balanced scale share the year's highest spoke: giving and fairness, on the same arm at last.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Generosity keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the arm now carries Faith rather than Joy, shared with Saturn's Patience, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The renaming fits: faith is generosity extended toward a return that has not arrived yet, the open hand's oldest wager.

venus.name: Love
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Friday is Love's day — passion given enough patience to become form, the one virtue that belongs both to this theme's cross-cure pairs and to the older triad of Faith, Hope and Love. Its cure crosses the dial to Saturday: Love dissolves Envy, warmth loosening the cold grip on what a neighbor's field is growing.

[[The Plate]] The medallion cups two hands together into a nest around one rose in full bloom, a pair of doves circling above it in sunset-red evening light. The engraved sunburst field is tinted passionate red; the gold ring at the border keeps its laurel wreath, its four enamel roundels red, each bearing a tiny rose.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, when Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour fits exactly: evening is when the day's work releases the heart, when the material world of daylight softens into its feeling one.

[[The Lesson]] Love's own shadow is Jealousy, not its opposite — the fear of losing what is already loved, sitting on the very same arm and colored the very same red. Straight across the dial at 8h waits Saturn, and the cure runs both directions: Love dissolves Saturday's Envy, and Patience, in turn, heals Friday's own Jealousy — the lover teaching the farmer to cherish his own field rather than covet another's, the farmer teaching the lover that what is worth having is worth waiting for.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), Love's own Passion at the evening — the base reading, the hour the evening star herself rises.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps Love at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is a wavelength rather than a pigment, and the evening burns straight in it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Love's nested hands shift to the 18h sunset arm in iron-blood crimson (#A02020), the estate of the Soldier, the Sword for its object — the same red the virtue already wears, now sharpened: Love's own shadow is Jealousy, the fear of losing what is loved turned defensive enough to draw a blade, the Soldier simply naming what the rose's nest was always capable of becoming.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel that sunset arm belongs to the Elder, the harvest of a life, and Love deepens to ember (#FF6A3C) — apt beyond metaphor: love given time enough to become form, the doves circling the rose, is precisely what a life's harvest is made of, patience turned finally into keeping.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Love stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Mars' Courage. Rose beside shield, as in every old myth: autumn's red is harvest and blood and passion at once, and it takes both virtues to say so.
variants.trio: Love does not move for the Trinity: its own arm is already the Trinity's Love arm, the same 20h in the same red. Courage joins it, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — one virtue keeping what is loved safe, the other keeping it remembered.

saturn.name: Patience
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Saturday is Patience's day — the farmer who sows and waits and does not stare too long at the next field, learned from a planet that takes thirty years to close one circle around the sun. Its cure crosses the dial to Friday: Patience heals Jealousy, the slow art of waiting mending the fear of losing what is loved.

[[The Plate]] The medallion kneels a farmer in a green field at dawn, planting a single seed beside a slow hourglass, its sand barely falling, while a sapling from an earlier, already-answered planting stands grown tall beside him. The engraved sunburst field is tinted renewal green; the gold ring at the border keeps its laurel wreath, its four enamel roundels green, each bearing a tiny hourglass.

[[On the Dial]] His home arm sits at dial 8h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning, dew and growth, the week renewing itself on the one day set aside for rest. No hour suits waiting better: every morning the field looks slightly different, and every seed is a small wager that the waiting will work.

[[The Lesson]] Patience and Envy share the same green because growth and its shadow are the same color seen two ways — the green of the sapling coming up, and the bitter green of watching someone else's harvest come in first. Straight across the dial at 20h waits Venus, and the cure runs both ways: Patience heals Friday's Jealousy, and Love, in turn, dissolves Saturday's own Envy — the farmer teaching the lover that what is worth having is worth waiting for, the lover teaching the farmer to love his own field rather than covet another's.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 8h arm in field green (#007E00), Patience's own Renewal at the morning — the base reading, dew on a field that looks a little different every day.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns Patience's morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than the field itself. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world Patience looks up before it looks down.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass Patience's kneeling farmer shifts to the dawn arm, 06h, in field green (#3E8914), the estate of the Farmer, the Plough for its object — the closest fit the whole table offers: the virtue was already a farmer sowing and waiting, and the Compass simply hands him the Plough his own medallion was already holding.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is Birth, and Patience wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the wager fits exactly: every seed sown is itself a small Birth not yet arrived, and Patience is the discipline of trusting the mist will lift before the sapling ever breaks the ground.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Patience stands at 6h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: sowing season, its own festival. It shares the spoke with Serenity, the Moon's own stillness — two patient virtues tending what cannot be hurried, one a season, the other a night.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Patience from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Generosity, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. It is the oldest sermon there is — sowing is faith performed with the hands, every seed a small creed buried, waited on, and believed in before it is seen.

sun.name: Pride · Servility
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Sunday keeps no arm of its own, and the Sun's shadow is double for the same reason its virtue is: Pride is what the Ruler becomes when the crown forgets it is lent rather than owed; Servility is what the Servant becomes when the bent spine forgets what it is bowing to. Two vices, one throne — argued in the same white-gold and black-and-silver as the one medallion body that carries both.

[[The Lesson]] Christian tradition already keeps a scale for exactly this kind of loss, though it usually weighs only one side at a time. Lucifer's fall was upward — pride that would not bow; Judas's was downward — a bowing that betrayed rather than served. Set both ends on the center's own beam and the double face reads as that scale: Pride is Lucifer's end of it, the self mistaking itself for the throne's source; Servility is Judas's, the self so sure it has no claim to the seat that it will sell whoever sits in it. Both empty the throne. Neither is safe merely for falling the opposite direction from the other.

[[The Plate]] The Ruler's badge catches Pride mid-lie: a crowned figure gazing into a tall gilded mirror that doubles and enlarges his own crown, peacock feathers fanned behind the throne into a second, false halo — Glory admiring itself instead of shining outward. Its dark twin, cut from the same blackened iron, shows Servility instead: a bowed figure on both knees, wrists bound by ribbons disguised as royal sashes, thin strings rising from the shoulders into the dark like a puppet's — kneeling that has stopped being chosen, the light gone before its own honest hour of Awe.

[[The Lesson]] Because nothing stands opposite the center, neither cure ever leaves it: Humility, the crown physically set down rather than merely spoken of, answers Pride from the same throne; Justice, the scale that will not bend for its own convenience, straightens Servility's slack spine back into the service of what is right rather than of whoever is asking. The center is the one place on the dial that must heal itself — which is exactly why losing it, in either direction, costs the most.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel the center keeps its old shape even in shadow: white-gold, belonging to no hour, both faces sharing the one point — Pride bright when the light is looked at rather than looked through, Servility dark when the same point stops radiating and starts merely obeying. The six arms still decompose around it; the vice, like the virtue, never becomes a spoke of its own.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around the center but cannot change what sits there: white-gold stays white-gold, because in the light world white really is every color at once — and a lie about owning all of it is exactly as total, whichever face is showing. Newton's reading only sharpens what the throne already risks.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the Sun's noon arm now wears royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of **the King**, judged by the Crown alone. Pride is exactly what happens when a King forgets the crown was only ever lent: seated in the same regalia that names the office, the sin discovers it never has more to answer for than the object it wears.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is **Youth** — life's blazing height — and the Sun wears sun-yellow (#FFE800). Pride is Youth's own delusion stretched onto a throne: the crown convinced its noon will never dim, the same blindness by which a young man mistakes his own zenith for a permanent address.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Pride stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, beside Jupiter's own Excess — the year's two overreaching sins sharing a single spoke, the doubled crown and the overflowing table both certain that abundance is a right rather than a gift. Servility has no season of its own: it lives only inside the center, never on the wheel's rim.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the center's shadow exactly where the center's light stays — around the same white-gold point, now the underside of Faith, Love and Hope all read from one throne. Pride is the wager that the throne owns the three virtues it merely radiates; Servility is the fear that it owns none of them, and so surrenders all three at once.

faces.ruler:
Pride is the Ruler's own face gone wrong, worn in the same white-gold as Justice because it costs nothing to counterfeit — the throne looks identical from outside whether the king remembers he serves or has forgotten it. It is Phaethon's error before it is anyone else's: the son who could drive the chariot for one day and mistook the ride for ownership of the sun.

The medallion catches the exact moment the lie completes itself: the crowned Ruler gazing into a tall gilded mirror that doubles and enlarges his own crown, peacock feathers fanned behind the throne into a second, false halo. He is not looking outward at the kingdom the light is meant to reach — he is looking at the light's own reflection and calling it his. On the Judas–Lucifer scale this is the upward failure, refusal dressed as majesty: the seat lost by a self too large for it rather than too small.

Its cure never leaves the room: Humility, the same king with the crown lifted from his own head, one bare foot set in a basin of water. Glory, his true mood at that hour, needs no mirror at all — it radiates without checking the reflection. Pride is not cured by a smaller throne, since there is no smaller throne to give it — only by the same hands that raised the crown learning to set it down.
faces.servant:
Servility is the Servant's own grace curdled, black with silver trim like Humility but with the will bled out of it — the same bent spine, now serving the throne instead of the truth. Where Humility kneels because kneeling is right, Servility kneels because kneeling is safe, and safety is a much smaller reason to bend.

The badge draws the difference in one detail: the kneeling looks the same as Humility's, but wrists are bound by ribbons disguised as royal sashes, thin strings running up from the shoulders into the dark like a puppet's. Nothing here was stolen — it was handed over, a little at a time, by a figure who stopped believing his own hands were worth keeping. On the Judas–Lucifer scale this is the downward failure: not refusal but surrender, the seat betrayed by someone convinced he had no claim to defend it.

Its cure crosses the center's own inner axis rather than reaching outward: Justice, the Ruler's scale that will not tip for anyone's comfort, is the only thing that can straighten a spine that has forgotten it is allowed to stand. Its true mood, Awe, is fear and wonder held in one honest look upward; Servility is what Awe becomes when the looking-up never lifts its own eyes again. It mistakes itself for the safest posture in the room — it is only the quietest way to lose the throne.

moon.name: Fear
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Fear is Monday's blue turned cold, the Moon's own dark face and, by the old reckoning, older than any other sin on the list — the unlit version of exactly the same night that, faced rather than fled, is Serenity. The physician keeping the pre-dawn watch and the frightened patient in the same bed are looking at the identical darkness; only one of them has stopped believing it will end.

[[On the Dial]] It rules the same 04h arm as Serenity, deep blue (#002FFF), because Fear is not a different hour from Calm — it is Calm's own hour lost. The small hours are when the body's watch is lowest and the mind's imagining is highest, and Fear is what happens when the second outruns the first: the shadow on the wall growing a life of its own, the guttering candle read as an omen instead of just a candle running low.

[[The Plate]] The badge shows the whole mechanism in one figure: a small hunched shape shrinking from its own shadow, cast impossibly long and monstrous by a cold blue moon, one hand clutching a candle burned down to its last flame. Nothing in the frame is actually dangerous — the moon, the wall and the candle are the physician's own instruments — which is the sin's real warning: Fear rarely needs a monster, only an honest shadow read wrong in the dark.

[[The Lesson]] Its cure stands straight across the dial: Mars' Courage, twelve hours around the wheel at 16h, the soldier's steadied nerve reaching over the center to answer the watch that has lost its footing. Courage does not put the candle out or paint over the shadow — it simply stays until dawn proves, once again, that the shape on the wall was never more than a shape.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), Fear's own pre-dawn watch — the same hour Serenity keeps, held instead by a hand that has stopped trusting the dark.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps Fear at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's muted dread becomes light's colder, more electric one. The watch is the same length; the small hours simply glare instead of glow.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the deep-night arm now burns lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of **the Scholar**, the Book its object. Fear is what that same hour becomes without the lamp: the Scholar reads the dark and finds only a shadow on the page; Fear is denied even the book, and reads the identical dark as a verdict already written against it.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the deep-night arm belongs to **the Unborn**, and the Moon takes on moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the hour of those not yet arrived and those already gone. Fear is that same threshold turned inward: not ancestors watching or children waiting, but a mind that has started fearing both, certain the dark holds a verdict from one direction or the other.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Fear moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — sharing the spoke with Saturn's Envy. Two patient vices on one arm: the sower's bitterness and the healer's dread, both the cost of professions that are supposed to know how to wait.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Fear at 04h but renames the arm's better half Hope, shared with Mercury's Greed. Fear is exactly what Hope looks like with the future subtracted — the same blue watch, kept by a hand that no longer expects the fever to break.

mars.name: Wrath
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Wrath is Tuesday's orange loosed on its own side — Mars' fire with no hand left on the reins, the shout on the war god's face where his own shield reads as ruin instead of defense. The same heat that holds a line, unaimed, simply burns whoever is standing closest, friend or enemy alike.

[[On the Dial]] It rules the same 16h arm as Courage, ember orange (#DC9600), because Wrath is not a different fire from the working afternoon's — it is that fire with the discipline knocked out of it. Zeal is the ember banked to forge with; Wrath is the same ember let loose the moment the day's grind stops feeling worth the grinding, mistaking exhaustion for justification.

[[The Plate]] The badge shows its own aftermath: a shattered shield and a broken spear crossed beneath a fist wreathed in ember flame, storm clouds boiling behind. Note what is missing — nothing is left standing to defend, because Wrath spends the very gear Courage needed to hold the line. It always feels, from inside, like strength; the wreckage in the frame is the honest report of what it actually cost.

[[The Lesson]] Its cure comes from the pre-dawn arm: Monday's Serenity, the calm that banks a furnace before it spills rather than after. Straight across the dial the physician's steady hand answers the soldier's raised one — not by extinguishing the fire, which Mars needs to keep, but by keeping a hand on it before the reins are what breaks first.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the fire of the working afternoon spent on itself instead of on the line it was meant to hold.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps Wrath at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — red and green fused into the hottest reading on the wheel. The hour is unchanged; in Newton's world the loosed fire simply shows exactly how hot it really got.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the afternoon arm now trades in copper (#C87533) — the Walk of **the Merchant**, the Coin its measure. Wrath sits oddly on a scale built to weigh fair value: the same hour that should be counting cost instead closes its fist over nothing, spending fury the way an angry man spends a coin he will never get back.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the afternoon is **Maturity**, and Mars wears working amber (#FFB400) — the age that should have already learned to hold its temper. Wrath is Maturity's oldest failure: the grip that ought to have steadied by now instead closes tighter, mistaking the exhaustion of a long day's climb for a right to burn whoever stands closest.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Wrath stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Venus' Jealousy. Mars beside Venus, the oldest pairing turned to its worst use: blood and a wilting rose on the same arm, both sins that mistake grip for strength.
variants.trio: On the Trinity, Wrath moves to 20h onto Love's red arm beside Venus. It is love's own armor gone rigid — the sentry who has forgotten what he is guarding and now simply fights, the fist raised long after the thing behind him has gone quiet.

mercury.name: Greed
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Greed is Wednesday's purple gone grasping — Mercury's own shadow, the merchant's clear-eyed understanding of value curdled into simple appetite for it. The same mind that can weigh anything correctly begins, in Greed, to weigh everything as a thing to be kept.

[[On the Dial]] It rules the same midnight arm as Wisdom, Tyrian purple (#542E85) — history's own joke on the sin, since that purple was itself the costliest pigment of the ancient world, a color that was a trade empire. Midnight is the accounting hour, when the market has closed and what remains is Sorrow's own task: weighing what the day was worth. Greed is what happens when that honest grief stops asking whether the day was fair and starts asking only whether it was enough — and the answer is never yes.

[[The Plate]] The badge's joke is exact: grasping hands clutch an overflowing chest, coin spilling uncounted through fingers too full to close around anything, a padlock still snapped shut on a lid that is already open. Nothing here is being protected — the hoard has already spilled — which is Greed's real interior logic: it does not lock the treasure, it locks the hand, unable to open even when opening would cost nothing.

[[The Lesson]] Its cure falls the whole height of the dial: Jupiter's Generosity at noon, the open hand answering the closed one across the full vertical spine. The merchant is not asked to stop counting — counting well is Wisdom's own gift — only to remember that a scale is for finding the fair weight, not for making sure none of it leaves the table.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the closed-fist reading of the ledger the base article tells whole.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns Greed's purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue in no rainbow, invented by the eye where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting: at midnight the hoarding mind wears a color that, like its own math, exists only in the wanting.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the evening arm now drapes in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of **the Artist**, the Mask its craft. Greed is what happens when that same quick mind stops performing for an audience and starts performing only for itself: the Mask kept on long after the curtain falls, a clever face still calculating what it can keep rather than what it can give.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the evening is **Old Age**, and Mercury wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — the hour for totaling what a life has actually been worth. Greed is Old Age's refusal to close the ledger honestly: the hand that should be settling its accounts instead keeps counting, unable to call any total, however large, enough.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Greed keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — alone, sharing it with no one. Even its cure has no partner here: the one sin that can sit at the bottom of the wheel by itself and still refuse to balance the books.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Greed off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Fear. It is hope's own arithmetic gone wrong — not faith that tomorrow provides, but the refusal to believe it will unless everything is already counted and kept.

jupiter.name: Excess
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Excess is Thursday's yellow overflowing — Jupiter's own vice, generosity that never learned where the floor is. The royal table that fed everyone becomes the royal table that cannot stop being spread, past the guest's hunger and past the host's own good sense.

[[On the Dial]] It rules the same noon arm as Generosity, bright yellow (#F8E600), because Excess is not a different gift from the one Jupiter gives at noon — it is that same gift with no one left minding when it should stop. Noon's own mood is Joy, which asks nothing back for its light; Excess is what happens when a mood that asks nothing back also forgets to notice it has already given everything worth giving.

[[The Plate]] The badge shows the argument already lost: a banquet table collapsing under its own plenty, a golden goblet spilling wine that never stops, a bloated figure slumped back in gluttonous exhaustion. The table is not attacked from outside — it fails from its own weight, which is Excess's real warning: a gift with no measure eventually cannot be received by anyone, least of all the one still trying to give it.

[[The Lesson]] Its cure rises from the opposite pole: Mercury's Wisdom at midnight, the deep counting mind teaching the high, giving hand when enough has already been reached. Generosity does not need to shrink to be cured — it needs the merchant's honest ledger, the one discipline that can tell a king the difference between a full table and a collapsing one.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the overflow of the base article's own collapsing table.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns Excess's noon to green (#00DC00) — the brightest wavelength the eye can hold. The hour keeps its overflow; light simply measures exactly how far past the peak the table has already gone.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the morning arm now wears alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of **the Priest**, the Bell calling the hour. Excess is the Priest who never stops ringing it: the same open hand that blesses freely keeps giving long after the blessing has already landed, generosity that has forgotten a sermon, like a bell, is supposed to end.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the morning is **Childhood**, and Jupiter wears spring green (#7CE577) — the age that has not yet learned where enough sits. Excess is Childhood's own hunger left uncorrected: the gift asked for again and again, not from want but from a mind too young still to notice the table is already full.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Excess stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, beside the Sun's own Pride — the year's two overreaching sins on one spoke, the overflowing table and the doubled crown both certain that too much is simply proof of how much there is to give.
variants.trio: On the Trinity, Excess keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the arm now reads Faith, shared with Saturn's Envy. It is faith that has stopped waiting for its own harvest: a table set before the seed has even had its season, generosity spent on a promise that has not yet come due.

venus.name: Jealousy
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Jealousy is Friday's red curdled — not love's opposite but its own shadow, the fear of losing what is already held. It begins exactly where Love does, in caring enough that the caring matters, and goes wrong only in the next step: caring so much that holding on replaces trusting.

[[On the Dial]] It rules the same evening arm as Love, deep red (#B60000), because Jealousy is not a colder feeling than Love — it is the same warm one, Passion itself, with a grip added. Evening is when the day's work softens into its emotional life, the theatre lights coming up on whatever the heart actually wants; Jealousy is what happens when that same heart starts rehearsing the ways it might lose the thing it wants, until the rehearsal becomes the relationship.

[[The Plate]] The badge shows the grip made visible: a serpent coiled possessively around a rose already wilting inside it, one suspicious eye watching from behind a half-drawn curtain. The rose was never in danger from outside — it is wilting from the coil itself, which is Jealousy's real warning: the thing it is trying hardest to keep is the thing its own grip is killing.

[[The Lesson]] Its cure crosses from the morning arm: Saturn's Patience, insecurity outwaited rather than argued with. The two slow arts, waiting and loving, mend each other — Patience does not ask the heart to care less, only to hold the rose the way the farmer holds a season: certain enough of the harvest that it does not need to squeeze the stem to prove it is still there.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the evening's own warmth turned to a grip in the base article's reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps Jealousy at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. The feeling loses none of its heat by being named more precisely; if anything the coil shows plainer.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the sunset arm now wears iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of **the Soldier**, the Sword drawn to defend. Jealousy is love conscripted into that same posture: the heart standing guard over what it already holds, sword raised against a rival that is, more often than not, only its own fear of losing the thing it is failing to trust.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the sunset arm is **the Elder** — the harvest of a whole life gathered in — and Venus wears ember (#FF6A3C). Jealousy is the Elder who cannot yet trust the harvest is in: still rehearsing every way the gathered love might be taken, unable to let the ember simply glow instead of being watched.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Jealousy stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Mars' Wrath. Venus beside Mars at their worst: the rose gripped and the fist raised are the same failure of the same old pairing, love and war both mistaking force for care.
variants.trio: Jealousy does not move on the Trinity: Love's arm is its own 20h in its own red, the sin that never has to travel to find its virtue — only to remember it. The Soldier's Wrath keeps the same spoke, guarding the same dark hours with the same fire aimed the wrong way.

saturn.name: Envy
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Envy is Saturday's green soured — Saturn's own bitter shadow, and by the old reckoning the oldest failure of anyone who has learned to wait. It starts as Patience's own eye, the one that can see how another's field is doing, and goes wrong in the next breath: watching becomes wanting, and wanting becomes resentment that the watching was ever necessary at all.

[[On the Dial]] It rules the same morning arm as Patience, field green (#007E00), because Envy is not a different color from growth — it is Renewal's own hue looked at sideways, over a fence, at a stalk that is not one's own. The farmer's whole faith is that waiting works; Envy is what happens when the same farmer starts measuring his wait against someone else's harvest instead of his own soil.

[[The Plate]] The badge draws the wall low on purpose — easy to see over, impossible to cross with dignity: a gaunt figure peering at a neighbor's greener field, eyes faintly green-tinged, one hand reaching through the fence toward wheat that stays, on principle, just out of grasp. The wheat was never guarded to spite him; the fence is only as tall as his own attention keeps making it.

[[The Lesson]] Its cure reaches from the evening: Venus' Love, warmth dissolving the cold arithmetic of what someone else has. Love does not lower the neighbor's fence or raise the farmer's harvest — it simply gives the farmer something to tend that is worth more, to him, than what is on the other side of it.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the morning's own growth soured into the fence-line stare of the base article.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns Envy's morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than the field. In the light world the sin still looks outward before it looks down, up over the fence before down at its own row.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the dawn arm keeps field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of **the Farmer**, the Plough turning his own soil. Envy is the Farmer who has stopped looking at his own furrow: patience curdled into a fence-line stare at a neighbor's greener stalks, the plough still in hand but pointed, in spirit, at ground that was never his to work.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is **Birth**, and Saturn wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the hour before anything has proven itself. Envy is Birth measured against a birth not its own: resentment already forming before the first light has even cleared, certain someone else's dawn arrived with a better field attached.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Envy stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — sharing the spoke with the Moon's Fear. Two patient professions' failures on one arm: the grower's grudge and the healer's dread, both the cost of professions that are supposed to know how to wait and, this once, didn't.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Envy from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Jupiter's Excess. It is faith that has stopped believing its own seed is enough — envy dressed as ambition, wanting the height of someone else's table instead of trusting the height of its own field.

sun.name: Glory · Awe
sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Sunday alone is doubled, and this theme's whole system rests on that seam: at the wheel's center sit not one mood but two, Glory bright and Awe dark, the same throne wearing two lights. The plate that carries Glory shows the sun at its exact zenith, a crowned figure standing in the blinding noon halo and casting no shadow at all, arms thrown open to the light it has become; the plate beside it drops to the bottom rim, where a servant kneels low, face lifted, one hand half-raised against the glare, watching that same sun blaze from far underneath, his own shadow stretched long behind him. Every other day of the week gets one mood and one hour. Sunday gets a mood that has no hour, doubled into a mood that has no hour either — only a direction to look.

[[On the Dial]] Both faces share the same address on the dial: the center, which belongs to no arm and therefore to no clock-hour at all. Glory is not noon, though it wears noon's own gold — it is what every hour looks like at its most complete, not one hour but the sum of all of them seen at their best. Awe keeps the same seat but wears it from underneath, in black with a thin silver trim rather than a color of its own, because a servant does not get to redecorate the throne he is kneeling in front of.

This is the axis every other opposite pair on the dial has to build across twelve hours of arm — Calm answering Zeal, Sorrow answering Joy, Passion answering Renewal, each mood facing its opposite from the far side of the wheel. Glory and Awe cannot do that; nothing stands opposite the center, so their opposition has nowhere to travel to. It collapses into one figure looked at from two heights: Glory is being seen, Awe is seeing. Psychology's own wheel agrees down to the compound — fear plus surprise is exactly Plutchik's own recipe for awe, the feeling of standing under something larger than yourself — and the dial keeps the same casting: whoever holds the center is watched from below, and whoever watches from below keeps the center honest.

[[The Hour]] That is also why the pair has no hour to rule the way Calm rules four o'clock or Joy rules noon. Every mood the week passes through — noon's Joy, midnight's Sorrow, the Renewal of Saturday morning — is a version of the same light the center already holds in full, and Glory's discipline is to sit under all of them without spending itself on any one. Awe is the mood that remembers to look up while it happens.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel Glory keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are its own light taken apart, every rim mood a fraction of what the center holds whole. This is the pair's home reading — the source, not a spoke — with Awe waiting, unseen, at the same address.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around it but cannot touch the center: Glory stays white-gold, because in the light world it is literally true that white is the sum of every color. Newton's reading only proves what the throne already claimed of itself.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the noon arm crowns itself in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of **the King**, the Crown its whole office. Glory is the King seen at that exact crown, majesty owned rather than merely worn; Awe is the same throne watched from below, the servant's own gaze finding a Crown too bright to meet head-on.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is **Youth** — the blazing zenith of a life — and the Sun wears sun-yellow (#FFE800). Glory is Youth at its own peak, unspent and certain of itself; Awe is the same zenith glimpsed from beneath, the feeling of standing under a height not yet one's own to claim.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Glory stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice — the sun's own festival — sharing the spoke with Joy itself. Two golds on one arm: the mood with no hour and the mood that owns noon, holding the year's longest light together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity, Glory keeps the center, and the court beneath it narrows to three arms: Faith's yellow carrying Joy and Renewal, Love's red carrying Zeal and Passion, Hope's blue carrying Sorrow and Calm. Six rim hours folded into three spokes under one unmoved white-gold sum — the oldest argument the center ever made, read here in moods instead of virtues.

faces.ruler:
Glory is Sunday's bright face, the one mood on the wheel that is not tied to any hour because it does not need to wait for one — it is noon's own gold read at its most complete, the sun at its exact zenith with a crowned figure standing in the halo and casting no shadow at all, arms thrown open to a light it has stopped resisting.

The plate keeps the family's silver cameo and its gradient sky-disc, but at its brightest reading: pure white-gold blazing at the very center, fading outward to a deep noon blue at the rim, a polished silver ring worked with the family's sundial hour-ticks running around the edge, four small white-gold enamel roundels marking the sun at its own zenith.

Glory's opposite is Awe, and the two need each other more than any of the six paired hours do, because they cannot even stand across the dial from one another — the center has no far side. Glory is being seen; Awe is seeing. It is also, without needing an hour of its own, secretly present at every one of the day's other peaks: it is what noon's Joy is reaching for, what midnight's Sorrow has temporarily lost, the light the whole week is measured against.
faces.servant:
Awe is Sunday's dark face, kept at the same center as Glory but read from the opposite height: the plate drops to the bottom rim, where a servant kneels low, eyes wide, one hand half-raised to shield them, watching the same sun blaze at the zenith from directly below — fear and wonder held in a single upturned look, his own shadow, unlike the ruler's, stretching long behind him.

The badge keeps the family's silver cameo and sky-disc gradient, but reads it inverted: pale silver-blue pooling at the base where the servant kneels, climbing to one blinding point of white-gold high in the field where the sun still burns, a polished silver ring with the same sundial hour-ticks, small black-enamel roundels trimmed in silver bearing a tiny upturned crescent.

Its opposite is Glory, and psychology already keeps the recipe for the distance between them: fear plus surprise compound, in Plutchik's own wheel, into exactly this feeling — the sense of standing under something larger than yourself.
