Glory is being seen, Awe is seeing, and the dial's own logic is that a center watched from below stays honest; take away the servant's upturned face and the ruler's light would have nothing left to answer to.

moon.name: Calm
moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Calm rides Monday's blue, the temperature of a world still asleep. The plate holds the whole mood in a single standing leg: a heron motionless at the blue hour before sunrise, a crescent moon and the morning's first star mirrored on water that has not yet decided to move — no ripple, no wingbeat, nothing that would count as an event.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 4h in deep blue (#002FFF), the stillest hour of the whole night. The placement is not gentle — it is the hardest test the mood has: four in the morning is where yesterday is fully spent and tomorrow has not yet arrived, and the water only holds its stillness because nothing has come along yet to ask it to move.

Its opposite is Zeal, twelve hours around the dial on Tuesday's orange at 16h — the one other mood measured by how a body holds itself, a heron's stillness against a blacksmith's motion. Each is the picture of the other's absence: Zeal is effort with nowhere left to hide it, Calm is effort that has, for one hour, nowhere left to go.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because that is where stillness is worth the most and costs the most: the darkest, longest stretch of the night, when it would be easiest to panic and the heron does not. The dial's map of feeling puts this hour first for a reason — every day's other six moods get to move; this one's whole argument is that, for one hour, nothing has to.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 4h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), Calm at the hour before dawn — the heron's stillness, as the base reading holds it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps Calm at 4h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's hushed night becomes light's more electric one. The hour and the stillness both hold; only the water's color changes under it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the deep-night arm now reads by lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of **the Scholar**, the Book held open in the stillest hour. Calm is exactly the Scholar's own condition: a mind awake while the rest of the night sleeps, untroubled because it has found something steady to hold its attention rather than its fear.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the deep-night arm belongs to **the Unborn**, and the Moon wears moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the hour of those not yet arrived and those already gone. Calm is the stillness that hour asks of anyone caught between two lives not their own: the heron's steadiness borrowed by a mind simply waiting, unhurried, for whichever life comes next.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Calm moves to the 6h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — sharing the spoke with Renewal. Two patient moods on one arm: the stillness that waits and the growth that is the waiting's reward, standing together at the hour when night finally lets go.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Calm at 4h but renames the arm: blue becomes Hope, shared with Sorrow, who moves up from midnight to join it, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. No pairing reads truer — the stillest hour and the darkest hour turn out to want the same thing from the dawn.

mars.name: Zeal
mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Zeal rides Tuesday's orange, the ember of the working afternoon rather than noon's blaze or evening's open flame. The plate stacks two kinds of exertion in one frame: a blacksmith's hammer striking sparks off molten iron and a runner mid-stride trailing embers past the same anvil, forge-work and foot-work sharing one fire.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600) — past noon's freshness, before evening lets the day go. The placement is deliberately unglamorous: this is not the dawn charge or the victory lap, it is the hour when the excitement has already worn off and the work is still not finished.

Its opposite is Calm, twelve hours around the dial on Monday's blue at 4h — a heron's stillness against a hammer's motion, and each mood is what the other one is not doing. Where Calm is a body holding itself perfectly still, Zeal is a body that has found a rhythm it can sustain past the point where enthusiasm alone would have quit.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the afternoon because that is when the mood is actually earned: anyone can feel it at dawn, when the day is still a promise; the harder trick is still feeling it at four, past noon's glory and short of evening's passion, with nothing left but the discipline of continuing.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), Zeal at the height of the working afternoon — the banked fire of the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps Zeal at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — red and green fused in light where they only sat side by side in pigment. The hour and its Zeal hold; the fire simply reads hotter.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the afternoon arm now trades in copper (#C87533) — the Walk of **the Merchant**, the Coin weighed fairly. Zeal is the same discipline turned to labor rather than ledger: the steady, unglamorous push of an afternoon that keeps working because the work, like a fair trade, is worth finishing properly rather than abandoning halfway.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the afternoon is **Maturity**, and Mars wears working amber (#FFB400) — the age that has traded youth's blaze for something that lasts longer. Zeal fits that age exactly: not the first excitement of a young effort but the steadier burn of someone who has learned how to keep working past the hour enthusiasm alone would have quit.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Zeal moves to the 18h arm — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Passion. The oldest pairing in the sky, read in moods instead of gods: autumn's red belongs to the ember and the flame both, effort and desire meeting on one arm.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves Zeal from 16h to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Passion, which does not move — and together the two guard the evening from 16h to 24h. The pairing explains itself: effort sustained long enough starts to look like love; Zeal is simply Passion before it admits what it is working for.

mercury.name: Sorrow
mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Sorrow rides Wednesday's purple, red mourning dissolved into blue stillness rather than despair outright. The plate holds it in a single fallen drop: a cloaked figure bowed beneath a midnight sky, one tear shaped like an amethyst falling toward water dark enough to have nearly stopped reflecting anything at all.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in deep Tyrian purple (#542E85), history's own quiet joke on the mood: the costliest pigment the ancient world ever made, worn at the one hour when nothing is being bought or sold. Midnight is the accounting hour of feeling the same way it is the accounting hour of trade — the day is over, and what is left is to weigh what it cost.

Its opposite is Joy, twelve hours around the dial on Thursday's yellow at noon — the day's two extremes of light, one at its brightest and one gone entirely. Where Joy scatters outward, arms thrown wide, Sorrow gathers inward, one bowed figure and one falling drop; each mood is what the other spends all its light avoiding.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow rules midnight because every honest day closes there: the grave of the day's light, where whatever the day was worth is finally told apart from what it merely looked like. It is not despair — it is depth, the mind's own hour, thought running on alone after the world outside it has gone quiet.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), Sorrow at the hour of the closed ledger — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns the purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents only where the spectrum's two ends meet. A fitting hour for Sorrow to wear a color that exists only in the mind that is doing the mourning.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the evening arm now drapes in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of **the Artist**, the Mask its instrument. Sorrow is the Mask taken off after the performance ends: the evening's own quiet reckoning, feeling weighed rather than performed, the same violet that dressed the stage now dressing the private grief left when the audience has gone.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the evening is **Old Age**, and Mercury wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — the hour for weighing what a whole life has amounted to. Sorrow belongs there honestly: not despair but depth, the accounting a long life owes itself once the day's noise has finally gone quiet enough to hear the total.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Sorrow keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and shares it with no other mood at all. The year's own midnight belongs to exactly one figure: the mood that can sit at the bottom of the wheel, alone, and still keep its composure.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Sorrow off midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Calm, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The move names the mood's real secret: the ledger that closes in sorrow at midnight reopens in hope by morning, because grief that does not expect an answer is only despair wearing a nicer coat.

jupiter.name: Joy
jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Joy rides Thursday's yellow, the day's own generosity given a shape. The plate scatters rather than shines: a figure laughing with arms thrown wide beneath a golden noon sun, bursts of light breaking off them like fireworks that never quite land, because the light is not being aimed at anything — it is simply too much to keep in one place.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600) — "jovial" means, literally, of Jupiter — and noon is the one hour of the day that needs no lamp: the peak, when light is given away more freely than at any other point on the wheel.

Its opposite is Sorrow, twelve hours around the dial on Wednesday's purple at midnight — noon's brightest reading answered by the one hour with none at all. Where Sorrow gathers a single figure inward around one falling drop, Joy throws its own figure's arms open and lets the light scatter outward without keeping any of it back.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon for the same reason the sun does: it is the hour that asks nothing back for its own light. Every other mood on the wheel is earned by an hour's particular hardship — Calm's stillness, Zeal's grind, Sorrow's weight — but noon simply gives, and Joy is what receiving that gift, undeserved and unrepayable, feels like.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), Joy at the day's own peak — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the very summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye can register. Joy keeps its hour; the light world simply measures the peak instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the morning arm now wears alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of **the Priest**, the Bell ringing the hour freely for anyone within earshot. Joy is that same open-handed ringing given a mood instead of an office: light given away because morning asks nothing back for it, the bell's plain gladness needing no congregation to justify itself.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the morning is **Childhood**, and Jupiter wears spring green (#7CE577) — the age that has not yet learned to measure its own delight. Joy is Childhood's native condition: laughter thrown wide before anyone has taught it to be careful with its own light, the gift of a life not yet old enough to hold anything back.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Joy stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice, sharing the spoke with Glory itself. Two golds on the year's highest arm: the mood that owns noon and the mood that has no hour, holding the longest light of the year together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity, Joy keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, and Joy shares the arm with Renewal, who moves up to join it from the morning, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The reading explains itself: Joy is what faith looks like once it has already been rewarded.

venus.name: Passion
venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Passion rides Friday's red, the sky itself turned the color of the mood as the evening star opens over the sunset. The plate is caught mid-gesture rather than at rest: two dancers turning in flame-red silk against a burning horizon, hands reaching toward each other and never quite arriving, because passion's whole tension is the reach, not the meeting.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the evening — the exact hour Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour fits the mood precisely: evening is when the day's work lets go of the heart it was holding all along, when the material world of the daylight hours softens into its own feeling.

Its opposite is Renewal, twelve hours around the dial on Saturday's green at 8h — one mood reaching toward a partner across a burning sky, the other reaching down into soil that has not yet produced anything to reach back. Passion wants what is already in front of it; Renewal waits for what has not arrived yet.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because passion is what the daylight hours were always for: the day earns, and the evening is where all of that earning finally gets to be felt rather than spent. The dancers' hands not quite touching are the whole argument in one image — the mood is not the arrival, it is the entire distance still being crossed.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), Passion at the evening star's own hour — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps Passion at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, with nothing mixed into it. In Newton's world Passion is not a pigment but a wavelength, and the evening simply burns in it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the sunset arm now wears iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of **the Soldier**, the Sword raised in earnest. Passion borrows the same readiness for a gentler war: the evening's whole reaching toward another person fought with exactly the Soldier's nerve, a heart that advances on what it wants with a blade's own certainty, if not its violence.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the sunset arm is **the Elder** — a life's harvest gathered in — and Venus wears ember (#FF6A3C). Passion at that age is memory more than reach: the dancers' hands from years of evenings still not quite touching, the same fire banked into a warmer, quieter glow that remembers wanting more clearly than it still wants.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Passion stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Zeal. Autumn's red belongs to both moods at once, effort and desire meeting on the year's own turning point.
variants.trio: Passion does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is its own 20h in its own red, since love is this mood's whole material in both readings at once. Zeal joins it there, and together the two guard the evening from 16h to 24h — one mood keeping what is wanted safe, the other keeping the wanting itself alive.

saturn.name: Renewal
saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Renewal rides Saturday's green, dew and growth on turned soil rather than any grander gesture. The plate needs only one small fact to make its case: a single sprout breaking through dew-soaked morning earth, sunrise gilding the freshly plowed field it grew from — nothing dramatic, just a thing that was buried, coming up anyway.

[[On the Dial]] Its home arm sits at dial 8h in field green (#007E00), the morning. The paradox is the whole point: the slowest planet in the sky is given the freshest hour of the day, because no mood on the wheel understands better than this one that mornings keep coming back regardless of how long the wait before them felt.

Its opposite is Passion, twelve hours around the dial on Friday's red at 20h — one mood reaching toward a partner it can already see, the other trusting toward a harvest it cannot see yet. Where Passion is the feeling of wanting, Renewal is the feeling of having waited long enough that wanting has turned into something steadier.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because that is where the mood's whole faith actually lives: every morning the field looks slightly different than it did the day before, and every seed sown into it is a small bet that the waiting will have been worth it — a bet this mood keeps making, on schedule, every single week.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 8h arm in field green (#007E00), Renewal at the morning — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns the morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than the field itself. Renewal keeps its hour; in the light world it simply looks up before it looks down.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the dawn arm keeps field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of **the Farmer**, the Plough already turning new ground. Renewal is the Farmer's whole creed made into a mood: the field looking slightly different every morning, and the plough returning to it anyway, certain that what was buried is already on its way back up.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is **Birth**, and Saturn wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the hour before anything has had the chance to prove itself yet. Renewal and Birth are nearly the same wager: a field sown on faith and a life begun on none, both betting that the mist will lift into something worth the waiting.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Renewal stands at 6h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: sowing season, its own festival — sharing the spoke with Calm. Two patient moods together at the hour night finally lets go: the growth that waits and the stillness that waited for it.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Renewal from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Joy, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. The oldest sermon there is, read as mood instead of virtue: growth performed patiently enough starts to look exactly like joy that arrived on time.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Zeus is the storm made into law — the sky-father who ended the age of the Titans and then had to learn to govern the unruly family he freed. The drawing gives him the full regalia of that office: crowned and enthroned, a golden thunderbolt gripped in one fist and an eagle-topped scepter in the other, eagles carved into the armrests and a live one circling above, behind the throne an oak heavy with acorns — the tree of his oracle at Dodona, where the god spoke through rustling leaves. The Planetary roster hung this plate on Thursday; the Pantheon reads rank — the king of the gods takes the throne of the week.

[[On the Dial]] On the six-armed dial he takes no arm at all: Sunday sits in the center, colored white-gold. This is the founding sentence of the whole design — white light is the sum of all colors, so the six arm-hues around him are one kingdom decomposed: the star is the prism, and the throne is what goes into it. Olympus drawn as optics — six provinces radiating from one seat, each day one color of the king's light.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Justice, the guest-law he enforces precisely because he is bound by it himself, and his vice is Pride, the myths its catalog: a hundred disguises, the king believing the rules could bend around him where the oath could not. His cure crosses no axis; the center heals itself. Yet the center is DOUBLE, two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat, argued here by blood: the throne above wears Justice, Pride and Glory, while the throne below — his brother Hades, this Sunday's shadow face — wears Humility, Servility and Awe. Three brothers drew lots for the world; the dial refuses to forget the sky and the underworld came out of the same urn.

[[The Hour]] That is also why he rules no single hour. Glory is his weather: noon's Joy is the court in session, evening's Passion the feast after it, pre-dawn Calm a kingdom asleep under one law — every mood on the wheel is the throne's weather at a different angle.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six moods radiate. The king who spent the Planetary roster on Thursday's crown now takes the hub itself — Sunday does not sit among the days; the days sit around Sunday, the way the Olympians sit around one throne.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette nothing about him changes — still the center, still white-gold. Every arm around him translates from pigment to beam, but white is the sum in both worlds; the throne is the one point where the two readings agree — fitting for the keeper of oaths, whose word is supposed to bind in every world it is spoken in.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the noon arm crowns itself in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of **the King**, the Crown its very office — and no seat on the wheel fits its occupant more plainly than this one fits Zeus. The king of Olympus needs no argument for the King's estate; the Crown he already wears on every other reading is simply, here, the wheel's own object as well as his own.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is **Youth** — life at its blazing height — and Zeus wears sun-yellow (#FFE800), the same brightness he threw off as a boy hidden from his father's own hunger. Youth suits the god who overthrew a whole generation of Titans before he ever sat still enough to become the aging Sky-Father the later myths remember.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross his arm at 12h is Summer, colored yellow, and he shares it with Apollo. The cross arms point at the solstices and equinoxes: this is the midsummer arm, the year's longest light, where the throne and the son who answers on its behalf hold the peak of the year together — the same summit they have always shared, read now from the father's side.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Zeus keeps the center, and the star around him thins to three arms — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue, the painter's own primaries. The founding sentence holds at its simplest for the keeper of oaths: three virtues instead of six moods — three things a king swears to uphold — and all of them still one white light taken apart.

faces.ruler:
Zeus is the Ruler face of the Sunday throne by the oldest right a king can claim — not a borrowed sun but the sky-father who ended the age of the Titans and then had to learn to govern the unruly family he freed, the throne every other Olympian throne answers to.

The medallion holds him crowned and enthroned, a golden thunderbolt gripped in one fist and an eagle-topped scepter in the other, eagles carved into the throne's armrests and a live one circling above, an oak tree heavy with acorns rising behind the throne — the same bronze-and-gold relief the whole Greek set wears, the laurel-wreath meander running the border whole.

His virtue is Justice, the law of guest and host he enforces on every mortal precisely because he is bound by it himself — the king who cannot break the oath without breaking the throne under him — but that same authority tempts Pride, and the myths are a catalog of it: a hundred disguises taken because the king believed the rules could bend around him even where the oath could not. His mood is Glory, the storm parting to gold light, the throne at its fullest hour — noon with a crown on.
faces.servant:
Hades is the Servant face, not a lesser god but Zeus's own brother, drawing the throne no one else wanted when the three of them split the world by lot — proof that the dial's shadow face is not evil, only the same royal blood facing the other direction.

The plate keeps the Sunday set's own bronze-and-gold finish and laurel-meander ring, but the scene is lightless: Hades enthroned on a dark iron seat inside a starless cavern, the horned helm of invisibility held loosely in one hand rather than worn, his other hand resting on Cerberus's middle head with the two flanking heads turned watchfully outward, the key of the underworld's gate hanging heavy at his belt, the Styx cut in low relief winding into total dark behind the throne — the border's roundels carrying the same Sun glyph his brother's throne wears above.

His virtue is Humility — of the three brothers, only Hades never left his kingdom to scheme for a better one, ruling the worse-esteemed lot as faithfully as Zeus rules the visible sky — but that same constancy risks Servility, mistaken by outsiders for mere guarding rather than reigning. His mood is Awe, an empty throne room's hush, dark majesty rather than terror — Glory's own far side, the same crown worn where no one claps.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Poseidon is the sea as a person — not a god who rules water from somewhere dry but the water itself given a throne, one of three brothers who drew lots for the world and kept the two-thirds that move. The drawing shows him rising chest-deep where a calm sea has just gone to storm around him: a great trident gripped in one fist, his beard and mane running with seawater, a full moon riding low behind him with a visible tide-line drawn taut between the disc and the swell, dolphins circling in the calm nearside water while breakers crash on the far side, a wrecked hull's ribs showing where the storm has already passed through. The Greeks called him Earth-Shaker as often as Sea-Lord — the same restlessness under both titles.

[[On the Dial]] His home on the hexa paint dial is the arm at 4h, deep blue, the hour before dawn — Calm. The lower half of the dial is the night, and 4h is its stillest chamber; but Poseidon's claim to that hour is not borrowed the way it was for Selene, it is physical: the Moon literally drags his tides, twice a day, whether he consents or not. Blue is the sea's own color before any myth is added to it, and silver is his metal, crowning the dial's midnight tip the way gold crowns its noon.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Serenity and his vice is Fear, and no figure on the dial earns that pair more literally: the same water is glass at dawn and a grave by dusk. He is the Physician all the same — the cult title Poseidon Asphaleios, "the Securer," was invoked exactly as a healer is invoked, to steady what shakes, whether a ship's deck or the ground itself. Across the dial stands Artemis: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath — the huntress and the sea-lord bound to one axis.

[[The Hour]] Calm rules the hour before dawn because that is where the sea is tested honestly: no wind yet to prove it gentle, no storm yet to prove it cruel — only the water, and whatever is trusted to cross it.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 4h arm, painter's deep blue, Monday's Calm in the hour before dawn — the sea-lord keeping the stillest watch of the night, the one hour even his own tides seem briefly to hold their breath before turning again, patient the way only something that has never once missed a turning can afford to be.
variants.hexa_light: The same 4h arm, but the blue turns electric — in the light wheel, blue is one of the three primaries of light itself, not a mixture poured from other colors. Calm read through the prism is bedrock, not blend: the sea's own stillness taken down to the one wavelength that makes it blue at all.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the deep-night arm now reads by lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of **the Scholar**, the Book its instrument — an odd seat for a sea-god until the trident is set down: Poseidon Earth-Shaker studies the deep the way a scholar studies a text he can never fully close, tides worked out by patient calculation long before any storm proves the sum correct.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the deep-night arm belongs to **the Unborn**, and Poseidon wears moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the hour of ancestors and of those not yet come. The sea already keeps both: every wrecked hull returned to it and every ship not yet launched across it, the god of the water simply presiding over the one arm built from what has not yet happened as much as what already has.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he holds the 6h arm — Spring, green — and shares it with Demeter. The arm points at the spring equinox, the year's own dawn: the sea-lord who steadies what shakes and the grain-mother whose daughter has just come back up share the year's morning between them.
variants.trio: The Trinity leaves Poseidon on his 4h arm but renames the post: the blue now means Hope, shared with Athena, and with the pre-dawn hour at its very tip it guards the morning third, 0h to 8h. The Securer who steadies the shaking ground now guards the hour that promises the shaking will stop — calm turned to face what comes.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Artemis is the hunt without the parade the way her brother rules the light without the throne — a virgin huntress the other Olympians learned early not to provoke. The drawing catches her mid-stride through dense forest, bow drawn to full draw and an arrow already loosed toward the edge of the frame, a quiver on her back and hunting boots laced to the knee, a stag frozen mid-leap just ahead of the arrow's path, hounds coursing at her side, antlers and a hunter's net hung as trophies on a tree behind her — the net a quiet joke on every hunter who ever thought himself the one hunting. Actaeon learned that lesson by becoming the stag himself; Niobe learned it by losing every child she had boasted of to Artemis's arrows and Apollo's together.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the arm at 16h, orange, the working afternoon — Tuesday's Zeal. Orange is fire domesticated, and the hunt is exactly that: not the single dramatic kill but the hours of the stalk before it, the patient tracking that makes the strike look effortless when it finally comes. 16h is the soldier's drill-and-march hour, and the hunt is a soldier's craft in every particular but the uniform.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Courage and her vice is Wrath, and the bow that feeds a village is the same bow that answers an insult with annihilation — Actaeon's hounds and Niobe's children are Tuesday's fire pointed the wrong way. Her cure hangs straight across the dial in Poseidon's blue: Serenity quells Wrath, while her own Courage, in return, cures his Fear — the huntress and the sea-lord healing each other along one axis.

[[The Hour]] Zeal rules the working afternoon because the hunt is rarely won in the first hour: it is won by whoever is still moving quietly when the light starts to fail.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 16h arm, ember orange, Tuesday's Zeal of the working afternoon — the huntress keeping the hour of the patient stalk rather than the hour of the kill, when the bow is drawn but nothing has been loosed yet — patience with an edge, which is what the ember always was underneath the blaze.
variants.hexa_light: The same 16h arm, but the prism has no pigment orange to offer — the ember flares to full yellow, red and green light summed to their brightest. Zeal read as light rather than paint is louder and harder to look away from: the stalk lit up as if the forest itself were watching.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the afternoon arm now trades in copper (#C87533) — the Walk of **the Merchant**, the Coin its measure of worth — a stranger seat for a huntress than the Soldier's used to be, yet no goddess reads a fair value more exactly: Artemis takes only what a village needs and no more, the hunt itself a kind of honest accounting between what the forest can give and what is owed back to it.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the afternoon is **Maturity**, and Artemis wears working amber (#FFB400) — patience over blaze, the steady stalk that makes the strike look effortless only because the discipline came first. No huntress on the dial earns that age more plainly: the bow drawn full is not a young hand's excitement but an old hand's certainty.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she stands at 18h — Autumn, red — and shares the arm with Hera. It points at the autumn equinox, the year's dusk: the huntress who answers every insult in full and the queen who answers every rival the same way hold the season of the falling leaf together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Artemis moves to 20h and stands on Love's red arm beside Hera. Together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h. Love reads through the huntress as its watch kept armed: the same bow that punished Actaeon stands sentry now so that what the queen keeps can sleep undisturbed.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Athena is the mind that plans rather than merely reacts — not a goddess who happens to be clever but Wisdom's own person, sprung fully armed from Zeus's skull the day a headache became a birth. The drawing shows her in calm, unhurried command: a crested helmet pushed back off her brow rather than lowered for battle, the Aegis with the gorgon's face worn loosely across one shoulder, a spear held upright rather than raised, a small owl perched on her outstretched forearm turning its head toward the viewer, a coiled scroll and a stack of tactical relief-maps at her feet beneath an olive branch — the branch she planted to win Athens over Poseidon's own salt spring, judged the better gift by the very city that bears her name.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the arm at the dial's bottom, purple, midnight — Wednesday's Sorrow. Why hand the goddess of clear daylight strategy the darkest hour? Because the owl sees precisely where daylight fails, and purple on this dial is already ruled the night of the MIND — wisdom won in the dark, not the realm of darkness itself, which lives elsewhere on the wheel. Midnight is where a council actually decides something, after the arguing is done.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Wisdom by the seat's own name, and her vice is Greed — the same counsel that plans a city's defense can start hoarding the plan as a possession rather than a gift to the polis. Her cure stands straight overhead: Apollo at noon, whose Generosity cures Greed, while her Wisdom, in return, curbs his Excess.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow suits midnight because that is the hour real counsel is paid for: not the speech given in daylight for applause, but the plan reasoned out alone, after everyone who wanted credit has gone to bed.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the midnight arm at the dial's bottom, mourning purple, Wednesday's Sorrow — the owl's hour, when the goddess of daylight strategy does her actual thinking, far from the applause any plan collects once it works, where no one is yet convinced she is right.
variants.hexa_light: The same midnight post, but the purple becomes magenta — the prism's impossible color, found in no rainbow, existing only where the eye joins the two far ends of the spectrum. No hue suits the strategist better: a mind that sees the whole board by joining what looks unconnected.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the evening arm now drapes in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of **the Artist**, the Mask its craft — and Athena wears it as strategy rather than performance: the owl's calm sees exactly where the Mask conceals and where it reveals, counsel composed as carefully as any tragedy and delivered, like the best of them, after the audience has already gone quiet.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the evening is **Old Age**, and Athena wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — a fitting hour for the goddess whose whole gift is counsel weighed rather than impulse spent. Old Age is Wisdom's own season, and no figure on the dial has done more with a night's patient thinking than the one who was never young in the ordinary sense, born already armed.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she keeps the bottom of the dial — Winter, blue — and shares the arm with no one. It points at the winter solstice, the year's own midnight: the goddess who plans alone stands that watch alone, exactly as her own owl always has, through every winter the calendar has kept.
variants.trio: The Trinity calls Athena up from midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Poseidon, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The strategist is handed the opposite door: not the counsel reasoned out in the dark but the plan already drawn for what comes — hope being wisdom's forward march.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Apollo is light made into prophecy — Zeus's favorite son, given Delphi as his own oracle and made to answer, through the Pythia's smoke, questions his father would rather leave unspoken. The drawing stands him radiant beside the Delphic tripod, its cauldron trailing a thin coil of prophetic smoke, a laurel crown pressing his temples, a golden lyre held loosely and offered outward rather than played, his other hand open in a gesture of giving, a quiver of arrows leaning unused against the tripod's leg as a reminder of the god's harder side, sun-rays fanning behind his head like a second, gentler halo to his father's throne.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the top of the dial, the 12h arm, yellow — Thursday's Joy at the day's peak. He inherits the crown position honestly: Phoebus, "the radiant one," was already half a sun-title before the later poets finished the conflation, and Delphi made him the Priest and Teacher outright — the one Olympian whose whole office was answering, freely, whatever was asked of him.

[[The Lesson]] His virtue is Generosity and his vice is Excess — prophecy and music given without a price is the open hand, and the same hand loosed the plague-arrows on the Greek camp and skinned Marsyas alive for daring to compete. His cure stands at the dial's other post: Athena's midnight Wisdom curbs Excess, while his Generosity, poured down the same axis, cures her Greed.

[[The Hour]] Joy rules noon because noon is generosity performed by the sky itself, and Apollo — oracle, healer, musician, archer — is simply that noon given a face and asked to keep giving past the hour it should have stopped.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the noon arm at the dial's top, radiant yellow, Thursday's Joy at the day's peak — the oracle's own hour, when the tripod's smoke is easiest to read and the god has the least reason to withhold an answer, the whole day arranged so nothing stays ambiguous.
variants.hexa_light: The same crown position, but the prism turns his yellow to green — light's own primary at the top of the wheel, the band the human eye sees most keenly. In the light reading the oracle wears the color of maximum sight: nothing asked of him at noon escapes his notice.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the morning arm now wears alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of **the Priest**, the Bell calling whoever will listen — Apollo's own office in everything but name: Delphi already made him the Priest outright, oracle and healer both, the one Olympian whose entire calling was answering, freely, whatever question was brought to his door.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the morning is **Childhood**, and Apollo wears spring green (#7CE577) — an hour that suits the god of music and prophecy more gently than noon's crown ever did: the fresh, untested brightness of a gift still being discovered rather than already proven, the lyre picked up before the day has asked anything hard of it yet.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he returns to the top: 12h, Summer, yellow, sharing the arm with Zeus. It points at the summer solstice, the year's longest light, where the throne and the son who answers on its behalf hold the peak of the year together, as they have always shared a summit.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Apollo keeps the top: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, shared with Demeter, and the arm centers the day's third, 8h to 16h, noon at its tip. Faith reads naturally through the oracle: prophecy is faith given a working method, an answer trusted before the outcome is proven.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Hera is marriage raised to the rank of an institution — queen before she was ever called wife, sister to Zeus before his consort, and the one Olympian whose whole portfolio is the vow itself rather than what the vow permits. The drawing seats her in full queenly composure: a tall diadem crown catching the light, a wedding veil draped formally over one shoulder rather than worn, a scepter topped with a small cuckoo held upright as the marriage-oath emblem — the very bird Zeus once turned himself into, wounded and shivering, to win the pity that became her hand — a peacock with its tail fanned wide standing sentinel beside the throne, its hundred eyes reading as devotion from the front and as watchfulness from the side.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the arm at 20h, deep red, the evening — Friday's Passion. The placement holds even for an institution: the marriage that survives is the one renewed every evening when the work is done, not merely sworn once at the altar. Red is doubly hers by then — the sunset's color and the hearth-fire's.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Love as vow rather than impulse, and her vice is Jealousy — not love's opposite but its shadow, and no myth on the dial illustrates a vice more thoroughly than hers: Io, Leto, Semele, Heracles, a whole catalog of rivals answered one by one. Her cure stands across the dial in green morning: Demeter's Patience heals Jealousy, while her own Love, in return, dissolves Envy. She is the Artist by the seat's own rule, and the wedding hymn is her medium: a vow is a composed thing, built to be kept in the same way a poem is built to be remembered.

[[The Hour]] Passion rules the evening because that is when the day's business ends and the marriage — the thing actually chosen, not merely inherited — is what remains.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 20h arm, deep red, Friday's Passion of the evening — the queen's own hour, when the day's business ends and the vow renewed at the hearth outlasts whatever the daylight demanded of it, kept quietly, with no audience left to perform it for.
variants.hexa_light: The same evening arm, but the red goes pure — light's first primary at full strength. In the prism world the marriage vow is not a pigment mixed and softened on a palette but an elemental beam: the one color that needs nothing added to be believed on sight.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the sunset arm now wears iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of **the Soldier**, the Sword drawn in defense — and no goddess on the dial has defended a vow more relentlessly: Hera's marriage is a fortress held against Zeus's own wandering, Io and Leto and Semele each a campaign fought to keep one oath standing.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the sunset arm is **the Elder** — a life's harvest gathered in — and Hera wears ember (#FF6A3C). The queen of Olympus wears it as an institution's own long memory: marriage is not sworn once and forgotten but renewed at every evening's close, the ember of a vow that has already survived more seasons than most gods bother to count.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she stands at 18h — Autumn, red — sharing the arm with Artemis. It points at the autumn equinox: the queen who answers every rival and the huntress who answers every insult share a single arm, two different disciplines for the same refusal to be crossed.
variants.trio: Hera does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red, for Love alone belongs to both the seven-arm set and the theological three. Artemis joins her there, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the vow and the watch that keeps it safe standing one arm apart.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Demeter is the harvest as a mother's patience — grain and grief in the same body, for no other Olympian's private sorrow ever became a season. The drawing seats her with a sheaf of ripe wheat cradled in one arm and a lit torch raised in the other — the torch she carried searching the world without rest — a young woman's hand just visible clasping hers at the frame's edge as if newly returned, a bare wintered field on one side of the throne and the same field green and sprouting on the other, poppies growing at her feet. She taught Triptolemus the art of sowing and sent him out to spread it, which makes her agriculture's first teacher as much as its goddess.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the arm at 8h, green, the morning — Saturday's Renewal. Unlike the titan who once claimed this same arm, there is no paradox to explain here: the grain goddess needs no forcing onto the color of new growth. Green is dew and shoot, and Saturday is the day of rest whose planet, in Hebrew, is named for the Sabbath itself.

[[The Lesson]] Her virtue is Patience, learned across the exact nine days she searched before Zeus intervened, and her vice is Envy — not one myth but a mood: the grief that begrudges every other field its daughter, every other mother her spring, while hers stays below. Her cure stands across the dial in Hera's red evening: Love dissolves Envy, while her own Patience, in return, heals Jealousy.

[[The Hour]] Renewal rules the morning because Persephone's return is the myth Renewal was named after: a third of every year spent in true grief, followed by the exact rebirth green promises. No figure on the dial pays the hour off more literally.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 8h arm, harvest green, Saturday's Renewal of the morning — the grain-mother keeping the hour when whatever was buried through her nine days of searching finally shows itself above the soil, green proof the waiting was never wasted.
variants.hexa_light: The same morning arm, but the green cools to cyan — morning read as sky rather than field, the pale clean light before the first shoot has broken ground at all. In the light reading renewal belongs to the air a moment before it belongs to the wheat itself.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the dawn arm keeps field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of **the Farmer**, the Plough turning new soil — and no goddess needs less persuading into that estate than the one who taught Triptolemus to sow in the first place. Demeter is agriculture's own first teacher, the Farmer's whole craft traced back to a single grieving mother's hand.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is **Birth**, and Demeter wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the exact hour her own myth keeps repeating. Persephone's yearly return is Birth argued as fact rather than metaphor: a third of the year spent in true loss, answered every spring by a green that has never once failed to arrive.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she keeps 6h — Spring, green — and shares the arm with Poseidon. It points at the spring equinox, the exact season Persephone comes back: the sea-lord who steadies what shakes and the mother whose whole year finally steadies share the year's dawn.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Demeter to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Apollo — the grieving mother and the god who answers every question about what is coming, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads through her as the hardest patience of all: to trust a daughter's return every single year, and mean it again each time.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Isis is Great of Magic, heka's own mistress and the most venerated goddess Egypt ever raised — sister and wife to Osiris, mother of Horus, and the one deity whose cult outlived the pharaohs themselves, carried by soldiers and merchants until her temples stood as far as the Thames and the Tiber. Her name, Aset, means throne, and she wears that meaning on her own brow: the hieroglyph she carries is the seat of kingship itself, for it is Isis who makes a king by seating one on it.

[[The Plate]] Egypt drew her mid-labor over the body of her husband: the throne sign rising gold from her brow, her arms spread into broad feathered wings as she gathers Osiris's scattered form back into wholeness beneath her, the tyet knot — her own protective emblem — crossed with an ankh at her waist, a collar of beads catching what little light survives in the room where a murdered god is being made whole again by love and craft alone.

[[On the Dial]] On the dial she keeps Monday, riding the deep blue arm at 4h — Calm, the hour before dawn, the stillest watch of the night. No goddess earns that blue more honestly: it is the color of the wing-vigil she kept over the infant Horus hidden among the marsh reeds of Chemmis, and Egypt named her tears in the Nile flood itself — the yearly rise called Isis weeping for Osiris, silver water falling from a silver sky. Silver, the Moon's own metal at the dial's bottom tip, is hers by that same right.

[[The Lesson]] Her two faces are exactly the arm's pair of Serenity and Fear. She worked her spells in perfect calm even while hunted through the marshes with a newborn god at her breast, and cured him of a scorpion's sting by naming every poison it could carry — magic as the Serenity that answers Fear rather than denies it. The Physician's arm fits no goddess better: she reassembled a murdered husband and raised a hunted son on nothing but craft and nerve. Her cure stands straight across the dial at 16h: Horus, the falcon she raised in secret, whose Courage cures the Moon's Fear — while Isis's Serenity, in return, is the steady hand that cools her son's Wrath once the throne is finally his.

variants.hexa_paint: The home post: the 04h arm in deep pigment blue (#002FFF), Calm, the hour before dawn. Isis keeps the last watch before sunrise, wings still spread from the night's vigil, and the paint reads like Egypt's own night pigment — the ground lapis the texts gave the gods for hair.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps her at 04h and sharpens the blue to #0040FF — the pure primary, Calm read as light instead of paint. It suits her doubly: the moon carries only borrowed light across the dark, and Isis's own magic works the same way — heka drawn from the gods' true names and given back as healing, borrowed power returned as light.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the deep-night arm now reads by lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of **the Scholar**, the Book its craft — and Isis fills that seat by trade as much as by night: heka's own mistress, who reassembled a murdered husband and named a scorpion's poison correctly by knowing its text exactly, magic itself a kind of scholarship worked by lamplight when no one else is still awake to help.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the deep-night arm belongs to **the Unborn**, and Isis wears moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the hour of ancestors and of those not yet born, and few goddesses on the dial have stood at that threshold more literally: she gathered a dead god back into wholeness with one hand and raised a hidden infant king with the other, keeping watch over both ends of the same silver hour.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: her arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, and she shares it with Osiris. Wife and husband on one spoke is Egypt's own resurrection told twice — Isis gathered him, and the sown Osiris-beds sprout every spring exactly where her mourning ends: the healer handing the season its returning life.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Isis at her 04h post but renames the watch: the blue arm (#002FFF) now stands for Hope, and she shares it with Thoth — the record-keeper beside the mother who out-magicked him for the sun's own secret name. The arm guards the morning third from 0h to 8h, and no one holds better title to it: Isis proved, gathering a murdered god piece by piece, that even the ended can be hoped whole again.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Horus is the falcon in the sky itself — his right eye the sun, his left eye the moon, and his outstretched wings the heavens from horizon to horizon. Every living pharaoh claimed to BE him: the king's first and oldest title, older than "Pharaoh," is simply his Horus name. Osiris's murdered son, raised in secret by Isis among the marsh reeds, he grew to fight Set for eighty years over the throne of Egypt — a war the temple at Edfu still tells in stone, ending the only way Egyptian justice allows: the rightful heir seated, the usurper cast down.

[[The Plate]] Egypt marked him by the wound that made him famous before the crown that made him king: falcon-headed, eyes rimmed sharp in black, the double pschent uniting Upper and Lower Egypt worn already earned, the Wedjat — his own eye, torn out by Set and healed whole again — carved bright in relief at his chest, a bronze harpoon raised over a coiled serpent pinned beneath his talons: the avenger who does not sheathe the weapon the day he wins.

[[On the Dial]] He stands on Tuesday because Horus is Egypt's own word for a war fought with a right on its side: dies Martis, Mars' burning hour, given not to force alone but to force with a cause. The hexa arm at 16h burns orange — Zeal, the mood of the working afternoon — and no god campaigns closer to that tempo: eighty years of contest, tribunal after tribunal before the Ennead, a claim pressed without ever once abandoning it. The Soldier is this arm's profession, and Horus is the soldier who never stopped filing the case even while he fought it.

[[The Lesson]] Mars carries Courage and Wrath, one fire with two uses, and no myth on the dial draws the line as cleanly as this one: Horus's Courage is a son's claim pressed for eighty years without breaking, while his Wrath is the same fire loosed the day he finally corners Set and will not let the other gods intervene. His remedy stands straight across the dial at 4h: Isis, the mother who raised the falcon in hiding, whose Serenity quells his Wrath — while Horus sends Courage back along the axis to cure the Moon's Fear, the son repaying the mother who hid him from a murderer until he was strong enough to face one.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 16h, ember orange, Zeal — Horus's campaign hour, the working afternoon when a claim is pressed and pressed again; the falcon rides the day's stubborn fire.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette Horus keeps 16h but the ember flares to pure yellow — pigment's orange has no seat in the prism wheel, so the afternoon burns at light's brightest hue. For this god the flare is doctrine: his right eye IS the sun, and the war-arm blazing solar yellow is simply Horus looking back at himself.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the afternoon arm now trades in copper (#C87533) — the Walk of **the Merchant**, the Coin weighed fairly — an unlikely seat for the falcon of war until the myth is read whole: Horus's eighty-year claim against Set was pressed tribunal after tribunal before the Ennead, a case argued and re-argued like an honest ledger rather than simply seized by force.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the afternoon is **Maturity**, and Horus wears working amber (#FFB400) — patience earned rather than given, fitting the god whose war for the throne was won by outlasting an usurper, not by a single young strike. The Wedjat eye, torn out and healed whole again, is Maturity's own scar: proof carried rather than merely claimed.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Horus takes the 18h dusk arm, red for Autumn, pointing at the autumn equinox — and shares it with Bastet, the cat who guards what the falcon has already won. Egypt likes the pairing: the avenger beside the protector at the hour the sun goes west — the throne finally won standing next to the hearth that now gets to keep it.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves Horus to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Bastet — each arm's tip centers its third of the day, and this pair guards the evening from 16h to 24h. Horus finally wears his own red, and Love reads through him as protection: the falcon on the wall so that the throne he won can be kept — courage standing guard over what is now safe to love.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Anubis is Egypt's oldest funeral rite made into a god — jackal-headed because jackals were the animal Egyptians actually watched prowling the desert cemeteries, and the wisest answer to that danger was to put the jackal in charge of the tomb instead of fighting it. He is the god who invented embalming on the very first corpse: when Set scattered Osiris, it was Anubis who wrapped the gathered pieces and performed the rites that made a dead king rise as a god, and every mummy wrapped in Egypt afterward was wrapped in his name.

[[The Plate]] Egypt marked him not as a horror at the tomb door but as its rite-master: jackal-headed in solemn black — the black of fertile Nile silt and of resin-dark preserved flesh, not of death — robed for office, a golden scale balanced in his outstretched hand between a small heart and the feather of Ma'at, an ankh held close at his other side, his stance unhurried and priestly, the calm of someone who has performed this exact rite ten thousand times and will perform it ten thousand more.

[[On the Dial]] His arm is the crown itself: the top vertex at 12h, yellow, noon — the mood of Joy, at the very tip the star points at true solar noon. It looks like the wrong hour for a god of the dead until the ledger is read whole: the Hall of Two Truths is where Thursday's Generosity is finally totaled, every gift given and withheld weighed against one feather, and no priest anywhere audits more fairly. Anubis's gold is the same noon-gold the crown wears, because judgment done in full daylight, hiding nothing, is its own kind of generosity.

[[The Lesson]] Thursday's virtue is Generosity and its vice is Excess, and Anubis reads both off the same scale. Generosity is the heart light enough to rise — a life spent giving leaves little weight behind — while Excess is the heart so swollen with what it hoarded that Ammit waits open-jawed at the scale's foot to eat it. The cure hangs across the dial at midnight: Thoth's Wisdom curbs Anubis's Excess by writing the verdict down exactly as the scale tips, while Anubis's Generosity pours down the same axis to cure Thoth's Greed — the two officers of the Hall of Two Truths, the weigher and the scribe, still balancing each other on the dial the way they did at every judgment in the Duat.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm is the crown of the star: the dial's top at 12h, noon yellow, the mood of Joy — and Anubis's scale was drawn for that seat, held exactly where the top vertex points, at true solar noon. The rite-master in gold, weighing every heart in the one light nothing can hide from.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Anubis at the 12h crown but dresses the arm in green — the middle color of the spectrum, which the prism wheel seats at noon. A fitting robe for the rite-master: green was Egypt's own color of rebirth, painted onto Osiris's skin the day Anubis finished wrapping him — Joy read as the exact shade the dead wear when the rite has worked.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the morning arm now wears alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of **the Priest**, the Bell calling the hour before any scale is raised — and Anubis wears it as the rite-master he has always been: the embalmer's workshop opens at exactly this hour, calm and unhurried, long before the Hall of Two Truths ever needs its golden scale.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the morning is **Childhood**, and Anubis wears spring green (#7CE577) — the one age his office never judges, since no heart is weighed and no mummy wrapped before a life has even had the chance to accumulate anything worth balancing. Childhood is the rite-master's true rest: the ledger not yet opened, the scale not yet needed.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Anubis keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with Ra — the sun that watches every hour Anubis works the ones it cannot see. The summer solstice is the year at its most generous — the longest light, and the rite-master's whole craft is proving that even what goes into the dark is not lost by it.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Anubis keeps his crown: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, shared with Osiris, the very god whose body he first wrapped. The arm centers the day's third from 8h to 16h with noon at its tip. Faith is the rite-master's native virtue in the end: every rite he performs is a bet that the wrapped and weighed will rise again — belief in the unseen, practiced with his own hands.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Bastet began as a lioness and Egypt loved her enough to gentle her. In the earliest texts she stands beside Sekhmet as one more form the war-eyed Eye of Ra could take — but by the New Kingdom the claws sheathed themselves and the goddess of Bubastis became the cat the household actually kept: patroness of music, fertility, joy, and the home's protection from disease and evil, presiding over a yearly festival at her city that Herodotus swore poured out more wine than the whole rest of Egypt's year combined.

[[The Plate]] Egypt drew her exactly that gentled: the sleek cat-headed goddess in an aegis collar of beaded gold, a sistrum rattle raised in one hand — the same rattle Hathor shakes, for the two share the temple's music — and a woven basket of kittens cradled warm in the other arm, her tail curling content around her own ankles, nothing in the frame remembering the lioness she used to be except the readiness still coiled in her stance.

[[On the Dial]] She belongs to Friday because Friday belongs to whatever love feels like once the danger has gone out of it. Her arm is 20h, deep red, the mood of Passion — the hour work ends and a household actually gets to enjoy itself, music starting the way it started every year at Bubastis. The lioness Egypt tamed into a housecat is exactly the myth this hour needs: passion that has learned to purr instead of pounce, without ever quite forgetting how.

[[The Lesson]] Love is her virtue and Jealousy her vice, and no animal on the dial argues the vice more honestly: a cat's affection is total and its territory is absolute, the same purring warmth turning instantly to claws if a kitten or a favorite lap is threatened. Bastet's temple guarded granaries and homes precisely by that possessiveness — she kept vermin and evil out because she considered the whole household hers to defend. Her cure stands across the dial at 8h: Osiris, the grain god whose harvest her own temple cats literally protected, whose Patience heals Jealousy — while Bastet's Love, in return, dissolves Envy, guarding the buried seed the way she once guarded the granary above it.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 20h, blood red, Passion — Bastet on the arm of the evening a household finally gets to enjoy, the sistrum shaking the way it shook every year at Bubastis.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Bastet at 20h and turns her red to the prism's full voltage — pure red, light's own primary. Passion undiluted: the goddess whose one festival reportedly drank more wine than the rest of Egypt's year gets the strongest red light itself can mix.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the sunset arm now wears iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of **the Soldier**, the Sword raised in defense — and the lioness Egypt gentled into a housecat still remembers the posture: Bastet's temple guarded granaries and homes by the same fierce territorial instinct a soldier guards a border, claws sheathed but never quite forgotten.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the sunset arm is **the Elder** — a life's harvest gathered in — and Bastet wears ember (#FF6A3C), the color of a hearth kept warm long after the wine of Bubastis's own festival has stopped pouring. The Elder's ember suits a goddess whose whole later myth is warmth banked down from a lioness's original fire, purring rather than pouncing, but still glowing.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Bastet holds the 18h arm, red for Autumn, sharing it with Horus — protector and avenger on one arm, the household cat and the falcon who finally cleared it of danger. The autumn equinox is the year's dusk, the harvest brought in and guarded — exactly the granary Bastet's own temple cats were kept to defend.
variants.trio: Bastet does not move at all: the Trinity's red Love arm is her own 20h, and Love is already her whole estate — the one virtue standing in both the seven pairs and the theological three. Horus joins her there, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h: music on one side of the door and a falcon on the other, passion and courage keeping the dark third of the day safe enough to enjoy.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Where the Planetary Odin walks, the Pantheon Odin sits: the Allfather enthroned upon Hlidskjalf, the seat that sees into all nine worlds. A heavy cloak replaces the traveler's cowl; his traded eye-socket sits bare beneath a crown; Gungnir stands upright like a scepter, not a walking-staff; Huginn and Muninn perch one to each armrest instead of his shoulders; the wolves Geri and Freki lie fed at his feet, so the god need never beg at another's table. Everything that once marked the wanderer now marks the ruler — the same purchases, worn differently.

[[On the Dial]] His seat is the center, white-gold, Sunday — the throne every arm answers to. He once answered only for Wednesday's midnight, the wanderer's own purple arm; the Pantheon promotes him to the hub they all turn on, since no other northern god sat atop so many myths before the throne was offered: an eye traded for wisdom, nine nights hanged for runes, knowledge gathered under one hood now crowned.

[[The Lesson]] And the center is DOUBLE, as always: two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat. As Allfather he practices Justice, a wanderer-king's own, earned testing mortal halls in disguise before judging one from a throne — but that claim is stalked by Pride, the hunger that hanged him on the tree, now certain no secret is worth leaving unbought. As the Wanderer he practices Humility, the beggar turned from doors by hosts who never knew him — but Servility is that disguise gone wrong, knowledge gathered by a king who never answers for how the beggar got in. Glory is Valhalla's light, warriors under a hall bought by sacrifice already made; Awe is the tree itself: nine nights hanging, pierced by his own spear, given to himself.

[[The Hour]] So his mood, like Sól's before him, is not one of the six but their spring — except where she lit every hour equally, he has already bargained for what each one privately knows. Ragnarok is the one bill even the Allfather cannot cancel, and his throne does not slow the wolf already loose.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the center of the dial, white-gold, the throne every arm already answers to. Sunday does not ride the wheel of hours — it is the hub the Allfather claimed the day he stopped merely wandering it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism reading repaints every arm, but not him: white stays white in both worlds, the one color pigment and beam never argue about. Odin alone stands unchanged at the center while the six seats he once merely visited change color around him.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the noon arm crowns itself in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of **the King**, his own hard-bought office — and Odin wears it honestly for once: Hlidskjalf already seats him above all nine worlds, an eye and nine nights on the tree paid in advance for exactly this Crown.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is **Youth** — life's blazing zenith — and Odin wears sun-yellow (#FFE800), an odd hour for a god famous for what age and sacrifice cost him. Yet Youth fits the wanderer who traded an eye before he ever wore a crown: knowledge bought early enough that its whole life was spent, unspent, on ruling well.
variants.cross: His arm at dial 12h points at the summer solstice, colored summer yellow, shared with Tyr. The throne that sees every world and the hand that was given to guarantee one oath hold the season of longest light together — sight and sacrifice, the sky's two costliest gifts on one arm.
variants.trio: The Trinity is the theological palette: Odin returns to the hub with three arms only — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue — all decomposed from his white-gold center, exactly as they once were decomposed from Sól's. Every secret he ever bought still radiates outward from the same throne.

faces.ruler:
Odin the Allfather is the Ruler face — the wanderer-king enthroned rather than walking, the same god who bought every secret at a price now sitting where all those secrets finally pay their due.

The medallion sets him upon Hlidskjalf, the high seat that sees into every world: a heavy cloak thrown over gilded armor, the empty socket of his traded eye left bare and unhidden beneath a crown rather than a wanderer's hood, the spear Gungnir held upright like a scepter at his shoulder, Huginn and Muninn perched one to an armrest, the wolves Geri and Freki watchful at his feet.

His virtue is Justice, the wanderer-king's own — the god who walked every road in disguise before he ever judged a hall from a throne, so his verdicts carry a beggar's memory inside a king's authority; but that same claim to have seen everything is stalked by Pride, the same hunger that hanged him nine nights on the tree for wisdom now certain no secret in any of the nine worlds is worth leaving unbought. His mood is Glory, Valhalla's own hall-light: warriors chosen, half the slain, feasting under a roof built from a sacrifice already paid before the first of them arrived.
faces.servant:
Odin the Wanderer is the Servant face — not a separate god but the same Allfather with the crown set aside, the beggar-guise he wore across every hall long before Hlidskjalf ever needed defending.

The plate reuses the standing wanderer-sage exactly as the Planetary Wednesday medallion renders him: the eyepatch over the traded eye, Gungnir gripped low in his fist with runes only faintly glowing rather than commanding, Huginn and Muninn still perched at shoulder and spear-arm, but a bare, ordinary tree behind him now instead of a throne — the same body, stripped of every ornament that argued rule.

His virtue is Humility, the god who let himself be turned away at doors, insulted, and tested by hosts who never once suspected who stood dripping on their threshold; but that same disguise curdles into Servility whenever the wandering becomes an excuse — knowledge gathered by a king who never once has to answer for how the beggar got in. His mood is Awe, the specific dread of the tree itself: nine nights hanging, no food, no water, pierced by his own spear and given to himself, wisdom bought at the one price that could have simply killed him instead.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Hel sits in perfect stillness at the edge of Helheim: one side of her body a fair young queen in unmarked flesh under a plain crown, the other a rotted blue-black corpse to the bone, both halves seated with the same unmoving calm. Her hall Eljudnir rises out of cold mist behind her; the hound Garm waits chained at the gate; and a ledger of the dead's names lies open, untouched, across her lap — the record of everyone who was never going to fall in battle.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the 04h arm in deep blue, Monday's arm, the hour before dawn, mood Calm. Where Máni merely crossed this hour nightly, hunted, Hel simply belongs to it: the pre-dawn stillness is the closest the living world comes to her own kingdom, half-lit, half-dark, neither one nor the other. The arm's profession is the Physician, and it lands on her with a colder honesty than it ever did on him — she is the ledger every physician's chart eventually closes into, the diagnosis that does not miss.

[[The Lesson]] Blue carries her pair exactly as it carried Máni's: Serenity is the virtue, purer here than anywhere on the dial — the half-dead queen is the stillest thing in the whole cosmology, calm because she is already past whatever could disturb her. Fear is the vice, and it is not hers to feel but to BE: Monday's dark face made a throne rather than a wolf's chase. Across the dial at 16h, in the Pantheon's own reseating, stands Thor: his Courage cures her Fear, and her Serenity quells his Wrath in turn — the same axis Máni and Tyr once kept, worked now by a queen who never fights for her calm and a god who never stops fighting for his.

[[The Hour]] So Calm rules the hour before dawn because it most resembles her — the world briefly neither night nor morning, exactly Hel's own body split down the middle, and the dial gives its stillest watch to the one queen for whom stillness was never an achievement, only a fact.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 04h arm, deep pigment blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — Monday resting one arm below the sunrise line, exactly where a half-dead queen keeps her stillest watch.
variants.hexa_light: Same arm, same hour, but the pigment gives way to the prism's pure blue (#0040FF) — Calm read as light. Of all the arms this one changes least between the two worlds; death, unlike the moon, was never a matter of physics to begin with.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the deep-night arm now reads by lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of **the Scholar**, the Book its instrument — and no queen keeps a more exact ledger: Hel's own record of the dead, open untouched across her lap, is the Scholar's Book made literal, a text that never once needs correcting.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the deep-night arm belongs to **the Unborn**, and Hel wears moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the hour of ancestors and of what has not yet come, and her half-and-half body already argues the same threshold in flesh: one side young and unmarked, the other already past whatever end waits for it.
variants.cross: Her arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, shared with Freyr. The half-dead queen and the harvest lord hold the sunrise arm together — Hel keeps only the coldest half of the year, and lends the warmer half freely to whoever is alive enough to use it.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Hel at her 04h post but renames the watch: the blue arm (#002FFF) now stands for Hope, shared with Loki, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. A strange grace for the queen of the dead — yet earned: she has already proven that even the stillest night ends in a reckoning, and every reckoning, eventually, opens a door.

mars.base:
[[The Figure]] Thor is shown at full swing: winged helmet, braided beard, both hands locked on the short haft of Mjolnir with the rune-carved hammer swept across his body, knees bent in a wide braced stance, the girdle of might at his waist and the cape flaring behind him. It is not a portrait of majesty but of labor — thunder is not something Thor has, it is something he does, and the whole relief is caught mid-blow.

[[On the Dial]] His seat in the Pantheon is the 16h arm in ember orange, Tuesday — not his own Thursday but the mars seat Tyr held in the Planetary set, reseated now for the fight rather than the feast. The arm's profession is the Soldier, and this Thor needs no argument for it: Mjolnir mid-swing is a weapon in the instant of use, not resting between a wedding's blessings, and no god on the dial hits harder or gets hit by more.

[[The Lesson]] Orange carries the Soldier's own pair, and Thor supplies both without straining for either: Courage is the virtue, Midgard's guardian meeting every giant and every serpent without flinching; Wrath is the vice, and no god's temper is better documented — the rages that split mountains, that nearly capsized the boat hauling Jormungandr up from the deep. His cure stands across the dial at 04h, in Hel: her Serenity quells his Wrath, and his own Courage cures her Fear in turn — the soldier and the still queen healing each other along one axis.

[[The Hour]] Zeal, the working afternoon's own fire, suits him even better here than Joy once did at noon: not the table but the swing, banked heat instead of full blaze — the same hammer, the same god, simply working a harder shift than the one his own day used to give him.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 16h arm, ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — Tuesday's fire banked into steady heat, the hammer never far from the forge that makes it ring.
variants.hexa_light: Same 16h arm, but the prism burns the ember to bright yellow (#FFFF00): zeal read as raw light instead of glowing coal — lightning's own color standing in for iron's.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the afternoon arm now trades in copper (#C87533) — the Walk of **the Merchant**, the Coin its fair weight — a strange seat for a hammer mid-swing, yet Thor's whole war against Jormungandr is itself a kind of ledger: giants and serpents kept in balance against Midgard, a debt collected with Mjolnir instead of a scale.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the afternoon is **Maturity**, and Thor wears working amber (#FFB400) — the age of a guardian who has done this fight enough times that the swing no longer needs the rage of a first battle. Maturity is banked heat, and no god on the dial works a harder or steadier shift than the one still swinging at Ragnarok's own approach.
variants.cross: His arm at dial 18h points at the autumn equinox, colored autumn red, shared with Frigg. The hammer that guards Midgard and the devotion that guards one household hold the red season together — two different scales of the same promise, kept.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Thor shifts to 20h and stands under Love's deep red (#B60000), sharing the arm with Frigg — the hammer and the household guarding the evening from 16h to 24h. Love reads through Thor as plain defense: he never asks who deserves the shelter, only that the roof holds.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Loki grins out of the relief — the only smiling god on the dial. Red-gold braids frame the smirk; one hand grips a spear whose head glows poison-green above green-lit runes, the other lifts a golden woven net; a serpent coils over a small fire at his feet and a wolf snarls at the lower edge. Every object is either his invention or his sentence: the net is the tool the gods rebuilt from his own ashes to catch him as a salmon, the serpent drips venom on his bound face, the wolf is Fenrir, his son.

[[On the Dial]] His seat in the Pantheon is the 0h arm at the very bottom of the dial, mourning purple, Wednesday — not his own Saturday but the mercury seat Odin held in the Planetary set, reseated now for cunning rather than wisdom bought at a price. The arm's profession is the Merchant, and it suits a trickster at least as well as it ever suited a wanderer: every object in his own plate is already a transaction, a debt, a trade gone sideways.

[[The Lesson]] Purple carries the mind's own pair, reread as mischief rather than sacrifice: Wisdom is the virtue, cunning sharpened until it works — every trap the other gods eventually escape only because Loki built the lock as carefully as the door; Greed is the vice, and unlike Odin's hunger for knowledge, his is a hunger for advantage, an angle worked for no reason but its own sake.
