His cure stands across the dial at 12h, in Tyr: Tyr's Generosity cures Loki's Greed, and Loki's own Wisdom curbs Tyr's Excess — the merchant and the priest correcting each other from opposite hours.

[[The Hour]] Sorrow, midnight's own color, lands on him strangely and then does not: every plan Loki spins ends exactly the way the myths already know it will, at Ragnarok, on the losing side of his own making — a trickster clever enough to see the ending and unable to stop building toward it anyway.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 0h arm at the very bottom of the dial, mourning purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of midnight — Wednesday at the day's nadir, exactly the hour a trickster's schemes are hardest to see coming.
variants.hexa_light: Same midnight arm, but the prism electrifies the purple into magenta (#BD00BD): sorrow with a neon edge, the midnight mystery read as strange light rather than mourning cloth — mischief caught mid-flicker instead of hidden.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the evening arm now drapes in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of **the Artist**, the Mask its craft — and no god on the dial wears a mask more literally: Loki is the only smiling face in the whole gallery, every trap and trade he builds a small performance staged for an audience he is usually also fooling.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the evening is **Old Age**, and Loki wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — the hour for totaling a life honestly, which is exactly the reckoning the trickster keeps outrunning. He can see every ending the myths already promise him, Ragnarok included, and keeps building toward it anyway, clever enough to read the ledger and unable to close it in his own favor.
variants.cross: He keeps the 0h arm, pointed at the winter solstice and colored winter blue — and alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; Loki has no partner here either, which suits the one god this dial never fully trusted to share anything.
variants.trio: The Trinity raises Loki from midnight to the 4h arm of Hope, blue (#002FFF), shared with Hel — an odd pairing until it isn't: the schemer and the queen of the dead both already know exactly how the story ends, and keep working anyway. Hope is what a wandering mind does with an ending it cannot cancel.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Tyr raises his right arm like a banner, and the arm ends at the wrist: a bandage-wrapped, blood-streaked stump, held high and unhidden, with a snarling wolf's head at his shoulder and a bloodied sword gripped point-down in his remaining hand, runes arcing through the field around him. The stump is the whole story — when the gods bound Fenrir with a ribbon they swore was harmless, only Tyr would lay his hand in the wolf's mouth as pledge, knowing the pledge was false and the hand was forfeit.

[[On the Dial]] His seat in the Pantheon is the 12h arm crowning the dial, noon yellow, Thursday — not his own Tuesday but the jupiter seat Thor held in the Planetary set, reseated now for the oath rather than the storm. The arm's profession is the Priest and Teacher, and the given hand argues it better than any hammer could: a sacrifice made once, in public, so that every oath sworn after it would be believed on his word alone.

[[The Lesson]] Yellow carries the priesthood's own pair, reread through sacrifice rather than feast: Generosity is the virtue, and no god on the dial gave more plainly — a hand, traded outright, no bargain attached, so that Fenrir could be bound at all; Excess is the vice read against the grain, not a table that overflows but a justice severe enough to take a hand and still call the trade fair. His cure stands across the dial at 0h, in Loki: Loki's Wisdom curbs Tyr's Excess, and Tyr's own Generosity cures Loki's Greed — the priest and the trickster correcting each other from opposite hours.

[[The Hour]] Joy, noon's own crown, rests oddly on a god missing a hand — until the myth finishes itself: Fenrir bound is the whole world kept safe one more full turn, and joy is exactly what a promise, kept at cost, buys for everyone else.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 12h arm crowning the dial, noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — Thursday at the top of the world, the given hand held up where everyone can see the pledge still holding.
variants.hexa_light: Same crowning arm, but the prism turns noon green (#00DC00) — the middle band of the spectrum for the middle of the day. Joy read as light: the oath-keeper's peak hour in the color of full leaf.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the morning arm now wears alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of **the Priest**, the Bell calling on the strength of a vow alone — and Tyr fills it by sacrifice rather than sermon: a hand given outright so that every oath sworn after it would be believed on his word, the one relic honesty needs no further preaching to prove.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the morning is **Childhood**, and Tyr wears spring green (#7CE577) — an unlikely hour for a god defined by one costly, adult choice, yet Childhood is exactly what his sacrifice protects: Fenrir bound is a whole world's next generation kept safe one more turn, a hand traded so that youth, elsewhere, gets to stay young a while longer.
variants.cross: His arm at dial 12h points at the summer solstice, colored summer yellow, shared with Odin. The hand that was given to guarantee one oath and the throne that sees every world hold the season of longest light together — sacrifice and sight, the sky's two costliest gifts on one arm.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Tyr keeps the crown at 12h in Faith's yellow (#F8E600), sharing the arm with Freyr, as the two guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads plainest through Tyr of all the gods on this dial: a hand given once is a promise that never needs repeating.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Frigg stands composed and regal, and nothing in her hands is a weapon: a ring of household keys at her belt, a distaff spinning pale cloud-thread in one hand, her other hand pressed flat over her heart. A single sprig of mistletoe rests apart in the lower field — the one plant she never thought to ask an oath from — and her seat, Hlidskjalf, is glimpsed empty behind her, hinting at everything she could see and could not change. Where Freya's image argues love and the sword in the same two hands, Frigg's argues devotion and its one blind spot in the same two.

[[On the Dial]] Her home is the 20h arm in deep red, Friday, the evening hour. Venus' arm is the Artist's, and hers is authored in a different key from Freya's evening star — not the passion that rises with the star but the devotion that stayed up every night before it, extracting a promise from fire, water, iron, sickness, every beast and tree, that none would ever harm her son.

[[The Lesson]] Red carries her pair, reread: Love is the virtue, and it is devotion rather than desire — securing an entire world's goodwill on behalf of one child, oath by individual oath. Jealousy is the vice, transmuted: not fear of a rival but a mother's fierce, all-consuming protection, jealous not of another lover but of every harm that might still reach him. She missed only the mistletoe, too young to swear, and Loki's arrow found that exact gap. Her cure stands across the dial at 08h, in Freyr, the field's own patience: his Patience heals a protection stretched past its breaking point, and her proven Love would dissolve his Envy of a harvest that always risks failing once.

[[The Hour]] Passion, in her case, is grief wearing the evening's own color: not the hour the day's wanting begins but the hour a mother discovers exactly how much she was willing to ask of the whole world — and how the one thing she forgot to ask was the only thing that mattered.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 20h arm, deep pigment red (#B60000), the evening hour — Friday glowing where the evening star rises, though the fire she keeps is a hearth's, not a lover's.
variants.hexa_light: Same evening arm, but the prism saturates the red to full light (#FF0000): devotion as a beam instead of an ember — the evening at maximum intensity, every oath she ever asked for burning at once.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the sunset arm now wears iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of **the Soldier**, the Sword raised in defense — and Frigg fought that exact campaign without ever lifting one: an oath extracted from fire, water, iron and every beast and tree, a mother's whole war waged in promises instead of blades, undone by the one recruit she forgot to muster.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the sunset arm is **the Elder** — a life's harvest finally gathered in — and Frigg wears ember (#FF6A3C), the color of grief banked rather than raging. Her harvest is measured in oaths kept by an entire world on her son's behalf, and the one gap in it, the mistletoe too young to swear, is the single stalk her Elder's ember still cannot stop tending.
variants.cross: Her arm at dial 18h points at the autumn equinox, colored autumn red, shared with Thor. The queen who guards a household and the god who guards a whole world hold the red season together — devotion and defense on one arm, each protecting what the other cannot reach alone.
variants.trio: Frigg stands still: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red (#B60000), for Love alone belongs to both sets. Thor joins her, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the household's devotion and Midgard's own hammer guarding the dark third, love with a shield behind it as well as a heart.

saturn.base:
[[The Figure]] Freyr stands in a ripened field, and every object he carries argues abundance rather than war: the golden-bristled boar Gullinbursti gleams beside him like struck sunlight, a great sheaf of wheat cradled in one arm, a stag's antler gripped in the other hand in place of the sword he traded away for love. His folded ship Skidbladnir rests small and bright at his feet, and ripe grain bows around him in every direction. Where Loki's saturn plate is built from a trickster's inventions and sentences, Freyr's is built from a harvest that never had to be earned by cunning at all.

[[On the Dial]] His home is the 08h arm in green, Saturday, the morning hour, mood Renewal, profession the Farmer. Loki held this seat only by the owner's own paradox — Cronus' empty chair, a bound trickster standing in for a bound Titan on a day the north never gave a god. Freyr needs no such argument: no figure in any pantheon on this dial looks more naturally at home behind the plow, and the arm that once belonged to him by irony now belongs to him by simple fact.

[[The Lesson]] Green still carries a pair, gentler than Loki's: Patience is the virtue, the taught truth of every planted seed, proven each spring whether or not last year's harvest failed. Envy is the vice, read out of the same gold rather than any one act of his — the shine of Gullinbursti beside a neighbor's thinner field, abundance that makes its own envy simply by existing, the one bad year that turns every good one bitter in the retelling. His cure stands across the dial at 20h, in Frigg: her devoted Love dissolves the sourness abundance breeds, and his field-taught Patience heals a protection she has stretched past what any single oath can hold.

[[The Hour]] Renewal ruled Loki's morning only because he forced the paradox to work; it needs no forcing for Freyr, who is simply the green season keeping its promise — the same grain that closes the dial's arm every autumn was already sworn to return the moment his hand first opened it.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 08h arm, morning green (#007E00), the Renewal of the young day — Saturday, the week's rest and restart, and for once a seat that needed no argument to belong to its god.
variants.hexa_light: Same morning arm, but the prism turns the green to cyan (#00DDDD) — renewal read as water-light, the same rain that ripens Freyr's own grain rather than any trick that once caught another god as a fish.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the dawn arm keeps field green (#3E8914) — the Walk of **the Farmer**, the Plough turning ripened ground — and no god on the dial needs less argument for the seat: Freyr already stands in his own harvest, sword traded for love, a sheaf of wheat closer to his hand than any weapon ever was.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the dawn arm is **Birth**, and Freyr wears predawn mist (#8FA8C8) — the hour before a season has proven anything yet. Birth suits the god whose whole calling is what grows after the sunrise: the same grain that closes his arm every autumn was already sworn to return the moment his hand first opened it.
variants.cross: His arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, shared with Hel. The harvest lord and the half-dead queen hold the sunrise arm together — the season that undoes winter, held by the one god who makes it grow and the one queen who never has to wait for it.
variants.trio: The Trinity gives Freyr the plainest promotion on the dial: the 12h Faith arm, yellow (#F8E600), beside Tyr, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith on the harvest lord needs no argument at all — the seed sworn to become grain and the hand sworn to an oath are the same trust, kept two different ways.

sun.base:
[[The Figure]] Perun is the thunder-god, the axe-bearer, the chief of the old Slavic sky — and in this roster he is seated where his rank has always pointed: the throne itself. His tree is the oak, his weapon the axe and the hurled thunderstone, and his sign is the six-petalled thunder-mark — the gromovnik rosette the Slavs carved into their roof-beams to turn the lightning away. When the Rus' princes swore their treaties they swore 'by Perun' first and every other god after: the sky-court's law came before the goods it protected. His great myth is the storm itself: the serpent Veles steals the cattle and dams the waters, and Perun hunts him across the sky and strikes him with the bolt — the combat this Sunday now carries whole, storm above and water below.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion shows him as a broad, bearded warrior in ornate armor and a winged helm, hurling a bolt of brilliant blue lightning from his raised right hand while a great battle-axe hangs ready in his left. Storm-clouds boil around him, mountains stand below, and forked lightning splits the sky. The border is woven from oak leaves and acorns set with lightning-bolts and runes — read at this seat as a king's regalia rather than one arm's livery.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Sunday — nedelja, the 'no-work' day, the day the week rests before it begins again. Perun does not take an arm at all: he holds the CENTER of the dial, white-gold, the still hub the six colored arms radiate from — and those six arms ARE his six-petalled gromovnik, the thunder-mark drawn at full size with the thunderer himself seated at its heart.

[[The Lesson]] The center is DOUBLE — two virtues, two vices, two moods in one seat — and this roster fills it with the combat myth itself. Perun's face is Justice, the sky-court's oath-keeping law, shadowed by Pride, the storm's own arrogance; his mood is Glory, the bolt's white flash. And beneath him the same Sunday wears Veles — the Humility of the yielded throne below, its curdled Servility, and the Awe of deep water. The two old enemies are not king and rival here but crown and root of one day: the storm above is only half the Sunday; the water it falls into is the other half.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette the center is the one color no brush can reach: pigments mix by subtraction, and no blending of pigments ever yields white. So Perun's white-gold hub is the unmixable center — the six arms of his own gromovnik carry the colors out, yet no mixing of them climbs back to the crown. The sky-court hands the law down; nothing painted below can hand it back.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the logic reverses: the six colored beams of the arms, summed at the hub, add up to exactly white. Here Perun's white-gold center is a radiance that is the sum of all six — the thunder-mark proven by arithmetic, every petal's color returning to make the light whole, the sky-court's verdict assembled from every voice beneath it.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the noon arm crowns itself in royal gold (#F0C420) — the Walk of **the King**, the Crown his own by ancient rank — and Perun wears it as the sky-court's own law: the Rus' princes swore treaties 'by Perun' first, the thunderer's Crown backing every oath sworn beneath it before any other god's name was added.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel noon is **Youth** — life's blazing height — and Perun wears sun-yellow (#FFE800), the bolt's own brightness read as an age rather than a weapon. Youth suits the god still hunting Veles across the sky every retelling: a combat fought at full strength, never once softened into an older god's patience.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps a true crossing — four arms meeting at one point — and Perun keeps that meeting-place, the hub where the year's four quarters intersect. The thunderer holds the still center around which spring, summer, autumn and winter wheel — the sky-court seated where every season must pass its judgment on the way to the next.
variants.trio: In the trio — Faith, Hope, Love — the sun keeps the center, and from it the yellow Faith arm rises straight up the 12h vertical (Faith at top, Hope at 4h, Love at 20h). Perun is the white-gold source, and Faith is the first thing that climbs out of him toward noon: the oath sworn 'by Perun' made into the upward-pointing virtue, the vertical his bolt travels turned the other way.

faces.ruler:
Perun is the Ruler face of Sunday — the supreme thunderer enthroned, axe-bearer and chief of the old Slavic sky, his oak the world's most sacred tree and his six-petalled thunder-mark, the gromovnik, the very rosette the clock's own hexagram pointer echoes at the crown of the dial.

The medallion keeps him exactly as the thunder-god has always stood — a broad, bearded warrior in ornate armor and a winged helm, hurling a bolt of brilliant lightning from his raised hand while a battle-axe hangs ready in the other, storm-clouds boiling and mountains standing below — read now as the sky-court's own throne-image: the same boiling storm a canopy over a seated king rather than weather simply passing through.

His virtue is Justice — the sky-court's law, the oath every Rus' prince once swore 'by Perun' before a word of it was written down, the thunderbolt that falls on the oath-breaker and no one else. His shadow is Pride, the storm's own arrogance, the god who can mistake the crash of his own weather for judgment rightly passed. His mood is Glory — the high white flash at the bolt's own strike, the sky-court at its most blinding.
faces.servant:
Veles is the Servant face of Sunday — not a lesser king but the throne BELOW, the horned serpent-lord of waters, herds and the dead whom the old combat myth sets against Perun on this very same day: storm above, water and root beneath, the two faces one Sunday has always worn.

The dual plate changes nothing from Veles's own medallion — the same horned, long-bearded god enthroned on living tree-roots, a shepherd's crook in one hand and a lyre in the other, cattle grazing at his knees and the great serpent coiled through the roots at his feet — because the throne below needs no new likeness; it only needs reading at the bottom of the same day Perun crowns at the top.

His virtue is Humility — the serpent who yields the sky and asks only for the roots and the waters, losing the storm's duel in every retelling and never once contesting the crown again. Its shadow is Servility, humility curdled into bare submission, the throne below mistaken for no throne at all. His mood is Awe — not Glory's blaze but the hush at the bottom of the world, the deep water Perun's own rain becomes once it has finally fallen all the way down.

moon.base:
[[The Figure]] Mokoš is the great goddess — the only goddess Prince Vladimir set among the idols of his Kyiv pantheon, standing alone among the gods. She is the spinner: the flax and the wool, the distaff and the thread, and by extension the thread of fate itself, measured out and cut. And she is Moist Mother Earth, Mati Syra Zemlya — her very name holds the wet: mok-, moisture itself. That name is why this roster seats her here: the blue seat is the water seat, and Mokoš is the one Slavic deity whose name IS water — the damp ground, the well, the rain soaked in and held.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion — the same plate the Planetary roster hangs on Friday, reseated here unchanged — shows her veiled in a copper-bronze robe, a shawl over her long hair, turning a distaff wound with pale flax; a harvest-sickle lies across her lap, full sheaves of wheat stand to either side, and a serpent coils at her feet along the ground. Read at this seat the picture changes meaning without changing a line: the veil is a nurse's veil, and the hands that spin are the hands that tend.

[[On the Dial]] Her day is Monday — ponedeljak, the day 'after nedelja,' the first step out of rest and still half in the dark. Mokoš takes the 4h arm, low on the left of the dial, in deep blue — the hour before dawn, when the household sleeps and someone is nevertheless awake, keeping it. Her mood is Calm: not the held breath of an empty night but the settled quiet of a watched one — the Physician's hour, the mother sitting up with whoever needs sitting up with.

[[The Lesson]] Her axis is Serenity against Fear. The dark is where fear breeds — every shape a threat, every sound a wolf — and Mokoš answers it the way water answers fire: with the deep, damp steadiness of the ground itself, the wet mother whose stillness soaks the panic out. Her cure lies across the dial in the mars arm: the Courage of Svetovid is what ends the night her Serenity holds. Care keeps the dark bearable; courage makes it end.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Mokoš holds the 4h arm in a deep blue (#002FFF) — the pigment of the pre-dawn hour, night thinned but not yet broken. It is the mixing-blue of the darkest part of the wheel, and hers by name: the color of damp earth before first light, the wet mother's own hour, when the dew settles and the ground drinks.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the same 4h arm burns as pure primary blue (#0040FF) — one of the three beams the white hub decomposes into. Mokoš is no longer a mixed pigment but a fundamental ray: the blue that light itself is built from, water's own frequency standing clean in the prism wheel, the moist mother reduced to her element.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the deep-night arm now reads by lamplit ink blue (#1F5FA8) — the Walk of **the Scholar**, the Book its instrument — and Mokoš keeps that watch the way she keeps every thread: patiently, by hand, the same care that spins flax also holding a sick-house steady through the hour the household most needs a text it can trust.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the deep-night arm belongs to **the Unborn**, and Mokoš wears moonlight silver (#C8D7F0) — the hour of ancestors and of those not yet come, fitting for the goddess whose own name means the wet ground that holds every seed before it rises. Moist Mother Earth already keeps both ends of that silver threshold, buried and unborn, in the same damp soil.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Mokoš to the 6h arm, colored spring green and aimed straight at the vernal equinox — and here she shares the arm with Morana, the saturn body. The pairing is the rite itself: the vernal equinox is the day Morana's straw effigy is drowned, and the water that receives it is Mokoš's. The wet mother and the winter-death stand on one spring arm — the drowning and the water that does it.
variants.trio: In the trio Mokoš keeps the 4h arm, but it is now the Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF) — and she shares it with Svarog, the mercury body. The last dark hour becomes the hour of Hope: the mother who tends the night and the smith who rests from it stand together, the care that outlasts the dark as the promise that the sun will rise.

mercury.base:
[[The Figure]] Svarog is the heavenly smith — the sky-fire forge-god the old Hypatian Chronicle glosses onto the Greek Hephaestus, father of Dažbog the giving sun, so every ray his son pours out began as a spark in his father's forge. His name carries the old root svar-, the sky's own brightness, and the chroniclers credit him with dropping the first tongs from the heavens and binding one husband to one wife under a single forged law — order struck out of raw ore the way a blade is struck out of raw iron. Where Perun rules by the bolt and Veles by the flood, Svarog rules by the hammer: the sky is his workshop, and every fixed star is a spark he never let go out.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion seats him bearded and broad-shouldered at his celestial forge, a solar-fire hammer raised over a dark iron anvil. Sparks scatter upward into a field of stars he is shown forging one at a time, and tongs in his other hand grip a half-formed sun-disc glowing white-hot — the sun his son will one day carry across the sky. Banked coals glow behind him like a second dawn, and the border is the theme's own rodovik solar-wheel band, wheat-sheaf roundels worked through with tiny hammers and tongs.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Wednesday — sreda, 'the middle,' the hinge of the week, as it has always been for whoever holds this seat. Svarog takes the very BOTTOM of the dial, the 0h/24h arm at midnight, in mourning purple — not a grave this time but a cooling forge, coals banked to embers while the smith rests between one day's work and the next. His mood is Sorrow: the hour the hammer falls silent and the day's sparks settle into the sky as fixed, cold stars, waiting to be struck alight again at dawn.

[[The Lesson]] His axis is Wisdom against Greed. The smith-god is Wisdom itself — order struck out of raw ore, the craft that turns chaos into law and tool — but the same forge that shapes the world can close its doors, and that is Greed: the craft hoarded, the fire kept for one hand only. His cure is across the dial in the jupiter arm: Dažbog's Generosity, the open horn his father's forge first hammered into being, melts the smith's Greed back down. Svarog makes the sun-disc; only his son's giving completes the gift.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Svarog holds the 0h arm in mourning purple (#542E85) — a mixed, bruised color, red and blue ground together into the hue of a forge gone cold. It is the pigment of the smith's own midnight, the deepest, most blended dark on the wheel, the color of the banked coals Svarog tends until dawn relights them.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette his purple resolves to magenta (#BD00BD) — the strange color the prism itself cannot make, that only appears where the spectrum's two ends are bent around to touch. Fitting for the god who welds two things into one: Svarog wears the hue born only where two ends are forced together, the smith's own trick played back to him by light itself.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the evening arm now drapes in stage-velvet violet (#7A2E8E) — the Walk of **the Artist**, the Mask its craft — an unlikely seat for a smith until the forge is read as a stage: Svarog hammers each fixed star into the sky one at a time, a maker's performance played out in sparks instead of paint, every constellation a finished piece he never signs.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the evening is **Old Age**, and Svarog wears dusk violet (#9C6BD4) — the hour a long day's work finally banks its coals. Old Age suits the sky-smith exactly: not exhaustion but earned rest, the hammer set down only because the work of an entire heaven has, for once, been finished for the day.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Svarog on the 0h arm, aimed at the winter solstice and colored winter blue — and alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; the smith shares none. The forge holds the year's longest night by itself, the hammer resting at the bottom of the year and of the dial alike — the one night even the sky-smith lets his own fire go all the way down.
variants.trio: In the trio Svarog climbs to the 4h Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF), and shares it with Mokoš, the moon body. The smith rises from his midnight forge to stand on the arm of Hope beside the calm night-mother — the ordering Wisdom leaning toward dawn, a night's worth of banked sparks finally catching light at the promise of morning.

jupiter.base:
[[The Figure]] Dažbog is the giving sun — his very name binds dati, to give, to bog, god: the god who gives. He is the son of Svarog the sky-smith, and he rides the daylight across the heavens in a chariot, dealing out warmth and grain and fortune to whoever stands beneath him. The Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the Slavs themselves Dažbog's grandchildren, vnutsi Dazhbozhi — a whole people descended from an ancestor who asks nothing and pours out everything. In this roster his seat is written by his own name: Dažd-bog, the giver, on Generosity's day.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion — the Planetary Sunday plate, reseated here unchanged — paints him as a radiant youth of silver skin crowned with a golden winged helm, an eagle spreading its wings above his brow and a corona of long golden rays behind his head like a second sun. In the crook of one arm he cradles a horn of plenty spilling silver coins and ripe wheat; in the other hand he lifts a scepter capped with a blazing sun-star, flames of amber and gold filling the field and wolves and glowing runes prowling the border.

[[On the Dial]] His day is Thursday — četvrtak, 'the fourth' — and he takes the TOP of the dial, the 12h arm, the crown of the star, in noon yellow. His mood is Joy: the high, ringing gladness of the sky at its brightest — and no figure earns noon more honestly, because noon is the hour the sun gives hardest, the horn tipped all the way over, the whole day's warmth handed down at once.

[[The Lesson]] His axis is Generosity against Excess. The giving is his name and his nature — the day dealt out like bread, the horn that empties itself over every field alike — but a sun that gives without measure scorches what it means to ripen, and that is Excess, the gift that gives too hard. His cure is across the dial in the mercury arm: the Wisdom of Svarog — his own father, the smith who knows exactly when the metal is done. The son pours; the father knows when to stop pouring. Generosity fills the horn; wisdom decides its tilt.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Dažbog crowns the 12h arm in noon yellow — the pure high pigment of midday, the brightest color the wheel puts at the top. The crown-petal glows the yellow of the sky at noon, Joy given the boldest hue on the dial, straight up where the giving is heaviest and the light pours hardest.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the top arm resolves to green — because green sits at the exact middle of the visible spectrum, and the middle of the spectrum belongs at the middle of the crown. Dažbog's noon becomes green here: the giving sun wearing the balance-point of all light, the center wavelength raised to the zenith — the gift weighed exactly even.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the morning arm now wears alb ivory (#EDEDE0) — the Walk of **the Priest**, the Bell ringing freely for whoever stands beneath it — and Dažbog needs no ordination for the office: his own name means the god who gives, a horn of plenty tipped over an entire people who call themselves, in the old song, his grandchildren.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the morning is **Childhood**, and Dažbog wears spring green (#7CE577) — the giving sun's warmth still rising rather than yet at its noon crest. Childhood suits the god whose whole myth is generosity offered before it is ever asked for, the horn already pouring while the day itself is still young.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Dažbog at the 12h arm, colored yellow and aimed at the summer solstice — and he shares it with Perun, the sun body. The giving sun and the thunderer stand together at the top of the year: high summer is the season of both the ripening light and the ripening storm, the horn and the rain crowning the dial as one.
variants.trio: In the trio Dažbog keeps the crown at 12h, now the Faith arm in Faith yellow, shared with Morana, the saturn body — and he guards the day third of the dial from 8h to 16h. The giver at the top of the vertical holds the arm of Faith over the bright working hours: the trust that the horn will pour again tomorrow, which is the only faith a working day truly runs on.

venus.base:
[[The Figure]] Lada is the folk-songs' own goddess of love and the spring wedding — never carved among the idols Prince Vladimir set on his hill above Kyiv, but sung into being a thousand times over in the refrain that still opens half the wedding and ritual songs of the Slavic world: oj, Lado, lado moje. Serbian girls still carry her name every spring in the lazarice and kraljice processions, wreathed and singing door to door before Easter for a good harvest and a good match. Honesty owes her one caveat the other gods don't need: serious Slavicists still argue whether Lada was ever a worshipped deity, or whether chroniclers built a goddess out of a refrain that once meant no more than 'dear' — the songs kept singing her regardless, and a thousand years of wedding choirs outvote a footnote.

[[The Plate]] The owner's medallion crowns her with a woven wreath of spring flowers, a wedding garland of interlaced blossoms draped across her open arms as though handing it to the viewer. Her robe is carved with a running band of folk embroidery — the red-and-white geometric vez every Slavic bride still stitches into her dowry — and a pair of doves settle on her shoulders while ripe red berries and a trailing bridal ribbon coil at her feet. The border is the theme's own rodovik solar-wheel band, wheat-sheaf roundels worked through with tiny woven wreaths.

[[On the Dial]] Her day is Friday — petak, 'the fifth' — and folk memory keeps a warmer name behind it too, the day the wedding songs claim as their own and still count lucky for a match. On the dial she takes the 20h arm, deep in the evening, in blood red, and her mood is Passion — the warm fire of the kolo circling at dusk, the same red heat the venus arm has always carried, now sung rather than spun.

[[The Lesson]] Her axis is Love against Jealousy. Lada is Love by name and by custom — the wreath offered, the ribbon tied, an entire village singing a couple into the kolo — but the songs keep one dark verse for the shadow: Jealousy, the beloved who will not let the dance end. Her cure is across the dial in the saturn arm: the Patience of Morana, the frozen ground that waits out a whole winter without grasping, steadies the Jealousy. A love that can wait for spring need not hold the wreath so tight.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Lada holds the 20h arm in blood red — the deep mixed crimson of the evening, of the wedding ribbon, the ripe rowan berry, and the blush the kolo dance raises. It is the warmest pigment on the lower dial, the color of Passion given body, the evening turned the red of the love-song.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette her arm resolves to pure red — one of the three primary beams the white hub breaks into. Lada's evening becomes a fundamental ray: not the mixed crimson of the pigment but the clean elemental red that light itself is built from, Passion stripped down to the note the folk-song was singing all along.
variants.octa_paint: On the Compass the sunset arm now wears iron-blood crimson (#A02020) — the Walk of **the Soldier**, the Sword raised in earnest — a strange dress for a wedding goddess, yet the wreath she offers is fought for just as fiercely: a village's whole chorus mustered every spring to defend one match against a hard winter and a harder harvest, oj Lado sung like a battle-cry for love.
variants.octa_light: In the Ages wheel the sunset arm is **the Elder** — a life's harvest finally gathered in — and Lada wears ember (#FF6A3C), the kolo's fire banked into memory. The Elder suits a goddess sung rather than carved: a thousand years of wedding choirs keeping her ember lit long after any single festival's flames have gone out.
variants.cross: The seasons cross sets Lada on the 18h dusk arm, colored red and aimed at the autumn equinox — shared with Svetovid, the mars body. Autumn was the season the wedding feasts were sung, once the grain stood threshed and the bare fields held room for a kolo — the same red dusk shared by the sickle and the sword that kept the harvest safe to dance over.
variants.trio: In the trio Lada keeps the 20h arm — and here the red Love arm is truly hers, the virtue that is her whole nature turned into a direction. She shares it with Svetovid, the mars body: the love-goddess's Love and the war-god's guard on the same evening arm, the wedding song and the sword that keeps the dancers safe to sing it.

rows: At the top of the trio, in the noon yellow of Joy, stands The One — God read as the union of the two below him. He loves himself AND questions himself, holding Pride and Weakness in a single person, and because he carries both he corrects his own deeds and can be neither manipulated nor steered. His own verse names him: "I am the Alpha and the Omega... who is, and who was, and who is to come" (Revelation 1:8) — and on this dial Omega already keeps the 24h seat while Sigma is the union of the two, so the geometry says what the scripture says. E PLURIBUS UNUM: out of many, one; "I AM THAT I AM" (Exodus 3:14), the name that needs no other.

As the Judge he is the one who knows all and sees all, and therefore the only one fit to weigh the case. He sits at the PRESENT — the sole ideal seat, for the past never happened and the future never comes — and from it he answers the Prosecutor's zeal and the Advocate's mercy without becoming either. NOVUS ORDO SECLORUM is his standing prophecy: Virgil's "the great order of the ages is born anew" (Eclogue IV), a new One always coming to lead mankind toward a better day.

On the red evening arm of Passion sits The Devil — Pride, love turned back upon itself, and in these articles the face of enjoyment and hedonism. His is the Boss syndrome, the appetite that would have everyone please him; the tempter of Matthew 4 who offers the bread, the glory and all the kingdoms of the world at once. His creed is "let us eat and drink; for tomorrow we die" (1 Corinthians 15:32, after Isaiah 22:13) — the future devoured now, in one sitting.

As the Prosecutor he is the Accuser, KATEGOROS (Revelation 12:10), the adversary who walks the earth in Job and stands at Joshua's right hand to accuse in Zechariah 3. He wants to punish and enjoys punishing, and he strikes FORWARD — the one who seeks to ruin a future the way the red arm forever imagines and wants MORE. His Wrath is real, and only the cross-dial mercy answers it.

On the blue pre-dawn arm of Calm stands Jesus — Weakness, love turned wholly toward others, and in these articles the face of renunciation. His is the Servant syndrome, the one who pleases everyone at his own cost: "let him deny himself, and take up his cross" (Matthew 16:24), "not my will, but thine, be done" (Luke 22:42). Where the Devil keeps everything, he keeps nothing back.

As the Advocate he is literally the PARAKLETOS (1 John 2:1), the one who always steps in to defend the other. He works in the PAST — the advocate pleads for deeds already done, and the blue arm is the gaze that stays behind; his shadow is Judas's, to drown in old mistakes, and Hope is the medicine he must not refuse ("forgetting those things which are behind" — Philippians 3:13).

At the center of the reveal burns the Eye of Providence — the union of the three arms, the Great Seal's All-Seeing Eye read through DOMY. Its triangle shows the three arm colors, one side each for the Judge, the Prosecutor and the Advocate; its iris is the only uncolored glass on the dial, objectivity as the clear light that no hue bends. ANNUIT COEPTIS — Providence favors the undertaking — is the Eye's own motto, the watching that steadies the court: the family's union is the fire that warms, the court's is the Eye that never sleeps.

At the apex, in spring green, stands The Child — new, growing, unformed, the one the two below STRIVE FOR. In the paint trio the top is the one who JUDGES; in the light trio the top is the one who is SERVED — the same triangle read once as the hierarchy of power and once as the hierarchy of care. Small and in need of safety, the Child's given love flows to the Father, the pillar of the house: the smallest sleeps soundest behind the Shield.

As the Dawn he is the new morning, rebirth without the past — the day born clean, needing no more thought of what is finished. He learns what love looks like by watching it happen above him, for "the most important thing a father can do for his children is to love their mother" (Theodore Hesburgh). The home runs against the clock: love in a family flows backward in care and forward in growth, and the Dawn is where it returns.

On the light-red arm — Venus's own red, love embodied, on the day already given to the woman — stands The Mother. Her given love pours to the Child, where the flame is newest and most fragile, as nature itself commands; she is the queen of the hearth, its high seat and its leadership. The circuit needs her to be carried in turn, for the one who holds everyone cannot hold herself.

As the Heart she carries the whole house — the Heart in open hands, the healer's mark of Renewal. Someone must warm HER, and warming her warms the whole house through her, which is why the Shield bends toward the Heart. Her gift up the circuit is Leadership, the small Crown of the hearth: the household as her kingdom.

On the light-blue arm of trust and Calm — Hope's arm in the paint trio, the guardian's color — stands The Father. His given love flows to the Mother, not the Child: the Heart carries everyone, so someone must carry the Heart. The strongest bends toward the one who holds everyone else, and in doing so teaches the Child by example what love is.

As the Shield he is security and protection, the pillar the small turn to. He covers the Heart because warming her warms the house, and he stands between the Child and the dark so the Dawn can sleep. This is the family circuit's meaning: safety flows backward to its source, care flows forward to its future — "a threefold cord is not quickly broken" (Ecclesiastes 4:12).

At the center glows the Hearth — the family's union, mirroring the court's Eye: the court's union is the Eye that WATCHES, the family's is the fire that WARMS. Three members, one flame; each gives what they are to the one who needs exactly that, and the love CIRCULATES rather than pooling, so no one is left dry and no one hoards. Theology has a word for this circling dance of love — PERICHORESIS — and the Hearth is its household form, the triangle that is really a circle.

On the summer arm at the top, in the year's longest yellow, burns the Choleric — yellow bile, the element of fire, hot and dry. He is the Commander, the fire that drives, close kin to the Boss syndrome when the blaze turns on its own house: his bright face is drive and decisiveness, his dark face Wrath and tyranny. Hippocrates' oldest reading of man laid on the season of Joy.

His age is The Prime — the noon of a life, powers at their full, the summer of a man. Across the humor square his total opposite is the Phlegmatic (summer against winter, fire against water — the one pair opposed in BOTH qualities), and the winter Calm is the cure his fire most needs.

On the autumn arm, in the deepening red of Passion, sits the Melancholic — black bile, the element of earth, cold and dry. He is the Thinker, depth bought with heaviness, the humor of the artist and the scholar; his bright face is depth, precision and memory, his dark face brooding and despair, the weight turned inward. Autumn's red is Goethe's light going down fighting.

His age is Middle Age — the turn of a life, when the harvest is counted and the evening first shows. His opposite across the wheel is the Sanguine (autumn against spring, heaviness against laughter, the evening of life against its morning), and spring's Renewal is the medicine for his brooding.

On the winter arm at the bottom, in the year's longest blue, rests the Phlegmatic — phlegm, the element of water, cold and moist. He is the Peacemaker, the Calm that outlasts every storm; his bright face is Serenity, endurance and mercy, his dark face sloth and passivity, the calm that never moves at all. Winter blue is Goethe's first color of darkness, the Servant's own hue.

His age is Old Age — the still evening of a life, the water gone quiet. His total opposite is the Choleric at the top of the year (water against fire), and the summer's drive is what his stillness most lacks and most fears.

On the spring arm, in new green, laughs the Sanguine — blood, the element of air, warm and moist. He is the Companion, quick to love and quick to laugh, everyone's friend within an hour; his bright face is openness and joy, his dark face fickleness — he starts everything and finishes nothing, and promises too easily. Spring green is the Renewal of the whole wheel.

His age is Childhood and Youth — the morning of a life, all beginning and no ending yet. His opposite is the Melancholic across the dial (spring against autumn, the morning against the evening), and the Thinker's depth is exactly the ballast his lightness needs.

At the center stands the Throne — the dual seat of the year, ruler and servant in one body. It is Goethe's two poles held together: the summer yellow above (the first color of light, the Ruler, the longest day) and the winter blue below (the first color of darkness, the Servant, the longest night), with the equinox green their exact union. On Sundays the Seasons light BOTH solstice arms — the two faces of one throne, a year apart and one dial tall. Its virtues are Justice and Humility, and "to every thing there is a season" (Ecclesiastes 3:1) is the law it keeps.

On the summer arm of fire stands the Lion — the creature of Mark, whose gospel opens with a voice roaring in the wilderness. He is one of the four living creatures Ezekiel saw carrying the throne by the river Chebar, and the four that stand around the throne in heaven "and they rest not day and night, saying, Holy, holy, holy" (Revelation 4:8). The Tetramorph does not act; it WITNESSES — the eighth archetype, the only one not human, a bestiary standing outside the seven the way the Ninths stand outside the eights. In the older sky the Lion is Leo, the fixed sign of fire, our Choleric summer given a witness.

On the autumn arm of earth stands the Ox — the creature of Luke, whose gospel opens with a temple sacrifice, Zechariah at the altar. Patient under the yoke, he is the beast of labor and offering, the witness whose element is the soil itself. Among the four faces Ezekiel and John beheld, he is the strength that bows its neck; in the zodiac he is Taurus, the fixed sign of earth, our Melancholic autumn keeping watch around the throne.

On the winter arm of water stands the Eagle — the creature of John, whose gospel flies highest of the four: "In the beginning was the Word" (John 1:1). He is the far-seeing witness, the one who looks straight into the light without blinking. In the ancient sky his sign is Scorpio, whose nobler older symbol was the eagle, the fixed sign of water — our Phlegmatic winter lifted on wings around the throne.

On the spring arm of air stands the Man — the creature of Matthew, whose gospel begins with Christ's human genealogy, the Word traced through generations of men. Often drawn as a winged man or angel, he is the witness wearing our own face among the beasts. In the zodiac he is Aquarius, the fixed sign of air — our Sanguine spring, the morning of the year, standing in vigil around the throne with the Lion, the Ox and the Eagle.

At the center stands the Throne — the dual seat of the year, ruler and servant in one body. It is Goethe's two poles held together: the summer yellow above (the first color of light, the Ruler, the longest day) and the winter blue below (the first color of darkness, the Servant, the longest night), with the equinox green their exact union. On Sundays the Seasons light BOTH solstice arms — the two faces of one throne, a year apart and one dial tall. Its virtues are Justice and Humility, and "to every thing there is a season" (Ecclesiastes 3:1) is the law it keeps.

At the top, in yellow, stands The One — God, and the quality is LOVE: the shared summit where love of others (Weakness) and love of self (Pride) meet at the exact point they become sin. He is the union of the two natures the wheel splits below him, the apex of the triangle the banknote draws in G, M and N over the Eye. His diagonal is Love against Hatred — The One against the Devil at the bottom of the dial.

Love here is not the sentiment but the source: the thing every arm is a facet of, and the thing that at its extreme turns to sin. The One holds it whole where the others hold only a face of it — Courage a part, Humility a part, Pride and Fear its ruin. "God is love" (1 John 4:8), and the One is where that love is still undivided.

On the orange arm of Zeal stands Michael the Archangel, and his quality is COURAGE. Revelation 12 holds the whole cast in one chapter: "there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon" (12:7), and the Accuser is thrown down (12:10). He balances the wheel three-and-three — The One, Jesus and Michael against Lucifer, the Devil and Judas.

Courage is the virtue that stands between despair and rage, and Michael's diagonal is Fear against Courage — the Archangel against Judas, the one who FIGHTS against the one who DESPAIRED. His is the fighting virtue turned to its right use: not the Wrath of the red arm but the sword raised for another.

On the red arm of Passion stands Lucifer, and his quality is PRIDE — his Scale window already burns ember-red, so no new glass is cut for him. His is the sin of ascent: "I will ascend into heaven, I will exalt my throne above the stars of God" (Isaiah 14:13). He gazes only forward, drunk on a gorgeous future he feels entitled to.

Pride is Courage overreached, the virtue of the arm beside him turned to madness. His diagonal is Humility against Pride — Lucifer against Jesus, the one who wanted to ascend against the one who chose to descend: "whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased" (Luke 14:11). The cure he refused was Love-in-deed, given to others, now.

At the bottom, in purple Sorrow, sits The Devil, and his quality is HATRED — the shared ROOT of the virtue pair, not their summit. Turned inward it drags Humility down into the self-loathing of Judas; turned outward it turns Courage into Wrath, madness and overreach, the sin of Lucifer. Hatred is the poison that either extreme drinks.

His diagonal is Hatred against Love — the Devil against The One on the vertical axis, midnight against noon. Where the top holds love still undivided, the bottom holds its exact negation; the whole wheel turns on this axis, and every other person on it leans toward one pole or the other.

On the blue arm of Calm stands Judas, and his quality is WEAKNESS — Fear; his Scale window is already midnight-blue, reused here. He is the one who despaired, who could not believe forgiveness was for him, and who stared so long at his own past mistake that the past became a grave. His is the blue arm's shadow: to dwell on what was, the road to depression.

His diagonal is Fear against Courage — Judas against Michael, the one who despaired against the one who fought. The medicine was Hope, the cross-dial virtue he refused: "forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before" (Philippians 3:13). Fear is not cured by argument but by a courage borrowed from across the wheel.

On the green arm of Renewal stands Jesus, and his quality is HUMILITY — renunciation, the same nature as his Trinity seat, love turned wholly toward others. He is the one who descended on purpose, the counterweight to Lucifer's climb: "he that humbleth himself shall be exalted" (Luke 14:11). Green is the arm of things made new, and Humility is the virtue that renews a soul from the root.

His diagonal is Humility against Pride — Jesus against Lucifer, the descent against the ascent. With the six persons seated, every diagonal becomes a person-pair, and this one is the sharpest: the same love, once bent toward the self and once poured out, once climbing and once kneeling.

At the center is the Seal — the banknote hexagram, the rosette whose overlapping heart is CLEAR glass: objectivity as the only glass that does not color the light passing through it. Its six vertices circle six letters drawn from the Great Seal's mottos, colored to our wheel, and they spell G + MASON: God crowning the Eye, and MASON picked out of the letters as the folk-famous dollar-bill star has always done. Its two interlaced triangles are our two doctrines — the Trinity (G, M, N: God, the Master, the Nazarene) and the Union (S, O, A: Sigma, Omega, Alpha) — the court above and the alphabet of time below, interlaced over the Eye, under IN GOD WE TRUST.

At the top, in green, is Gratitude — the highest and purest form of love, shown as GROWTH: the answer that all giving quietly waits for. It is Jupiter's Generosity conjugated between two people, the virtue of the yellow seat spoken in the dual and worn in the light wheel's green. Where one gives and the other is truly thankful, the bond grows the way the green arm grows.

Its shadow is Taking for Granted — the giving received as if it were owed, the fastest way to starve a home. The cure stands across the wheel: Gratitude cures Score-keeping, and Tolerance cures Taking-for-granted — each diagonal healing the other, as the whole dial's cross-cures always do. "Love... beareth all things, believeth all things" (1 Corinthians 13:7).

On the yellow arm of the daylight shoulder is Support — my Courage placed at your service, Mars's virtue conjugated into the bond. It is being someone's working light: standing beside them at the frontier of whatever they face, so that neither fights alone. In the light wheel it wears yellow, the color of the sun you become for another.

Its shadow is the Fight — Wrath between two, courage turned against the partner instead of the trouble. Its diagonal cure is Trust: Support cures Suspicion, and Trust disarms the Fight — the Moon and Mars axis spoken in the dual. "Two are better than one... for if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow" (Ecclesiastes 4:9-10).

On the red arm is Passion — love embodied, Venus's own virtue on Venus's own hue, needing no conjugation because it was always relational. It is the fire that keeps two people turned toward each other, the heat the whole bond banks against the cold. Red is where the light wheel burns hottest, and Passion is what it burns for.

Its shadow is Jealousy — the fire turned to suspicion of the other. Across the wheel Passion burns away Contempt and Respect calms Jealousy (the Venus and Saturn axis conjugated): the flame and the patience cure each other's excess. Love "envieth not" (1 Corinthians 13:4).

At the bottom, in magenta, is Tolerance — knowing the other is NOT you and never will be, and loving them anyway. It is Mercury's Wisdom conjugated, the midnight-talk virtue: two can never love and do all the same things, and Tolerance is the room a bond leaves for that difference. Understanding and empathy fold into it — the same target, one seat.

Its shadow is Score-keeping — keeping a ledger of who gave what, Mercury's Greed spoken in the dual. Its diagonal cure is Gratitude, and it in turn cures Taking-for-granted: the famous verse names it two thousand years early — love "keepeth no record of wrongs" (1 Corinthians 13:5).

On the blue arm of Calm is Trust — the relational face of honesty, the owner's own conjugation law: honesty is a trait of the individual, Trust its counterpart in the bond. It is the Moon's Serenity between two, calm BECAUSE of the other; the body's proof of it is sleeping soundly beside someone. Blue is the still, trusting hour of the dial, and Trust is what stills it.

Its shadow is Suspicion — Fear conjugated, the sentry who never stands down. Its diagonal cure is Support: Trust disarms the Fight and Support cures Suspicion. "Love... believeth all things" (1 Corinthians 13:7) — the bond's decision to credit the other before demanding proof.

On the cyan arm is Respect — what only years can build, Saturn's Patience conjugated, for Saturn IS time. It is the clear, cool air you leave around what you respect: respect keeps a clean distance, and cyan is that clear air made a color. The slowest pillar to raise and the surest once raised.

Its shadow is Contempt — the bond's deadliest poison, Envy soured into looking down. Across the wheel Respect calms Jealousy and Passion burns away Contempt: the patience and the fire trade cures. Love "is patient... it is not proud... it is not rude" (1 Corinthians 13:4-5) — the exact anatomy of a marriage read off the dial's geometry.

At the center is the Union — Zajednistvo, mutual servant-kingship: each RULES and SERVES the other, the throne's dual nature conjugated between two. Its two faces are Odanost, the day face (the union KEPT, the word held before the world) and Bliskost, the night face (the union FELT: the shared topics, the private world of two that exists nowhere else). Its shadow is tyranny of one or erasure of the other; its Ninth is the Child, the union made flesh, carrying the family circuit where everyone gives and everyone receives. "They shall be one flesh" (Genesis 2:24); "a threefold cord is not quickly broken" (Ecclesiastes 4:12) — and Aristotle, asked what a friend is: "one soul dwelling in two bodies."

At noon, the throne hour, stands the King in royal gold — power at its zenith, the day at its height. The Walks are matter, and his matter is the crown's own metal: "by me kings reign, and princes decree justice" (Proverbs 8:15). Across the wheel his opposite is the Wanderer at midnight, the crown against the open road — Pride against Humility once more.

His object is the Crown — the circle of gold that says the power is the office, not the man. It is the noon arm's substance, brightest on the whole day-arc, and the estate every other Walk measures itself against.

In the afternoon, when the market reaches its peak and the deal is closed before dusk, stands the Merchant in copper. His is the hour of exchange, and his substance the coin's own metal; across the dial his opposite is the Scholar at deep night — what a thing COSTS against what a thing is WORTH, the eternal argument of price and value.

His object is the Coin — copper struck round, the token that lets one good become any other. Where the Scholar weighs worth in silence, the Merchant weighs cost in the open, and the day needs both scales.

At sunset, the frontier hour where day meets night, stands the Soldier in iron-blood crimson — the guard at the threshold, the color of the metal and of what it spills. His opposite across the wheel is the Farmer at dawn: "they shall beat their swords into plowshares" (Isaiah 2:4), the sword against the plough, the two iron tools of the same earth.
His object is the Sword — iron given an edge, the estate that holds the line at the border of the light. Courage is his virtue and Wrath his shadow, and the frontier is where both are decided.

In the evening, after dark, when stage and feast and music begin, stands the Artist in stage-velvet violet — the deep cloth of the theatre, the hour of performance. His opposite across the wheel is the Priest at morning: the stage against the altar, two performances that both reach past this world, one toward the crowd and one toward heaven.
His object is the Mask — the face put on to tell a truth the bare face cannot. Velvet is his substance, the evening arm's own dark richness, and the Mask is how the Artist makes the many feel as one.

At midnight, outside the walls and owning nothing but the road, stands the Wanderer in road-dust indigo-charcoal — the darkest arm of the whole day-arc, the Odin-the-Wanderer hour the Norse dual already walks. His opposite is the King at noon: the open road against the crown, Humility against Pride, the man who has let everything go against the man who holds everything.

His object is the Staff — the one possession of the one who possesses nothing, the walking-stick that is road and roof and weapon at once. His substance is the dust of the road itself, worn into the color of the deep night he crosses.

In the deep night, the only one awake with the stars, sits the Scholar in lamplit ink blue — the student at the lamp, the color of ink and the small hours. His opposite across the wheel is the Merchant in the crowded afternoon: what a thing is WORTH against what it COSTS, the quiet ledger against the loud one.
His object is the Book — the lamp-blued page where worth is weighed in silence. His is the hour no one else keeps, and the estate that trades the sleep of the body for the waking of the mind.

At dawn, up at first light with the plough in hand, stands the Farmer in field green — the growing color on the growing hour, the estate closest to the earth. His opposite across the wheel is the Soldier at sunset: "they shall beat their swords into plowshares" (Isaiah 2:4), the plough against the sword, and the same iron between them.

His object is the Plough — iron turned to feeding instead of fighting, the tool that opens the ground the Soldier only defends. Patience is his virtue: he sows and WAITS, and the green arm is the reward of that waiting.

In the morning, at the hour of the mass and the school bell, stands the Priest in alb ivory — the linen of the vestment, the teacher's and the celebrant's hour. His opposite across the wheel is the Artist in the evening: the altar against the stage, two performances reaching past this world, one calling the many to heaven and one to the play.

His object is the Bell — rung for mass and for school alike, the sound that gathers the town at the day's beginning. Ivory is his substance, the pale linen of the morning arm, and the Bell is how the estate of teaching speaks before a word is said.

At noon, the zenith, is Youth in sun-yellow — life at its blazing height, the noon of a man read onto the noon of the sky. No age fits the top of the wheel better than the one that owns strength before the descent. Shakespeare gave the world seven ages of man ("all the world's a stage", As You Like It); this wheel gives his stage an eighth player, and Youth is the one the whole crowd watches.

In the afternoon, in amber, is Maturity — the yoke years, the full green of Youth deepened into fruit-bearing strength. The blaze of noon has steadied into work that lasts; the light has begun, almost unnoticed, to lean toward evening. Amber is the day-arc's turn from gold toward ember, the hour a life stops climbing and starts building.

At sunset, in ember, is the Elder — the harvest of a life, when what was sown across the whole day is finally counted. His is the fruit-laden hour, memory and authority ripened to their fullest before the dark. Ember is the sunset arm's own color, the light richest just as it begins to go — the gathered warmth of everything the day has been.

In the evening, in dusk violet, is Old Age — the leaf-fall, the slow letting-go after the harvest is in. The colors cool; the day's work is done and the long quiet begins. Violet is the twilight arm, purple's gentle light, the hour a life turns from doing toward watching.

At midnight, in pure white, is Death — and in the light register death goes INTO the light, not out of it. "A time to be born, and a time to die" (Ecclesiastes 3:2); the Serbian tongue says a dying man PREDADE DUH, surrendered the spirit — Christ's own word on the cross, "Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit" (Luke 23:46). White is the sum of all colors and the color of the swan's last song; the arm that looks darkest on the paint wheel here burns brightest.

Before dawn, in moonlight silver, is the Unborn — and this wedge holds TWO peoples at once: the ancestors who have passed and the not-yet-born who wait, one moonlight pool of souls. It is the community of souls a life returns to and comes from, the seed in the dark soil and the phoenix in the ash. Here the wheel is about to close and open again — NOVUS ORDO SECLORUM enacted on the dial, the order of the ages born anew with every turn.

At first light, in predawn mist, is Birth — the age that steps OUT of the mist, the sprout breaking soil at the edge of the dark. The rose of the old dawn has retired; birth is the pale grey-blue of the moment before color, life arriving before it has a hue of its own. The wheel that closed in silver and white opens again here: after death and the unborn, dawn births.

In the morning, in spring green, is Childhood — the sapling, the swallow, the growing thing on the growing hour. The mist of birth has burned off into the clear green of a day just begun; everything is reaching upward and nothing yet has weight. Green is the Renewal arm's own promise, and Childhood is the age that keeps it.

Aries.base:
[[The Sky]] Aries opens the zodiac at the point astronomers still call the First Point of Aries, the spring equinox. On the year wheel it begins at 270 degrees, the left side of the dial, the year's own six in the morning, and guards 21 March to 19 April, when everything begins again.

[[The Plate]] The owner paints the ram rearing inside a dark disc, forelegs lifted in mid-leap, one horn wound into a full spiral, while the constellation is only a bent line of a few stars with one bright bend. So much force spent on so modest a piece of sky.

[[On the Dial]] It rides the green arm of Renewal, Saturday's arm borrowed from Saturn, where the virtue is Patience and the shadow is Envy: a hard school for a headlong ram whose ruler Mars burns far away on the orange arm of Tuesday. Its arm-mate Taurus walks the same green path at a slower, steadier pace.

variants.paint: In the paint world Aries wears deep pigment green, the painter's mixture of yellow and blue. It is the green of first grass and wet spring earth, a color made of matter, and it grounds the ram: fire held inside growing things, impatience buried in a seed that must wait for its season.
variants.light: In the light world the spring arm glows cyan, green and blue light added together on the screen. The ram turns electric and cool, dawn air instead of soil, and its charge is no longer buried in earth but broadcast as pure signal, renewal read as energy rather than matter.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the ram leaps out of spring's green into the evening arm at 20h, wearing deep pigment red (#B60000), the hour whose mood is Passion. This is Venus's own arm, and the old story tells itself again in color: Mars's headlong sign lodged in the rooms of his mythic lover, Ares caught in Aphrodite's evening. The southern ram keeps its fire but points it at the heart instead of the season — impatience ripened into ardor, the charge of first grass become the charge of first love — and its shadow shifts with it, no longer Envy but that vice's hotter cousin Jealousy, passion that has begun to grip what it should only warm.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette the ram's evening arm burns pure red (#FF0000), the first primary of the additive world. The cool cyan dawn of its northern reading is gone; what was buried charge is now emitted flame, the ram as beacon rather than seed. Courage broadcast as desire, at full brightness, in Venus's own hour — a signal that does not wait for its season but announces it.

Taurus.base:
[[The Sky]] Taurus is the bull of the oldest sky, remembered as the white shape Zeus wore to carry Europa across the sea. It guards 20 April to 20 May, the arc from 300 to 330 degrees, the heart of spring when the year climbs steadily toward its summit at the top of the dial.

[[The Plate]] The owner's bull stands inside a dark disc with its head raised and one foreleg lifted, horns curving upward, all chest and haunch. The constellation repeats those horns as a long open fork of stars, two bright tips diverging from the cluster at the bull's eye.

[[On the Dial]] It stands on the green arm of Renewal, Saturday's arm under Saturn, teaching Patience against the vice of Envy, and no sign wears that lesson more naturally than the unhurried bull. Its ruler Venus keeps house on the red arm of Friday, and its arm-mate Aries supplies the spark that Taurus turns into steady ground.

variants.paint: In pigment Taurus wears deep green, yellow and blue ground together into the color of pasture and leaf. The subtractive world of matter is the bull's own element: color you can till, weigh and hold, renewal as something grown slowly out of soil rather than switched on.
variants.light: In the prism world the bull's arm shines cyan, blue and green light summed on the screen. The pasture becomes clear water and cold sky, and Taurus grows lighter than itself: the same patience, but read as a calm transparent frequency instead of heavy fertile earth.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the bull comes home: the mirrored year seats Taurus on the evening arm of Passion, Venus's own house, in pigment red (#B60000), an ox-blood crimson as heavy with earth as its northern green was with grass. The myth shifts weight along with the hue, for the shape Zeus wore to carry Europa was always a passion story, and the unhurried grazer becomes the steadfast lover: the same patience re-voiced as devotion, the old envy warming into the arm's own shadow, jealousy, love that holds on the way a bull holds ground.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette the bull's evening arm blazes pure red (#FF0000), the additive primary, and the reading sharpens: no longer pasture or wine-dark clay but signal, desire announced at full brightness. Venus's homecoming beast keeps its slowness, but a slow fire is the longest-burning kind — passion as steady emission, devotion broadcast rather than buried in the field.

Gemini.base:
[[The Story]] Gemini is Castor and Pollux, one twin mortal and one divine, and the myth turns on a gift: Pollux split his immortality so neither brother would be lost alone. The sky keeps them as two parallel chains of stars, two figures standing side by side.

[[The Plate]] The owner draws them as twin women in long gowns, hands joined between them, each raising a leafy sprig in her free hand, mirrored like a reflection. The glyph is two pillars bridged at top and bottom, the Roman two.

[[On the Dial]] Gemini guards 21 May to 20 June, the arc from 330 degrees to the very top of the dial, spring ripening into summer. It sits on the yellow arm of Joy, Thursday's arm under Jupiter, whose virtue is Generosity and whose vice is Excess, the exact moral of its own myth. Cancer shares that arm just across the solstice, and Gemini's ruler Mercury lives on the purple arm of Wednesday.

variants.paint: In the paint world Gemini wears pure pigment yellow, a painter's primary that no mixing can produce. That suits the twins: something first-hand and unmixed, the brightest matter on the palette, joy laid on thick before any other color has touched it.
variants.light: In the light world the noon arm turns pure green, a primary of the prism as yellow is of the palette. Gemini keeps its rank but changes its voice: primary in both worlds, now glowing at the eye's most sensitive wavelength, quick conversation carried as living signal.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and Gemini drops to the midnight arm, and the fall is a homecoming: the bottom of the dial is Wednesday's post, the house of its own ruler Mercury. Wearing purple (#542E85), red and blue pigment folded into the dye of mourning and of kings, the twins show the other face of their myth — Pollux's gift was also a grief, half of all their days spent below — and noon's quick talk deepens into the mood of Sorrow, wisdom traded in low voices, conversation kept like a vigil through the dark.
variants.light_south: In the light palette the southern midnight arm glows magenta (#BD00BD), red and blue light added into a color no single wavelength can carry. Nothing suits the twins better: two lights joined into one impossible hue, Castor and Pollux as pure signal, sorrow emitted rather than worn — the vigil turned luminous, midnight answering itself in light.

Cancer.base:
[[The Sky]] Cancer begins exactly at the top of the dial: its first point is the summer solstice, the anchor on which DOMY's whole year wheel is pinned, and the Tropic of Cancer still carries its name. It guards 21 June to 22 July, the first arc of high summer, 0 to 30 degrees.

[[The Plate]] The myth is small and stubborn: the crab Hera sent to pinch Heracles, crushed underfoot and set among the faintest stars of the zodiac, a dim slender Y in the sky. The owner restores its dignity, drawing the crab from above with both pincers raised open and eight legs fanned out in perfect symmetry.

[[On the Dial]] It sits on the yellow arm of Joy, Thursday's arm under Jupiter, learning Generosity and shadowed by Excess, with Gemini as its neighbor on the same arm across the solstice. Its ruler is the Moon, at home on the blue arm of Monday: a child of night standing guard at the year's brightest gate.

variants.paint: In pigment Cancer wears primary yellow, the brightest matter a brush can carry. There is a tenderness in giving the Moon's shy creature the sunniest paint on the wheel: the crab keeps its shell, but Thursday's yellow warms it into open-handed, generous gold.
variants.light: In the light world Cancer's arm shines pure green light at the top of the star. The moonchild trades gold for the prism's own primary, and its softness turns luminous: not the glare of noon but the green of things that grow through it, joy as emitted light.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and Cancer's solstice gate opens onto midwinter: the crab crosses to the bottom midnight arm and wears purple (#542E85), red and blue pigment folded into Mercury's dye of Sorrow. Thursday's open-handed gold is gone; here the Moon's shy creature learns the dark arm's curriculum, wisdom shadowed by greed, and its shell turns from ornament to shelter. It is the same stubborn guard Hera once sent, but re-tempered — pincers closed around what little warmth the long night allows, patient as tidewater, generous only after it has endured.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette Cancer's midnight arm glows magenta (#BD00BD), the one hue no rainbow contains, conjured by the eye where the spectrum's far ends meet. Nothing suits the Moon's child better: moonlight too is borrowed radiance, and the crab becomes the lantern of the year's darkest gate, sorrow emitted rather than absorbed — a faint, invented shining that the sky cannot make and the mind refuses to put out.

Leo.base:
[[The Sky]] Leo is the Nemean lion, the first labor of Heracles, whose hide no weapon could pierce, set into the sky with Regulus, the little king, burning at its heart. It guards 23 July to 22 August, the arc from 30 to 60 degrees, the furnace weeks just past the solstice.

[[The Plate]] The owner's lion strides in profile, mane flaring like fire around its lifted head, tail carried in a long S with a tufted tip. The stars draw the same mane as the Sickle, a backwards question mark curling above the body.

[[On the Dial]] It burns on the orange arm of Zeal, Tuesday's arm under Mars, where the virtue is Courage and the vice is Wrath, the lion's two temperatures. Virgo shares that arm as its quieter half. And Leo's ruler is unique: the Sun does not live on any arm but at the very center of the dial, the white light of Sunday from which all six arm colors are decomposed. The lion answers to the source itself.

variants.paint: In pigment Leo wears burnt orange, red and yellow milled together into the color of embers and late July. It is a mixed color for a proud sign, and that is its lesson: even the lion's fire is composed, courage blended from passion and joy, matter warmed rather than burning.
variants.light: In the light world Leo's arm blazes pure yellow, red and green light added until they sum toward the Sun's own brightness. Nothing is milled or muted here; the lion becomes a lamp, zeal as radiation, the closest of the six lights to the white center it answers to.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the lion crosses to the pre-dawn arm, trading its embers for blue (#002FFF), the deep worked pigment of the hour called Calm under the Moon. The hide no weapon could pierce becomes a night watchman's patience: Regulus, the little king, keeps the throne while its ruler the Sun is still below the horizon, courage cooled into composure, wrath banked to a guarded stillness, and the arm's own shadow, fear, faced down simply by staying.
variants.light_south: In the light palette the southern lion glows blue (#0040FF), the beam instead of the pigment. Regulus turns pilot light: the king's furnace reduced to one steady signal in the dark before dawn, zeal refined into vigil, a small unwavering lamp that answers fear with the promise of the Sun's return.

Virgo.base:
[[The Sky]] Virgo is the star-maiden, Astraea who left the earth last of the immortals, or Demeter of the grain; either way the sky agrees, hanging Spica, the ear of wheat, as the bright star in her hand. She guards 23 August to 22 September, from 60 degrees down to the autumn equinox at 90, closing the summer at the dial's right side, the year's dusk.

[[The Plate]] The owner draws her winged, head bowed so her long hair veils her face, a sprig held up in one hand, her gown falling in folds; the constellation sprawls wide around that bright hand-star.

[[On the Dial]] She stands, surprisingly, on the orange arm of Zeal, Tuesday's arm under Mars, beside Leo, her louder arm-mate. The pairing is the lesson: the arm's virtue is Courage and its vice is Wrath, and Virgo shows courage in its harvest form, exact, patient and unarmed. Her ruler Mercury keeps its home on the purple arm of Wednesday.

variants.paint: In pigment Virgo wears harvest orange, red and yellow mixed into terracotta and ripe grain. The subtractive world suits her: a color of clay, bread and September fields, zeal made useful, the soldier's hue mellowed into everything the soldier fights to protect.
variants.light: In the light world her arm turns pure yellow, red and green light summed brighter than either. The gleaner becomes lucid: precision as illumination, the lamp on the desk rather than the fire in the field, her quiet zeal emitted steadily at the screen's warmest primary.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the maiden crosses to the Moon's pre-dawn arm, trading the soldier's orange for blue (#002FFF), the weighed-out lapis of the old workshops.
